hilbert_space.name: Hilbert space
hilbert_space.description_popular: Imagine an infinite room where each point is a possible state of a particle. Hilbert space is a mathematical home for quantum states, like an album for all possible photographs of Schrödinger's cat: both alive and dead at the same time.
hilbert_space.fun_fact_popular: In Hilbert space, you can "rotate" quantum states like a magic kaleidoscope, and get superpositions - mixtures of different realities.
hilbert_space.description_simple: Hilbert space is a generalization of our ordinary three-dimensional space to the case of many dimensions, where each dimension corresponds to a certain quantum state. Like in a musical score, where each note sets the pitch, here each coordinate sets the probability of a particular measurement outcome.
hilbert_space.history_simple: The concept was introduced by the German mathematician David Hilbert in the early 20th century as part of research into integral equations. Soon Erwin Schrödinger used it to formulate quantum mechanics, representing wave functions as vectors in Hilbert space. John Stewart Bell in the 1960s applied it to prove the nonlocality of quantum entanglement.
hilbert_space.how_it_works_simple: In quantum physics, the state of any system, for example, an electron, is described by a vector in Hilbert space. Measurement is like projecting this vector onto one of the axes. The inner product of vectors gives the probability of transition between states. The evolution of the state over time is a "rotation" of the vector, determined by the Schrödinger equation.
hilbert_space.description: Hilbert space is a complete vector space with an inner product, a generalization of Euclidean space to the infinite-dimensional case. In quantum mechanics, pure states of a system are represented by rays in a complex separable Hilbert space. Observables correspond to self-adjoint operators. The inner product of state vectors defines probability amplitudes. The state space of a composite system is constructed as a tensor product of subsystem spaces, giving rise to quantum entanglement phenomena.
hilbert_space.history: The concept of Hilbert space was developed by David Hilbert in 1904-1910 while studying integral equations. In the 1920s, Werner Heisenberg and Erwin Schrödinger independently formulated quantum mechanics: Heisenberg used matrix mechanics, and Schrödinger used wave mechanics, but both approaches were unified within the framework of Hilbert space. In 1932, John von Neumann gave a rigorous mathematical axiomatization of quantum mechanics based on Hilbert spaces. Bell's work (1964) and experimental tests by Aspect (1980s) confirmed the fundamental role of Hilbert space in describing entangled states.
hilbert_space.how_it_works: A quantum system is described by a vector |ψ⟩ in Hilbert space. Observables are operators Â, whose eigenvalues are possible measurement results. The probability of obtaining result a when measuring in state |ψ⟩ is given by the Born rule: P(a) = |⟨a|ψ⟩|². Evolution obeys the Schrödinger equation: iℏ d|ψ⟩/dt = Ĥ|ψ⟩. The superposition principle allows the system to be in a linear combination of states. Entanglement appears when the state of the system does not factorize into a tensor product.
key_problems: The measurement problem: how and why does the state reduction occur if the evolution by Schrödinger is unitary?
Compatibility with general relativity: how to describe quantum states in curved spacetime?
Interpretation of quantum mechanics: what is the nature of the wave function - reality or knowledge?
hilbert_space.fun_fact: Hilbert space can be not only infinite-dimensional but also "rigged" to account for states like a perfectly localized particle, reminiscent of mathematical tricks with taming infinity.

formulas.description: Schrödinger equation (time evolution)
formulas.meaning: Determines how the state vector changes over time under the action of the Hamiltonian

formulas.description: Born rule for probability
formulas.meaning: Probability of obtaining value a when measuring observable Â

entropy.name: entropy
entropy.description_popular: Entropy is a measure of disorder. Imagine a child's room: when toys are neatly arranged on shelves, entropy is low. But after a fun game, everything is scattered, and you can only restore order by making an effort. In the Universe, entropy always increases.
entropy.fun_fact_popular: If you shuffle a deck of cards, you will never return it to its original order just by shaking. That's entropy in action - disorder wins.
entropy.description_simple: Entropy is a physical quantity that shows how much energy in a system is "devalued" and cannot be used for work. Like water flowing down a mountain loses its ability to turn a mill, heat dissipates, increasing entropy. In an isolated system, entropy only increases.
entropy.history_simple: The concept of entropy was introduced by Rudolf Clausius in 1865, generalizing the second law of thermodynamics. Ludwig Boltzmann gave a statistical interpretation: entropy is related to the number of ways particles can be organized. James Maxwell proposed a thought experiment with a "demon" sorting molecules, which deepened the understanding of the connection between entropy and information. In the 20th century, Jacob Bekenstein and Stephen Hawking discovered that black holes have enormous entropy related to their area.
entropy.how_it_works_simple: At the microscopic level, entropy grows because there are many more disorder states than order ones. Toss a handful of coins: almost never will all land heads up, because there are unimaginably many ways to scatter them mixed up. Similarly, gas molecules tend to fill the entire volume - that's the most probable, high-entropy state.
entropy.description: Entropy S is a thermodynamic state function characterizing the measure of irreversible energy dissipation. In statistical physics, it is defined as S = k ln W, where W is the number of microstates corresponding to a given macrostate. The second law of thermodynamics states that in an isolated system, entropy does not decrease. Entropy is also a measure of missing information about a system and underlies information theory. In black hole physics, Bekenstein-Hawking entropy links the entropy of the event horizon to its area, uniting thermodynamics, gravity, and quantum theory.
entropy.history: Rudolf Clausius introduced the term "entropy" in 1865, formulating the second law. Ludwig Boltzmann in the 1870s linked entropy with probability, proposing the formula S = k log W. The thought experiment of "Maxwell's demon" (1867) stimulated research into the connection between entropy and information, culminating in the works of Leo Szilard (1929) and Shannon (1948). In the 1970s, Jacob Bekenstein suggested that black holes have entropy proportional to the horizon area, and Stephen Hawking in 1974 confirmed this by discovering Hawking radiation and derived the exact formula.
entropy.how_it_works: According to the second law of thermodynamics, the total entropy of a closed system grows, approaching a maximum. This is due to the statistical nature of molecular motion: the system evolves from less probable states to more probable ones. Entropy is related to free energy: F = U - TS. In quantum theory, von Neumann entropy S(ρ) = -Tr(ρ ln ρ) generalizes the classical concept. For black holes, the Bekenstein-Hawking entropy S_BH = (k A)/(4 l_P²), where A is the horizon area, and l_P is the Planck length. This formula hints at the holographic principle.
key_problems: Black hole information paradox: what happens to information when a black hole evaporates? Contradiction between quantum unitarity and thermodynamics.
Arrow of time: why was the entropy of the Universe low at the moment of the Big Bang? Is it related to cosmological inflation?
Fundamental limit of computation: how do entropy constraints affect computational capabilities?
entropy.fun_fact: Your body is an entropy-producing machine. Each day you live, you increase the disorder in the Universe by about 10,000 joules per kelvin.

formulas.description: Statistical definition of entropy (Boltzmann's formula)
formulas.meaning: k is the Boltzmann constant, W is the number of microstates

formulas.description: Bekenstein-Hawking entropy for a black hole
formulas.meaning: A is the area of the event horizon, l_P is the Planck length

wave_function.name: wave function
wave_function.description_popular: The wave function is a mathematical description of a cloud of probabilities. Imagine you throw a pebble into a pond: the ripples on the water show where the particle might be. It's like a musical chord that sounds all notes at once, until you press one key.
wave_function.fun_fact_popular: If you measure the wave function, it "collapses" into one value - as if from a whole ocean of possibilities, one fish is picked.
wave_function.description_simple: The wave function is like a probability map for finding a particle. If you imagine the sea surface, the height of the wave at each point corresponds to the probability of finding the particle there. It is not the particle itself, but information about it.
wave_function.history_simple: Erwin Schrödinger introduced the wave function in 1926, trying to describe the behavior of electrons in atoms.
wave_function.how_it_works_simple: When we don't observe, the electron is in all possible places simultaneously, like a smeared ripple on a pond. Measurement forces it to take a specific position - just as when you touch water, the circles disappear and we see the point of impact.
wave_function.description: The wave function is a mathematical representation of the quantum state of a system as a complex probability amplitude. It provides a complete description of the system in Hilbert space, and its time evolution is determined by the Schrödinger equation. The square of the modulus of the wave function gives the probability density of finding the particle at a given point, which is the basis of the probabilistic interpretation of quantum mechanics.
wave_function.history: Erwin Schrödinger derived his famous equation in 1925-1926, generalizing de Broglie's ideas about matter waves. Max Born proposed the probabilistic interpretation, and John von Neumann introduced a rigorous mathematical formalism. John Stewart Bell in 1964 showed that local hidden variables do not agree with the predictions of quantum mechanics, which was later confirmed by Alain Aspect in experiments with entangled photons.
wave_function.how_it_works: The wave function ψ(x,t) is a solution to the Schrödinger equation: iℏ ∂ψ/∂t = H ψ, where H is the Hamiltonian of the system. The superposition principle allows any combination of states to be admissible. Measurement causes wave function collapse according to the projection postulate. Entanglement is described by the non-separability of the overall wave function, confirmed by the violation of Bell's inequalities. Noether's theorem connects symmetries of the wave function with conserved quantities.
key_problems: Measurement problem (explanation of collapse)
Compatibility with general relativity
Interpretation of the wave function (ontological or epistemological)
Boundary between quantum and classical worlds
Quantum gravity
wave_function.fun_fact: Schrödinger came up with the famous cat as a joke about quantum entanglement - the cat is both alive and dead until we open the box.

formulas.description: Schrödinger equation (time-dependent)
formulas.meaning: Determines the evolution of the wave function over time

formulas.description: Probability density
formulas.meaning: Probability of finding the particle at point x

formulas.description: Time-independent Schrödinger equation
formulas.meaning: States with definite energy

neutrino.name: neutrino
neutrino.description_popular: The neutrino is a tiny ghost particle that barely interacts with matter. Imagine billions of invisible bullets piercing you every second. They are emitted by the Sun and other stars.
neutrino.fun_fact_popular: About 100 billion neutrinos from the Sun pass through every square centimeter of your body each second, and you don't notice it.
neutrino.description_simple: The neutrino is the lightest known particle, having no electric charge. Like elusive spirits, it passes through planets and stars, leaving almost no trace. Its mass is so small that for a long time it was thought to be zero.
neutrino.history_simple: The existence of the neutrino was first postulated by Wolfgang Pauli in 1930 to explain the missing energy in beta decay. The particle was experimentally detected by Clyde Cowan and Frederick Reines in 1956.
neutrino.how_it_works_simple: Neutrinos are born in nuclear reactions, for example, in the center of the Sun. Like moths flying through a dense forest, they easily bypass atoms and even entire planets because they participate only in the weak interaction.
neutrino.description: The neutrino is a fundamental particle of the lepton family, having three generations: electron, muon, and tau neutrinos. It is described in the Standard Model as massless, but the discovery of oscillations requires nonzero mass, making neutrinos an important window into physics beyond the Standard Model. Extremely weak interaction is due to participation only in the weak and gravitational interactions, making it an ideal probe for astrophysical processes.
neutrino.history: In 1930, Wolfgang Pauli postulated the neutrino to conserve energy in beta decay. Frederick Reines and Clyde Cowan in 1956 first detected neutrinos from a nuclear reactor. Ralph Alpher and others predicted the cosmic neutrino background after the Big Bang. The phenomenon of neutrino oscillations was established at the end of the 20th century, leading to Nobel Prizes for Arthur McDonald and Takaaki Kajita in 2015, confirming nonzero rest mass.
neutrino.how_it_works: The neutrino participates only in the weak interaction through the exchange of W and Z bosons. The general theory is described by the local gauge symmetry SU(2)_L × U(1)_Y. Oscillations arise due to flavor mixing via the Pontecorvo–Maki–Nakagawa–Sakata matrix, where flavor quantum states are superpositions of mass states. This violates lepton number conservation separately. The IceCube neutrino telescope detects Cherenkov radiation in Antarctic ice from high-energy neutrinos. The Pauli principle plays a key role for neutrinos as fermions.
key_problems: Absolute masses of neutrinos
Nature of neutrinos - Dirac or Majorana
CP symmetry in the lepton sector
Sterile neutrinos
Neutrino picture of the early Universe
neutrino.fun_fact: Neutrinos have three "flavors" and can transform into each other - this phenomenon is called neutrino oscillations.

formulas.description: Mixing matrix
formulas.meaning: Flavor states as superposition of mass states

formulas.description: Oscillation probability
formulas.meaning: Probability of flavor change over distance L

formulas.description: Cherenkov radiation
formulas.meaning: Radiation angle, used for detection

electric_field.name: electric field
electric_field.description_popular: An electric field is an invisible force around charged objects. Like water around a magnet creating circles, the electric field draws lines along which a charge will move. When you touch synthetic clothing and hear a crackle, you are feeling the electric field.
electric_field.fun_fact_popular: Lightning is a giant discharge that occurs due to the huge difference in electric fields between clouds and the ground.
electric_field.description_simple: An electric field is a spatial network of forces created by electric charges. It is like an invisible hand that pushes or pulls other charges. The closer you are to the source, the stronger this field, like the heat from a campfire intensifies as you approach.
electric_field.history_simple: The concept of the field was introduced by Michael Faraday in the 19th century, and James Clerk Maxwell later described it mathematically in his famous equations.
electric_field.how_it_works_simple: Imagine that around each charge - like around a pebble thrown into water - ripples spread. These ripples show where another charge will move. Positive and negative charges create fields that either merge or cancel each other out.
electric_field.description: The electric field is a vector field created by charged particles and changing magnetic fields. It describes the force acting on a test charge at each point in space. In classical electrodynamics, electric and magnetic fields are combined into the electromagnetic tensor. Coulomb's law, Gauss's law, and Maxwell's equations define its structure and evolution. In quantum physics, the electric field is considered as a manifestation of the exchange of virtual photons.
electric_field.history: Michael Faraday in 1831 introduced the concept of lines of force to visualize electric and magnetic interactions. James Clerk Maxwell in 1864 generalized the discoveries into equations, predicting electromagnetic waves. Hendrik Lorentz introduced the concept of the electromagnetic field as a separate entity and the Lorentz force for moving charges. Henri Poincaré studied the symmetries of Maxwell's equations and pointed out relativistic effects. The development of quantum electrodynamics in the works of Dirac and others built a bridge to the microworld.
electric_field.how_it_works: The electric field E is defined by the force per unit test charge and obeys superposition. Maxwell's equation ∇·E = ρ/ε₀ (Gauss's law) relates the field to charge density. For electrostatics, the field is conservative, and the potential φ satisfies Poisson's equation. In dynamics, the electric field is also generated by a changing magnetic field (Faraday's law). In the relativistic formulation, the electromagnetic tensor F^{μν} combines the fields E and B. The quantum limit is described by Feynman diagrams with the photon as the U(1) gauge boson. Coulomb's law for a point charge gives E = k q / r².
key_problems: Unification with gravity
Nature of electric charge
Self-interaction of point particles
Hidden dimensions? (string theories)
electric_field.fun_fact: If we could see electric fields, every object would turn into a glowing jellyfish of shimmering lines.

formulas.description: Coulomb's law
formulas.meaning: Field of a point charge

formulas.description: Gauss's law
formulas.meaning: Flux of the field through a closed surface is related to the charge inside

formulas.description: Lorentz force
formulas.meaning: Force on a moving charge in an electromagnetic field

gravitational_waves.name: gravitational waves
gravitational_waves.description_popular: Gravitational waves are like ripples on water from a thrown stone, only they travel through the entire Universe. When two huge black holes collide, they shake space-time itself, and this shudder spreads in all directions. We don't feel it, but special instruments can catch this 'tremor', just as a seismograph catches an earthquake.
gravitational_waves.fun_fact_popular: The first gravitational waves we detected were born from the collision of two black holes a billion light-years away. It was like listening to a rustle from the other end of the galaxy through a giant microphone.
gravitational_waves.description_simple: Imagine that space is a stretched trampoline, and planets and stars are balls warping it. Gravitational waves are waves running across this 'fabric' when massive objects suddenly change their motion. They were predicted by Einstein as ripples in the fabric of spacetime. These waves carry information about the most powerful events in the cosmos: mergers of black holes and neutron stars.
gravitational_waves.history_simple: The idea of gravitational waves dates back to Henri Poincaré, who proposed their existence in 1905. Albert Einstein derived them from general relativity in 1916 but doubted their reality for a long time. The first attempts at experimental detection were made by Joseph Weber in the 1960s using aluminum cylinders. The real breakthrough came with the direct observation in 2015 by the LIGO detectors.
gravitational_waves.how_it_works_simple: Gravitational waves compress and stretch space in perpendicular directions, as if you were squeezing and stretching a rubber band. LIGO detectors use laser beams traveling along 4-kilometer arms. When a wave passes, the length of the arms changes ever so slightly — less than the diameter of a proton! This change is captured by an interferometer.
gravitational_waves.description: Gravitational waves are disturbances in the curvature of spacetime, propagating at the speed of light and described within the framework of Einstein's general relativity (GR). Their sources are the accelerated motion of massive bodies with a variable quadrupole moment: mergers of compact objects (black holes, neutron stars), supernova explosions, rotating neutron stars with deformations ('mountains'), and cosmological processes. The wave amplitude is characterized by a dimensionless strain h ≈ 10⁻²¹ for typical events. LIGO and Virgo detectors use Michelson laser interferometers with suspended mirrors to measure phase difference changes. Gravitational-wave astronomy has opened a new window on the Universe, allowing us to study black hole properties, the expansion rate of the Universe, and fundamental laws of physics.
gravitational_waves.history: Henri Poincaré, in his 1905 work 'On the Dynamics of the Electron', first suggested the existence of gravitational waves propagating at the speed of light. Albert Einstein, in 1916–1918, derived the linearized approximation of GR and obtained solutions in the form of plane gravitational waves. Their reality was long disputed due to the difficulty of separating physical effects from coordinate effects. In 1957, at the Chapel Hill conference, it was proved that gravitational waves carry energy. Joseph Weber, in the 1960s, built the first detectors based on massive bars and claimed to have detected events in 1969, but the results were not confirmed. The development of laser interferometers began in the 1970s. On September 14, 2015, the LIGO collaboration (USA) announced the direct observation of gravitational waves from the merger of two black holes (event GW150914). In 2017, the merger of two neutron stars was recorded, accompanied by electromagnetic signals.
gravitational_waves.how_it_works: According to Einstein's equations, accelerating masses with a non-zero quadrupole moment radiate gravitational waves. They are transverse waves with two polarizations ('plus' and 'cross'), propagating at the speed of light. Passing through a detector, the wave causes a relative change in the arm length of an interferometer: ΔL/L = h(t)/2. In a Michelson interferometer, a laser beam is split and travels along the arms, then interferes. The phase difference change is proportional to h. Sensitivity is limited by quantum and seismic noise. Matched filters and numerically calculated signal templates are used to extract signals. A black hole merger is described in three stages: inspiral, merger, ringdown. Analysis of the waveform yields masses, spins, and distance. In cosmology, gravitational waves allow testing models of the early Universe (primordial gravitational waves) through B-mode polarization of the cosmic microwave background.
key_problems: Detection of gravitational waves from supernovae and isolated rotating neutron stars
Creation of next-generation detectors (space-based and underground) to observe the full frequency range
Using gravitational waves to measure the Hubble constant in an independent way
Investigating the nature of dark matter through observations of mergers involving primordial black holes
Searching for primordial gravitational waves to prove the inflationary model of the Universe
gravitational_waves.fun_fact: The energy emitted during the merger of two black holes in the form of gravitational waves, in a fraction of a second, exceeds the combined brightness of all the stars in the Universe. Fortunately, these waves interact very weakly with matter and pass through us unnoticed.

formulas.description: Amplitude of gravitational wave from a binary system
formulas.meaning: h — dimensionless amplitude, G — gravitational constant, c — speed of light, μ — reduced mass, v — orbital velocity, r — distance to source

formulas.description: Energy radiated during merger
formulas.meaning: E — radiated energy, M — mass of system, R — characteristic radius (e.g., gravitational radius)

formulas.description: Hubble's law (used to determine distance from standard sirens)
formulas.meaning: v — recession velocity, H0 — Hubble constant, d — distance; joint measurement of gravitational and electromagnetic waves provides an independent estimate of H0

big_bang.name: big bang
big_bang.description_popular: Imagine that the entire Universe once fit into a tiny point, smaller than an atom. And then — BANG! — it began to expand rapidly, like a balloon being inflated. This is the Big Bang — not an explosion in space, but the birth of space and time themselves.
big_bang.fun_fact_popular: The light from the Big Bang is still flying towards us as faint microwave radiation. When you turn on an old TV and see 'snow', some of that interference is actually the afterglow of the Big Bang!
big_bang.description_simple: The Big Bang theory explains how our Universe arose from a super-dense and hot state. Just as a huge tree grows from a tiny seed, galaxies, stars, and planets were born from a microscopic singularity over billions of years.
big_bang.history_simple: In the 1920s, Belgian priest and physicist Georges Lemaître first proposed that the Universe was expanding from an initial point. In 1929, Edwin Hubble confirmed this by discovering the recession of galaxies.
big_bang.how_it_works_simple: Imagine baking raisin bread. The dough rises, and the raisins move apart. The farther apart two raisins are, the faster they recede. So it is with galaxies: the expansion of space itself pushes them apart, and we observe the redshift of their light.
big_bang.description: The Big Bang is a cosmological model describing the development of the Universe from an extremely high-density and high-temperature state. According to this model, about 13.8 billion years ago, space-time and matter emerged from a singularity, after which a phase of inflationary expansion began, leading to the formation of the modern large-scale structure. Over time, the Universe cooled, allowing quarks, protons, neutrons, and then atoms to form. The recombination of hydrogen made the Universe transparent to radiation, which we now detect as the cosmic microwave background — the 'echo' of the Big Bang.
big_bang.history: The concept of an expanding Universe was born from the equations of Einstein's general relativity, which Alexander Friedmann solved in 1922–1924 without the cosmological constant, predicting non-stationarity. In 1927, Georges Lemaître independently proposed the 'primeval atom' model and linked expansion to the redshift of galaxies. Edwin Hubble in 1929, based on observations, confirmed the proportionality between the recession velocity of galaxies and their distance, providing direct evidence for expansion. In 1948, George Gamow, Ralph Alpher, and Robert Herman developed the theory of Big Bang nucleosynthesis, and in 1965, Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson discovered the cosmic microwave background, firmly establishing the Big Bang theory.
big_bang.how_it_works: The key principle is the expansion of space-time itself, described by the Friedmann equations derived from general relativity. According to Hubble's law, the recession velocity of galaxies is v = H₀ r, where H₀ is the Hubble constant. In the early stages, radiation dominated, followed by matter, and now dark energy, which influences the expansion rate. Big Bang nucleosynthesis explains the abundance of light elements: hydrogen (≈75%), helium (≈25%), and traces of lithium. The cosmic microwave background is the cooled radiation that reached us from the epoch of recombination; its spectrum perfectly matches Planck's law for black-body radiation.
key_problems: What caused the Big Bang?
The nature of dark matter and dark energy, which govern the dynamics of expansion
What happened at the moment of singularity, and how to unify quantum mechanics with gravity?
The problem of the initial singularity and the birth of time
big_bang.fun_fact: The name 'Big Bang' was coined by astronomer Fred Hoyle in 1949, and he was actually an opponent of the theory and meant it as a joke. Now it is the most accepted cosmological model!

formulas.description: Hubble's law — recession velocity of a galaxy is proportional to its distance
formulas.meaning: v — velocity, H₀ — Hubble constant (about 70 km/s/Mpc), d — distance

hydrogen.name: hydrogen
hydrogen.description_popular: Hydrogen is the simplest and lightest atom. Imagine a tiny solar system: at the center, a nucleus consisting of a single proton, and around it, like a planet, orbits a single electron. Most of the visible Universe is built from such 'bricks' — stars, gas clouds, and even the water in your glass.
hydrogen.fun_fact_popular: Every second, inside the Sun, 500 million tons of hydrogen are converted into helium, releasing energy that warms our planet. And your body is about 10% hydrogen — mostly in the form of water!
hydrogen.description_simple: Hydrogen is the fuel of stars. Just as wood burns in a campfire, hydrogen 'burns' in stellar cores through thermonuclear reactions, turning into helium and emitting light. Without hydrogen, there would be no Sun or life.
hydrogen.history_simple: Hydrogen as a chemical element was discovered by Henry Cavendish in 1766. However, its stellar role was first recognized by Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin in 1925 — she showed that stars consist mostly of hydrogen. The spectral lines of hydrogen had been studied earlier by Johann Balmer in 1885.
hydrogen.how_it_works_simple: The hydrogen spectrum is like a unique barcode. When electricity or heat excites the atom, the electron jumps to a higher 'floor', then falls back, emitting light of strictly defined colors. By these colors, astronomers can tell that a distant star contains hydrogen.
hydrogen.description: Hydrogen (H) is the first element of the periodic table; its atom consists of one proton and one electron. It is the most abundant element in the Universe, making up about 75% of baryonic mass. In molecular form, H₂ is a light, colorless, odorless, flammable gas. Hydrogen is a key element in stellar nucleosynthesis: in the interiors of stars, it is converted into helium via the pp-chain or CNO cycle, releasing energy. The spectral lines of hydrogen (Lyman, Balmer, Paschen series, etc.) played a fundamental role in the development of quantum mechanics, as their explanation was a triumph of the Schrödinger equation. In cosmology, the recombination of hydrogen led to the formation of neutral atoms and the appearance of the cosmic microwave background.
hydrogen.history: Hydrogen was first obtained and described by Henry Cavendish in 1766, but its spectral patterns remained a mystery until 1885, when Johann Jakob Balmer empirically derived a formula for the visible lines. In 1913, Niels Bohr proposed a quasi-modern atomic model that explained the origin of the spectra, but the complete quantum mechanical description was given by Erwin Schrödinger in 1926. The astrophysical significance of hydrogen became clear thanks to Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin, who in 1925, analyzing stellar spectra, proved that stars predominantly consist of hydrogen, which changed our understanding of the evolution of the Universe.
hydrogen.how_it_works: The hydrogen atom is the simplest quantum system, obeying the Schrödinger equation, which predicts discrete energy levels: E_n = -13.6 eV / n². Transitions of the electron between levels produce characteristic spectral lines. In astrophysics, hydrogen lines (e.g., the 21-cm line of hyperfine splitting) are used for mapping galaxies. In stellar interiors, hydrogen participates in fusion reactions, releasing energy according to the mass-energy equivalence E=mc². Big Bang nucleosynthesis, occurring in the first minutes after the Big Bang, explains the observed abundance of hydrogen.
key_problems: Achieving sustained controlled thermonuclear fusion for energy (ITER reactor)
Producing and containing metallic hydrogen — a potential superconductor
Ultra-precise measurements of the hydrogen spectrum to test quantum electrodynamics and search for new physics
hydrogen.fun_fact: Hydrogen is the only element that has distinct names in scientific jargon for its isotopes: protium (regular hydrogen), deuterium (heavy hydrogen), and tritium (super-heavy, radioactive). Moreover, hydrogen in the metallic state could be a room-temperature superconductor!

formulas.description: Balmer's formula for the wavelengths of the visible hydrogen spectrum (historically the first)
formulas.meaning: λ — wavelength, B ≈ 364.56 nm — Balmer's constant

formulas.description: Energy levels of the hydrogen atom from the Schrödinger equation
formulas.meaning: E_n — electron energy at level n, n — principal quantum number

transit_method.name: transit method
transit_method.description_popular: Imagine looking at a light bulb, and a moth flies in front of it. The bulb dims slightly for a moment. That's exactly how scientists find planets around distant stars: they watch for tiny 'eclipses' when a planet passes across its star's disk.
transit_method.fun_fact_popular: If aliens looked at the Sun using the transit method, they would notice Earth blocking just 0.008% of its light! Such precision is only possible with space telescopes.
transit_method.description_simple: The transit method is a way to detect exoplanets by the dip in a star's brightness. Just as a hand passing in front of a flashlight casts a shadow, a planet passing between us and a star blocks some of its light. By the depth and duration of the 'eclipse', astronomers estimate the planet's size and orbit.
transit_method.history_simple: The idea of using planet transits for detection originated in the mid-20th century, but the real revolution was made by the Kepler telescope, launched in 2009. Its principal investigator, William Borucki, had fought for decades to get this project approved — and ultimately discovered thousands of worlds.
transit_method.how_it_works_simple: Imagine a light curve of a star — a graph like a cardiogram. When a planet eclipses the star, a 'dip' appears on the graph. The depth of the dip shows how big the planet is, and the time between dips shows how fast it orbits the star. Sensitive photometers record brightness changes of hundredths of a percent.
transit_method.description: The transit method is a photometric method for detecting exoplanets based on recording periodic dips in a star's brightness as a planet passes across its visible disk. The transit depth ΔF/F is directly related to the ratio of the planet's radius to the star's radius: (R_p / R_s)². The method allows determining the planet's radius, orbital period, orbital inclination, and even atmospheric composition (via transmission spectroscopy). Combined with the radial velocity method, the planet's density can be calculated. Space telescopes such as Kepler, TESS, and CHEOPS specialize in transit surveys, while the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) and Hubble are used for detailed study of transiting planets' atmospheres.
transit_method.history: The first successful detection of an exoplanet transit occurred in 1999 for the star HD 209458. The theoretical possibility of the method had been discussed since the mid-20th century, but technical difficulty required space-based surveys. The breakthrough was achieved by William Borucki, who since the 1980s had been developing the concept of the Kepler mission. Launched in 2009, Kepler observed about 150,000 stars simultaneously and discovered thousands of candidates, proving that planets are common in the Galaxy. In 2018, TESS followed, continuing transit surveys of nearby stars.
transit_method.how_it_works: When a planet crosses a star's disk, the total radiation flux decreases by an amount proportional to the planet's disk area: ΔF/F = (R_p/R_s)². The transit light curve has a characteristic shape with gradual ingress/egress and a flat bottom (if the star is uniform). The period between transits gives the orbital period, and the transit duration gives the impact parameter and inclination. The secondary transit (when the planet passes behind the star) allows measuring the planet's own emission. Transit spectroscopy reveals absorption lines in the atmosphere — for example, water vapor, methane, carbon dioxide.
key_problems: High rate of false positives due to binary stars or starspots
Difficulty in detecting Earth-like planets with long periods because of shallow and infrequent transits
Limitations of ground-based telescopes due to the atmosphere, requiring space instruments
transit_method.fun_fact: The most famous transit in history was the passage of Venus across the Sun's disk in 1769, which allowed calculation of the Earth-Sun distance. The modern transit method has found a planet resembling Tatooine from Star Wars — Kepler-16b, orbiting a binary star!

formulas.description: Transit depth (fraction of starlight blocked by the planet)
formulas.meaning: ΔF/F — relative flux drop, R_p — planet radius, R_s — star radius

speed_of_light.name: speed of light
speed_of_light.description_popular: Light travels at an incredible but constant speed — faster than anything we know. Imagine it as the fastest arrow that nothing can outrun. Light from the Moon to Earth takes less time than the blink of an eye!
speed_of_light.fun_fact_popular: If you could travel at the speed of light, you would circle Earth's equator 7 times in one second.
speed_of_light.description_simple: The speed of light in a vacuum is a fundamental limit; nothing can travel faster, just as the speed of sound is a limit for an aircraft. Only light and other massless particles always travel at this speed.
speed_of_light.history_simple: As early as the 17th century, Galileo tried to measure the speed of light using lanterns, but without success. In 1676, Danish astronomer Ole Rømer, observing the moons of Jupiter, made the first estimate. Later, in the 19th century, James Clerk Maxwell created the theory of electromagnetism, which implied that light is an electromagnetic wave, and calculated its speed. The Michelson-Morley experiment showed that the speed of light does not depend on the motion of the source or observer.
speed_of_light.how_it_works_simple: Light propagates like ripples on water from a thrown stone, but in three dimensions and at colossal speed. This speed is embedded in the very fabric of space and time, so that the faster you move, the slower time passes for you — this ensures the constancy of the speed of light.
speed_of_light.description: The speed of light in vacuum (c = 299,792,458 m/s) is a fundamental physical constant, playing a central role in special relativity. It is the maximum speed for the propagation of information and interactions. In general relativity, c links space and time into a single continuum. Maxwell's equations predict that electromagnetic waves propagate at speed c. This constant also sets the scale in Einstein's relation E = mc².
speed_of_light.history: The first estimates of the speed of light were made by Rømer (1676) from the eclipses of Jupiter's moons. In 1849, Fizeau conducted the first terrestrial measurement using a rotating toothed wheel. In 1865, Maxwell derived the speed of light from his equations of electromagnetism. The Michelson-Morley experiment (1887) demonstrated the independence of the speed of light from the motion of the ether, leading to the principle of the constancy of the speed of light. The works of Lorentz and Poincaré paved the way for Einstein's special relativity (1905), where the constancy of the speed of light became a postulate.
speed_of_light.how_it_works: According to special relativity, the speed of light in a vacuum is the same in all inertial frames of reference. This leads to relativistic effects — time dilation and length contraction. Formally, this is described by the Lorentz transformations. In quantum field theory, the speed of light determines the scale of the causal structure of spacetime. In general relativity, locally the speed of light is constant, but globally it can change in a gravitational field (Shapiro effect). The principle of the constancy of the speed of light underlies all modern physics.
key_problems: Is the speed of light an absolute limit, or are violations possible at the quantum level?
Why does the speed of light have this particular value? Is it a fundamental constant or a consequence of deeper principles?
Has the speed of light changed in the early Universe? Some variable speed of light theories attempt to explain cosmological problems.
speed_of_light.fun_fact: When you look at the stars, you see them in the past. Light from the Sun takes 8 minutes to reach us, and from the nearest star — more than 4 years.

formulas.description: Mass-energy equivalence, linking mass and the speed of light
formulas.meaning: The rest energy of a particle equals its mass multiplied by the square of the speed of light

formulas.description: Time transformation in different reference frames
formulas.meaning: Time dilation for a moving object (consequence of constant c)

spacetime.name: spacetime
spacetime.description_popular: Spacetime is like a fabric that unifies space and time into a single whole. Imagine the Universe as a giant trampoline. If you place a heavy ball (a star) on it, it will sag, and everything around will start moving toward that depression. Time also slows down near massive objects—as if clocks run slower when they are near the ball.
spacetime.fun_fact_popular: If you could travel at a speed close to the speed of light, time would flow slower for you, and you could "travel into the future" faster than people on Earth!
spacetime.description_simple: Spacetime is a single arena where all events unfold. It can be imagined as a river, where space is the channel and time is the current. Massive objects, like boulders in the channel, curve spacetime, altering the flow of events. Gravity is not a force of attraction but a consequence of this curvature: as if a boat sailed along a curved channel, skirting around a boulder.
spacetime.history_simple: The idea of spacetime was introduced by Hermann Minkowski in 1908, unifying space and time into a four-dimensional continuum. Later, Albert Einstein developed it into the general theory of relativity (1915), showing that gravity is the curvature of spacetime. Scientists such as Henri Poincaré and Karl Schwarzschild contributed to understanding its properties.
spacetime.how_it_works_simple: Curved spacetime manifests as gravity: planets revolve around the Sun not because something attracts them, but because the Sun warps spacetime, and they move along the straightest possible lines in this curved world—geodesics. It is as if an ant crawled on a crumpled sheet of paper: to it, the path seems straight, but in fact it goes around a dent.
spacetime.description: Spacetime is a fundamental physical concept that unifies three spatial dimensions and one temporal dimension into a single four-dimensional continuum. In special relativity, space and time are linked by Lorentz transformations such that the speed of light is constant in all inertial reference frames. In general relativity, gravity is described as a geometric property of spacetime, whose curvature is produced by mass and energy according to Einstein's equations. The spacetime metric defines the intervals between events, and the motion of test particles occurs along geodesic lines. Cosmological solutions, such as the Friedmann–Lemaître–Robertson–Walker metric, describe the expansion of the Universe. Quantum gravity attempts to unify it with quantum mechanics.
spacetime.history: In 1905, Henri Poincaré and Albert Einstein independently laid the foundations of special relativity, where space and time ceased to be absolute. In 1908, Hermann Minkowski formulated four-dimensional spacetime. In 1915, Einstein completed the general theory of relativity, where the field equations (Einstein equations) linked curvature with energy-momentum. In 1916, Karl Schwarzschild found the first exact solution for a spherical mass, predicting black holes. In 1927, Georges Lemaître applied the equations to cosmology, showing the expansion of the Universe, later confirmed by Edwin Hubble.
spacetime.how_it_works:
The properties of spacetime are described by the metric tensor g_{\mu
u}, which defines distances and causal structure. Mass curves spacetime, and curvature dictates motion, as expressed in Einstein's equations: G_{\mu
u} + \Lambda g_{\mu
u} =  rac{8\pi G}{c^4} T_{\mu
u}. Special relativity is based on the principle of the constancy of the speed of light, which states that the speed of light in vacuum is the same for all inertial observers. Motion along geodesics explains gravity without forces. In cosmology, the Friedmann equations, derived from Einstein's equations, together with Hubble's law describe the expansion of spacetime on large scales.
key_problems: Singularities of black holes and the Big Bang require quantum gravity
The nature of dark energy, described by the cosmological constant Λ, remains a mystery
Lack of a self-consistent theory of quantum gravity
The problem of time in quantum cosmology: how does the arrow of time arise?
Can closed timelike curves (time machines) be created?
spacetime.fun_fact: GPS navigation accounts for time dilation due to Earth's gravity: clocks on satellites run faster than on the surface, and without general relativity corrections, position error would accumulate by 10 km per day.

formulas.description: Invariant interval in Minkowski space
formulas.meaning: Defines the distance between events in flat spacetime

formulas.description: Einstein equations
formulas.meaning: Relates the curvature of spacetime (Einstein tensor) to the distribution of matter and energy (energy-momentum tensor); Λ is the cosmological constant

superconductivity.name: superconductivity
superconductivity.description_popular: Superconductivity is when certain materials, upon strong cooling, become perfect conductors of electricity: current can flow in them forever without losing energy. Imagine a slide down which a sled glides without friction—it would slide endlessly. Similarly, electrons in a superconductor move without resistance.
superconductivity.fun_fact_popular: If you set a current in a superconducting ring, it will circulate for years without decaying—like water in a river that never stops.
superconductivity.description_simple: Superconductivity is a macroscopic quantum phenomenon in which electrical resistance drops to zero and the magnetic field is expelled from the material. Analogy: just as bees in a hive coordinate synchronously, forming a single "organism," electrons in a superconductor pair up into Cooper pairs and move as a single collective, without scattering off defects.
superconductivity.history_simple: Superconductivity was discovered by Heike Kamerlingh Onnes in 1911, by cooling mercury to the temperature of liquid helium. Later, in 1933, the Meissner effect was discovered, and in 1957, Bardeen, Cooper, and Schrieffer developed the BCS theory, explaining the mechanism through phonon exchange.
superconductivity.how_it_works_simple: At ultra-low temperatures, electrons, which repel each other, find a way to attract through deformation of the crystal lattice—as if two balls on a spring mattress were drawn together, indenting it. Cooper pairs form, condense into a single quantum state, and flow without resistance.
superconductivity.description: Superconductivity is a quantum mechanical state of matter that occurs in certain materials below a critical temperature Tc, characterized by zero electrical resistance, perfect diamagnetism (Meissner effect), and coherence of a macroscopic wave function. According to the BCS theory, electron pairing occurs through virtual phonon exchange, forming Cooper pairs with zero total spin, which obey Bose–Einstein statistics. Condensation of such pairs into a single ground state leads to an energy gap Δ in the excitation spectrum, suppressing scattering by thermal vibrations and impurities. In high-temperature superconductors (cuprates), the pairing mechanism is still unclear and is believed to be related to strong electronic correlations. Supercurrent is unattenuated and can exist in closed circuits, creating stable magnetic fields in devices such as solenoids.
superconductivity.history: The discovery of superconductivity occurred in 1911, when Heike Kamerlingh Onnes measured the disappearance of resistance in mercury at 4.2 K. In 1933, Walther Meissner and Robert Ochsenfeld discovered that a superconductor expels a magnetic field. The Ginzburg–Landau theory (1950) phenomenologically described superconductivity based on an order parameter. The microscopic BCS theory (1957) explained electron pairing via phonons. In 1986, Georg Bednorz and Alex Müller discovered high-temperature superconductivity in ceramics, giving rise to a new class of materials.
superconductivity.how_it_works: The key principle is the formation of Cooper pairs and their Bose condensation. Electrons with opposite spins and momenta attract through virtual phonon exchange, overcoming Coulomb repulsion. The condensate wave function is described by a macroscopic order parameter, and its coherence leads to supercurrent. The BCS theory provides an expression for the critical temperature, where the Debye frequency and electron-phonon coupling potential are important. The electrodynamics of superconductors is described by the London equations, which explain the Meissner effect and perfect conductivity by linking supercurrent to fields. Superconductivity is a macroscopic manifestation of quantum mechanics, where the Schrödinger equation for the Cooper pair condensate describes a single wave function.
key_problems: The mechanism of high-temperature superconductivity in cuprates remains unresolved
Search for materials with superconductivity at room temperature and atmospheric pressure
Theoretical prediction of Tc for new superconductors
Understanding the pseudogap phase in underdoped cuprates
Practical application: creating stable flexible superconducting cables and devices on large scales
superconductivity.fun_fact: Magnetic levitation of superconductors over magnets is not just a trick: the superconductor is firmly "suspended" in the air due to the effect of quantum flux pinning.

formulas.description: Critical temperature of a superconductor in the BCS theory
formulas.meaning: Dependence of the transition temperature on the electron-phonon coupling parameters and density of states

formulas.description: London equations
formulas.meaning: Relate the supercurrent J_s to the electromagnetic fields, explaining the Meissner effect and perfect conductivity

exoplanet.name: exoplanet
exoplanet.description_popular: An exoplanet is a planet that orbits another star, not our Sun. Imagine our Solar System as one apartment in a huge apartment building, and each apartment has its own planet tenants. Some exoplanets are similar to Earth, others to Jupiter, and there are truly strange worlds.
exoplanet.fun_fact_popular: The first exoplanet was discovered in 1992 — but not around an ordinary star, but a pulsar! That is a dead star that spins rapidly and sends out pulses like a cosmic lighthouse.
exoplanet.description_simple: An exoplanet is a celestial body orbiting a star outside the Solar System. Just as fireflies betray their presence by glowing, exoplanets reveal themselves when they pass in front of their star (transit), slightly dimming it, or by their gravitational influence, causing the star to wobble.
exoplanet.history_simple: The first confirmed discovery of exoplanets took place in 1992 around the pulsar PSR B1257+12. In 1995, Michel Mayor and Didier Queloz discovered the first exoplanet around a solar-type star — 51 Pegasi b, for which they received the Nobel Prize. Since 2009, the Kepler space telescope (with William Borucki involved in its development) has discovered thousands of planets by the transit method.
exoplanet.how_it_works_simple: Exoplanets are found by observing the star's light: when a planet passes between us and the star, the star dims by a fraction of a percent—as if a fly flew in front of a light bulb. Another method: the planet, by its gravity, makes the star wobble slightly, and from the spectrum shift we determine its mass.
exoplanet.description: An exoplanet is a planet belonging to a planetary system of a star other than the Sun. Observational methods are divided into indirect (transit, radial velocity method, microlensing) and direct (observation in the visible or infrared range). The transit method detects periodic dips in stellar brightness as the planet crosses its disk, allowing measurement of the planet's radius. The radial velocity (Doppler) method is based on registering periodic shifts in the star's spectral lines due to its orbital motion around the common center of mass with the planet; it provides an estimate of the minimum mass. Combining methods yields density and composition. Astrometry, pulsar timing, and gravitational microlensing complement the arsenal. Key parameters: orbital period, semi-major axis, eccentricity. Atmospheric characteristics are studied with spectroscopy during transits. Exoplanets are classified by mass and size: terrestrial, super-Earths, Neptunes, Jupiters.
exoplanet.history: In 1992, Alexander Wolszczan and Dale Frail discovered two planets around the pulsar PSR B1257+12. In 1995, Michel Mayor and Didier Queloz discovered 51 Pegasi b — the first exoplanet around a main-sequence star, sparking intense interest. With the launch of the Kepler mission (2009) under William Borucki's leadership, an era of statistical discoveries began, confirming thousands of transit candidates. In 2014, the Kepler spacecraft discovered Kepler-186f — the first Earth-sized exoplanet in the habitable zone. The TESS telescope continues to search for nearby systems. Atmospheric spectroscopy has become possible with the Hubble and James Webb telescopes.
exoplanet.how_it_works: Exoplanet detection is based on physical principles: according to the law of universal gravitation, the star and planet revolve around a common center of mass, so the star exhibits periodic Doppler shift of spectral lines (Doppler effect), defined as Δλ/λ = v_r/c. From the radial velocity curve, the planet's mass is determined. The transit method relies on measuring the eclipse depth δ = (R_p/R_s)^2, where R_p and R_s are the radii of the planet and star. The orbital period is related to the semi-major axis by Kepler's third law: P^2 = (4π^2/G(M_s+m_p)) a^3. Atmospheric parameters are derived from the spectral dependence of transmission. Microlensing uses gravitational lensing to detect planets around distant stars.
key_problems: Determining atmospheric composition and searching for biomarkers on Earth-like planets
Direct imaging of Earth-type exoplanets in the habitable zone
Detection of exomoons and rings
Understanding the formation and evolution of planetary systems in binary stars
Statistical estimates of the frequency of habitable worlds require more data
exoplanet.fun_fact: Some exoplanets are so hot that they have rains of molten iron or clouds of rubies and sapphires!

formulas.description: Kepler's third law for a planetary system
formulas.meaning: Relates the orbital period P to the semi-major axis a and the masses of the star M_* and planet m_p

formulas.description: Transit depth
formulas.meaning: The relative drop in stellar brightness during transit allows estimating the planet's radius R_p if the star's radius R_* is known

cosmic_microwave_background.name: cosmic microwave background
cosmic_microwave_background.description_popular: After the Big Bang, the Universe was hot, like a red-hot stove. Then it cooled and expanded like a balloon. Now, from that heat, a faint glow remains, but we can hardly see it — it has turned into invisible microwaves. Imagine a campfire that has long gone out, but the stones around are still warm. The cosmic microwave background is that kind of "heat" from the birth of the Universe.
cosmic_microwave_background.fun_fact_popular: About 1 percent of the "snow" on the screen of an old TV, when there's no signal, is the echo of the Big Bang. You were literally seeing a piece of the Universe's birth!
cosmic_microwave_background.description_simple: Imagine you are baking raisin bread. When the dough was compressed and hot, there was a lot of light in it. As it expanded, the light stretched and turned into microwaves. The cosmic microwave background is the most ancient light that has reached us. It fills the entire Universe as a uniform background with a temperature of about –270°C.
cosmic_microwave_background.history_simple: In 1948, Ralph Alpher and Robert Herman predicted the existence of such radiation based on the Big Bang theory. In 1965, Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson accidentally discovered it using a large antenna, for which they later received the Nobel Prize.
cosmic_microwave_background.how_it_works_simple: After the Big Bang, the Universe was a plasma of particles and light. When it cooled down about 380,000 years later, electrons were able to combine with nuclei (the era of recombination), and light began to travel freely. Since then, it has "cooled" as space expanded and now reaches us as microwaves. Its almost perfect uniformity with tiny ripples shows where galaxies later formed.
cosmic_microwave_background.description: The cosmic microwave background (CMB) is thermal radiation left over from the recombination era in the early Universe. It is nearly isotropic blackbody radiation with a temperature of 2.72548 K and tiny anisotropies that carry information about the physics of the early Universe, its composition, and cosmological parameters. The spectrum of the radiation matches the Planck law to an extremely high degree, which is the strongest confirmation of the hot Big Bang model.
cosmic_microwave_background.history: The theoretical prediction was made in the works of George Gamow, Ralph Alpher, and Robert Herman in 1948. They estimated the temperature of the remnant radiation at several kelvins. The experimental discovery occurred in 1965 by Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson, who detected excess noise in an antenna, which turned out to be radiation with a temperature of about 3 K. Subsequent satellite missions (COBE, WMAP, Planck) measured the CMB spectrum and anisotropies with extreme precision, turning cosmology into an exact science.
cosmic_microwave_background.how_it_works: The CMB arises at the epoch of recombination (about 380,000 years after the Big Bang), when the Universe became transparent to photons. Its spectrum is described by the Planck law for a black body. Temperature anisotropies reflect initial quantum density fluctuations amplified by inflation. Acoustic oscillations in the photon-baryon plasma produce characteristic peaks in the angular power spectrum, from which cosmological parameters (baryon and dark matter densities, the Hubble constant, and others) are determined. These processes are described by the Friedmann equations and the physics of non-equilibrium thermodynamics.
key_problems: Anomalies in large-scale fluctuations: "axis of evil," alignment of the quadrupole and octupole.
Tension in measurements of the Hubble constant (discrepancy between Planck data and local measurements).
The nature of dark matter and dark energy, which affect the anisotropy spectrum.
The need for direct confirmation of inflation through the search for B-mode polarization.
cosmic_microwave_background.fun_fact: The cosmic microwave background is almost the same in all directions (differences are millionths of a degree). But these tiny temperature fluctuations are the seeds from which all stars and galaxies grew.

formulas.description: Planck's law of radiation (blackbody spectrum)
formulas.meaning: Spectral radiance of a black body, accurately describing the observed spectrum of the CMB

formulas.description: CMB temperature
formulas.meaning: Current mean thermodynamic temperature of the cosmic microwave background

angular_momentum.name: angular momentum
angular_momentum.description_popular: When you spin on a chair with your legs stretched out and then pull them in, you start spinning faster. This is because you have "rotational stubbornness" — angular momentum. The farther the mass is from the axis of rotation, the stronger the "stubbornness," but if it's pulled in, the rotation speeds up. Ice skaters use this for fast pirouettes!
angular_momentum.fun_fact_popular: Do you know why a bicycle doesn't fall when it moves? The wheels are spinning, and their angular momentum helps maintain balance. Even a falling cat, by twisting, uses its angular momentum to land on its feet!
angular_momentum.description_simple: Angular momentum is a measure of how much rotation is "stored" in something. Imagine a spinning top: the faster it spins and the more massive its rim, the harder it is to stop or tilt it. It is conserved: if there are no external disturbances, a rotating object will not change its angular momentum. It's like the law of inertia for rotation.
angular_momentum.history_simple: The idea arose from observations of planetary motion: Kepler noticed that planets move faster near the Sun (law of equal areas). Later, Newton and Euler formulated the concept of angular momentum as a physical quantity. In the 20th century, Henri Poincaré showed that its conservation is linked to the symmetry of space.
angular_momentum.how_it_works_simple: Imagine a spinning figure skater: with arms outstretched, she spins slowly, and when she pulls her arms in, she speeds up. This is because her angular momentum (depending on mass, shape, and speed) remains constant, and as the distance to the axis decreases, the speed increases. The same principle explains why neutron stars spin hundreds of times per second: when a massive star collapses into a tiny ball, its rotation accelerates tremendously.
angular_momentum.description: Angular momentum (moment of momentum) is a vector physical quantity characterizing rotational motion, defined as the cross product of the particle's radius vector and its momentum: L = r × p. In isolated systems, it is strictly conserved, which is a consequence of the isotropy of space (Noether's theorem). It plays a key role in classical mechanics, quantum physics (orbital and spin angular momenta), and general relativity (Kerr rotating black holes).
angular_momentum.history: The concept of angular momentum dates back to Kepler's law of areas (1609), which was explained by Newton and later expressed as conservation of angular momentum. Leonard Euler in the 18th century developed the mathematical apparatus for rotational motion. Henri Poincaré in the late 19th century explored the connection between conservation laws and symmetries. In quantum mechanics, angular momentum was quantized and given an interpretation in terms of operator eigenvalues. In black holes, its role is described by Kerr solutions, and in black hole thermodynamics, by the works of Bekenstein and Hawking.
angular_momentum.how_it_works: In classical mechanics, angular momentum is conserved in the absence of an external torque. For a rigid body, it is expressed as L = I ω, where I is the inertia tensor. In quantum mechanics, there are operators for orbital (L̂) and spin (Ŝ) angular momentum with discrete values, e.g., L = ħ√(l(l+1)). In general relativity, a rotating black hole is described by the Kerr metric, where the spin parameter a = J/Mc introduces frame-dragging effects. Conservation of angular momentum is tied to the isotropy of space through Noether's theorem, and for black holes, to the generalized laws of mechanics.
key_problems: The angular momentum problem in galaxy formation ("angular momentum catastrophe"): models underestimate the observed rotation speeds of disks.
The black hole information paradox: how is information preserved during Hawking radiation taking angular momentum into account?
Quantization of gravity at extremely high spins (e.g., in the context of string theory).
Precise measurement of black hole spin by observational methods (gravitational waves, X-ray spectroscopy).
angular_momentum.fun_fact: Neutron stars are supernova remnants that can spin up to 700 times per second. They preserved the angular momentum of the parent star but compressed to the size of a city, so the rotation speed increased, like a figure skater pulling in her arms.

formulas.description: Classical angular momentum (cross product)
formulas.meaning: Angular momentum of a point particle about the origin

formulas.description: Angular momentum of a rigid body via moment of inertia
formulas.meaning: For rotation about a principal axis: the magnitude of angular momentum equals the product of moment of inertia and angular velocity

quantum_entanglement.name: quantum entanglement
quantum_entanglement.description_popular: Imagine two twins who always dress alike, even if they are in different cities. If one puts on a red shirt, the other instantly puts on a blue one, and vice versa. Quantum entanglement is like such an invisible bond between particles: if one particle changes its state, the other changes immediately, no matter how far apart they are. This is not information transfer, but simply a surprising property of the quantum world.
quantum_entanglement.fun_fact_popular: Entangled particles can be separated by an entire galaxy, yet they still 'feel' each other instantly. Einstein called this 'spooky action at a distance'. And what's more: quantum entanglement is used in quantum computers for ultra-fast calculations.
quantum_entanglement.description_simple: In nature, an analogy can be made with two spinning tops that always spin in opposite directions. If you look at one and see it is turning clockwise, you instantly know the other is turning counterclockwise, without even looking. In quantum entanglement, particles behave similarly, but with quantum states such as spin or polarization. The most striking thing is that this correlation persists over any distance and apparently violates our usual notion of locality.
quantum_entanglement.history_simple: The concept of quantum entanglement was first described in 1935 in the famous paper by Einstein, Podolsky, and Rosen (EPR paradox), which questioned the completeness of quantum mechanics. Later, in 1964, physicist John Bell proposed inequalities that allow experimentally distinguishing entanglement from classical correlations. Decisive experiments conducted by Alain Aspect in the 1980s confirmed the existence of entanglement and the violation of Bell's inequalities.
quantum_entanglement.how_it_works_simple: Quantum entanglement occurs when two particles interact and form a single system with a shared state. Imagine two coins that always land opposite sides: if one is heads, the other is tails, but before the toss both are in a superposition of heads and tails. The moment one coin is measured, the state of the other is instantly fixed, regardless of distance. This non-local correlation is the basis of quantum information and teleportation.
quantum_entanglement.description: Quantum entanglement is a phenomenon of quantum mechanics in which two or more particles become correlated in such a way that the state of one particle cannot be described independently of the other, even if they are spatially separated. Mathematically, it is described by a non-separable state vector in Hilbert space. Entanglement underlies effects such as the violation of Bell's inequalities, quantum teleportation, superdense coding, and quantum computing. It does not allow information to be transmitted faster than light, but demonstrates the non-locality of the quantum world.
quantum_entanglement.history: In 1935, Albert Einstein, Boris Podolsky, and Nathan Rosen formulated a paradox pointing to the incompleteness of quantum mechanics. Erwin Schrödinger introduced the term 'entanglement' that same year and emphasized the fundamental importance of this phenomenon. In 1964, John Stewart Bell derived a theorem showing that any local hidden variables cannot reproduce all predictions of quantum mechanics; Bell's inequalities became a testable criterion. Experiments by Alain Aspect in 1981–1982, using polarized photons, for the first time unequivocally demonstrated the violation of Bell's inequalities and confirmed the non-locality of entangled states. Subsequent work by Zeilinger, Clauser, and others closed possible loopholes, strengthening the understanding of quantum non-locality.
quantum_entanglement.how_it_works: Entanglement arises from particle interactions if their joint wave function does not factorize. The Schrödinger equation describes the unitary evolution of such a superposition, and measurement leads to collapse and instantaneous correlation of states — this is consistent with Heisenberg's uncertainty principle. Bell's inequalities mathematically constrain correlations for local realistic theories; their violation proves non-locality. Entanglement measures such as von Neumann entropy quantify it, and the no-communication theorem is consistent with the constant speed of light. Entanglement is a resource for quantum information science, but decoherence limits its application.
key_problems: The measurement problem in quantum mechanics: why does wave function collapse occur?
Scaling entanglement for quantum computers and suppressing decoherence.
The role of entanglement in black hole and quantum gravity physics (holographic principle, ER=EPR).
Creating and maintaining entangled states of macroscopic objects.
quantum_entanglement.fun_fact: Entangled states of three or more particles exist; they are called GHZ states. Entanglement is used for absolutely secure data transfer — quantum cryptography. And with its help, one can teleport the quantum state of a particle, but not the matter itself.

formulas.description: Bell state — a maximally entangled two-qubit state
formulas.meaning: correlations between two qubits

formulas.description: Von Neumann entropy as a measure of entanglement of subsystem A
formulas.meaning: degree of entanglement with the environment

black_hole.name: black hole
black_hole.description_popular: Imagine a huge whirlpool in the ocean that sucks in everything around it. A black hole is a place in space where gravity is so strong that nothing can escape, not even light. It forms when a very large star dies and collapses into a tiny point.
black_hole.fun_fact_popular: If you fell into a black hole, you would be stretched like spaghetti due to the difference in gravitational pull at your head and feet — this is called spaghettification. And time inside a black hole flows completely differently!
black_hole.description_simple: A black hole is a region of spacetime where gravity is so strong that nothing, including light, can escape it. It arises from the collapse of a massive star: after the star exhausts its nuclear fuel, its core shrinks under its own weight to an infinitely small size, forming a singularity surrounded by an event horizon — an invisible boundary from which there is no return. Black holes come in different sizes: from stellar masses to supermassive ones at the centers of galaxies.
black_hole.history_simple: The idea of an object from which light cannot escape dates back to the 18th century (John Michell and Pierre-Simon Laplace). However, modern understanding began with solutions to Einstein's equations found by Karl Schwarzschild in 1916 — he obtained an exact solution for a spherically symmetric black hole. The term 'black hole' was coined by John Wheeler in 1967. In the 1970s, Stephen Hawking showed that black holes can emit energy (Hawking radiation), and Jacob Bekenstein linked them to entropy.
black_hole.how_it_works_simple: Imagine spacetime as a stretched rubber sheet. A massive body presses a dent into it, and a black hole is such a deep funnel that even light rays slide inward and cannot get out. Everything that crosses the event horizon falls toward the center — the singularity, where density is infinite. This analogy illustrates how gravity bends the path of light and matter.
black_hole.description: A black hole is an astrophysical object predicted by Einstein's general relativity, representing a region of spacetime with such a strong gravitational field that the escape velocity exceeds the speed of light. Key elements: singularity (a point of zero volume with infinite density and curvature), event horizon (a surface bounding the region from which there is no return), photon sphere, and ergosphere for rotating black holes. Solutions to Einstein's equations: Schwarzschild (non-rotating), Kerr (rotating), Reissner–Nordström (charged), and Kerr–Newman (charged rotating). Black holes emit Hawking radiation due to quantum effects near the horizon, leading to their evaporation and the information paradox.
black_hole.history: In 1783, John Michell suggested the existence of 'dark stars', and in 1796 Pierre-Simon Laplace independently put forward a similar idea. In 1916, Karl Schwarzschild found the first exact solution to Einstein's equations for a point mass, describing a static black hole. A few decades later, in the 1960s, Roy Kerr obtained the solution for rotating objects. Jacob Bekenstein in 1972 proposed that a black hole possesses entropy proportional to the horizon area, which Stephen Hawking confirmed in 1974 by discovering quantum-mechanical radiation. Also crucial were the works of Roger Penrose and John Wheeler, who cemented the concept of a black hole in astrophysics.
black_hole.how_it_works: A black hole is described by the metric of the corresponding solution to Einstein's equations. The gravitational field is so strong that no material particle or electromagnetic radiation can leave it after crossing the event horizon. According to the second law of black hole thermodynamics, the event horizon area never decreases, which is analogous to entropy. Hawking radiation arises from quantum vacuum fluctuations: near the horizon, particle-antiparticle pairs are created, one of which may fall into the hole while the other escapes to infinity, carrying away energy and reducing mass. Also at work are Newton's law of universal gravitation for distant effects and the constant speed of light principle, which defines the horizon boundary.
key_problems: The information paradox: is information about matter falling into a black hole preserved?
The nature of the singularity and quantum gravity: what is at the center of a black hole?
Formation of supermassive black holes in the early universe
The connection between black hole entropy and the holographic principle
black_hole.fun_fact: Black holes can merge, generating powerful gravitational waves that have been detected on Earth since 2015. The largest known black hole, TON 618, has a mass of about 66 billion Suns. And the information paradox in black holes still boggles the minds of physicists.

formulas.description: Schwarzschild radius — the event horizon of a non-rotating black hole
formulas.meaning: horizon size for a given mass

formulas.description: Bekenstein-Hawking entropy
formulas.meaning: black hole entropy is proportional to the horizon area

formulas.description: Hawking temperature
formulas.meaning: temperature of black hole radiation

dark_matter.name: dark matter
dark_matter.description_popular: Imagine you're looking at a carousel spinning too fast, but you don't see all the people riding it. That means there's someone invisible whose mass makes the carousel spin faster. Dark matter is something like an invisible mass in space: we don't see it directly, but from the way stars and galaxies move, we understand that it exists and there's a lot of it.
dark_matter.fun_fact_popular: There is five times more dark matter in the Universe than ordinary matter, which makes up stars, planets, and you and me. So we are just the bright tip of a huge invisible iceberg!
dark_matter.description_simple: Dark matter is a hypothetical form of matter that does not interact with electromagnetic radiation (light), so it cannot be seen directly. However, its presence is betrayed by gravitational effects: galaxies rotate faster than they should if they contained only visible matter; galaxy clusters hold onto hot gas that would otherwise dissipate without additional gravity; and light from distant objects is bent (gravitational lensing) more than predicted by the visible mass. According to estimates, dark matter makes up about 27% of the total energy of the Universe.
dark_matter.history_simple: The first to speak of 'dark matter' was Swiss astronomer Fritz Zwicky in 1933. Studying the motion of galaxies in the Coma Cluster, he found that their velocities were too high for the observed mass and suggested the presence of hidden 'dark' mass. In the 1970s, American astronomer Vera Rubin, studying spiral galaxies, obtained convincing evidence: rotation curves remained flat at large distances from the center, indicating a massive invisible halo. These works made dark matter a central problem in cosmology.
dark_matter.how_it_works_simple: Imagine a snowball with a stone at its center. When the snowball rolls, the snow flies apart, but the stone remains. Dark matter plays the role of gravitational 'glue': it does not interact with light, but its gravity holds stars in galaxies, preventing them from flying apart due to rapid rotation. Exactly how it 'works' we don't know: candidates range from massive but weakly interacting particles (WIMPs) to axions or even primordial black holes.
dark_matter.description: Dark matter is a fundamental component of the standard ΛCDM cosmological model, not participating in electromagnetic interactions and manifesting itself exclusively through gravity. It dominates the dynamics of galaxies and clusters, explaining the anomalously high rotation velocities of peripheral stars (flat rotation curves), gravitational lensing, and the large-scale distribution of structures. Observations of the cosmic microwave background (CMB) by the WMAP and Planck satellites have shown that baryonic matter makes up only ~5%, while dark matter makes up ~27% of the total energy density of the Universe. The nature of dark matter particles remains unknown: WIMPs, axions, sterile neutrinos, and other hypothetical particles are being discussed.
dark_matter.history: In 1933, Fritz Zwicky applied the virial theorem to the Coma Cluster and calculated that much more mass than visible was required to hold the galaxies; he coined the term 'dark matter'. In the 1970s, Vera Rubin and Kent Ford, using a spectrograph to measure Doppler shifts, constructed rotation curves of spiral galaxies and showed that at the outskirts, velocities remain constant, which contradicts Newton's law of gravitation given the visible mass distribution — a huge invisible halo was required. Subsequent observations of gravitational lensing (e.g., the 'Bullet Cluster' — a collision of clusters) and the cosmic microwave background confirmed the existence of dark matter as the dominant non-baryonic component. Theoretical predictions within supersymmetry actively stimulated searches for WIMP particles in underground detectors.
