nuclear_fusion.history: Spectroscopic studies by Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin in 1925 showed that stars consist mainly of hydrogen and helium, laying the observational foundation. Theoretical understanding of thermonuclear reactions developed through the efforts of Eddington, Atkinson, Houtermans, and particularly Hans Bethe, who in 1939 detailed the proton-proton chain and CNO cycle. Modern experiments on controlled fusion began in the mid-20th century and continue on facilities like ITER and NIF.
nuclear_fusion.how_it_works: To overcome the Coulomb barrier, nuclei need high kinetic energy, which in stars is achieved by thermal motion at temperatures of millions of kelvins. Quantum tunneling allows reactions to occur at energies below the barrier height. The released energy creates radiation pressure that balances gravitational contraction. On Earth, magnetic confinement of plasma (tokamak) or inertial confinement (laser compression of targets) are used to initiate fusion.
key_problems: Stable maintenance of plasma with required parameters for long periods (confinement problem)
Production and replenishment of tritium, a rare hydrogen isotope
Degradation of reactor wall materials under neutron bombardment
Economic competitiveness of fusion energy
nuclear_fusion.fun_fact: The hydrogen bomb is an example of uncontrolled fusion, while scientists worldwide are trying to tame this process in tokamaks and laser facilities to obtain an inexhaustible source of clean energy.

formulas.description: Reaction of the proton-proton chain (one branch)
formulas.meaning: p — proton (hydrogen nucleus); ^4He — helium-4 nucleus; e^+ — positron; ν_e — electron neutrino; 26.7 MeV — total energy released per cycle of fusion of four protons

helium.name: helium
helium.description_popular: Helium is a weightless gas that makes balloons soar into the sky. Imagine breathing it in — and your voice becomes squeaky like a cartoon mouse! This gas hides deep underground and even in the Sun itself.
helium.fun_fact_popular: Helium is the only substance that does not freeze even in the cosmic cold: it remains liquid at -273°C, defying all rules!
helium.description_simple: Helium is the second lightest element after hydrogen. On Earth, it lurks in natural gas like a treasure in dungeons, while in the Universe it is abundant in stars, where it is born from hydrogen, like a butterfly from a cocoon.
helium.history_simple: Helium was first noticed not on Earth, but on the Sun: in 1868, astronomers saw a bright yellow line in the spectrum of sunlight. It was found on Earth only 27 years later.
helium.how_it_works_simple: Helium is so light that it escapes from the atmosphere like a balloon without a string. In stars, it serves as fuel: fusing helium nuclei ignites light and heat.
helium.description: Helium (He) is the second element of the Periodic Table, a noble gas with atomic number 2. It makes up about 24% of the baryonic mass of the Universe, second only to hydrogen. It is formed mainly in stellar nucleosynthesis and during primordial nucleosynthesis after the Big Bang. On Earth, helium is extracted from natural gas, where it accumulates due to radioactive decay of heavy elements. Unique properties of helium, such as superfluidity at temperatures below 2.17 K, make it an ideal laboratory for studying quantum macroscopic phenomena.
helium.history: Helium was first detected in 1868 during a solar eclipse by Frenchman Jules Janssen and Englishman Norman Lockyer by a bright yellow line in the spectrum of the chromosphere. Lockyer named the element after Helios, the Greek god of the sun. Only in 1895 did William Ramsay isolate helium from the mineral cleveite, proving its earthly existence. Johann Balmer made an important contribution to spectral methods, whose formula for hydrogen stimulated the search for new elements in stars. Later, Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin showed that helium is one of the main elements of stellar matter.
helium.how_it_works: Helium is formed as a result of thermonuclear fusion: in the proton-proton chain, four protons merge into a helium nucleus, releasing energy according to the mass-energy equivalence E=mc². As a noble gas, it is chemically inert. Superfluidity of helium-4 at temperatures below the lambda point (2.17 K) is explained by the bosonic nature of atoms and is described by quantum mechanics equations (the Schrödinger equation for a macrosystem), where the macroscopic wave function leads to absence of viscosity and quantized vortices.
key_problems: Why do helium-3 and helium-4 behave so differently at low temperatures?
Can stable chemical compounds of helium be created under extreme conditions?
How to predict the depletion of helium reserves on Earth and find alternative sources?
helium.fun_fact: Helium at ordinary pressure remains liquid even at absolute zero. It is the only substance that can be cooled to the point where it loses viscosity and seeps through microscopic gaps — a true liquid miracle!

carbon.name: carbon
carbon.description_popular: Carbon is what all living things are made of. If you burn a piece of wood, the black charcoal is almost pure carbon. It is like a magical constructor: from it you can build both the soft graphite of a pencil and a hard sparkling diamond.
carbon.fun_fact_popular: There is so much carbon in your body that you could make 9000 pencil leads from it!
carbon.description_simple: Carbon is a unique element capable of creating millions of compounds: from carbon dioxide in soda to DNA, which stores your heredity. It is like a universal glue holding together the molecules of life, and in its pure form it can be soft as graphite or sparkling as diamond.
carbon.history_simple: People have used coal and soot since ancient times, but the fact that diamond is the same carbon was proven only in 1772 when Lavoisier burned a diamond and obtained carbon dioxide.
carbon.how_it_works_simple: The secret of carbon is its ability to form four bonds, like four hands with which it grips other atoms. Thanks to this, it builds long chains and rings, creating all the diversity of organic chemistry — from gasoline to proteins.
carbon.description: Carbon (C) is an element with atomic number 6, the basis of organic chemistry and biochemistry. Its unique ability for catenation — formation of stable chains and rings through covalent bonds — generates an enormous variety of compounds. Carbon allotropes exhibit a striking contrast of properties: from electrically conductive graphite to superhard diamond and fullerenes. In astrophysics, carbon is synthesized in stars via the triple-alpha process, which requires fine-tuning of a resonance level predicted by Fred Hoyle — a vivid example of the anthropic principle.
carbon.history: The elemental nature of carbon was established at the end of the 18th century by Antoine Lavoisier, who obtained carbon dioxide by burning diamonds and proved that diamond, graphite, and coal are the same substance. Lavoisier introduced the term 'carbon' in 1789. The development of organic chemistry in the 19th century solidified the central role of carbon. Johann Balmer, by creating a formula for the spectral lines of hydrogen, opened the way for spectral analysis of carbon in flames and stars. Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin in the 20th century confirmed that carbon is one of the main elements of stellar composition.
carbon.how_it_works: The chemical flexibility of carbon is provided by four valence electrons capable of orbital hybridization (sp, sp², sp³), which determines bond geometry and types of allotropes. In stars, carbon is formed in the triple-alpha process: three helium-4 nuclei fuse into carbon-12, which requires precise energy matching described by quantum mechanics (the Schrödinger equation). The stability of carbon nuclei is also dictated by the Pauli principle, which forbids fermions from occupying the same state, playing a role in stellar evolution and preventing the collapse of white dwarfs.
key_problems: Why is life on Earth based on carbon and not on silicon?
What new carbon allotropes with unique electronic and mechanical properties can be synthesized?
How to predict the long-term stability of carbon nanostructures under real conditions?
carbon.fun_fact: Diamond and graphite consist of the same atom, but the difference in their hardness is like between glass and steel. It all depends on how the atoms are packed in space: in diamond they are rigidly bound in a three-dimensional lattice, while in graphite they are arranged in layers that slide over each other.

water.name: water
water.description_popular: Water is what we drink and splash in. It has no color or smell, but without it there would be no trees or animals. Water can be liquid, like in a river, solid, like ice on a rink, and invisible vapor in clouds.
water.fun_fact_popular: You yourself are 65% water! If all the water evaporated from you, you would become light as a feather.
water.description_simple: Water is the most anomalous substance on Earth. It is the only one that occurs in three states at once: liquid, solid, and gaseous. As a universal solvent, it dissolves almost everything, like a caring hostess, and transports heat around the planet, moderating the climate.
water.history_simple: Ancient philosophers considered water an element, but in 1781 Henry Cavendish proved that water is a compound of hydrogen and oxygen. Antoine Lavoisier determined its formula as H₂O.
water.how_it_works_simple: At the micro level, water is like tiny magnets: the molecules stick together, creating surface tension on which water striders run. Ice floats because its molecules are spaced more widely than in the liquid — like dancers keeping a distance.
water.description: Water (H₂O) is a binary inorganic compound, the foundation of the hydrosphere, climatic processes, and biochemistry. Its anomalous physical properties — high heat capacity, maximum density at 4°C, expansion upon freezing — are due to hydrogen bonds and the tetrahedral structure of molecules. As a universal polar solvent, water dissociates and hydrates ions, providing a medium for metabolism. In space, water has been found in the form of ice on comets, planetary moons, and in the atmospheres of exoplanets, making it a key marker for the search for life.
water.history: The idea of water as an element prevailed until the experiments of Henry Cavendish in 1781, who synthesized water from 'inflammable air' (hydrogen) and 'dephlogisticated air' (oxygen). Antoine Lavoisier then determined its composition and gave names to hydrogen and oxygen. In the 20th century, water research in space became groundbreaking: missions to Mars and Jupiter's moons found water ice, and the Kepler telescope (mission leader William Borucki) discovered thousands of exoplanets, many of which potentially have water in the habitable zone.
water.how_it_works: Hydrogen bonds are the key to water's anomalies: they provide strong intermolecular attraction, but upon freezing they lock into an open crystal lattice of ice, which is less dense than the liquid. Quantum mechanical effects in water, such as proton tunneling in hydrogen bonds, are described by the Schrödinger equation. Macroscopic thermodynamic properties, including phase transitions and heat transfer, follow the second law of thermodynamics.
key_problems: A definitive explanation of the Mpemba effect.
The detailed molecular structure of liquid water remains a subject of debate.
Is liquid water possible on planets without a magnetic field?
How to accurately model the role of water in the climate system?
water.fun_fact: Hot water can freeze faster than cold water! This strange Mpemba effect still puzzles scientists.

methane.name: methane
methane.description_popular: Methane is an invisible gas that escapes from the ground in swamps and from the stomach of a cow as it digests grass. Imagine the methane molecule as a tiny tetrahedral hedgehog: a carbon atom in the center holds four hydrogen 'spines'. It is lighter than air, so like a balloon, it rises into the sky if set free.
methane.fun_fact_popular: Methane is called swamp gas because it bubbles up in bogs — that’s how bacteria finish off plant remains without oxygen, like tiny chefs making an invisible soup.
methane.description_simple: Methane is the simplest hydrocarbon, its molecule shaped like a perfect tetrahedron: a carbon atom in the center and four hydrogen atoms at equal distances, like clover leaves. In nature, it is born in anaerobic conditions — where there is no oxygen: in the intestines of animals (cows, termites), at the bottom of swamps, rice paddies, and even in permafrost.
methane.history_simple: Methane was first identified by the Italian scientist Alessandro Volta in 1776. He collected gas from the bottom of Lake Maggiore and ignited it, proving it was a combustible substance.
methane.how_it_works_simple: The greenhouse effect of methane is like a down duvet: sunlight easily penetrates through it to the Earth, but the planet’s thermal radiation gets trapped because methane molecules vibrate and absorb infrared rays, like a stretched spring resonating at a certain frequency.
methane.description: Methane (CH₄) is the simplest alkane, a saturated hydrocarbon with an sp³-hybridized carbon atom, forming a tetrahedral structure with an H-C-H angle of ≈ 109.5°. It is the main component of natural gas (70–90%), a powerful greenhouse gas with a global warming potential 28 times higher than CO₂ over 100 years, and an important player in atmospheric chemistry, including the formation of tropospheric ozone and stratospheric water vapor. In astrophysics, methane is a biomarker: its simultaneous presence with oxygen in exoplanet spectra could indicate life.
methane.history: The history of methane begins with alchemical observations of 'flammable air' in swamps. In 1776, Alessandro Volta scientifically described it, isolating it from bottom sediments. In the 19th century, Michael Faraday liquefied methane, and John Tyndall in 1859 proved its greenhouse properties. The modern era is associated with the study of methane hydrates on the ocean floor and its role in the climate system.
methane.how_it_works: Methane traps heat through vibrational-rotational transitions: its molecule has several normal modes (symmetric and asymmetric C-H stretching, H-C-H bending vibrations) that actively absorb infrared radiation at wavelengths of about 3.3 μm and 7.7 μm. In the atmosphere, it is oxidized by hydroxyl radicals (OH•) over ~9 years, turning into CO₂ and water vapor. According to Planck’s radiation law, the absorption intensity depends on temperature, and the Doppler effect broadens spectral lines in a turbulent atmosphere.
key_problems: Predicting methane emissions from thawing permafrost bogs and destabilization of marine hydrates under warming conditions
Developing cost-effective catalysts for direct decomposition of methane into hydrogen and carbon (turquoise hydrogen)
methane.fun_fact: Termites are champions of methane production: every year they emit nearly 20 million tons across the planet, building their methane-factory towers.

topology.name: topology
topology.description_popular: Topology is the mathematics of what remains unchanged when things bend, stretch, and squeeze, but do not tear. Imagine you are sculpting from plasticine: a cup with a handle and a donut are topological twins because both have exactly one hole. And a T-shirt is like a disk with three glued-on sleeve-tubes.
topology.fun_fact_popular: If you were an ant on a Möbius strip, you could crawl over the entire surface and return to the starting point... but upside down, because this strip has only one side!
topology.description_simple: Topology studies properties of space that are preserved under continuous deformations — imagine a rubber universe where you can crumple and twist objects, but cutting or gluing points is forbidden. It does not distinguish a square from a circle, but it distinguishes a sphere from a torus: on a sphere all loops shrink to a point, but on a torus they do not.
topology.history_simple: The birth of topology is associated with Henri Poincaré, who in 1895 published 'Analysis situs', laying the foundations of algebraic topology. He also formulated the famous Poincaré conjecture about the three-dimensional sphere, proved only in 2003.
topology.how_it_works_simple: Topology works through invariants — like passport data of a shape that do not change under stretching. For example, the number of holes (birational genus) or the Euler characteristic: for a cube it is 2 (V-E+F=8-12+6=2), same as for a sphere, while for a torus it is 0. If invariants differ, the objects are definitely not equivalent.
topology.description: Topology is a fundamental branch of mathematics studying properties of topological spaces that are invariant under homeomorphisms — continuous bijections with a continuous inverse. It includes general topology (limits, compactness, connectedness), algebraic topology (homotopies, homologies, fundamental groups), and differential topology (smooth manifolds, fiber bundles). The key idea: to classify spaces up to deformation using invariants such as homology groups or characteristic classes.
topology.history: The roots of topology go back to Euler's solution of the Königsberg bridge problem (1736) and his polyhedron theorem (1758). Henri Poincaré (included in the scientists list) laid the foundations in a series of works 'Analysis situs' (1895–1904), introducing the fundamental group and homologies. In the 20th century, the theory flourished thanks to Brouwer, Hurewicz, Whitehead, and others; the Poincaré conjecture was proved by Perelman in 2003.
topology.how_it_works: Topological invariants extract algebraic information from a space. For example, the fundamental group π₁(X) encodes all possible loops up to continuous deformation: for a sphere, π₁ = {0}; for a circle, π₁ = ℤ. Homologies Hₙ(X) count 'holes' of various dimensions. For manifolds, the de Rham theorem, linking differential forms and cohomology, is often used. In physics, topology manifests through quantum anomalies, solitons, and string theory: Calabi-Yau spaces have non-trivial topology affecting the compactification of extra dimensions.
key_problems: Classification of smooth structures on four-dimensional manifolds (the exotic R⁴ problem)
The Hodge conjecture on the relation between algebraic cycles and cohomologies (one of the Millennium Problems)
Computing homotopy groups of higher-dimensional spheres in general
topology.fun_fact: Topologists have proved that you can turn a sphere inside out without tearing, simply by passing the surface through itself — this is called Smale's sphere eversion.

formulas.description: Euler's formula for convex polyhedra — the simplest topological invariant
formulas.meaning: χ (chi) — Euler characteristic (topological invariant), V — number of vertices, E — number of edges, F — number of faces

lie_group.name: Lie group
lie_group.description_popular: A Lie group is like a round dance where the figures can change smoothly, and each figure is obtained from the previous one by a precise rule. Imagine a kaleidoscope: you turn the tube — the pattern shimmers, and all possible rotations live in a special world of symmetries that mathematicians called a Lie group.
lie_group.fun_fact_popular: Using Lie groups, physicists have described all particles in the universe: quarks and electrons live in symmetrical patterns, like in a magical kaleidoscope where every shift is a new transformation.
lie_group.description_simple: A Lie group is both a smooth manifold and a group where multiplication and inversion are smooth maps. Visually: imagine a rotating sphere — all possible rotations form a group, and each rotation can be smoothly transitioned to another, like a camera on a tripod slowly being turned.
lie_group.history_simple: Norwegian mathematician Sophus Lie created this theory in the 1870s, inspired by Galois's ideas and seeking to create for differential equations the same symmetry tool that group theory provided for algebraic equations.
lie_group.how_it_works_simple: Instead of studying the whole group at once, Lie looked at infinitesimal transformations near the identity — this is the tangent space with the Lie bracket operation. Like a conductor setting the tempo with a small wave of the baton, the tangent algebra determines the structure of the entire group.
lie_group.description: A Lie group is a differentiable manifold endowed with a group structure such that the group operations of multiplication (m: G×G→G) and inversion (i: G→G) are smooth maps. The tangent space at the identity forms a Lie algebra — a vector space with a bilinear antisymmetric Lie bracket satisfying the Jacobi identity. Lie groups classify continuous symmetries and are the foundation of modern physics: from rotations in quantum mechanics (SO(3), SU(2)) to gauge theories of the Standard Model (U(1)×SU(2)×SU(3)).
lie_group.history: The theory of Lie groups emerged in the works of Sophus Lie (1870s) while studying symmetries of differential equations. Later, Wilhelm Killing and Élie Cartan classified simple Lie groups. The connection to physics was established through the works of Hermann Weyl and Eugene Wigner on group representations in quantum mechanics. In the second half of the 20th century, Lie groups became the foundation of the gauge principle.
lie_group.how_it_works: Key fact: Lie groups are locally determined by their Lie algebra via the exponential map exp: g → G. For matrix groups, exp(X) = Σ_{k=0}^∞ X^k/k!. The structure constants of the algebra determine all local properties of the group. Representation theory describes how the group acts on vector spaces: for example, SO(3) on spherical harmonics. In physics, gauge fields are connections in principal bundles with a structure group, and their curvature is the field strength (Yang-Mills equations). According to Noether's theorem, every continuous symmetry described by a Lie group leads to the conservation of some quantity.
key_problems: Complete classification of infinite-dimensional Lie algebras
Construction of quantum groups (noncommutative deformations of universal enveloping algebras)
Integrability of nonlinear systems and Kac-Moody algebras
lie_group.fun_fact: The group SU(3) — the main symmetry of the strong interaction between quarks — has dimension 8 and generates eight gluons that glue protons and neutrons together.

manifold.name: manifold
manifold.description_popular: Imagine an ant crawling on the surface of a huge ball. To it, the ball seems flat, like an endless plain. But in fact, it lives on a curved surface that looks flat up close. So a manifold is a space that in each small region looks like ordinary Euclidean space, but as a whole may be more complex, like the surface of the Earth or a donut.
manifold.fun_fact_popular: If we lived on the surface of a donut, we could walk in any direction and eventually return to the starting point, unaware that our space has a hole! Manifolds allow us to study such unusual shapes of the universe.
manifold.description_simple: A manifold is a mathematical concept that generalizes the idea of a surface in any number of dimensions. Just as a sphere is a two-dimensional surface in three-dimensional space, a manifold can exist on its own, without being embedded in a higher-dimensional space. It's like a soap bubble film: it's curved, but up close it looks flat.
manifold.history_simple: The idea of manifolds originated in the works of Bernhard Riemann in the 19th century, but the modern language of topology was given to them by Henri Poincaré, who formulated the famous Poincaré conjecture about three-dimensional manifolds in the early 20th century.
manifold.how_it_works_simple: Manifolds appear in physics: according to general relativity, spacetime is a four-dimensional manifold curved by mass and energy. Just as a stretched fabric sags under a heavy ball, a star causes spacetime to curve, creating gravity.
manifold.description: A manifold is a topological space locally homeomorphic to Euclidean space ℝ^n. The topology of a manifold is given by an atlas of compatible charts. A smooth manifold is additionally endowed with a differentiable structure, allowing the use of calculus on it. Manifolds are classified by dimension, orientability, presence of boundaries, and other features. They serve as the main stage for many branches of mathematics and physics: from Riemannian geometry and Lie group theory to general relativity and gauge field theories.
manifold.history: The concept of a manifold arose from attempts to mathematically describe complex geometric shapes. Bernhard Riemann, in his 1854 lecture 'On the Hypotheses Which Lie at the Foundations of Geometry', introduced a generalized concept of multidimensional space. At the end of the 19th century, works by Felix Klein and Sophus Lie gave rise to the theory of Lie groups — smooth manifolds with a group structure. In the early 20th century, Henri Poincaré laid the foundations of topology, in particular, formulating the conjecture about the homotopy equivalence of the three-dimensional sphere. Later, the development of manifold topology led to the classification of surfaces, the creation of knot theory, and modern differential geometry.
manifold.how_it_works: In physics, spacetime is modeled as a four-dimensional pseudo-Riemannian manifold, whose metric is determined by Einstein's field equations: R_{μν} - ½Rg_{μν} = (8πG/c⁴) T_{μν}. Here, the manifold sets the geometric properties of the universe, and curvature describes gravitational interaction. In quantum mechanics, wave functions are defined on infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, but their configuration spaces are often finite-dimensional manifolds (e.g., the Bloch sphere for a single qubit). Gauge symmetry groups (U(1), SU(2), SU(3)) are Lie groups naturally acting on corresponding manifolds.
key_problems: Classification of four-dimensional topological manifolds, including the smooth structures conjecture
The problem of constructing a quantum theory of gravity on a dynamical manifold background
Study of symplectic manifolds in the context of mirror symmetry and string theory
manifold.fun_fact: The Poincaré conjecture, proved only in 2003 by Grigori Perelman, was one of the 'Millennium Problems'. Interestingly, Perelman himself declined the million-dollar prize and the Fields Medal, modestly noting that he simply completed Richard Hamilton's work.

eigenvalue.name: eigenvalue
eigenvalue.description_popular: Imagine stretching a rubber band. It lengthens but stays straight — that's the direction in which the band 'obeys' the stretch. The eigenvalue is exactly how much it stretched in that direction. In mathematics, eigenvalues show how a transformation (e.g., stretching or compression) affects an object along special 'compliant' directions.
eigenvalue.fun_fact_popular: Eigenvalues explain why bridges don't collapse from wind: engineers calculate at what frequency the bridge will start to oscillate and ensure that these 'eigenfrequencies' do not match the wind frequency.
eigenvalue.description_simple: In linear algebra, an eigenvalue is a number characterizing how many times a vector is stretched or compressed under a linear transformation if the vector remains on the same line. It's like the shadow of a rotating stick: there are directions where the shadow only changes length but not direction — those are eigendirections, and the length change coefficients are eigenvalues.
eigenvalue.history_simple: The idea of eigenvalues emerged in the 19th century in the works of Augustin Cauchy and Charles Hermite while studying quadratic forms. But true fame came to them from quantum mechanics, where the Schrödinger equation is an eigenvalue problem: the energy of a particle turns out to be an eigenvalue of the Hamiltonian operator.
eigenvalue.how_it_works_simple: In quantum mechanics, a particle's state is described by a wave function. When measuring energy, we apply an operator to it, like a template that 'reveals' specific values — eigenvalues. Just as a sieve sorts sand by particle size, the energy operator 'sifts' through possible states and yields the allowed energy levels of an atom.
eigenvalue.description: An eigenvalue of a linear operator A is a number λ such that there exists a nonzero vector v (eigenvector) satisfying the equation A v = λ v. The spectrum of an operator (the set of eigenvalues) plays a key role in functional analysis and quantum mechanics. For Hermitian operators, eigenvalues are real, which physically is interpreted as possible measurement results of an observable. Generalization to infinite-dimensional spaces leads to self-adjoint operators with a continuous spectrum.
eigenvalue.history: The roots of the concept go back to the 18th century: Euler studied the rotation of a rigid body, identifying the principal axes of inertia. In the 19th century, Cauchy developed the theory of eigenvalues of symmetric matrices. A decisive contribution was made by David Hilbert, who developed the spectral theory of operators, paving the way for quantum mechanics. In 1926, Erwin Schrödinger formulated his famous equation Ĥψ = Eψ, where Ĥ is the Hamiltonian operator and E is the energy eigenvalue. Since then, eigenvalue problems have become the main tool for calculating electronic structures, vibrations, and stability of systems.
eigenvalue.how_it_works: A quantum-mechanical observable is represented by a Hermitian operator Â. The eigenvalue equation is: Â ψ_n = a_n ψ_n, where ψ_n is an eigenfunction and a_n is an eigenvalue. Heisenberg's uncertainty principle limits the possibility of simultaneous precise measurement of non-commuting observables whose operators do not share common eigenvectors. In engineering, eigenvalues of the stress tensor determine principal stresses in materials, and in graph theory, eigenvalues of the adjacency matrix characterize network connectivity.
key_problems: Computing eigenvalues of large sparse matrices is a fundamental problem of numerical analysis
Complete description of the Dirac operator spectrum in curved spacetime remains mathematically challenging
Relationship between eigenvalue distributions of random matrices and quantum chaos and number theory
eigenvalue.fun_fact: Eigenvalue search is used by Google's search engine: the PageRank algorithm is based on computing the eigenvector of the web page link matrix. And facial recognition systems turn photos into eigenvectors, extracting key features.

formulas.description: Eigenvalue equation
formulas.meaning: A — linear operator (matrix), v — eigenvector, λ — eigenvalue

formulas.description: Time-independent Schrödinger equation
formulas.meaning: Ĥ — Hamiltonian operator (total energy of the system), ψ — wave function, E — energy eigenvalue

pulsar.name: pulsar
pulsar.description_popular: Imagine a space lighthouse that spins at a furious speed and beams radio waves like a giant searchlight. When the beam sweeps across Earth, we see regular flashes — like a blinking lightbulb.
pulsar.fun_fact_popular: Pulsars are so precise that you can set your watch by them! Some tick with better accuracy than atomic clocks.
pulsar.description_simple: A pulsar is a rapidly spinning neutron star left after a supernova explosion. It has a very strong magnetic field that channels radio emission into a narrow beam. When the beam, like a lighthouse beam, points toward Earth, we detect periodic pulses.
pulsar.history_simple: Pulsars were discovered in 1967 by graduate student Jocelyn Bell, who noticed regular signals on a radio telescope. At first, they were even thought to be signals from aliens! Later, it was realized they were rapidly spinning neutron stars.
pulsar.how_it_works_simple: A neutron star, the size of a city, is compressed to the limit and retains its rotation, like a figure skater pulling in her arms. Its magnetic field twists and generates radiation that emerges from the poles in narrow beams. If Earth lies in the path of such a beam, we see a pulsation.
pulsar.description: A pulsar is a rotating neutron star with a strong magnetic field (10^8–10^15 G), emitting electromagnetic radiation in the form of directional beams. Due to rotation, the beams create a lighthouse effect, and an observer records periodic pulses. Pulsars can emit from radio waves to gamma rays. Their rotation slows down over time due to energy loss. Some pulsars in binary systems speed up by accreting matter from a companion — these are millisecond pulsars.
pulsar.history: The idea of neutron stars was proposed by Fritz Zwicky and Walter Baade in 1934, but the discovery of pulsars occurred in 1967 by Jocelyn Bell Burnell under the supervision of Antony Hewish. At first, the signals were named LGM-1 (little green men). In 1968, Franco Pacini and Thomas Gold independently linked pulsars to rotating neutron stars. Since then, thousands of pulsars have been discovered, including binary ones, which allowed testing of the theory of gravity. Fritz Zwicky was a pioneer in predicting neutron stars.
pulsar.how_it_works: A pulsar works due to conservation of angular momentum. During collapse, the core shrinks to a radius of ~10 km, increasing the rotation speed. The magnetic field intensifies, and radiation is generated in the polar caps, where charged particles are accelerated along field lines, producing coherent radiation. Precession and spin-down are described by equations of electrodynamics and general relativity, for instance, energy loss to gravitational waves for rapidly rotating asymmetric pulsars.
key_problems: The exact mechanism of coherent radiation generation is unknown.
Why do pulsars exhibit glitches (rotation irregularities)?
Can pulsars be used to detect low-frequency gravitational waves?
What is the structure of matter in the core of a neutron star?
pulsar.fun_fact: The fastest pulsar spins 716 times per second — faster than a kitchen blender!

formulas.description: Estimation of a pulsar's magnetic field from its period and spin-down rate.
formulas.meaning: B — magnetic field strength (gauss), P — rotation period (seconds), \dot{P} — period derivative (s/s). The constant 3.2×10^19 is an empirical coefficient.

supernova.name: supernova
supernova.description_popular: A supernova is a grand fireworks display, when a star explodes and momentarily shines brighter than an entire galaxy. It's like a balloon that's been inflated for millions of years until it pops.
supernova.fun_fact_popular: The most powerful supernova explosion was seen back in 1006 – and it was as bright as day at night!
supernova.description_simple: A supernova is a stellar explosion that makes the star billions of times brighter than the Sun. It happens when a massive star runs out of fuel and its core collapses, or when a white dwarf accretes too much matter.
supernova.history_simple: Supernovae were first observed by Chinese astronomers as early as 185 AD. In the 20th century, Fritz Zwicky defined them as a separate class and predicted that they leave behind neutron stars.
supernova.how_it_works_simple: In a massive star, nuclear fusion creates iron. But iron does not burn, and the core stops pushing outward. Gravity crushes the core into a neutron star or black hole, while the outer layers rebound and are flung into space.
supernova.description: A supernova is the catastrophic finale of a star's evolution, accompanied by a huge energy release (~10^44 J) and synthesis of heavy elements. Types are distinguished: I (no hydrogen in spectrum) and II (with hydrogen lines). Type II supernovae occur from the iron-core collapse of a massive star (>8 solar masses), type Ia from the thermonuclear explosion of a white dwarf in a binary system upon reaching the Chandrasekhar limit. The explosion creates neutron stars or black holes and scatters matter, enriching the galaxy with elements.
supernova.history: The term 'supernova' was coined by Fritz Zwicky and Walter Baade in 1934, suggesting that core collapse leads to the formation of a neutron star. Previously observed 'guest stars', such as in 1054 (the Crab Nebula), were interpreted as novae. A type classification was developed by Rudolph Minkowski in 1941 based on spectra. In 1987, supernova 1987A in the Large Magellanic Cloud provided an opportunity to test theories and record a neutrino burst from the collapse.
supernova.how_it_works: For core-collapse supernovae (Type II): when the core exhausts its nuclear fuel by fusing iron, degenerate electron pressure can no longer withstand gravity. The core collapses at up to 70,000 km/s, reaching nuclear density. Neutronization occurs: p + e- → n + ν_e. A neutron star is born, and the shock wave powered by neutrino heating tears apart the outer layers. For Type Ia: a white dwarf accretes matter, reaches the Chandrasekhar limit (~1.4 M☉), triggering runaway carbon fusion that completely destroys the dwarf.
key_problems: What is the exact explosion mechanism of core-collapse supernovae: how does the shock wave get revived?
Why do some massive stars fail to explode and instead collapse directly into a black hole?
Are Type Ia supernovae perfectly standard candles for cosmology?
What is the role of magnetic fields and rotation in explosion asymmetry?
supernova.fun_fact: Without supernovae, there would be no life: they scatter carbon, oxygen, and iron across the cosmos – the very elements we are made of!

formulas.description: The mass limit of a white dwarf, above which it collapses.
formulas.meaning: M_{Ch} — Chandrasekhar limit, M_{\odot} — solar mass (≈ 2×10^{30} kg). Exceeding this mass makes the white dwarf unstable and it explodes as a Type Ia supernova.

gravitational_lensing.name: gravitational lensing
gravitational_lensing.description_popular: Imagine the Universe as a giant aquarium, where massive objects bend space like a heavy ball on a rubber sheet. Light from distant stars, as it passes by, gets its path bent, and we see distorted, magnified images, as if through a warped lens.
gravitational_lensing.fun_fact_popular: A gravitational lens can magnify light so much that we see the same distant galaxy in multiple places — like in a hall of mirrors!
gravitational_lensing.description_simple: Gravitational lensing is the bending of light from distant cosmic objects by the gravity of massive bodies, such as galaxies or galaxy clusters. It creates magnified, distorted, or multiple images, similar to an optical lens.
gravitational_lensing.history_simple: Einstein predicted the bending of light in 1915, and in 1937 Fritz Zwicky suggested that galaxies could act as gravitational lenses. The first real lens was seen in 1979 — the double quasar Q0957+561.
gravitational_lensing.how_it_works_simple: A massive body curves spacetime around it, like a heavy ball denting a mattress. Light, traveling through curved space, bends around the obstacle and focuses at different points, creating unusual images.
gravitational_lensing.description: Gravitational lensing is the deflection of light in a gravitational field, predicted by Einstein's general theory of relativity. A massive object (lens) curves spacetime, causing light rays from a source to follow geodesics. An observer can see multiple images, rings (Einstein rings), arcs, or magnified images. Different types include strong lensing (multiple images, arcs), weak lensing (distortion of background galaxy shapes), and microlensing (brightness changes of stars when a compact object passes). It is used to map dark matter and study cosmology.
gravitational_lensing.history: The idea of light bending by a massive body had been discussed since the 18th century, but within Newtonian theory. In 1915 Einstein derived the correct deflection angle (twice the Newtonian value). In 1919 Eddington's expedition confirmed the deflection during a solar eclipse. In the 1930s Fritz Zwicky proposed using lensing by galaxy clusters as 'telescopes'. The first multiple system (double quasar) was found in 1979 by Dennis Walsh and colleagues. Today it is a key tool for cosmology, e.g., surveys by the Vera Rubin Observatory.
gravitational_lensing.how_it_works: According to general relativity, Einstein's equations describe spacetime curvature. Light follows null geodesics, and its trajectory is described by an effective refractive index n = 1 - 2Φ/c², where Φ is the gravitational potential. The deflection angle for a point mass is α = 4GM/(c²ξ), where ξ is the impact parameter. When source, lens, and observer align, an Einstein ring forms with angular radius θ_E = sqrt((4GM)/(c²) * (D_ls)/(D_l D_s)), where D are distances. For distributed mass, lensing yields distortions from which a density map of matter, including dark matter, is constructed.
key_problems: Can warm and cold dark matter be distinguished through small-scale lenses?
What is the nature of lensing on very large scales, affecting the cosmic microwave background?
Do primordial black holes exist that can be found via microlensing?
How to properly model complex lens potentials with substructure?
gravitational_lensing.fun_fact: Gravitational lensing helped discover the distant star Earendel, whose light traveled nearly 13 billion years – magnified thousands of times by a galaxy cluster!

formulas.description: Angular radius of the Einstein ring for a point mass.
formulas.meaning: θ_E — angular radius of Einstein ring (radians), G — gravitational constant (≈ 6.674×10⁻¹¹ m³/(kg·s²)), M — lens mass (kg), c — speed of light (≈ 3×10⁸ m/s), D_ls — angular distance from lens to source (m), D_l — distance to lens (m), D_s — distance to source (m).

jwst.name: JWST
jwst.description_popular: Imagine a cosmic eye that sees in the dark. The James Webb Space Telescope is a giant mirror in space that catches light invisible to our eyes, just as a cat sees in the dark. It looks back in time to see how the first stars and galaxies were born, like flipping through an old photo album of the Universe.
jwst.fun_fact_popular: Webb's mirror is made of gold and is the size of a tennis court. Its sunshield is as big as a bus stop, and it always keeps the telescope in the shade, like a beach umbrella.
jwst.description_simple: JWST is a space observatory that operates in the infrared range. Just as a snake senses the heat radiation of its prey, the telescope detects heat emitted by ancient objects. This allows it to peer through dust clouds and see processes hidden from ordinary telescopes.
jwst.history_simple: Launched on December 25, 2021, after decades of planning. Named after James Webb, NASA administrator during the Apollo program. Developed by NASA in collaboration with the European and Canadian space agencies.
jwst.how_it_works_simple: A huge segmented mirror collects infrared light and focuses it onto detectors. Just as a sunflower turns toward the sun, the thermal shield always blocks sunlight and heat from the Sun and Earth, cooling the instruments to cryogenic temperatures for maximum sensitivity.
jwst.description: The James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) is a next-generation infrared space observatory with a 6.5-meter segmented mirror. It is equipped with four main instruments operating in the 0.6 to 28 μm range, enabling the study of the early Universe, formation of galaxies, stars, and planetary systems. Infrared astronomy is critical for observing high-redshift objects, whose visible light has shifted into the infrared due to the expansion of the Universe.
jwst.history: The concept for JWST emerged in 1996 as the 'Next Generation Space Telescope' (NGST). The launch, initially planned for 2007, was delayed due to technical challenges and budget overruns. Named after James Webb, NASA administrator (1961–1968). The project involved NASA, ESA, and CSA. The main science goals were defined by the Decadal Survey of Astronomy and Astrophysics.
jwst.how_it_works: The telescope is placed at the Sun-Earth L2 point, providing a stable thermal environment. A five-layer sunshield made of Kapton passively cools the instruments to about 40 K. The segmented beryllium primary mirror with gold coating corrects wavefront errors using adaptive optics systems. Detectors register infrared radiation, which is then analyzed by spectrometers and cameras. Observations rely on spectroscopy and gravitational lensing (for amplifying distant objects).
key_problems: The need to refine stellar evolution models based on new data
Difficulty in interpreting exoplanet atmosphere spectra due to low resolution
Limited lifetime due to fuel consumption for station-keeping (expected 10-20 years)
jwst.fun_fact: JWST is so sensitive it could see the faint glow of a bumblebee at the distance of the Moon. And its orbit is at the L2 Lagrange point, 1.5 million kilometers from Earth – farther than any previous space telescope.

formulas.description: Angular resolution of the telescope
formulas.meaning: θ — angular resolution in radians, λ — wavelength of observed radiation, D — diameter of the primary mirror (for JWST D ≈ 6.5 m). At λ=2 μm, the resolution is about 0.074 arcseconds.

large_hadron_collider.name: Large Hadron Collider
large_hadron_collider.description_popular: The Large Hadron Collider is a giant underground racetrack for the tiniest particles. Imagine two streams that are accelerated to nearly the speed of light and collide, like two boats at full speed. At the moment of impact, new particles are born like splashes. Scientists study these 'splashes' to understand what everything is made of.
large_hadron_collider.fun_fact_popular: The collider's ring is 27 kilometers long, like the circumference of a city, and the magnets inside are so powerful that it would take the electricity of an entire nuclear power plant to power them. The temperature inside is lower than in outer space.
large_hadron_collider.description_simple: The LHC is the world's most powerful particle accelerator, speeding up protons and ions to record energies. Just as the collision of two bullets releases energy and fragments, here new particles are created at collision points, including the elusive Higgs boson, discovered in 2012.
large_hadron_collider.history_simple: Built at the European Organization for Nuclear Research (CERN) under the border between Switzerland and France. Construction began in 1998, the first run in 2008. In 2012 the discovery of the Higgs boson was announced.
large_hadron_collider.how_it_works_simple: Particle beams are accelerated by electric fields in a circular tunnel, while powerful superconducting magnets guide them around. At four detector points, the beams collide, and sensors record the debris of these micro-catastrophes, recreating conditions that existed shortly after the Big Bang.
large_hadron_collider.description: The Large Hadron Collider (LHC) is a proton-proton (and ion) collider located at CERN, with a ring circumference of 26.7 km, operating at a center-of-mass energy up to 13.6 TeV (as of 2023). It is designed to study fundamental particles and interactions of the Standard Model and to search for new physics, including dark matter, extra dimensions, and supersymmetry. The collider uses superconducting niobium-titanium magnets, cooled to 1.9 K, producing fields up to 8.3 T.
large_hadron_collider.history: The idea for the Large Hadron Collider dates back to the 1980s. The project was officially approved in 1994. Construction took place in the existing LEP (Large Electron-Positron Collider) tunnel. In 2008, the startup was postponed due to a helium cooling incident. After repairs and upgrades, the LHC reached design parameters and in 2012 made the historic discovery of the Higgs boson, predicted by Peter Higgs and others in 1964. During 2015–2018 (Run 2), the collider operated at 13 TeV, and in Run 3 (from 2022) the energy was increased to 13.6 TeV.
large_hadron_collider.how_it_works: Protons are accelerated in several stages: a linear accelerator, the Proton Synchrotron (PS), the Super Proton Synchrotron (SPS), and finally the LHC ring. Radiofrequency cavities create an electric field that accelerates the particles. The magnetic system, based on the Meissner effect in superconductors, keeps the beams in a circular orbit. At the beam crossing points (ATLAS, CMS, ALICE, LHCb) detectors record the products of collisions. Particle creation is described by equations of quantum field theory, and the analysis is based on conservation of energy and momentum (relativistic kinematics, E=mc²).
key_problems: The hierarchy problem and naturalness
The nature of dark matter
The absence of supersymmetric particles in current data
The matter-antimatter asymmetry in the Universe
large_hadron_collider.fun_fact: Inside the LHC, protons travel at almost the speed of light, making over 11,000 laps per second. And the vacuum in the tube is so profound that the pressure is ten trillion times lower than atmospheric pressure – like in interstellar space.

formulas.description: Mass-energy equivalence (Einstein's formula)
formulas.meaning: E — energy, m — rest mass, c — speed of light in vacuum (c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s). In the collider, the kinetic energy of colliding particles converts into the mass of new particles.

gluon.name: gluon
gluon.description_popular: Gluons are invisible threads that glue quarks in protons and neutrons, like superglue. Without them all matter would crumble into dust.
gluon.fun_fact_popular: Gluons are so sticky that they cling not only to quarks but also to each other! This makes the strong interaction very unusual.
gluon.description_simple: The gluon is like a courier that continuously carries a 'color package' from one quark to another. Unlike the photon (the electric charge courier), the gluon can change the color of the package and even create new identical packages.
gluon.history_simple: In 1979 at the PETRA collider in Germany, physicists first observed three-jet events in electron-positron collisions — direct evidence of gluon radiation by quarks.
gluon.how_it_works_simple: When quarks exchange gluons, they change their color, like players in tag exchanging colored ribbons. This vigorous exchange prevents quarks from flying apart, holding them inside hadrons like in a tight sack.
gluon.description: Gluons are the gauge bosons of the strong interaction, massless particles with spin 1 and negative parity. Unlike photons, gluons carry a color charge (combination of color and anticolor), leading to the existence of eight independent types of gluons. This feature makes QCD a non-Abelian theory, where gluons interact with each other, giving rise to asymptotic freedom and confinement.
gluon.history: The concept of gluons emerged with quantum chromodynamics in the early 1970s. The works of Gross, Wilczek, and Politzer (Nobel Prize 2004) demonstrated the property of asymptotic freedom, explaining the weakness of interaction at short distances. First experimental confirmation was obtained in 1979 at DESY (Hamburg) by observing three-jet events in e⁺e⁻ annihilation. Later, gluon properties were studied in detail at LEP, HERA, and LHC colliders.
gluon.how_it_works: The gluon field is described by the tensor G_{μν}^a within QCD with the gauge group SU(3). The Lagrangian contains gluon self-interaction terms (triple and quartic vertices), which leads to saturation of the gluon field at high energies and confinement. The exchange of gluons between quarks generates a force whose potential grows linearly with distance: V(r) ~ kr. When trying to isolate a color charge, the field energy reaches the threshold for quark-antiquark pair production, leading to hadronization and jet formation. Asymptotic freedom ensures that at ultra-short distances the interaction weakens, allowing the application of perturbation theory.
key_problems: Understanding the mechanism of confinement and the structure of the QCD physical vacuum.
The role of gluons in the spin structure of the nucleon (spin crisis problem).
Description of processes in the gluon saturation regime at extreme energy densities (e.g., Colour Glass Condensate).
gluon.fun_fact: The name 'gluon' comes from the English 'glue' — very accurately reflecting their role.

color_charge.name: color charge
color_charge.description_popular: Color charge is a special property of quarks and gluons, like paint on balls. Only instead of paints there are three colors: red, green, and blue. It helps particles 'communicate' via the strong interaction.
color_charge.fun_fact_popular: Quarks constantly change their color, like chameleons in the jungle. But in nature we see only 'colorless' combinations — like light from three colored flashlight beams.
color_charge.description_simple: Color charge works like electric charge, but for the strong interaction. Imagine that quarks are fish of three colors (red, green, blue), and the gluon is a fisherman who carries colored worms. Scientists agreed that mixing all three colors gives 'white', neutral — such particles survive.
color_charge.history_simple: Oscar Greenberg in 1964 invented color charge to solve the puzzle: how can three identical quarks be in one proton? It turned out they have different 'colors', which saves the Pauli exclusion principle.
color_charge.how_it_works_simple: Quarks bombard each other with gluons, swapping colors. The simplest way to stick together is for three quarks with different colors (as in a proton) or a quark and antiquark with opposite colors (as in a pion) to come together. The force between them grows with distance: the further they fly apart, the stronger the attraction.
color_charge.description: Color charge is a quantum number characterizing the transformations of quark and gluon fields with respect to the gauge group SU(3) in quantum chromodynamics. For quarks in the fundamental representation, three values are possible (colors: red, green, blue); for antiquarks, anticolors. Gluons are in the adjoint representation and are described by an octet of color states (e.g., red-antigreen). Color charge is strictly conserved in all interactions, but the confinement mechanism prohibits the existence of free colored objects in the low-energy limit.
color_charge.history: The concept was introduced by Oscar Greenberg in 1964 to explain the symmetry of baryon wave functions. Soon Yoichiro Nambu and Han Moo-Young proposed using a triplet structure. The final formulation in the framework of QCD was completed in the 1970s, when asymptotic freedom was discovered and the role of SU(3) as an exact gauge symmetry was understood. Experimentally, the existence of color charge is confirmed by analysis of hadron production cross sections in e⁺e⁻ collisions (ratio R = σ(e⁺e⁻ → hadrons)/σ(e⁺e⁻ → μ⁺μ⁻)).
color_charge.how_it_works: In QCD, each quark current changes color by emitting or absorbing a gluon. The interaction Lagrangian has the form g_s \bar{ψ} γ^μ t^a ψ A_μ^a, where t^a are the generators of SU(3) in the fundamental representation. Gluons themselves carry color, so their self-interaction leads to charge anti-screening: the effective coupling constant α_s increases with decreasing energy scale. This leads to confinement at distances of about 1 fm: the potential V(r) ≈ -4α_s/(3r) + σ r, where σ is the string tension. At high energies (small distances) α_s decreases (asymptotic freedom). Observed hadrons are always white (singlet under SU(3)).
key_problems: Proof of confinement and existence of an upper bound for the length of the color string.
Study of color superconductivity under conditions of extreme baryon density (neutron star core).
Search for glueballs — hypothetical states consisting only of gluons.
color_charge.fun_fact: Color charge is named by analogy with the three primary colors in painting, but it has nothing to do with real color. It's just a convenient language for physicists.

photon.name: photon
photon.description_popular: The photon is like a tiny clot of light, the smallest portion of energy emitted, for example, by a glowing ember or a distant star. Imagine a raindrop — seemingly a single droplet, but together they form a downpour. So too photons, flying out in billions, paint the entire visible world for us: from the blue of the sky to the colors of sunset.
photon.fun_fact_popular: Every second, about 100 million photons pass through the pupil of your eye from just one 100-watt light bulb. And from the Sun, about 100 billion of these tiny travelers strike every square centimeter of skin per second!
photon.description_simple: The photon is a quantum of light, the smallest possible portion of electromagnetic radiation. Imagine it as a messenger that tirelessly races at a constant speed and carries energy and information about distant objects. Unlike a marathon runner, the photon never tires — it has no mass and exists only while in motion. A photon is born when an excited atom transitions to a calm state, throwing out excess energy in the form of a flash. It's like sharply breathing out steam in the cold — a cloud appears, only instead of water droplets, light is born.
photon.history_simple: As early as the 17th century, Newton suggested that light consists of tiny particles. But the real revolution happened in 1905 when Albert Einstein explained the photoelectric effect — the ejection of electrons from metal by light — precisely with the help of light quanta. Although the word 'photon' itself appeared only in 1926, coined by chemist Gilbert Lewis, the idea of a light particle became one of the pillars of quantum physics.
photon.how_it_works_simple: When an electron in an atom jumps from a high orbit to a low one, it emits excess energy in the form of a photon. This process is like a stone thrown into a pond: the impact energy spreads in circles on the water. However, the photon manages to be both a wave and a particle at the same time. It is this dual nature that allows it, for example, to interfere or reflect, but also to be registered by a detector as a point flash.
photon.description: The photon is an elementary particle, the quantum of the electromagnetic field, and the carrier of the electromagnetic interaction. In the Standard Model, it belongs to the gauge bosons, has zero rest mass, zero electric charge, and spin 1. The photon always moves in a vacuum at the speed of light c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s, carries no electric charge, and does not decay in a vacuum. It is emitted and absorbed by charged particles (e.g., electrons during transitions between energy levels) and exhibits wave-particle duality: in some experiments it behaves as a wave (interference, diffraction), in others as a particle (photoelectric effect, Compton scattering). The photon's energy is proportional to its frequency: E = hν. Photons make up visible light, radio waves, X-rays, and gamma rays, differing only in energy. In quantum electrodynamics, they are described by the exchange of virtual photons between charges.
photon.history: The concept of the discrete nature of radiation originated in 1900 when Max Planck introduced energy quantization to explain the spectrum of a black body. Albert Einstein in 1905 developed the hypothesis, suggesting that light is not only emitted but also exists in the form of quanta, successfully explaining the photoelectric effect. In 1923, Arthur Compton experimentally confirmed the corpuscular nature by observing the scattering of X-ray photons on electrons. The term 'photon' was proposed by Gilbert Lewis in 1926. The subsequent development of quantum electrodynamics (Feynman, Schwinger, Tomonaga) ultimately established the photon as the exchange particle of the electromagnetic field. From the provided list, James Clerk Maxwell, whose equations describe electromagnetic waves, and Alain Aspect, who conducted experiments with photons to test Bell's inequalities, are associated with the photon.
photon.how_it_works: A photon is emitted when a quantum system (e.g., an electron in an atom) transitions from a higher energy state to a lower energy state, carrying away the energy difference. Conversely, absorption of a photon excites the system to a higher energy state. The photon always propagates at speed c in a vacuum, which is a consequence of the principle of the constancy of the speed of light. In an electromagnetic wave described by Maxwell's equations, the photon represents the elementary excitation of the field modes. Its energy and momentum are given by the formulas E = hν and p = h/λ; thus, the higher the frequency (shorter the wavelength), the greater the photon's energy. According to the Heisenberg uncertainty principle, it is impossible to simultaneously measure the arrival time of a photon and its energy precisely. Photons obey Bose-Einstein statistics and can accumulate in a single quantum state, which underlies the operation of lasers. Interaction with matter leads to phenomena such as Hawking radiation near black holes and gravitational lensing — the curvature of photon trajectories in gravitational fields.
key_problems: How exactly does a photon interfere with itself in the double-slit experiment under 'non-disturbing' measurement?
The nature of a single photon — can we obtain its complete spatio-temporal 'photograph'?
Creation of an ideal single-photon source of high purity for quantum communications.
photon.fun_fact: Photons do not age. The ancient light we observe as cosmic microwave background radiation has traveled to us for nearly 13.8 billion years — almost since the birth of the Universe.

formulas.description: Photon energy
formulas.meaning: E — photon energy (J); h — Planck constant, h ≈ 6.626×10^{-34} J·s; ν — photon frequency (Hz)

formulas.description: Photon momentum
formulas.meaning: p — photon momentum (kg·m/s); h — Planck constant, h ≈ 6.626×10^{-34} J·s; λ — photon wavelength (m)

interferometer.name: interferometer
interferometer.description_popular: The interferometer is like a clever detective that investigates the difference in the paths of two beams. Imagine you and your friend running towards each other from opposite ends of a maze; when you meet, you can tell who ran slower by how out of breath you are. The interferometer 'sees' even a difference of a thousandth of the thickness of a hair — by how the light and dark fringes shift, like sand in an hourglass.
interferometer.fun_fact_popular: The giant LIGO interferometers can detect a change in the length of their four-kilometer arms of less than one ten-thousandth the size of a proton — about the same as if the distance from Earth to the Sun changed by the thickness of a human hair!
interferometer.description_simple: The interferometer is an optical instrument in which a beam of light is split into two, travels along different paths, and then meets again. Due to the wave nature of light, the beams superimpose (interfere) and form alternating dark and light bands — an interference pattern. If one of the paths is slightly lengthened — for example, due to the passage of a gravitational wave — the entire pattern immediately shifts. The interferometer is like sensitive scales that measure not mass, but the slightest fluctuations of space.
interferometer.history_simple: The play of light on soap bubbles attracted the attention of Robert Hooke in the 17th century — these were the first observations of interference. But the instrument was built in 1881 by American physicist Albert Michelson, hoping to use it to detect the mysterious 'ether' in which light was supposedly propagating. The Michelson-Morley experiment found no ether, but proved the constancy of the speed of light. Today, laser interferometers LIGO and Virgo detect the tremors of spacetime from the merger of black holes.
interferometer.how_it_works_simple: Imagine two boats on a calm lake, rowing synchronously and creating circles from the oars. If the boats move strictly side by side, the circles meet and form calm and rough areas. The same thing happens in the interferometer: light waves from one source travel along two arms and then combine. If the distance traveled is slightly different, some waves arrive 'peak to peak' and amplify each other, while others arrive 'peak to trough' and cancel. By measuring how much the fringes have shifted, we learn the microscopic difference in paths.
interferometer.description: The interferometer is a high-precision measuring instrument based on the phenomenon of superposition of electromagnetic (usually light) waves. Principle of operation: a coherent beam is split into two or more beams; they travel optical paths of different lengths and are then recombined for interference. The resulting intensity distribution depends on the phase difference, which is determined by the path difference ΔL. Main schemes: Michelson (with two perpendicular arms and a return mirror), Mach-Zehnder, Fabry-Perot, Sagnac. Sensitivity reaches fractions of a wavelength, making it possible to detect displacements on the order of 10⁻¹⁸ m in gravitational-wave detectors. Applications: precision metrology, spectroscopy, testing fundamental theories, astronomy (very-long-baseline interferometers), laser gyroscopy, quantum optics.
interferometer.history: Interference phenomena were observed by Newton ('Newton's rings'), but systematic study began with Thomas Young in 1801 (double-slit experiment). The first interferometer was built by Albert Michelson in 1881 to test the hypothesis of the luminiferous ether. The famous Michelson-Morley experiment (1887) showed with the highest accuracy the absence of ether wind, which became a decisive argument for the constancy of the speed of light. In the mid-20th century, Joseph Weber built resonant gravitational antennas, but did not achieve decisive success. Modern laser interferometers (LIGO, Virgo) were proposed in the 1970s-80s and in 2015 for the first time directly detected gravitational waves (from the merger of black holes). Robert Hooke in 1665 described the colors of thin films, which was a manifestation of interference. Alain Aspect used interferometers in pioneering experiments on photon entanglement.
interferometer.how_it_works: In the Michelson interferometer, light from a laser hits a beam splitter, where half the intensity is reflected into a perpendicular arm, and half passes straight through. At the end of each arm, a mirror reflects the beam back. After returning, both beams partially pass through the beam splitter and reach a photodetector. The phase difference Δφ = 2πΔL/λ determines whether interference is constructive (light) or destructive (dark). The intensity of the detected light is described by the formula I = I₁+I₂+2√(I₁I₂)cos(Δφ). Any slightest change in ΔL (due to gravitational waves, thermal expansion, or vibrations) leads to a change in the signal. To enhance sensitivity, Fabry-Perot cavities are installed in the arms, greatly increasing the effective light path. Maxwell's equations guarantee the constancy of the speed of light, allowing interferometry to be used as a 'ruler' of exceptional precision.
key_problems: Overcoming the quantum limit of sensitivity due to photon shot noise.
Development of compact and stable interferometers for navigation systems (atomic gyroscopes).
Suppression of seismic and thermal noise in ground-based gravitational observatories.
interferometer.fun_fact: The interferometer suggested to Einstein that the speed of light is always the same for any observer, which later became the basis of special relativity.

formulas.description: Intensity of interference pattern
formulas.meaning: I — resulting intensity; I₁, I₂ — intensities of the two interfering beams; Δφ — phase difference, defined as Δφ = 2πΔL/λ, where ΔL is the optical path difference (m), λ is the wavelength (m); cos — cosine.

bose_einstein_condensate.name: Bose-Einstein condensate
bose_einstein_condensate.description_popular: Imagine a flock of birds that suddenly starts moving as a single whole — each bird repeats the movement of its neighbor, and the flock turns into a giant wave. A Bose-Einstein condensate is a 'flock' of atoms cooled to unimaginably low temperatures, where millions of particles lose their individuality and merge into one quantum droplet. In this state, atoms behave not as a chaotic crowd, but as one super-atom.
bose_einstein_condensate.fun_fact_popular: The condensate is the coldest substance in the Universe, only a few billionths of a degree above absolute zero. If you place a laser beam into it, light can slow down to the speed of a cyclist (about 60 km/h), or even come to a complete stop, as if frozen inside ice.
bose_einstein_condensate.description_simple: Bose-Einstein condensate (BEC) is an exotic state of matter that occurs when a dilute gas of bosons (particles with integer spin) is cooled to ultra-low temperatures. All the atoms as if 'agree' to occupy the same quantum state — like many frightened penguins huddling together in a tight bunch, only here they are not warming themselves, but achieving maximum quantum order. As a result, a substance appears that is at once a single giant matter wave, capable of flowing without friction and exhibiting strange quantum properties on a macroscopic level.
bose_einstein_condensate.history_simple: In 1924, Indian physicist Satyendra Nath Bose sent Einstein an article on a new statistics for particles of light. Einstein immediately recognized the importance of the idea and extended it to atoms, showing that with sufficient cooling a gas should 'condense' into the lowest energy state. But experimental realization proved extremely difficult. Only in 1995 did Eric Cornell and Carl Wieman obtain the first condensate from rubidium atoms, cooling them to 170 nanokelvins. For this discovery, they (together with Wolfgang Ketterle) were awarded the Nobel Prize.
bose_einstein_condensate.how_it_works_simple: Imagine a group of musicians tuning their instruments. While it's warm (ordinary temperature), each plays its own note — complete chaos. But as it cools, their 'breath' slows down, and they begin to synchronize until they sound in unison as one pure note. So too atoms: at room temperature, they rush around at different speeds, but upon cooling to nanokelvins, their quantum waves smear out and overlap, forcing all particles to occupy a single energy level. The condensate behaves as a single object — it can even be 'shaken' and made to vibrate as a single drop.
bose_einstein_condensate.description: Bose-Einstein condensate (BEC) is a phase state of an ideal or weakly interacting Bose gas, in which a macroscopic fraction of particles occupies the ground (lowest energy) quantum state. The phenomenon is described by Bose-Einstein statistics and occurs at temperatures below the critical Tc, when the thermal de Broglie wavelength λdB = h/√(2π m k_B T) becomes comparable to the average distance between particles. The wave functions of individual atoms overlap, forming a single macroscopic wave function — the condensate. BEC exhibits superfluidity, a high degree of coherence, interference of atom waves, and suppression of inelastic scattering. Interaction in the condensate at low densities is well modeled by mean-field theory — the Gross-Pitaevskii equation. Condensation is analogous to the emergence of coherence in lasers (with the difference that photons condense there). Cooper pairs in superconductors also exhibit properties of a Bose condensate.
bose_einstein_condensate.history: The theoretical prediction of BEC dates back to the works of Satyendra Nath Bose (1924) and Albert Einstein (1925). Bose derived a distribution for photons without a classical limit, and Einstein applied this statistics to non-relativistic atoms. He showed that below a certain temperature, the gas should dramatically change its properties due to the accumulation of particles in the lowest energy state. For many decades, the search for the right conditions (necessary to avoid condensation into liquid and solid) yielded no results. The breakthrough came in 1995: the group of Eric Cornell and Carl Wieman from JILA (USA) first cooled a cloud of rubidium-87 atoms to a temperature of 170 nanokelvins and observed the phase transition to BEC, and soon Wolfgang Ketterle from MIT obtained a condensate from sodium atoms. These experiments opened the field of quantum gases and earned the authors the Nobel Prize in Physics in 2001.
bose_einstein_condensate.how_it_works: A Bose gas at high temperatures is described by Maxwell-Boltzmann statistics. As the temperature decreases, the de Broglie wavelength of the particles increases: λdB ∝ 1/√T. When λdB exceeds the average interparticle distance d (i.e., when nλdB³ ~ 1, more precisely nλdB³ = ζ(3/2) ≈ 2.612), the wave packets of atoms begin to overlap, and one must apply quantum Bose-Einstein statistics. For bosons, unlike fermions, the Pauli principle does not prohibit multiple occupation of a single quantum state; moreover, at low temperatures this becomes energetically favorable. A phase transition occurs, and a macroscopic number of particles accumulates in the ground state. This condensate is described by a single wave function, which satisfies the second law of thermodynamics: the entropy of the system decreases during the transition, but is compensated by heat removal to the external environment. The Gross-Pitaevskii equation incorporates this wave function and accounts for weak interaction between atoms.
key_problems: Creation of a stable condensate under gravity conditions and exclusion of all external fields.
Study of quantum turbulence in BEC and its connection to classical hydrodynamics.
Development of practically useful devices based on BEC — atomic lasers, precision gyroscopes, and quantum simulators.
bose_einstein_condensate.fun_fact: Bose-Einstein condensate can be used to create an 'atom laser' — a beam of atoms moving in perfect synchrony. Such a beam is already used in ultra-precise atomic clocks and interferometers.

formulas.description: Condition for phase transition in BEC
formulas.meaning: n — atom concentration (m⁻³); λdB = h/√(2π m k_B T) — thermal de Broglie wavelength (m), where h — Planck constant, h ≈ 6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s, m — atom mass (kg), k_B — Boltzmann constant, k_B ≈ 1.381×10⁻²³ J/K, T — absolute temperature (K). When this equality holds, a Bose-Einstein condensate forms.

standard_model.name: Standard Model
standard_model.description_popular: The Standard Model is the recipe by which nature "bakes" everything around us. It explains which tiny "bricks" matter is made of and how these bricks exchange "letters"—special force-carrying particles.
standard_model.fun_fact_popular: The Standard Model predicted the existence of particles long before their discovery. For example, the Higgs boson was only found in 2012, 48 years after it was conceived in theory!
standard_model.description_simple: The Standard Model unifies everything we know about the subatomic world. It is a mathematical framework describing 17 elementary particles: quarks (which build protons and neutrons), leptons (such as electrons), and bosons (which carry forces). Analogy: if the Universe is a complex orchestra, the Standard Model is the score that explains which instruments play and how they interact.
standard_model.history_simple: The Standard Model was gradually formed throughout the 20th century. In the late 1800s, Maxwell unified electricity and magnetism. In the 1960s, Glashow, Weinberg, and Salam created the theory of the electroweak interaction. The model took its final form in the 1970s after the confirmation of quark structure and the discovery of W and Z bosons.
standard_model.how_it_works_simple: The Standard Model says that forces arise from the exchange of bosons. For example, two magnets attract each other because they "toss" photons—particles of the electromagnetic field. In the atomic nucleus, protons are held together by gluons, which, like threads, stitch the quarks together. And the weak interaction, similar to radioactive decay, is caused by W and Z bosons.
standard_model.description: The Standard Model (SM) is a relativistic quantum field theory describing the electroweak and strong interactions of elementary particles. It is based on the gauge symmetry group SU(3)×SU(2)×U(1), spontaneously broken to SU(3)×U(1) by the Higgs mechanism. It includes: 6 quarks (u, d, c, s, t, b), 6 leptons (e, μ, τ and corresponding neutrinos), 12 gauge bosons (photon, W⁺, W⁻, Z, 8 gluons), and the scalar Higgs boson. The theory is renormalizable and consistent with the Copenhagen interpretation of quantum mechanics. The SM predicts the anomalous magnetic moment of the electron to an accuracy of 10⁻¹², which is a triumph of quantum electrodynamics.
standard_model.history: The development of the SM began with Maxwell's unification of electricity and magnetism (1864), followed by the quantization of the electromagnetic field (Planck, Einstein). In the 1920s, Schrödinger and Heisenberg built quantum mechanics, and Dirac created the relativistic equation for the electron. In 1930, Pauli formulated the exclusion principle, explaining the structure of atoms. The theory of strong interactions took shape in the 1960s–70s with the quark hypothesis (Gell-Mann, Zweig) and gluons, confirmed in collider experiments. The electroweak model was proposed by Weinberg, Salam, and Glashow. The discovery of the W and Z bosons (1983), the top quark (1995), and the Higgs boson (2012) at the LHC completed the experimental verification of the SM.
standard_model.how_it_works: The foundation of the SM is the principle of gauge invariance. The Lagrangian of the SM contains kinetic terms for matter fields (fermions), gauge fields, and the Higgs sector, including Yukawa interactions that give masses to fermions. Quantum electrodynamics (part of the SM) is based on Maxwell's equations and is described by photon exchange, obeying Coulomb's law at macroscopic distances. The strong interaction is described by quantum chromodynamics (QCD) with color charge and confinement. The Schrödinger equation and its relativistic generalizations (Klein–Gordon, Dirac) underlie the description of free fields. The Pauli principle determines the filling of fermion states. Fermi–Dirac and Bose–Einstein statistics govern the behavior of particles at low temperatures. Noether's theorem connects symmetries with conservation laws.
key_problems: The hierarchy problem (why the Higgs mass is so small compared to the Planck mass).
The dark matter problem — no SM particle fits its role.
Quantum gravity — the SM does not include gravitational interaction.
standard_model.fun_fact: In the Standard Model, there is a particle called the neutrino, which is so elusive that every second trillions of these particles pass through your fingernail, and almost none are stopped. And more: the mass of particles is explained by the Higgs field, which can be imagined as a snow field—the more a particle sinks into it, the heavier it becomes.

formulas.description: time-dependent Schrödinger equation — the fundamental equation of non-relativistic quantum mechanics
formulas.meaning: i is the imaginary unit, ħ is the reduced Planck constant, ħ = h/(2π) ≈ 1.0545718×10⁻³⁴ J·s, ∂ψ/∂t is the partial derivative of the wave function with respect to time, ψ is the wave function of the system, Ĥ is the Hamiltonian operator describing the total energy of the system

superposition.name: superposition
superposition.description_popular: A spinning coin on a table is not just heads or tails—it carries both possibilities at once until it stops. An electron inside an atom is similar: it is simultaneously everywhere a little bit, and its exact location appears only at the moment of observation.
superposition.fun_fact_popular: A quantum computer uses superposition to try all answers at once—as if you could read all the books in a library at the same time, without opening them one by one.
