superposition.description_simple: Superposition is a state in which a quantum particle exists in all its possible forms at once. It's like a sounding musical chord: while it lasts, several notes live in it at once, but if you stop the sound and ask which note was last, the instrument will only show one.
superposition.history_simple: Erwin Schrödinger gave mathematical life to superposition in 1926, writing down an equation where a cat could be both alive and dead until the box is opened. His idea grew from Louis de Broglie's earlier insight that particles behave like waves.
superposition.how_it_works_simple: Before measurement, a particle resembles a smeared probability cloud. When we try to catch it, the cloud collapses into one point. An example is the double slit: a single electron flies through both at once, creating a wave pattern on the screen, but any attempt to peek destroys the pattern and leaves only a dot.
superposition.description: Quantum superposition is a fundamental principle by which a closed system can exist in all permissible states simultaneously. Mathematically, it is described as a linear combination of eigenstates with complex amplitudes. The measurement outcome is unpredictable but follows Born's probability rule. This property radically distinguishes the microworld from classical physics and serves as the basis for quantum computing, interference, and entanglement.
superposition.history: Erwin Schrödinger showed in 1926 that the wave function of an electron can be a superposition of several eigenstates corresponding to different energy values. Louis de Broglie had already suggested in 1924 that particles possess wave characteristics, and Max Born in 1926 gave a probabilistic interpretation of the wave function. The Schrödinger's cat paradox, proposed in 1935, still fuels debates about the boundary between the quantum and classical worlds.
superposition.how_it_works: The wave function ψ is a sum of possible states: |ψ⟩ = Σ c_i |φ_i⟩. The time evolution is governed by the Schrödinger equation. Upon measurement, a collapse occurs to one of the eigenstates |φ_i⟩ with probability |c_i|², and all interference patterns vanish. This principle underlies the operation of quantum logic gates and explains paradoxes such as the double-slit experiment with single particles.
key_problems: The measurement problem: why does observation cause the collapse of the wave function?
The boundary between the quantum and classical worlds (decoherence)
Testability of the many-worlds interpretation
Relationship of quantum superposition with the gravitational field
superposition.fun_fact: Schrödinger's famous cat is not a household pet at all, but a 1935 thought experiment devised to show the absurdity of applying quantum rules to the ordinary world.

formulas.description: Wave function as a superposition of states
formulas.meaning: |ψ⟩ is the wave function of the system; |φ_i⟩ are the eigenstates of a physical quantity; c_i are complex probability amplitudes; summation runs over all allowed states. Normalization condition: Σ_i |c_i|² = 1.

formulas.description: Measurement probability (Born's rule)
formulas.meaning: P_i is the probability of finding the system in state |φ_i⟩ upon measurement; c_i is the probability amplitude for that state, |c_i|² is the square of its modulus.

waveparticle_duality.name: wave-particle duality
waveparticle_duality.description_popular: Light is a universal actor: in the role of a wave, it bends around obstacles and paints rainbow rings; in the role of a particle, it knocks electrons out of metal, giving rise to an electric current. Any particle, even an electron or a massive molecule, possesses both roles.
waveparticle_duality.fun_fact_popular: Scientists passed huge fullerene molecules (almost a millimeter in size) through two slits and obtained interference patterns—the molecules behaved like waves, even though you could touch them.
waveparticle_duality.description_simple: Wave-particle duality is the two-faced nature of the quantum world: the same object shows particle properties in one experiment and wave properties in another. It's like a person who, depending on the situation, acts as a strict boss or a caring parent, but remains the same person.
waveparticle_duality.history_simple: Louis de Broglie proposed in 1924 that electrons and other particles have a wavelength related to their momentum. The idea was quickly confirmed by experiments: electron beams produced the same interference pattern as light waves. Thus quantum mechanics gained its central principle.
waveparticle_duality.how_it_works_simple: In the famous double-slit experiment, single electrons launched one by one still form bands on the screen—a typical wave pattern. But if you place a detector to find out which slit the electron went through, the magic disappears: each electron behaves like a tiny bullet, leaving only a single spot.
waveparticle_duality.description: Wave-particle duality is a fundamental property of matter and radiation, according to which all objects exhibit both wave and particle characteristics depending on the experimental conditions. Wave behavior is described by the de Broglie wavelength λ = h/p, particle behavior by energy E = hν and momentum p = h/λ. Bohr's complementarity principle states that these two descriptions are mutually exclusive in a single experiment but equally necessary for a complete picture.
waveparticle_duality.history: Louis de Broglie put forward a revolutionary hypothesis in 1924 about the wave properties of matter, assigning a wavelength λ = h/p to any moving particle. Erwin Schrödinger in 1926 built on this by creating wave mechanics, describing particles as waves distributed in space. The experiments of Davisson and Germer (1927) and independently G.P. Thomson with electrons confirmed the prediction, demonstrating electron diffraction by crystals.
waveparticle_duality.how_it_works: The duality is quantitatively expressed through Heisenberg's uncertainty principle: Δx·Δp ≥ ħ/2. The more precisely the particle characteristic (momentum) is measured, the more blurred the wave characteristic (position) becomes. The wave function ψ(x) contains complete information about the system, and its squared modulus gives the probability of finding the particle at a point. The Schrödinger equation governs the evolution of the wave pattern, and measurement collapses it into a localized particle.
key_problems: Why do quantum objects exhibit duality, but macroscopic ones do not? (the decoherence problem)
Are there hidden variables that predetermine particle or wave behavior?
What is the physical nature of the wave function?
waveparticle_duality.fun_fact: The word "corpuscle" comes from the Latin corpusculum—"little body". Newton used it to refer to particles of light when he argued against Huygens' wave theory. The dispute lasted three centuries and ended with the recognition that both were right.

formulas.description: de Broglie formula
formulas.meaning: λ is the de Broglie wavelength (m); h is Planck's constant, h ≈ 6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s; p is the momentum of the particle (kg·m/s). In the non-relativistic case, p = mv (m is rest mass, v is velocity). For particles moving at speeds close to the speed of light, p = γmv, where γ = 1/√(1 − v²/c²) is the Lorentz factor, c is the speed of light, c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s.

time_dilation.name: time dilation
time_dilation.description_popular: An hourglass on a spaceship shows that the sand falls more slowly than on Earth. This is not an optical illusion—time really stretches when you move very fast or are near a massive body like a black hole.
time_dilation.fun_fact_popular: GPS satellites fly at about 14,000 km/h, so their clocks fall behind by 7 microseconds every day. If engineers didn't correct for time dilation, your navigator would be off by several kilometers after just a couple of minutes.
time_dilation.description_simple: Time dilation is an effect whereby clocks that are moving or in a strong gravitational field run slower compared to stationary ones. Imagine that speeding on the highway compresses not only distance but also the very flow of minutes: the more fiercely the engine roars, the slower the hands on the dashboard creep for an outside observer.
time_dilation.history_simple: Hendrik Lorentz discovered the mathematical recipe for time stretching back in the late 19th century while trying to explain the mysteries of light. Later, in 1905, Albert Einstein turned this recipe into the elegant theory of relativity. The effect was first measured in practice in 1971 by sending ultra-precise atomic clocks on round-the-world flights.
time_dilation.how_it_works_simple: A space traveler accelerates to 99% of the speed of light and heads for the stars. For him, only a few years pass, but on Earth decades go by. The closer the speed to light's, the bigger the gap. And if you hover near the event horizon of a black hole, where gravity is immense, one hour could stretch into eternity for an outside observer.
time_dilation.description: Time dilation is a relativistic effect manifesting as an increase in the measured time interval between two events for an observer moving relative to the events, compared to the proper time. There are two types: kinematic time dilation (special relativity) and gravitational time dilation (general relativity). The magnitude of the effect depends on the relative velocity or gravitational potential and is described by Lorentz transformations and the Schwarzschild metric, respectively.
time_dilation.history: Hendrik Lorentz in the 1890s–1904 derived transformations describing length contraction and time dilation for moving systems, trying to reconcile electrodynamics with the results of the Michelson–Morley experiment. In 1905, Albert Einstein gave these transformations physical meaning in his special theory of relativity. Karl Schwarzschild in 1916 found an exact solution to the field equations for a spherical mass, which directly leads to the conclusion of gravitational time dilation. Observationally, the effect is confirmed by the increased lifetime of fast muons in the atmosphere and by direct experiments with transported atomic clocks.
time_dilation.how_it_works: In special relativity, the time interval Δt measured by a stationary observer is related to the proper interval Δτ of a moving object as Δt = γ Δτ, where γ = 1/√(1 − v²/c²) is the Lorentz factor. As v → c, γ grows without bound, and time for the moving object "freezes." In general relativity, gravitational time dilation is described by the expression Δτ = Δt √(1 − r_s/r), where r_s = 2GM/c² is the Schwarzschild radius; clocks located closer to a massive body run slower compared to distant ones.
key_problems: Compatibility of time dilation with quantum mechanics (the problem of time in quantum gravity)
The twin paradox in curved spacetime
Experimental verification of the effect at submicron scales
time_dilation.fun_fact: In the movie "Interstellar," the characters return from a planet near a black hole and find that their colleague has aged 23 years. This is not fantasy—gravitational time dilation works exactly like that, and astrophysicists confirm the filmmakers' calculations.

formulas.description: Lorentz factor (special relativity)
formulas.meaning: γ is the Lorentz factor (dimensionless, γ ≥ 1); v is the velocity of the moving object (m/s); c is the speed of light in vacuum, c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s. Time relation: Δt = γ Δτ, where Δτ is the proper time interval measured by a clock moving with the object.

formulas.description: Gravitational time dilation (weak-field approximation, Schwarzschild metric)
formulas.meaning: t₀ is the proper time measured at distance r from the center of a spherical mass M; t_f is the time measured by a distant stationary observer in flat spacetime; G is the gravitational constant, G ≈ 6.674×10⁻¹¹ m³/(kg·s²); M is the mass of the gravitating object (kg); r is the radial coordinate (m); c is the speed of light, c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s. The expression breaks down for r ≤ r_s = 2GM/c² (event horizon).

neutron_star.name: neutron star
neutron_star.description_popular: A neutron star is the remnant of a massive star after an explosion. Its matter is compressed so tightly that a teaspoonful would weigh as much as a mountain. Atoms are crushed, and the star consists almost entirely of neutrons.
neutron_star.fun_fact_popular: Neutron stars spin insanely fast—some make hundreds of revolutions per second. Like a spinning top that never stops.
neutron_star.description_simple: A neutron star is the final stage of evolution for stars 10–25 times more massive than the Sun. After a supernova explosion, the core collapses into a ball about 20 kilometers in diameter. That density is comparable to squeezing all of humanity into a sugar cube.
neutron_star.history_simple: In 1934, Walter Baade and Fritz Zwicky predicted the existence of neutron stars. The first was discovered in 1967 by Jocelyn Bell Burnell as a pulsar—a source of regular radio pulses.
neutron_star.how_it_works_simple: A neutron star remains visible thanks to residual heat and a powerful magnetic field. When it rotates rapidly, beams of radiation from its magnetic poles sweep past Earth like a lighthouse beam—that's how we detect pulsars.
neutron_star.description: A neutron star is a compact object representing a degenerate state of matter where the pressure of degenerate neutron gas counteracts gravitational collapse. It forms from the cores of stars with masses of 8–25 M☉ as a result of gravitational collapse during a Type II or Ib/c supernova explosion. Typical mass is 1.4–2.2 M☉, radius 10–13 km. It consists of an outer crust of iron, a neutron-rich inner crust, and outer and inner cores where exotic states may exist: hyperons, quark-gluon plasma. Characterized by extreme magnetic fields (10^8–10^15 G) and rapid rotation (periods from milliseconds to seconds), which produces pulsar emission.
neutron_star.history: The neutron star hypothesis was proposed by Walter Baade and Fritz Zwicky in 1934, who suggested that supernovae create superdense bodies of neutrons. In 1967, Jocelyn Bell Burnell, a graduate student of Anthony Hewish, discovered the first pulsar (CP 1919) by detecting strictly periodic radio pulses with a period of 1.337 s, confirming the existence of neutron stars. In 1974, Russell Hulse and Joseph Taylor discovered the binary pulsar PSR B1913+16, whose study proved the existence of gravitational waves. Fritz Zwicky also played a key role in early theory, and although Jocelyn Bell Burnell co-authored the landmark discovery, her supervisor received the Nobel Prize.
neutron_star.how_it_works: The structure of a neutron star is sustained by the equation of state of supranuclear density. The outer layers are a crystalline lattice of iron nuclei; below is the 'nuclear pasta' phase (complex nuclear shapes: gnocchi, spaghetti, lasagna, Swiss cheese). The core is dominated by neutron superfluid. Gravitational equilibrium is described by the Einstein equations of general relativity, as the compactness (GM/Rc²) approaches 0.2–0.3. The magnetic field arises from conservation of magnetic flux of the progenitor star. Acceleration of relativistic particles in the magnetosphere, especially in the light cylinder, explains the coherent emission of pulsars. Planck's radiation law describes the thermal spectrum, and the Doppler effect shapes the pulses as the star rotates. The mass is limited by the Tolman-Oppenheimer-Volkoff limit (about 2.2 M☉), beyond which collapse into a black hole occurs, related to the Pauli exclusion principle for neutrons.
key_problems: The equation of state at densities above nuclear is unknown: quark matter, hyperons?
The nature of magnetars with record-breaking magnetic fields (B > 10^15 G), where field energy exceeds rotational energy.
The mechanism of millisecond pulsars: spin-up by accretion or transformation of accreting X-ray pulsars?
Connection of neutron star mergers to gamma-ray bursts and kilonovae, synthesis of heavy elements (r-process).
neutron_star.fun_fact: On the surface of a neutron star, gravity is so strong that 'mountains' just a few millimeters high can cause tremors in the form of starquakes, detected as glitches in the rotation.

formulas.description: Average density of a neutron star
formulas.meaning: \rho — average density, M — mass of the neutron star, R — its radius; the formula is valid for a spherically symmetric body.

formulas.description: Minimum rotation period (centrifugal breakup limit)
formulas.meaning: P_{\min} — minimum rotation period, R — radius of the star, M — mass of the star, G — gravitational constant (G ≈ 6.67430×10^{-11} m³ kg⁻¹ s⁻²); for a shorter period, centrifugal force at the equator exceeds gravity and the star disintegrates.

galaxy.name: galaxy
galaxy.description_popular: A galaxy is a vast collection of stars, gas, and dust held together by gravity. The Milky Way is our galaxy, with about 200 billion stars. All the stars in a galaxy are like grains of sand on a huge beach.
galaxy.fun_fact_popular: The largest galaxies are hundreds of times more massive than the Milky Way. And there are dwarf galaxies with fewer stars than a small globular cluster.
galaxy.description_simple: A galaxy is a giant gravitationally bound system of stars, interstellar gas, dust, and dark matter. Our Milky Way is a spiral galaxy about 100,000 light-years in diameter. The central region harbors a supermassive black hole.
galaxy.history_simple: Before the 1920s, astronomers debated whether 'nebulae' were separate galaxies. In 1923, Edwin Hubble, observing Cepheids in Andromeda, proved that it was another galaxy. Hubble also created a classification of galaxies by shape.
galaxy.how_it_works_simple: Stars in a galaxy orbit around a common center of mass, following the law of universal gravitation. Spiral arms are density waves where gas compresses and new stars are born, like a traffic jam: stars move through the arms, but in the jam zone their density is higher.
galaxy.description: A galaxy is a large-scale astrophysical system consisting of 10^7–10^12 stars, interstellar matter (gas and dust), cosmic rays, and a dark matter halo that dominates in mass. Gravitational dynamics are described by Einstein's equations on large scales and Newtonian approximation within the galactic disk. Hubble's morphological classification includes elliptical (E0–E7), lenticular (S0), spiral (Sa–Sd), and irregular (Irr) galaxies. Galaxy formation occurs in the hierarchical ΛCDM model through mergers and gas accretion, accompanied by star formation and feedback from active nuclei and supernovae.
galaxy.history: The term 'galaxy' traces back to the ancient Greek 'milky circle'. In the 1750s, Immanuel Kant suggested that faint nebulae were 'island universes'. William Herschel in the late 18th century compiled the first catalog of nebulae. Decisive proof came from Edwin Hubble in 1923–1924 using the 100-inch Mount Wilson telescope, discovering Cepheids in M31 and Andromeda, which allowed distance measurements far exceeding the size of the Milky Way. Hubble classified galaxies in 1926, and later, with his redshift law, proved the expansion of the Universe. Vera Rubin in the 1970s confirmed the existence of dark matter through galactic rotation curves.
galaxy.how_it_works: The stellar and gas components of a galaxy move in a common gravitational potential, including the contribution of visible matter and the dark halo. Rotation curves of spiral galaxies show anomalously high velocities at the periphery, indicating the existence of dark matter (the law of universal gravitation would predict a velocity drop if all mass were visible). Spiral arms are explained by the density wave theory of Lin and Shu (1964): they are quasi-stationary structures where stellar orbits intersect and compress gas, triggering star formation. The formation of large-scale galaxy structure follows from primordial density fluctuations observed in the cosmic microwave background, described by the Friedmann equations in cosmology. Hubble's law links the redshift of galaxies to distance, which underlies the Big Bang model.
key_problems: Nature of dark matter: WIMPs or axions, alternative theories of gravity.
How do supermassive black holes at galaxy centers reach billions of solar masses in a short cosmological time?
Relation between galaxy morphology and environment: why do clusters have predominantly elliptical galaxies, while the field has spirals?
The role of active galactic nuclei and feedback in regulating star formation.
The cusp problem in dark halo density profiles: simulations predict a steep center, observations show a core.
galaxy.fun_fact: If you could travel at the speed of light, crossing the Milky Way would take 100,000 years—longer than human history. And the Andromeda Galaxy is approaching us and will collide with the Milky Way in 4 billion years.

formulas.description: Keplerian circular velocity for a spherical mass
formulas.meaning: v_c — circular velocity at distance r from the center, G — gravitational constant (≈ 6.67430×10^{-11} m³ kg⁻¹ s⁻²), M(r) — mass enclosed within a sphere of radius r.

formulas.description: Hubble's law
formulas.meaning: v — recessional velocity of the galaxy, H_0 — Hubble constant (≈ 70 km s⁻¹ Mpc⁻¹), d — distance to the galaxy; the law is empirical, follows from the expansion of the Universe in general relativity.

comet.name: comet
comet.description_popular: A comet is a small icy body that heats up as it approaches the Sun and releases gas and dust, forming a bright head and tail. A melting snowball leaving a trail of steam.
comet.fun_fact_popular: A comet's tail always points away from the Sun because sunlight and the solar wind push the gas away, like a hair dryer blowing away smoke.
comet.description_simple: A comet is a wanderer of the Solar System, consisting of frozen gases, water ice, and rocky dust. As it nears the Sun, the icy nucleus evaporates (sublimates), creating a coma—a cloud of gas around the nucleus—and a tail millions of kilometers long. The nucleus is usually the size of a small city.
comet.history_simple: In ancient times, comets were considered harbingers of disaster. Isaac Newton, using the law of universal gravitation, proved that comets move along orbits. In 1705, Edmond Halley calculated that the bright comets of 1531, 1607, and 1682 were the same one and predicted its return in 1758, which came true.
comet.how_it_works_simple: As a comet approaches the Sun, ice on its surface turns into gas, erupting in jets. Solar radiation and solar wind push away dust and ionized gas, forming two tails: a dust tail, curved along the orbit, and an ion tail, straight and pointing directly away from the Sun.
comet.description: A comet is a small Solar System body consisting of a nucleus (a conglomerate of ices H₂O, CO, CO₂, CH₃OH and refractory silicate particles) with a diameter from hundreds of meters to tens of kilometers. As it approaches the Sun (within about 2–3 AU), ice sublimation begins, forming a coma (a gas and dust atmosphere, radius up to 10⁵ km) and tails: the ion (plasma) tail is directed along the solar magnetic field lines, while the dust tail is pushed by light pressure and has a curved shape due to orbital motion. Comet sources are the Oort cloud and the Kuiper belt. Dynamics are described by the law of universal gravitation, taking into account non-gravitational forces (reactive accelerations from sublimation).
comet.history: Until the 17th century, comets were considered atmospheric phenomena. Tycho Brahe in 1577, by measuring parallax, showed their cosmic nature. Johannes Kepler suggested straight-line trajectories, but Isaac Newton in 'Principia' (1687) applied the law of universal gravitation to calculate parabolic orbits of comets. Edmond Halley, processing data on 24 comets, identified the comet of 1682 with previously observed ones and predicted its return in 1758. In the 1950s, Jan Oort proposed the hypothesis of a giant spherical cloud on the periphery. Fred Hoyle studied organic molecules in comets and their possible connection to the origin of life.
comet.how_it_works: A comet's orbit is determined by initial conditions and perturbations from planets. Long-period comets come from the Oort cloud with nearly parabolic orbits. During sublimation, reactive forces alter the orbit (non-gravitational acceleration). The brightness of the coma is modeled by the radiation transfer equation, accounting for dust scattering and gas fluorescence (e.g., CN, C₂ radicals). Tails are classified by type: type I — plasma, formed by ion interaction with the solar wind magnetic field; type II — dust, where solar radiation pressure F_{\text{rad}} = (S/c) Q_{\text{pr}} A, where S is the radiation flux density, c is the speed of light, Q_{\text{pr}} is the radiation pressure efficiency, A is the cross-sectional area of a dust grain. The Doppler effect contributes to emission when scattering on fast-moving particles. Wien's displacement law is used to estimate dust temperature.
key_problems: Origin of short-period comets: capture from the Oort cloud or formation in the Kuiper belt?
Nature of non-gravitational accelerations: detailed model of sublimation and surface activity.
Chemical composition of nuclei: the ratio of primordial ices and organics as a key to the early Solar System.
Why do some comets have no tail, being dead or covered with a crust?
comet.fun_fact: The nucleus of comet 67P/Churyumov-Gerasimenko, studied by the Rosetta probe, has the shape of a rubber duck and 'sings': the probe detected low-frequency plasma oscillations that were translated into audible sound.

formulas.description: Temperature of a dust particle as a function of distance from the Sun (radiative equilibrium)
formulas.meaning: T — temperature of the dust grain, T_0 — reference temperature at distance r_0 (e.g., at r_0 = 1 AU, T_0 ≈ 280 K for a rapidly rotating isothermal sphere), r — heliocentric distance; derived from the Stefan–Boltzmann law.

formulas.description: Acceleration from radiation pressure on a dust grain
formulas.meaning: a — acceleration of the dust grain, S — solar radiation flux density at a given distance (S = L/(4π r²), L — solar luminosity ≈ 3.828×10²⁶ W), c — speed of light in vacuum (≈ 2.9979×10⁸ m/s), Q_{\text{pr}} — radiation pressure efficiency factor (≲ 2), A — cross-sectional area of the particle, m — its mass.

sun.name: Sun
sun.description_popular: The Sun is the star that gives us light and heat. It is like an eternally boiling cauldron where hydrogen turns into helium, and every second it releases as much energy as humanity consumes in a million years.
sun.fun_fact_popular: The Sun is so huge that 1.3 million Earth-sized planets could fit inside it. And if we weighed the whole Sun, it would be 333,000 times heavier than our planet.
sun.description_simple: The Sun works like a natural thermonuclear reactor. Under tremendous gravity, hydrogen is compressed so much that its nuclei fuse, forming helium and releasing enormous energy. It's similar to how pressed hay can self-ignite, but billions of times more powerful.
sun.history_simple: People have known about the Sun since antiquity, but its nature was understood only in the 20th century. In 1925, astronomer Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin showed that the Sun is composed mainly of hydrogen and helium. Later, physicists like Hans Bethe explained that its energy comes from thermonuclear fusion reactions.
sun.how_it_works_simple: The Sun shines thanks to thermonuclear fusion in its core. It's like billions of hydrogen bombs exploding every second, but gravity holds everything together like an invisible shell. The energy takes hundreds of thousands of years to reach the surface, and we see it as sunlight.
sun.description: The Sun is a G2V yellow dwarf, the central body of the Solar System, providing Earth with energy. Its mass is 1.989×10³⁰ kg, radius about 696,000 km, effective surface temperature 5778 K. In the Sun's interior, in the radiative zone and convective zone, complex energy transfer processes occur. Energy release is provided by the proton-proton chain and, to a lesser extent, the CNO cycle, leading to the fusion of helium from hydrogen. The Sun also has a complex magnetic field that gives rise to solar activity, including sunspots, flares, and coronal mass ejections.
sun.history: Since ancient times, the Sun was deified, but scientific study began with Galileo's telescopic observations and the discovery of sunspots. In 1925, Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin, analyzing the Sun's spectrum, first established that it is predominantly composed of hydrogen, contradicting the prevailing views. In 1938, Hans Bethe and Carl von Weizsäcker independently described the chains of thermonuclear reactions that power the star. Modern helioseismology, developed since the 1960s, has allowed us to look inside the Sun, confirming theoretical models.
sun.how_it_works: The Sun's energy is generated in the core through thermonuclear fusion, mainly via the pp-chain, where four protons turn into a helium nucleus, releasing two positrons and two neutrinos. According to Planck's empirical radiation law, the Sun's spectrum is close to blackbody radiation with a peak in the visible range (Wien's displacement law). The balance between gravitational contraction and radiation pressure is maintained by the condition of hydrostatic equilibrium. Radiative transfer and convection equations describe energy transport from the core to the surface. Neutrinos produced in the chains freely leave the Sun, allowing their detection on Earth and testing theoretical models (solar neutrino problem). The Sun's outer layers—photosphere, chromosphere, and corona—are heated to millions of degrees by mechanisms of magnetic reconnection and Alfvén waves.
key_problems: The problem of solar corona heating (why the corona is hundreds of times hotter than the surface).
The exact mechanism of the solar dynamo and prediction of solar cycles.
Origin and acceleration of the solar wind.
sun.fun_fact: The Sun loses about 4 million tons of mass every second through energy radiation, but its reserves will last another 5 billion years. It also emits the solar wind—a stream of charged particles that causes auroras on Earth.

formulas.description: relation between the luminosity of a blackbody, its radius, and temperature (Stefan–Boltzmann law)
formulas.meaning: L — luminosity of the star, R — its radius, T — effective temperature of the photosphere, σ — Stefan-Boltzmann constant, σ ≈ 5.67×10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴

formulas.description: mass–energy equivalence underlying the Sun's energy release
formulas.meaning: E — energy released when mass m is converted into energy, c — speed of light in vacuum, c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s

hubble.name: Hubble Space Telescope
hubble.description_popular: The Hubble Space Telescope is a large camera orbiting Earth. It looks at stars and galaxies without the interference of our atmosphere, so the images are incredibly clear, as if we are in space ourselves.
hubble.fun_fact_popular: Hubble doesn't take pictures in color like our cameras; it captures light at specific wavelengths in black and white. Then scientists colorize the images so we can see space in all its glory.
hubble.description_simple: The Hubble Telescope is an orbital observatory launched in 1990. It works like a giant digital eye, collecting light from stars and galaxies and converting it into detailed images. Free from atmospheric distortion, Hubble sees 10 times more clearly than ground-based telescopes of that era.
hubble.history_simple: The telescope is named after Edwin Hubble, the astronomer who proved the expansion of the Universe. The project began in the 1970s, and launch took place on April 24, 1990, aboard the Space Shuttle Discovery. The first images were blurry due to a mirror defect, but in 1993 astronauts installed corrective optics, and the telescope began working at full capacity.
hubble.how_it_works_simple: Hubble uses a system of mirrors to focus light and digital sensors similar to camera matrices. It orbits Earth at about 27,000 km/h, completing a full orbit every 97 minutes. Solar panels power its instruments, and gyroscopes help it precisely point at targets, like a ship holding its course at sea.
hubble.description: The Hubble Space Telescope (HST) is an automated observatory in low Earth orbit, a joint project of NASA and ESA. It is equipped with a 2.4 m primary mirror and a suite of scientific instruments: spectrographs, wide-field cameras operating in ultraviolet, visible, and infrared ranges. The absence of atmospheric interference provides an angular resolution of about 0.05 arcseconds. The use of CCD matrices allows detection of objects down to 31st magnitude. High-resolution spectrographs provide key data on the chemical composition, temperature, and motion of celestial bodies through spectral line analysis.
hubble.history: The concept of a space telescope had been discussed since the 1940s, but full design began in the 1970s. Launch took place on April 24, 1990; spherical aberration of the primary mirror was soon discovered and corrected by servicing mission STS-61 in 1993. A total of five servicing missions were conducted (the last in 2009), upgrading instruments. The telescope is named after Edwin Hubble, whose work on redshift and Hubble's law laid the foundations of modern cosmology.
hubble.how_it_works: The Ritchey–Chrétien telescope collects light with a primary hyperbolic mirror and directs it to a secondary mirror, then to instruments. Stabilization is achieved with gyroscopes and magnetometers, precise pointing with guide star sensors. Scientific instruments include the Wide Field Camera 3, Cosmic Origins Spectrograph, and others. Data are transmitted via radio link through TDRS satellites. Hubble's law (v = H₀d) was empirically refined thanks to Hubble's observations of distant supernovae, leading to the discovery of accelerated expansion of the Universe. Gravitational lensing captured by the telescope has allowed mapping of dark matter in galaxy clusters. Transit spectroscopy of exoplanet atmospheres has revealed lines of water, methane, and other molecules.
key_problems: The Hubble tension—discrepancy between measurements of the Hubble constant by different methods.
Detection of the first galaxies, which remained invisible to HST.
Direct observation of dark matter through gravitational lensing is limited by resolution.
hubble.fun_fact: Hubble captured the famous Hubble Deep Field—a patch of sky the size of a grain of sand held at arm's length, revealing over 3000 galaxies. It also helped refine the age of the Universe—about 13.8 billion years.

formulas.description: Hubble's law—the relationship between galaxy recession velocity and distance, confirming the expansion of the Universe
formulas.meaning: v — recessional velocity of the galaxy, H₀ — Hubble constant, H₀ ≈ 70 (km/s)/Mpc, d — distance to the galaxy

polarization.name: polarization
polarization.description_popular: Light is a wave, and it can be imagined as a rope being shaken. Usually the rope oscillates in all directions, but if passed through a narrow fence, only oscillations along the slits remain. Polarized light is like that: it 'vibrates' strictly in one plane. Polarizing glasses work like such a fence: they block glare from water or asphalt—those horizontal oscillations that interfere with vision.
polarization.fun_fact_popular: Bees and many insects see the polarization of the sky even in cloudy weather and use it for navigation. And mantis shrimp distinguish far more polarization shades than humans, helping them hunt and communicate.
polarization.description_simple: Light is an electromagnetic wave with oscillating electric and magnetic fields. Polarization describes the direction of oscillation of the electric field. In sunlight, the directions are mixed randomly, but after reflection from smooth surfaces or passing through special materials, the oscillations align in one plane—the light becomes polarized.
polarization.history_simple: Polarization was discovered by the French engineer Étienne-Louis Malus in 1808, while looking through a calcite crystal at light reflected from a window. Later, James Clerk Maxwell in 1865 created the unified theory of the electromagnetic field, showing that light is waves with vector fields, and laid the precise foundation for understanding polarization.
polarization.how_it_works_simple: Light reflected from a horizontal surface is strongly horizontally polarized. Polarizing glasses contain a filter that only passes vertical oscillations. It's like microscopic blinds that cut out stray light—glare disappears, while the rest of the scene remains bright.
polarization.description: Polarization is a fundamental property of transverse waves, primarily electromagnetic, where the electric field strength vector oscillates in a specific direction perpendicular to the propagation direction. In the quantum picture, polarization corresponds to the spin state of a photon: circular polarization rightfully corresponds to a projection of +ħ or -ħ onto the momentum direction. The space of polarization states forms a two-dimensional Hilbert space, with the Poincaré sphere used for visualization. Polarization is applied in optics, telecommunications, and quantum informatics as a carrier of a qubit.
polarization.history: Étienne-Louis Malus discovered polarization by reflection in 1808, while observing light through a calcite crystal from the window of his home in Paris. In the 1810s, Augustin Fresnel and François Arago supplemented the wave theory of light with transverse oscillations and investigated the interference of polarized rays. James Clerk Maxwell in 1865 derived equations that explained polarization as the directionality of the electromagnetic field vectors. Quantum electrodynamics later linked polarization to photon spin, and Alain Aspect in 1982 conducted the famous experiment with polarization-entangled photons, violating Bell's inequalities.
polarization.how_it_works: According to Maxwell's equations, an electromagnetic wave consists of mutually perpendicular electric and magnetic fields oscillating in phase. The type of polarization is determined by the trajectory of the end of the E vector: if it oscillates along a line—linear polarization; if it rotates with constant amplitude—circular; the general case is elliptical. The intensity of light transmitted through a polarizer is described by Malus's law: I = I₀ cos²θ. Wave plates introduce a phase shift between orthogonal components, changing the polarization type. In quantum optics, a single photon exists in a superposition of two basis states, and measurement is projective and described by density matrices.
key_problems: Creation of deterministic single-photon sources with controlled polarization for quantum repeaters
Overcoming depolarization in scattering media without active compensation
Integration of polarization qubits into scalable quantum networks with error correction
polarization.fun_fact: The screen of a liquid crystal display (LCD) manipulates polarization. Each pixel contains a liquid crystal that, under voltage, rotates the polarization of backlight. The system of two polarizers at the input and output turns this rotation into a change in brightness.

formulas.description: Malus's law: intensity of light after passing an ideal polarizer
formulas.meaning: I is the output intensity, I₀ is the initial intensity, θ is the angle between the polarization plane of the incident light and the transmission axis of the polarizer

crystal_lattice.name: crystal lattice
crystal_lattice.description_popular: In a crystal, all particles—atoms, molecules, or ions—are arranged like rows of oranges on a fruit stand. Each orange sits exactly in its place, and this repeats layer after layer. Thanks to this strict order, crystals form smooth faces and sharp angles, like salt cubes or hexagonal snowflakes.
crystal_lattice.fun_fact_popular: Snowflakes grow from tiny ice crystals, and their hexagonal symmetry is embedded in how water molecules connect in the lattice. Although all snowflakes share common features, each is unique due to different growth conditions.
crystal_lattice.description_simple: A crystal lattice is a three-dimensional 'packing' of atoms, ions, or molecules that repeats in all directions. Imagine honeycombs: they consist of identical hexagonal cells, and if bees built them infinitely small and in three layers, you'd get a lattice similar to a magnesium crystal.
crystal_lattice.history_simple: The idea that crystals have a regular internal structure was already suggested by Robert Hooke in the 17th century while examining them under a microscope. The decisive moment came in 1912 when Max von Laue predicted and obtained X-ray diffraction from crystals, and William and Lawrence Bragg derived a simple equation for deciphering the lattice, for which they received the Nobel Prize in 1915.
crystal_lattice.how_it_works_simple: When water evaporates from a salty solution, sodium and chloride ions stop floating separately and line up in a strict checkerboard order, but in volume. Each sodium ion is surrounded by six chloride ions, and vice versa—this way the electric forces balance each other, and we get the familiar salt cube.
crystal_lattice.description: A crystal lattice is an infinite periodic structure defined by elementary translation vectors. Formally, it is described as a set of points (nodes) with an attached basis—a group of atoms repeated under all translations of the form R = n₁a + n₂b + n₃c, where a, b, c are basis vectors. The symmetry of the lattice is classified by the 14 Bravais lattices and 230 space groups. Periodicity determines diffraction patterns, phonon spectrum, and electronic band structure.
crystal_lattice.history: Robert Hooke in Micrographia (1665) first suggested that the shape of crystals reflects an ordered arrangement of particles. In the early 19th century, René Just Haüy formulated the law of rational indices, and Auguste Bravais in 1848 derived 14 lattice types. The decisive breakthrough was made by Max von Laue in 1912, proposing X-ray diffraction from crystals. William Henry Bragg and his son William Lawrence Bragg developed a method for analyzing diffraction peaks (Bragg's law) and in 1913 determined the structure of NaCl. This opened the era of structural crystallography.
crystal_lattice.how_it_works: The equilibrium configuration of atoms in a lattice minimizes free energy. In ionic crystals, electrostatic attraction (Coulomb's law) and short-range repulsion upon overlapping electron shells dominate. For covalent crystals (silicon, diamond), directional bonds dictate complex structures with tetrahedral coordination. The periodic potential of the lattice leads to the formation of a band structure (Bloch theorem), determining the properties of semiconductors. Diffraction of X-rays, neutrons, or electrons is described by Bragg's law nλ = 2d sin θ, where d is the interplanar spacing.
key_problems: Prediction of crystal structure from chemical composition (CSP—Crystal Structure Prediction problem) without empirical data
Nature of high-temperature superconductivity in cuprates and its connection to lattice dynamics
Complete control of defects in low-dimensional materials for quantum technologies
Creation of programmable three-dimensional crystals with specified geometry for nanoelectronics and metamaterials
crystal_lattice.fun_fact: The repeating block of a lattice is called the unit cell. There are approximately 10¹⁸ such cells in a grain of salt. If a cell were enlarged to the size of a cherry pit, the grain would become a cube with a side over a hundred meters.

formulas.description: Bragg's law: condition for constructive interference during diffraction from a crystal lattice
formulas.meaning: n is an integer (diffraction order), λ is the radiation wavelength, d is the spacing between atomic planes, θ is the glancing angle (between the incident beam and the plane)

expansion_of_the_universe.name: expansion of the universe
expansion_of_the_universe.description_popular: The universe expands like raisin dough in the oven: each raisin galaxy moves away from the others, and the farther away, the faster. The dough itself is space, which is getting bigger.
expansion_of_the_universe.fun_fact_popular: Light from the most distant galaxies has traveled to us for billions of years, and during that time, space has stretched so much that the light has become invisible to the eye—turning into microwave radiation.
expansion_of_the_universe.description_simple: The expansion of the Universe is an observed phenomenon: galaxies move away from each other, and light from them stretches, becoming redder. Just as an inflating balloon with drawn dots moves them apart, space itself grows in size.
expansion_of_the_universe.history_simple: In 1927, Belgian priest and astrophysicist Georges Lemaître first suggested expansion based on Einstein's equations. In 1929, Edwin Hubble confirmed it, discovering the proportionality between the distance to galaxies and their recessional velocity (redshift).
expansion_of_the_universe.how_it_works_simple: If you throw a stone into a pond, ripples spread out at the same speed. But the expansion of the Universe is different: space between galaxies grows over time, and the speed increases with distance according to a law similar to the rule: twice as far—twice as fast receding.
expansion_of_the_universe.description: The expansion of the Universe is a fundamental property of the Friedmann–Lemaître–Robertson–Walker cosmological model, based on general relativity. Observationally, it manifests as a redshift of spectral lines from distant objects, obeying the Hubble–Lemaître law: v = H₀ D, where H₀ is the Hubble constant. Expansion means an increase in the scale factor a(t) over time, with physical distance between comoving points proportional to a(t). At the end of the 20th century, accelerated expansion was discovered, explained by dark energy or the cosmological constant Λ. The expansion has no center and occurs uniformly in all directions, being a consequence of the Big Bang about 13.8 billion years ago.
expansion_of_the_universe.history: Theoretical foundations emerged in 1917 when Einstein added the cosmological constant for a static universe. In 1922–1924, Alexander Friedmann found non-stationary solutions to Einstein's equations, predicting expansion. In 1927, Georges Lemaître independently obtained the same solutions and compared them with Slipher's redshift data, proposing the concept of a 'primeval atom.' Edwin Hubble in 1929, based on observations by Vesto Slipher and Milton Humason, confirmed the linear relationship between redshift and distance. In 1964, Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson accidentally discovered the cosmic microwave background, which became decisive evidence for a hot beginning and expansion.
expansion_of_the_universe.how_it_works: The expansion is described by the FLRW metric: ds^2 = -c^2 dt^2 + a(t)^2 [dr^2/(1 - k r^2) + r^2 (dθ^2 + sin^2θ dφ^2)], where a(t) is the scale factor, k is the curvature parameter (-1, 0, +1). The evolution of a(t) is given by the Friedmann equations: (ȧ/a)^2 = (8πGρ + Λc^2)/3 - kc^2/a^2, and ä/a = -(4πG/3)(ρ + 3p/c^2) + Λc^2/3. In the current era, dark energy (Λ>0) dominates, leading to accelerated expansion. Redshift z is defined as 1+z = a(t_obs)/a(t_em). The Hubble law in the first approximation: v = H₀ D, where the Hubble constant H₀ ≈ 70 (km/s)/Mpc.
key_problems: The nature of dark energy causing accelerated expansion
Hubble tension: discrepancy in measurements of the Hubble constant by different methods
Fundamental causes of the initial expansion (inflation or quantum effects)
Composition and properties of dark matter affecting the expansion rate
Ultimate fate of the Universe: heat death, Big Rip, or collapse
expansion_of_the_universe.fun_fact: The most distant objects in the Universe are receding from us faster than the speed of light—this does not violate relativity, because it is space itself that moves, not objects through it.

formulas.description: Hubble-Lemaître law
formulas.meaning: v is the galaxy's recession velocity, H_0 is the Hubble constant (H_0 ≈ 70 (km/s)/Mpc), D is the proper distance

formulas.description: Friedmann equation determining the dynamics of the scale factor
formulas.meaning: a is the scale factor, ȧ is the time derivative, G is the gravitational constant (G ≈ 6.674×10^{-11} N·m²/kg²), ρ is the energy density, k is the curvature parameter, c is the speed of light (c ≈ 3×10^8 m/s), Λ is the cosmological constant

oxygen.name: oxygen
oxygen.description_popular: Oxygen is an invisible gas that fills our lungs with every breath. It is like a magical elixir of life: without it, fire goes out and fish in water stop breathing. Oxygen is born in the green leaves of plants under the sun, as a gift to us from generous nature.
oxygen.fun_fact_popular: Oxygen is the most abundant element on Earth: it makes up almost half the mass of the Earth's crust and two-thirds of your body's mass. And it is oxygen that colors blood red by combining with iron.
oxygen.description_simple: Oxygen is a chemical element without which combustion and respiration are inconceivable. Just as a mill uses the flow of water to grind grain, the cells of our body use oxygen to extract energy from food. This gas makes up 21% of the atmosphere, and its constant circulation in nature connects the world of animals and plants.
oxygen.history_simple: The honor of discovering oxygen is shared by Swedish apothecary Carl Scheele (1771) and English clergyman Joseph Priestley (1774). However, the real revolution was made by Frenchman Antoine Lavoisier: he gave the new gas the name 'oxygène' (acid-former) and proved that it is oxygen that supports combustion and respiration.
oxygen.how_it_works_simple: Oxygen reacts with nutrients, like wood in a campfire: slowly, without flame, but with energy release. In the lungs, it enters the blood and, like on a conveyor belt, is distributed by red blood cells to all organs, where it helps 'burn' glucose, giving us heat and strength.
oxygen.description: Oxygen (O) is a chemical element with atomic number 8, the most abundant on Earth. In free form it exists as a diatomic gas O₂, making up 21% of the atmosphere. It is a strong oxidizing agent, forming oxides with almost all elements. Oxygen is a component of water, all organic compounds, and many minerals. Its high electronegativity (3.44 on the Pauling scale) makes it a key agent in biochemical energy synthesis.
oxygen.history: Oxygen was first isolated by Scheele (1771) and Priestley (1774), but the decisive role was played by Lavoisier (1777), who refuted the phlogiston theory and showed that air consists of two gases: 'vital' (oxygen) and 'asphyxiating' (nitrogen). In 1781, Cavendish synthesized water from hydrogen and oxygen. In the 20th century, the Nobel Prize was awarded to Otto Warburg (1931) for his work on the respiratory enzyme using oxygen.
oxygen.how_it_works: In mitochondria, oxygen serves as the final electron acceptor in the electron transport chain, being reduced to water. This process, coupled with ATP synthesis, obeys the second law of thermodynamics: the energy of chemical bonds in food is converted into the energy of high-energy phosphate bonds in ATP with heat dissipation. Without oxygen, the cell switches to anaerobic glycolysis, which is 18 times less efficient.
key_problems: Creating stable and cheap catalysts for water electrolysis with oxygen release—key to hydrogen energy
Preventing oxidative stress during cryopreservation of organs for transplantation
Search for biomarkers (including oxygen) in exoplanet atmospheres as signs of life
oxygen.fun_fact: Pure oxygen is toxic at depth: divers breathe helium mixtures to avoid convulsions. Also, liquid oxygen is a pale blue liquid that boils at -183°C and makes even iron burn.

formulas.description: Overall equation of aerobic respiration
formulas.meaning: C6H12O6 is glucose, O2 is molecular oxygen, CO2 is carbon dioxide, H2O is water

iron.name: iron
iron.description_popular: Iron is the strongest metal from which airplanes and towers are built. But it is also inside us: tiny particles of iron in the blood grab oxygen and carry it to every cell, like tiny trucks delivering fuel throughout the body.
iron.fun_fact_popular: In ancient times, blacksmiths forged weapons from meteoritic iron that fell from the sky. Such iron does not rust and was more valuable than gold! And without impurities, it is viscous like plasticine—the famous Eiffel Tower is made of almost pure iron mined from local deposits.
iron.description_simple: Iron is the fourth most abundant element in the Earth's crust and the main metal of civilization. It is like the backbone of our technosphere: bridges, rails, skyscrapers depend on it. In living nature, iron is a key player: iron atoms in hemoglobin reversibly bind oxygen, working like magnets that either attract or release their load.
iron.history_simple: The Iron Age began around 1200 BC, but the first iron used was from meteorites. Smelting iron from ore was learned by the Hittites in Asia Minor. In 1747, French physician Pierre Menard de la Grosse proved the presence of iron in blood, and a century later Henry Bessemer invented the converter for mass steel production.
iron.how_it_works_simple: In a blast furnace, resembling a red-hot volcano, iron oxides are reduced by carbon—coal 'takes away' oxygen, leaving pure metal. In our body, iron, changing its charge, 'charges' with oxygen in the lungs and 'discharges' in tissues, like a battery ensuring continuous respiration.
iron.description: Iron (Fe) is a transition metal of group VIII with atomic number 26. It is a ferromagnet with a Curie temperature of 770°C, possessing high saturation magnetization. In the Earth's crust, it occurs as oxides (hematite Fe₂O₃, magnetite Fe₃O₄). In biochemistry, iron is part of heme—the prosthetic group of hemoglobin, myoglobin, cytochromes. Its coordination number of 6 allows reversible binding of O₂, CO₂, and other ligands.
iron.history: Iron smelting began in Anatolia around 1500 BC. Meteoritic iron was used as early as the 4th millennium BC. In 1747, Menard de la Grosse discovered iron in blood ash. In 1932, Hans Fischer deciphered the structure of heme. Iron's magnetism was studied by Gilbert (1600), but only Maxwell (1873) gave a rigorous theory of the electromagnetic field, explaining ferromagnetism as the result of microscopic currents.
iron.how_it_works: Ferromagnetism of iron is due to exchange interaction, aligning the spins of unpaired d-electrons. This creates macroscopic domains that reorient in an external field. According to Maxwell's equations for media with magnetic hysteresis, the hysteresis loop determines the coercive force and remanent magnetization. Upon heating above the Curie point, thermal fluctuations destroy magnetic order (Boltzmann statistics). In biology, oxidation of Fe²⁺ to Fe³⁺ allows cytochromes to transfer electrons in the respiratory chain.
key_problems: Understanding the exact mechanism of iron crystallization in the Earth's inner core to predict the evolution of the magnetic field
Development of rare-earth-free permanent magnets based on iron with specified coercivity
Overcoming the toxicity of free iron when creating drugs for treating anemia
iron.fun_fact: Pure iron is soft: its hardness on the Mohs scale is only 4 out of 10. But adding 0.5% carbon yields steel that cuts glass. And the Earth's core is a solid iron ball the size of the Moon, heated to 5500°C, rotating inside a liquid outer core, creating the planet's magnetic field.

formulas.description: Curie–Weiss law for magnetic susceptibility of a ferromagnet above the Curie point
formulas.meaning: \chi is magnetic susceptibility, C is the Curie constant (characteristic of the substance), T is absolute temperature, T_C is the Curie temperature (for iron ≈ 1043 K)

carbon_dioxide.name: carbon dioxide
carbon_dioxide.description_popular: Carbon dioxide is an invisible cloud that escapes from the lungs when exhaling. Plants eagerly catch it and, with the help of the sun, turn it into greenery and the sweetness of fruits, like cooks preparing a delicious meal from invisible ingredients.
carbon_dioxide.fun_fact_popular: The bubbles in soda are carbon dioxide, which fizzes and tickles the tongue. And dry ice, used to cool ice cream in cinemas, is solid carbon dioxide; it doesn't melt into a puddle but sublimates directly into vapor, enveloping everything in fog.
carbon_dioxide.description_simple: Carbon dioxide (CO₂) is a colorless, odorless gas (in small concentrations) exhaled by all animals. In the atmosphere, it acts like a greenhouse film: it freely transmits solar rays but traps the heat radiated by the Earth. This natural effect makes the planet habitable, but its intensification due to fuel burning threatens overheating.
carbon_dioxide.history_simple: In 1756, Scotsman Joseph Black called CO₂ 'fixed air,' showing that it is released from limestone and absorbed by alkalis. Later, Lavoisier identified it as a compound of carbon and oxygen. In 1859, Irish physicist John Tyndall measured the heat absorption of CO₂, laying the foundations of the greenhouse effect theory.
carbon_dioxide.how_it_works_simple: In a plant leaf, CO₂ enters through microscopic pores into green cells, where sunlight splits water and forces carbon dioxide to turn into sugars. This process—photosynthesis—feeds almost all living things. In the ocean, CO₂ dissolves, but excess acidifies the water, threatening corals and shells, like a weak acid shower eroding calcareous shells.
carbon_dioxide.description: Carbon dioxide (CO₂) is a linear triatomic molecule with C=O double bonds. At standard conditions, density is 1.98 kg/m³, solubility in water is 1.45 g/L (forming weak carbonic acid). It is the main anthropogenic greenhouse gas; its concentration has risen from 278 ppm (pre-industrial level) to 421 ppm in 2023. It absorbs IR radiation in the bands at 4.3 µm and 15 µm, creating radiative forcing.
carbon_dioxide.history: Black (1756) isolated CO₂ from limestone; Lavoisier (1770s) determined its composition. Tyndall (1859) measured heat absorption. Arrhenius (1896) quantitatively predicted global warming from rising CO₂. Keeling (from 1958) began monitoring at Mauna Loa, proving the trend. The carbon in CO₂ is formed in stars through the triple-alpha process, explained by Fred Hoyle and Margaret Burbidge in 1957.
carbon_dioxide.how_it_works: The greenhouse effect of CO₂ is based on its ability to absorb Earth's infrared radiation. The Earth radiates as a blackbody with a maximum near 10 µm (Wien's displacement law). Vibrations of the CO₂ molecule (antisymmetric stretching with a dipole moment) resonate with IR photons, re-emitting some energy back to the surface. Radiative forcing depends logarithmically on concentration: ΔF = 5.35·ln(C/C₀). According to the second law of thermodynamics, heat flow from a cold atmosphere to a warm surface is impossible without an external source, so re-emission merely slows cooling.
key_problems: Quantitative assessment of climate sensitivity (ECS)—the temperature rise from doubling CO₂—remains in the range 2–5°C due to uncertainties in feedbacks (clouds, ocean)
Creating efficient and cheap direct air capture (DAC) systems for CO₂ with subsequent mineralization
Forecasting the regional impacts of ocean acidification on marine ecosystems
carbon_dioxide.fun_fact: Every day, an average tree absorbs about 22 kg of CO₂, and a hectare of forest—up to 2 tons. But volcanoes emit even more CO₂ into the atmosphere: one eruption can release as much as all the world's cars in a year. In the distant past, when carbon dioxide was 10 times higher, giant ferns grew on Earth, becoming coal.

formulas.description: Radiative forcing from carbon dioxide
formulas.meaning: \Delta F is radiative forcing (W/m²), 5.35 is an empirical coefficient, C is the current CO₂ concentration (ppm), C₀ is the pre-industrial concentration (≈ 278 ppm)

group.name: group
group.description_popular: A group in mathematics is like a set of movements you can make with an object, and which can be combined. For example, rotations of a Rubik's cube: if you rotate one face, then another, you get a new movement. And you can always go back by making the reverse move. Importantly, these movements are closed: a combination of two always yields a movement from the same set.
group.fun_fact_popular: Groups help understand why snowflakes always have six arms, and why some tilings can be made without gaps. They hide behind any symmetry in nature.
group.description_simple: A group is a set of elements with an operation of combining them, similar to how we combine rotations of a square so that it coincides with itself. Four rotations (0°, 90°, 180°, 270°) form a group: their combinations don't go beyond these angles, and each rotation can be undone by the reverse one.
group.history_simple: The concept of a group originated in the work of Évariste Galois in the 1830s, when he was seeking a solution to algebraic equations in radicals. Galois showed that the group of permutations of the equation's roots determines its solvability. Later, group theory became the language of symmetries in physics and crystallography.
group.how_it_works_simple: A group operates by rules: closure (the result of an operation remains in the group), associativity (the order of parentheses doesn't matter), existence of a neutral element (doing nothing), and for each element an inverse (returning to the original state). Like in a kaleidoscope: combining reflections, we get all patterns, and any reflection can be reflected back.
group.description: A group in mathematics is a set G with a binary operation · satisfying the axioms: closure (a·b ∈ G), associativity (a·(b·c) = (a·b)·c), existence of a neutral element e (e·a = a·e = a), and an inverse element a⁻¹ (a·a⁻¹ = a⁻¹·a = e). Group theory studies algebraic structures that arise as sets of symmetries of objects. Groups are classified into types: finite, infinite, Abelian, solvable, simple, and serve as the foundation for algebra, geometry, topology, and physics (gauge theories, crystallography, quantum mechanics).
group.history: The roots of group theory go back to the permutation theory of Lagrange and Ruffini, but its birth is associated with Évariste Galois (1830), who introduced the term 'group' to describe symmetries of polynomial roots. Henri Poincaré applied groups to topology, laying the foundations of algebraic topology. In the 20th century, the classification of finite simple groups became a monumental project, completed by 2004. Lie groups linked symmetries with differential geometry, becoming the apparatus of the Standard Model.
group.how_it_works: A group acts on a set, moving its elements while preserving structure. This action gives orbits and stabilizers. Composition laws are expressed through Cayley tables or graphs. Representation theory translates abstract groups into linear algebra, which proves crucial in elementary particle physics. For example, SU(3) describes quark symmetries. According to Noether's theorem, every continuous symmetry (Lie group) corresponds to a conserved quantity.
key_problems: The classification of finite simple groups is only conceptually complete, but the proof is scattered and requires simplification (the 'second generation' project).
The word problem in group theory: to recognize whether two words are equivalent in a given group presentation?
Establishing all possible gauge groups for a unified field theory.
group.fun_fact: The largest simple group ever described—the 'monster'—has an order of about 8×10⁵³ elements. Its existence was predicted by mathematician Bernd Fischer, and constructed by the joint efforts of many scientists, including Robert Griess.

formulas.description: Axiom of associativity
formulas.meaning: For any elements a, b, c of group G, the result of composition does not depend on the placement of parentheses.

formulas.description: Existence of neutral and inverse elements
formulas.latex: \exists e \in G: e \cdot a = a \cdot e = a; \forall a \in G \exists a^{-1} \in G: a \cdot a^{-1} = a^{-1} \cdot a = e
formulas.meaning: e is the neutral element of the group (often 0 or 1), a^{-1} is the inverse element of a.

quasar.name: quasar
quasar.description_popular: A quasar is a lighthouse at the center of a distant galaxy. There, a supermassive black hole devours gas and stars, causing a blinding light to flash that is visible across the entire universe.
quasar.fun_fact_popular: Quasars shine brighter than a trillion stars, but are no larger than our solar system!
quasar.description_simple: A quasar is like a cosmic whirlpool: gas and dust swirl around a supermassive black hole at the galaxy's center. Friction heats the matter to millions of degrees, and it shines like a giant searchlight, often ejecting narrow jets of plasma.
quasar.history_simple: The first quasars were noticed in the 1950s as point-like radio sources. In 1963, Dutch astronomer Martin Schmidt measured the redshift of 3C 273 and realized: the object is incredibly distant and therefore monstrously bright. Calculations were based on Hubble's law, discovered by Edwin Hubble.
quasar.how_it_works_simple: Gravity accelerates infalling matter to near-light speeds. When particles collide, kinetic energy turns into heat — the accretion disk glows white-hot. Excess energy escapes as narrow jets shooting from the poles of the rotating hole.
quasar.description: A quasar is an active galactic nucleus whose radiation is caused by accretion of matter onto a supermassive black hole with a mass of millions to billions of solar masses. The inner part of the accretion disk heats up to temperatures of about 10⁷ K, generating powerful electromagnetic radiation from radio waves to gamma rays. Collimated relativistic jets are also characteristic, accelerated by magnetohydrodynamic processes near the ergosphere.
quasar.history: In the 1950s, radio telescopes recorded compact sources similar to stars but with anomalous spectra. In 1963, Martin Schmidt identified hydrogen lines in the spectrum of 3C 273, redshifted by 16%, which, within the framework of Hubble's law, implied a distance of about 750 Mpc. The conclusion of monstrous luminosity forced a search for the energy source — it turned out to be accretion onto a supermassive black hole, predicted by the Schwarzschild and Kerr solutions in general relativity. By the end of the 20th century, it became clear that most large galaxies had gone through a quasar phase in the past.
quasar.how_it_works: Within Einstein's equations, the gravitational field of the black hole accelerates gas-dust material to relativistic speeds. Energy release obeys the virial theorem: gravitational potential energy transforms into thermal energy, radiated according to the Stefan–Boltzmann law (L = σT⁴A). Simultaneously, magnetic fields amplified by differential rotation form collimated jets via the Blandford–Znajek effect. The redshift of radiation is determined by the Doppler effect for cosmological expansion.
key_problems: Nature of the Blandford–Znajek mechanism and spin energy of the black hole
Formation and acceleration of jets to γ-factors >10
AGN feedback and galaxy evolution
Causes of active nucleus type changes (Seyfert, LINER, Blazar)
Measuring black hole masses in near-IR/optics at high redshift
quasar.fun_fact: The nearest quasar to us, 3C 273, is 2.4 billion light-years away, but through an amateur telescope it appears as a modest 13th-magnitude star.

formulas.description: The Schwarzschild radius formula describes the size of the event horizon of a non-rotating black hole of a given mass.
formulas.meaning: R_s — Schwarzschild radius, G — gravitational constant (6.67430×10⁻¹¹ m³ kg⁻¹ s⁻²), M — mass of the black hole, c — speed of light in vacuum (2.99792458×10⁸ m/s)

formulas.description: The Stefan–Boltzmann law relates the radiative power of a blackbody to its temperature and surface area.
formulas.meaning: L — luminosity (total radiated power), R — radius of the emitting region, σ — Stefan–Boltzmann constant (5.670374×10⁻⁸ W m⁻² K⁻⁴), T — effective surface temperature

asteroid.name: asteroid
asteroid.description_popular: An asteroid is a rocky boulder racing around the Sun. There are millions of such cosmic cobblestones between Mars and Jupiter in the asteroid belt.
asteroid.fun_fact_popular: Some asteroids are so small that if you had a car the size of a house, you'd run them over like a pebble on the road.
asteroid.description_simple: An asteroid is a celestial pirate without a tail. Unlike comets, it contains almost no ice and hangs around the inner regions of the solar system. The largest of them, Ceres, is a dwarf planet about 940 km in diameter, a true king of the asteroid belt.
asteroid.history_simple: The first asteroid, Ceres, was discovered by Giuseppe Piazzi in 1801. At first it was considered a planet, but soon Pallas, Juno, and Vesta were found in the same region of the sky — it became clear that this was a new class of objects. The term 'asteroids' (star-like) was proposed by William Herschel.
asteroid.how_it_works_simple: Asteroids are leftover construction debris from the time of planet formation. Jupiter's gravity prevented them from sticking together into a single body, and now they circle like a swarm of gnats around a lantern, occasionally colliding and breaking into pieces.
asteroid.description: An asteroid is a solid body of the Solar System, ranging from a few meters to hundreds of kilometers in size, orbiting the Sun primarily between the orbits of Mars and Jupiter. Chemically, they are divided into carbonaceous (C-class), silicate (S-class), and metallic (M-class) asteroids. Their shape is often irregular due to insufficient gravity to achieve sphericity.
asteroid.history: After the discovery of Ceres in 1801 by Giuseppe Piazzi as part of the search for a supposed planet between Mars and Jupiter, several more large bodies were quickly found. By the mid-19th century, their number exceeded a hundred. Photographic methods of the 20th century, and later space surveys (LINEAR, Pan-STARRS), increased the catalog to hundreds of thousands. Today, specialized missions study them: NEAR Shoemaker, OSIRIS-REx, Hayabusa.
asteroid.how_it_works: The motion of asteroids is described by Kepler's third law and perturbations from planets, especially Jupiter. Their collisional evolution is governed by the conservation laws of angular momentum and energy. The equilibrium temperature is determined by the Stefan–Boltzmann law, taking into account albedo and distance from the Sun. Spectral classification (by absorption lines in the infrared range) relies on Maxwell's equations to interpret radiative properties.
key_problems: Origin of metallic asteroids like Psyche
Mechanisms of water and organic delivery to Earth
Precise mapping of all potentially hazardous objects
Dynamic evolution of collision families
Geological structure of binary asteroids
asteroid.fun_fact: Asteroid Itokawa resembles a pile of rubble: its average density is less than that of water, and it is a heap of debris held together only by its own weak gravity.

formulas.description: Kepler's third law relates the orbital period of a body to its semi-major axis and the mass of the central body.
formulas.meaning: T — orbital period, G — gravitational constant (6.67430×10⁻¹¹ m³ kg⁻¹ s⁻²), M_⊙ — solar mass (1.989×10³⁰ kg), a — semi-major axis of the asteroid's orbit

nebula.name: nebula
nebula.description_popular: A nebula is a giant cloud of gas and dust in space. It looks like smoke from a campfire, but hundreds of light-years across. New stars are born inside such clouds.
nebula.fun_fact_popular: The most beautiful nebula is perhaps the Horsehead Nebula. It resembles a foal's head floating in space, though it is actually dust obscuring the light of a distant star.
nebula.description_simple: A nebula is an interstellar cloud of gas and dust, illuminated by nearby stars or stirred up by shock waves. In dark nebulae, like in cocoons, stellar embryos hide, and planetary nebulae are the ejected shells of dying stars, resembling soap bubbles.
nebula.history_simple: Charles Messier first cataloged nebulae in the 18th century, mistaking them for comets. Later, William Herschel divided them into star clusters and true clouds. Photographic spectra in the 19th century revealed their gaseous composition — the work of William Huggins.
nebula.how_it_works_simple: Gas in the nebula is ionized by ultraviolet light from young hot stars. Electrons, falling back onto nuclei, emit light of very specific colors — a palette by which chemists recognize hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen. Radiation pressure inflates the material, and supernova shock waves stir the layers like a spoon in a pot.
nebula.description: A nebula is a vast interstellar cloud of gas and dust, observed either in emission lines of ionized hydrogen (H II region), as reflection glow (starlight reflected by dust), or as a dark silhouette against bright backgrounds. Planetary nebulae are outflowing shells of stars on the asymptotic giant branch, while supernova remnants are shock-compressed filaments after the explosion of a massive star.
nebula.history: The first telescopic observations of nebulae were recorded by Galileo, but the first systematization belongs to Charles Messier, who published a catalog of 110 objects to avoid confusing them with comets. In the 19th century, William and John Herschel compiled a general catalog, later becoming the New General Catalogue (NGC). Spectral studies by William Huggins (1864) proved the gaseous nature of many nebulae. Modern theory of star formation in nebulae builds on the work of James Jeans, while emission mechanisms rely on quantum mechanics (the Schrödinger equation for energy levels).
nebula.how_it_works: The main emission source of emission nebulae is recombination and cascade transitions in hydrogen atoms, described by the Schrödinger equation and selection rules. The spectral shape is explained by Maxwell's equations for moving charges and Planck's radiation law for the thermal background of dust. The balance between gravitational contraction and gas pressure is regulated by Noether's theorem (energy conservation) and the second law of thermodynamics during radiation. In reflection nebulae, light polarization is described by Mie scattering theory, and the particle size distribution follows power laws.
key_problems: Precise dependence of the initial mass function on nebula chemical composition
Role of magnetic fields in suppressing or stimulating star formation
Mechanism of filament fragmentation into protostellar cores
Origin of giant molecular clouds and their life cycles
Modeling 3D structure from 2D images
nebula.fun_fact: In the Orion Nebula, easily visible to the naked eye, there is a whole 'maternity ward' of hundreds of protostars shrouded in gas-dust cocoons.

formulas.description: Planck's law describes the spectral radiance of a blackbody at a given temperature.
formulas.meaning: B_ν — spectral radiance (intensity per unit frequency), h — Planck constant (6.62607015×10⁻³⁴ J·s), ν — frequency of radiation, c — speed of light (2.99792458×10⁸ m/s), k — Boltzmann constant (1.380649×10⁻²³ J/K), T — temperature

formulas.description: The Doppler effect for low velocities allows relating the spectral line shift to the radial velocity of the cloud.
formulas.meaning: Δλ — wavelength shift (observed minus laboratory), λ₀ — laboratory rest wavelength, v — radial velocity of the cloud (positive when receding), c — speed of light

wormhole.name: wormhole
wormhole.description_popular: A wormhole is a tunnel through spacetime connecting two distant points. It's as if a sheet of paper is folded in half and a pencil pierces through — so the universe curves, creating a shortcut.
wormhole.fun_fact_popular: If a wormhole existed between Earth and Alpha Centauri, the journey would take not years but moments — but its entrance might look like a black hole.
wormhole.description_simple: A wormhole is a hypothetical object predicted by general relativity. Just as a worm bores through an apple, creating a direct path instead of going around the surface, a wormhole pierces the fabric of spacetime, connecting distant regions.
wormhole.history_simple: First mathematically described in 1916 by Karl Schwarzschild (as a solution to Einstein's equations), but the idea of tunnels in spacetime was developed by Einstein and Rosen in 1935, and the term 'wormhole' was coined by John Wheeler in 1957.
wormhole.how_it_works_simple: A thrown stone creates ripples on a lake's surface — that's a disturbance in spacetime. A wormhole is a sudden whirlpool, sucking in and ejecting elsewhere. To prevent such a whirlpool from collapsing, a special 'anti-gravity' matter is needed.
wormhole.description: A wormhole (Einstein-Rosen bridge) is a topological feature of spacetime, representing a 'handle' in the manifold that connects two distant regions or different universes. In general relativity, it is described as a solution to Einstein's equations, usually with a non-zero stress-energy tensor. The simplest metric is the Morris-Thorne metric, which requires exotic matter with negative energy density to keep the throat open. Wormholes can be traversable or non-traversable; the latter, like the Einstein-Rosen bridge, have event horizons and resemble two connected black holes. The main problem is stability: without exotic matter, the throat collapses on a time scale of the order of the Planck time.
wormhole.history: The concept emerged after the publication of the Schwarzschild solution in 1916, which contains an inner tunnel, but it is hidden behind an event horizon. In 1935, Albert Einstein and Nathan Rosen showed that the equations could describe a 'bridge' between two regions, avoiding singularities; later, John Wheeler connected such bridges with particles and coined the term 'wormhole' in 1957. In 1988, Kip Thorne and Michael Morris proposed a traversable wormhole without a horizon, stimulating research into exotic matter. Modern efforts include attempts to construct wormholes in quantum gravity.
wormhole.how_it_works: A wormhole acts as a spacetime tunnel described by the metric ds^2 = -e^{2Φ(r)} dt^2 + (1 - b(r)/r)^{-1} dr^2 + r^2 (dθ^2 + sin^2θ dφ^2), where Φ(r) is the redshift function and b(r) is the shape function. For traversability, the throat must have no horizon, i.e., e^{2Φ} must remain finite, and tidal forces must be tolerable. The averaged null energy condition must be violated, which is possible due to quantum effects like the Casimir effect. Traveling through the wormhole means an observer moving along a timelike geodesic through the throat.
key_problems: Stability of the throat without exotic matter
Existence of macroscopic exotic matter with negative energy density
Time travel paradoxes and causality violation
Lack of observational evidence or ways to search for it
Quantum corrections and possible destruction at Planck scales
wormhole.fun_fact: Through a wormhole, one could theoretically travel in time: if one end is accelerated to near-light speeds, time there slows down, and you'd exit before you entered.

formulas.description: Condition for the opening of the wormhole throat
formulas.meaning: b(r) — shape function defining the geometry of spatial sections, r_0 — throat radius, where b(r_0) = r_0

spacetime_curvature.name: spacetime curvature
