spacetime_curvature.description_popular: Spacetime bends like a rubber sheet when a heavy ball is placed on it. Massive objects — stars and planets — dent it, forcing other bodies to move along curved trajectories, which we call orbits.
spacetime_curvature.fun_fact_popular: The Sun curves space so much that light from distant stars passing nearby is deflected by a tiny angle — this was proven by Eddington during the 1919 eclipse.
spacetime_curvature.description_simple: General relativity describes gravity not as a force, but as geometry. Just as a river's current flows around stones, changing direction, so mass curves spacetime, prescribing the paths of everything, including light.
spacetime_curvature.history_simple: The idea of curved space dates back to Bernhard Riemann in 1854, but the physical meaning was given by Albert Einstein in 1915, completing the general theory of relativity. The first exact model of curved spacetime for a point mass was found by Karl Schwarzschild in 1916.
spacetime_curvature.how_it_works_simple: If you place a watermelon on a trampoline, it creates a dent. An orange thrown nearby will roll along a curve — similarly, the Earth moves in the well created by the Sun's mass. The more massive the object, the deeper the 'pit', and even light cannot escape from a black hole.
spacetime_curvature.description: Spacetime curvature is the key concept of general relativity, replacing Newton's notion of gravitational force with a geometric property of the manifold. In the presence of mass and energy, the spacetime metric ceases to be flat, and geodesics — the shortest paths — curve. Curvature is quantitatively described by the Riemann tensor, which, via Einstein's equations, is linked to the stress-energy tensor. Along with local effects like gravitational redshift and Shapiro delay, global curvature determines cosmological evolution and can lead to exotic objects such as black holes and wormholes.
spacetime_curvature.history: The concept of non-Euclidean geometry was developed by mathematicians Gauss, Lobachevsky, and Bolyai, but Riemann in 1854 introduced the notion of a curved manifold of arbitrary dimension. Gregorio Ricci and Tullio Levi-Civita developed tensor calculus, which became the language of the theory. In 1915, Einstein, using these tools and the equivalence principle, formulated the field equations linking curvature to matter. Karl Schwarzschild in 1916 obtained the first exact solution for a spherically symmetric body, predicting black holes. Subsequently, Friedmann and Lemaître applied the theory to the entire universe, showing its expansion.
spacetime_curvature.how_it_works: Mathematically, curvature is described by the metric tensor g_{μν}, which defines the invariant interval ds^2 = g_{μν} dx^μ dx^ν. Curvature is determined through Christoffel symbols and the Riemann tensor R^ρ_{σμν}. Einstein's equations: R_{μν} - (1/2) g_{μν} R + Λ g_{μν} = (8πG/c^4) T_{μν}, where R_{μν} is the Ricci tensor, R is the scalar curvature, Λ is the cosmological constant, T_{μν} is the stress-energy tensor. In the weak gravitational limit, the equations yield Newton's law of universal gravitation. Curvature has been experimentally verified through effects: light deflection, signal delay, perihelion precession of Mercury, gravitational waves.
key_problems: Quantization of gravity and the nature of spacetime at the Planck scale
Problem of the cosmological constant and dark energy
Description of singularities in black holes and the initial Big Bang singularity
Unification with quantum field theory without divergences
Nature of dark matter and its influence on large-scale curvature
spacetime_curvature.fun_fact: Near the Earth's surface, time runs slightly slower than in orbit — due to spacetime curvature. GPS satellites constantly adjust their clocks, otherwise navigation would drift by kilometers.

formulas.description: Einstein's equations linking curvature to matter
formulas.latex:
R_{\mu
u} -  rac{1}{2} g_{\mu
u} R + \Lambda g_{\mu
u} =  rac{8\pi G}{c^4} T_{\mu
u}
formulas.meaning:
R_{\mu
u} — Ricci tensor, g_{\mu
u} — metric tensor, R — scalar curvature, Λ — cosmological constant, G — gravitational constant (G ≈ 6.674×10^{-11} N·m²/kg²), c — speed of light (c ≈ 3×10^8 m/s), T_{\mu
u} — stress-energy tensor

redshift.name: redshift
redshift.description_popular: When an ambulance passes by, its siren changes tone: higher when approaching, lower when receding. The same happens with light from distant galaxies: if a galaxy is moving away, its light becomes slightly redder — this is redshift. The faster the galaxy recedes, the stronger the shift.
redshift.fun_fact_popular: Thanks to redshift we know the Universe is expanding. Edwin Hubble noticed that almost all galaxies are redshifted, and the farther they are, the redder they appear.
redshift.description_simple: Redshift is the stretching of light waves from a receding source, analogous to how waves from a stone thrown into water become longer if we move away in a boat. In space, the expansion of space itself stretches photons on their way to us.
redshift.history_simple: The Doppler effect for sound was described by Christian Doppler in 1842. Astronomer Vesto Slipher began measuring shifts of lines in the spectra of nebulae back in the 1910s. But the decisive step was made by Edwin Hubble in 1929, discovering the proportionality of redshift to the distance of galaxies.
redshift.how_it_works_simple: Atoms emit light at specific wavelengths — like fingerprints. When a galaxy recedes, the wave crests arrive less frequently, and the entire spectrum shifts toward longer wavelengths — to the red. From the amount of shift, the recession velocity is calculated. It's like a comb with sparse teeth: if the source moves, the 'teeth' of the spectrum become even sparser.
redshift.description: Redshift (denoted z) is defined as z = (λ_observed – λ_emitted)/λ_emitted. In cosmology, it is caused by the expansion of the Universe according to General Relativity; the scale factor a(t) relates the wavelength at emission to today's: λ_obs / λ_em = a_today / a_then. The shift also includes the Doppler effect from peculiar velocities and gravitational redshift from spacetime curvature. For z<0.1 the linear Hubble law approximation v = H₀ D works; for larger z, the cosmological model (ΛCDM) must be taken into account.
redshift.history: The idea of an expanding Universe arose from Friedmann's equations (1922) and Georges Lemaître's observations (1927), but gained wide acceptance after Edwin Hubble's work (1929) showing a linear relationship between redshift and distance. Lemaître was the first to interpret this as an expansion of space. Later, measurements of type Ia supernovae in 1998 led to the discovery of accelerated expansion (dark energy).
redshift.how_it_works: Redshift is measured spectroscopically: lines of known elements (e.g., hydrogen) are shifted by a factor (1+z). The Hubble law v = H₀ d relates recession velocity and distance. For large z, the relationship becomes nonlinear and is determined by the cosmological model. Peculiar velocities of galaxies make an additional, often negligible contribution. There is also gravitational redshift, predicted by General Relativity: photons lose energy when escaping a gravitational well. Redshift measurements rely on the Doppler effect and Hubble's law.
key_problems: The nature of dark energy causing accelerated expansion remains a mystery.
The exact value of the Hubble constant H₀ — discrepancy between local measurements and cosmic microwave background data (Hubble tension).
Possible evolution of redshift over time due to unknown interactions between dark matter and energy.
redshift.fun_fact: The highest recorded redshift for galaxy GN-z11 is z≈11, its light has been traveling to us for over 13 billion years, from an era when the Universe was only 400 million years old.

formulas.description: Definition of redshift
formulas.meaning: z is the redshift, λ_obs is the observed wavelength, λ_em is the wavelength at emission.

formulas.description: Hubble's law
formulas.meaning: v is the galaxy's recession velocity, H₀ is the Hubble constant (H₀ ≈ 70 (km/s)/Mpc), d is the distance to the galaxy.

lepton.name: lepton
lepton.description_popular: Leptons are tiny building blocks of matter that do not feel the strong nuclear force. The most familiar is the electron, which runs around the nucleus in atoms. There are also elusive neutrinos, which fly through the Earth almost without noticing it.
lepton.fun_fact_popular: About 10¹¹ neutrinos from the Sun pass through your fingernail every day, but you feel nothing — so ghostly are leptons.
lepton.description_simple: Leptons are a family of light particles (from the Greek 'light'), including the electron, muon, tau, and three types of neutrinos. They are like schools of fish in an ocean of fields: each lepton has its own field, just as each fish species has its own school, and they interact electromagnetically (if charged) and weakly, but avoid strong interactions.
lepton.history_simple: The electron was discovered by J.J. Thomson in 1897. The muon was found by Carl Anderson in 1936 and initially mistaken for a meson. The tau lepton was discovered in 1975 at the Stanford Linear Accelerator. Neutrinos were first detected in 1956. Wolfgang Pauli predicted the existence of neutrinos in 1930.
lepton.how_it_works_simple: Leptons manifest themselves in decays and collisions. The muon is a heavy cousin of the electron, lives for a couple of microseconds, and decays into an electron and neutrinos. Neutrinos oscillate, changing their flavor along the way like a chameleon changing color. All this is governed by the weak interaction, and for charged leptons, also by electromagnetism.
lepton.description: In the Standard Model, leptons are fundamental fermions with spin 1/2, not participating in strong interactions. Six flavors: electron (e⁻), muon (μ⁻), tau (τ⁻) and corresponding neutrinos (ν_e, ν_μ, ν_τ). Each charged lepton carries a charge of -1e, while neutrinos are neutral. Leptons are conserved by generation in an axial form in the Standard Model, but neutrino oscillations violate lepton number by flavor. Neutrino masses are extremely small, pointing to physics beyond the Standard Model.
lepton.history: J.J. Thomson identified the electron as a particle in 1897 by measuring its e/m ratio. The muon was discovered in 1936 in cosmic rays and was first confused with Yukawa's meson. The tau lepton was detected by Martin Perl's group in 1975. The existence of neutrinos was postulated by Wolfgang Pauli in 1930 to save the energy conservation law in beta decay; neutrinos were first captured by Reines and Cowan in 1956.
lepton.how_it_works: Leptons interact through the weak interaction (exchange of W±, Z bosons), and charged ones also electromagnetically (photon). The Dirac equation describes free charged leptons. Neutrino oscillations require nonzero mass and mixing, described by the Pontecorvo-Maki-Nakagawa-Sakata matrix. Charged leptons are stable (electron) or decay via the weak interaction (muon, tau). The Pauli principle forbids two identical leptons from occupying the same quantum state, which determines the structure of atoms and neutron stars.
key_problems: Why are there three generations of leptons and what is their mass hierarchy?
Are neutrinos Dirac or Majorana particles (is matter and antimatter identical or not)?
What is the absolute mass scale of neutrinos and does a sterile neutrino exist?
Unification of leptons and quarks in Grand Unified Theories.
lepton.fun_fact: In neutrino observatories like IceCube, they use a cubic kilometer of Antarctic ice to catch a flash from a single neutrino colliding with an atom.

formulas.description: Dirac equation for a free charged lepton
formulas.meaning: ψ is the four-component spinor of the lepton field, i is the imaginary unit, ħ is the reduced Planck constant (ħ ≈ 1.05×10⁻³⁴ J·s), γ^μ are the Dirac matrices (4×4), ∂_μ is the partial derivative with respect to coordinates, m is the lepton mass, c is the speed of light (c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s).

quantum_number.name: quantum number
quantum_number.description_popular: Electrons in an atom are like birds in a tree. Each bird occupies its own branch, at a certain height and with a certain flight direction. Quantum numbers are a precise description of the place: which branch (energy), from which side (orbital shape), in which nest (magnetic number), and which way it's facing (spin). No two addresses are the same.
quantum_number.fun_fact_popular: The spin quantum number of an electron takes only two values: +1/2 or -1/2, like a coin that always lands heads or tails, but never on its edge. It is precisely thanks to the Pauli principle, which forbids identical sets of numbers, that atoms do not collapse and form all the variety of elements.
quantum_number.description_simple: In a beehive, each bee knows its role and place. Quantum numbers are the 'passport' of an electron, defining its energy level, cloud shape, orientation, and spin. Just as bees distribute themselves among the honeycombs, electrons fill orbitals according to these numbers.
quantum_number.history_simple: The principal quantum number n was introduced by Niels Bohr in 1913 to explain hydrogen spectra. In 1916, Arnold Sommerfeld added the orbital and magnetic numbers. The concept took its final form in 1925, when Wolfgang Pauli discovered spin and formulated the exclusion principle.
quantum_number.how_it_works_simple: An electron in an atom can only be in strictly defined states. The four numbers are like the coordinates of a parking space in a multistory garage: floor (n), row (l), spot (mₗ), and direction of the car (spin). No two cars can occupy the same position — that's how orbitals are filled.
quantum_number.description: In quantum mechanics, the state of a particle is fully described by a wave function, and quantum numbers arise as eigenvalues of operators of observable quantities. For an electron in a hydrogen atom, from the Schrödinger equation, we get the principal n (energy), orbital l (angular momentum), and magnetic mₗ (projection). The fourth — spin s and its projection mₛ — follows from the Dirac equation and describes the intrinsic angular momentum. The Pauli principle forbids two fermions from having the same set of these numbers, determining the structure of atoms and the periodic law.
quantum_number.history: The idea of quantum numbers originated in Bohr's atomic model (1913) with the introduction of the principal number n. Arnold Sommerfeld generalized the model in 1916, adding orbital and magnetic numbers to explain fine structure. A decisive contribution was made by Wolfgang Pauli, who proposed spin and the exclusion principle in 1925. Erwin Schrödinger created wave mechanics in 1926, where the numbers n, l, mₗ received a rigorous interpretation as eigenvalues of operators, and the Dirac equation (1928) gave spin a relativistic foundation.
quantum_number.how_it_works: The wave function of an electron is determined from the Schrödinger equation. When separating variables in a central potential, quantum conditions arise: the radial equation gives n = 1, 2, 3…, the angular one gives l = 0, …, n−1 and mₗ = −l, …, l. Spin is introduced via the Dirac equation and for the electron takes values s=1/2, mₛ = ±1/2. The Pauli principle forbids two electrons from occupying the same quantum state, which explains the sequential filling of shells and the periodicity of chemical properties. Thus, quantum numbers are a direct consequence of the Schrödinger equation and the Pauli principle.
key_problems: Why do such specific quantum numbers and symmetries exist?
Measurement problem: Are quantum numbers real before the act of observation?
How will quantum numbers change in the regime of quantum gravity, at Planck scales?
quantum_number.fun_fact: The electron's spin has no classical analogue: it's not the rotation of a tiny ball, but an intrinsic property manifested in magnetic moments. Sometimes spin is compared to rotation, but it's actually a quantum 'left-right' that cannot be seen directly.

formulas.description: Energy of an electron in a hydrogen atom
formulas.meaning: E_n is the energy of level n, m_e is the electron mass (≈9.11×10⁻³¹ kg), e is the electron charge (≈1.602×10⁻¹⁹ C), ε₀ is the electric constant (≈8.854×10⁻¹² F/m), h is Planck's constant (≈6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s), n is the principal quantum number (n=1,2,3...)

formulas.description: Orbital angular momentum
formulas.meaning: L is the magnitude of the orbital angular momentum, l is the orbital quantum number (l=0,1,...,n-1), ℏ is the reduced Planck constant (h/2π ≈ 1.0546×10⁻³⁴ J·s)

event_horizon.name: event horizon
event_horizon.description_popular: The boundary of a black hole from which not even light can escape. Like the edge of a waterfall: as you approach, the boat can still row, but beyond a certain line the current relentlessly carries it down. Here the falling speed of spacetime equals the speed of light.
event_horizon.fun_fact_popular: The Sun, compressed into a black hole, would have a horizon only 6 kilometers in diameter. Earth would continue to move in the same orbit, but eternal darkness and cold would set in.
event_horizon.description_simple: An invisible sphere around a black hole. Anything that crosses it disappears forever from our Universe. Compare to a whirlpool in a bathtub: as long as the water is far away, you can swim out, but at a certain distance it sucks you in irretrievably.
event_horizon.history_simple: Karl Schwarzschild in 1916 found an exact solution to Einstein's equations for a point mass and obtained a radius at which the metric becomes unphysical. Later, in 1974, Stephen Hawking showed that the horizon emits faint radiation due to quantum effects.
event_horizon.how_it_works_simple: A massive object bends spacetime, like a ball on a rubber sheet. At a certain distance, the slope becomes so steep that even light cannot get out. That's the horizon. For a distant observer, the time of a falling object slows down and stops right at the boundary.
event_horizon.description: The event horizon is a closed null hypersurface in spacetime that bounds a region from which no causal signals can reach a distant observer. For a static uncharged black hole, its radius equals the Schwarzschild radius: R_s = 2GM/c². In general relativity, it is a global concept defined as the boundary of the past for light rays that escape to infinity.
event_horizon.history: The first solution of Einstein's equations containing a horizon was found by Karl Schwarzschild in 1916. Initially it was considered a mathematical fiction, but in 1939 Oppenheimer and Snyder showed the reality of gravitational collapse. Key discoveries in singularity theory and horizon properties were made by Roger Penrose and Stephen Hawking in the 1960s-70s. Jacob Bekenstein and Hawking linked the horizon to thermodynamics, proving that black holes have entropy and temperature.
event_horizon.how_it_works: According to Einstein's equations, a massive body curves spacetime. During collapse, a region forms where light cones tilt inward, and any path leads to the singularity. At the horizon, the coordinate singularity of the Schwarzschild metric disappears in suitable coordinates (e.g., Kruskal–Szekeres). Quantum effects near the horizon lead to Hawking radiation, with its temperature and entropy related to the horizon area by the Bekenstein–Hawking formulas.
key_problems: Information paradox: Does information about matter that fell into a black hole disappear after it evaporates?
Is there a 'firewall' at the horizon that violates the equivalence principle?
What is the microscopic nature of horizon entropy in quantum gravity?
event_horizon.fun_fact: From the point of view of a distant observer, any object will never reach the horizon — it will infinitely redshift and freeze. But the falling observer crosses the horizon in finite time, noticing nothing special (except catastrophic tidal forces in small holes).

formulas.description: Schwarzschild radius
formulas.meaning: R_s is the event horizon radius, G is the gravitational constant (≈6.674×10⁻¹¹ m³/(kg·s²)), M is the object's mass, c is the speed of light (≈3×10⁸ m/s)

formulas.description: Bekenstein–Hawking entropy
formulas.meaning: S is the black hole entropy, k_B is the Boltzmann constant (≈1.38×10⁻²³ J/K), A is the event horizon area, l_P is the Planck length (≈1.616×10⁻³⁵ m)

enthalpy.name: enthalpy
enthalpy.description_popular: Energy that a substance stores as heat and the ability to push or expand. By heating a pot of water, you increase its enthalpy: part goes to heating, part to expansion. It's like a battery: it's not just hot, but also exerts pressure on the pipes to heat the room.
enthalpy.fun_fact_popular: Melting ice absorbs 334 J per gram without raising the temperature — its enthalpy jumps. That's why a handful of snow thrown into a fire hisses and cools down faster than it seems.
enthalpy.description_simple: The system's total 'heat plus work'. Imagine a traveler: the ticket is the internal energy, and the surcharge for luggage and visas is the work against the external environment. The sum of these costs is enthalpy. At constant pressure, heating or cooling equals the change in this sum.
enthalpy.history_simple: The term 'enthalpy' was proposed by the Dutch physicist Heike Kamerlingh Onnes in 1909, but the idea of latent heat and heat content was developed earlier by Rudolf Clausius in the mid-19th century.
enthalpy.how_it_works_simple: In an ordinary chemical reaction in air, pressure doesn't change, so the released heat equals the change in enthalpy. Methane combustion: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O releases 890 kJ/mol — that's the reaction enthalpy. In our bodies, the 'burning' of glucose with oxygen is also measured by enthalpy.
enthalpy.description: Enthalpy H = U + PV is a thermodynamic potential most convenient for processes at constant pressure. Its change dH = dU + P dV = δQ at P=const, i.e., equals the heat received. In statistical mechanics, enthalpy is computed through the isothermal-isobaric ensemble. It is widely used in chemistry (Hess's law) and engineering (calculation of steam turbines, air conditioners).
enthalpy.history: The roots of the concept go back to the work of Hermann Helmholtz and Rudolf Clausius (1850s), who called it 'heat content'. In 1909, Heike Kamerlingh Onnes introduced the modern name from the Greek 'to heat'. Later, with the development of statistical physics, enthalpy received a microscopic foundation as the average energy of a system at fixed pressure and temperature.
enthalpy.how_it_works: From the first law of thermodynamics: dU = δQ - P dV. At constant pressure, dH = dU + P dV = δQ. Therefore, the heat effect of a reaction at P=const equals ΔH. The second law of thermodynamics, through the Gibbs free energy G = H - TS, determines the direction of spontaneous processes. In engineering, the enthalpy of steam or refrigerant allows calculation of cycle efficiencies.
key_problems: Accurate quantum-chemical calculation of enthalpy of complex molecules at high temperatures.
Prediction of enthalpy of substances in extreme states (planetary interiors, stellar envelopes).
Relationship of enthalpy with microscopic fluctuations in nanoscale systems.
enthalpy.fun_fact: The most powerful known chemical reaction — combustion of dicyanoacetylene in fluorine — releases about 10,000 kJ per gram, heating the flame to 5000 °C. The enthalpy of such a flame is comparable to the surface of a small star.

formulas.description: Definition of enthalpy
formulas.meaning: H is enthalpy, U is internal energy, P is pressure, V is volume

formulas.description: Enthalpy change in an isobaric process
formulas.meaning: ΔH is the change in enthalpy, Q_p is the heat transferred to the system at constant pressure

cosmic_dust.name: cosmic dust
cosmic_dust.description_popular: Cosmic dust is the invisible dirt of the Universe. Microscopic grains of carbon and silicates float between stars, obscuring their light. Without it, there would be no planets and us: rocks stick together from dust and worlds are born.
cosmic_dust.fun_fact_popular: Every day, about 100 tons of cosmic dust fall to Earth.
cosmic_dust.description_simple: Interstellar dust is solid particles smaller than a micron, consisting mainly of carbon, silicates and ices. It makes up only 1% of the mass of the interstellar medium, but absorbs and scatters almost half of starlight, making it redder.
cosmic_dust.history_simple: Herschel already noticed dark gaps in the Milky Way, but the nature of interstellar absorption was explained in the early 20th century by Edward Barnard. In the 1930s, astronomers proved that the dimming of light is caused by dust, not gas. Later, Fred Hoyle showed that heavy elements synthesized in stellar interiors later condense into dust grains.
cosmic_dust.how_it_works_simple: Dust grains act like light filters: blue light is scattered more strongly, so through a layer of dust stars appear red — an effect familiar from the setting Sun. The grains themselves stick together in dense clouds, forming planetesimals — the embryos of planets.
cosmic_dust.description: Cosmic dust is solid microparticles scattered in the interstellar, interplanetary, and intergalactic medium. Typical size ranges from several nanometers to a micron. They consist of amorphous silicates (olivine, pyroxene), carbon compounds (graphite, amorphous carbon, PAHs), ices (H₂O, CO, CO₂, NH₃, CH₄), and refractory minerals. Dust is responsible for interstellar extinction, polarization, and infrared emission. It plays a key role in the thermal balance of clouds and chemical evolution: catalytic reactions occur on the surface of dust grains, forming molecular hydrogen. Dust is the raw material for planet formation in protoplanetary disks. It is observed in all types of galaxies and is actively studied in the infrared, submillimeter, and microwave ranges.
cosmic_dust.history: The existence of interstellar light absorption was suspected since the 19th century (discovery of dark nebulae). In 1909, Edward Barnard catalogued dark regions, hypothesizing that they were dense clouds of 'absorbing matter'. In the 1930s, Robert Trumpler quantitatively showed that interstellar reddening is caused by scattering, not a true color change of stars. Fred Hoyle and Wickramasinghe in the 1960s developed a model of dust as a mixture of silicates and carbon. Margaret Burbidge contributed to understanding the formation of heavy elements that condense into dust. The Infrared Astronomical Satellite IRAS (1983) and subsequent missions (Spitzer, Herschel) discovered giant dust reservoirs in galaxies.
cosmic_dust.how_it_works: The interaction of dust with radiation is described by Mie theory for spherical particles. The refractive index is complex: m = n + i k. The extinction cross-section Q_ext = Q_abs + Q_sca depends on the size parameter x = 2πa/λ. For x << 1 (Rayleigh scattering) Q_sca ∝ λ⁻⁴, causing reddening. Polarization arises with elongated particles aligned by the Galactic magnetic field. Dust also reprocesses ultraviolet stellar radiation into infrared: absorption of UV photons heats the grain to an equilibrium temperature of 10–100 K, and it re-emits in the far-IR. Emission features at 3.3, 6.2, 7.7, 8.6, 11.3 μm are attributed to polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs). In dynamics, dust is subject to radiation pressure, gas drag, and gravitational settling in disks. The Smoluchowski coagulation equation describes particle growth. A crucial process is H₂ formation on surfaces: hydrogen atoms adsorb, recombine, and desorb, heating the grain. This mechanism explains the high efficiency of molecular clouds.
key_problems: Uncertainty in the size distribution and composition of dust grains in different environments
Dust formation in supernovae and stellar winds: quantitative agreement between production and destruction
The role of dust in delaying star formation in dark clouds
The exact nature of carriers of unidentified infrared emission bands (UIR bands)
cosmic_dust.fun_fact: Some dust clouds contain complex organic molecules, including amino acid precursors.

formulas.description: Interstellar reddening
formulas.meaning: E(B-V) — color excess in B and V bands, A_B and A_V — extinctions in blue (B) and visual (V) bands in magnitudes

formulas.description: Extinction parameter
formulas.meaning: R_V — ratio of extinction, for the average interstellar medium R_V ≈ 3.1

formulas.description: Equilibrium dust grain temperature
formulas.meaning: T_d — dust grain temperature, L* — stellar luminosity, r — distance to the star, σ — Stefan-Boltzmann constant (σ ≈ 5.67×10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴), Q_abs^UV and Q_abs^IR — absorption efficiencies in the ultraviolet and infrared ranges

red_dwarf.name: red dwarf
red_dwarf.description_popular: A red dwarf is a star the size of Jupiter, with hydrogen burning slowly inside. It will almost never cool down.
red_dwarf.fun_fact_popular: Red dwarfs live for trillion years, longer than the entire Universe.
red_dwarf.description_simple: A red dwarf is the most common type of star. It smolders like slow coal rather than blazing fiercely.
red_dwarf.history_simple: The class was first identified by Einar Hertzsprung in the early 20th century. Systematic study of red dwarfs — by Willem Luyten.
red_dwarf.how_it_works_simple: In the interior, helium is slowly synthesized from hydrogen. Convection carries heat outward, like in a boiling pot.
red_dwarf.description: A red dwarf is a low-mass main-sequence star of spectral class M. Less than 0.6 solar masses, surface temperature 2500–4000 K. Fully convective, which ensures complete burning of hydrogen. Lifetime exceeds the age of the Universe.
red_dwarf.history: Einari Hertzsprung pointed out the difference between giants and dwarfs in 1905. The classification of red dwarfs as class M was developed later. The study of their spectra and luminosities continues.
red_dwarf.how_it_works: Thermonuclear reactions of the proton-proton chain occur at low temperatures. The star is in hydrostatic equilibrium. Energy transfer is by convection. Due to their low luminosity, they are hard to observe.
key_problems: The exact mechanism of flare activity
Influence on exoplanet atmospheres
The initial mass function in the low-mass region
red_dwarf.fun_fact: Proxima Centauri is a red dwarf with a planet in the habitable zone.

white_dwarf.name: white dwarf
white_dwarf.description_popular: A white dwarf is the core of a star after death: a hot ball the size of Earth, cools forever.
white_dwarf.fun_fact_popular: A spoonful of white dwarf material weighs as much as an elephant.
white_dwarf.description_simple: A white dwarf is the remnant of a star like the Sun. Electrons push against each other, preventing the star from collapsing into a point.
white_dwarf.history_simple: The first white dwarf — Sirius B — was discovered by Alvan Clark in 1862. Chandrasekhar established their mass limit in 1931.
white_dwarf.how_it_works_simple: Gravity tries to squeeze the stellar corpse, but electron repulsion holds its shape, like a spring.
white_dwarf.description: A white dwarf is a compact object consisting of degenerate electron gas. It has no thermonuclear energy sources; it shines due to thermal reserves. Typical mass ~0.6 M☉, radius ~10^4 km. It cools over billions of years.
white_dwarf.history: In 1910, Henry Norris Russell discovered the white dwarf 40 Eridani B. In 1926, Ralph Fowler explained its structure using quantum mechanics. Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar in 1931 theoretically derived the maximum mass of a white dwarf — the Chandrasekhar limit.
white_dwarf.how_it_works: The pressure of degenerate electrons counteracts gravity. The state is described by Fermi-Dirac statistics. There is the Chandrasekhar mass limit (~1.4 M☉), above which collapse to a neutron star or a Type Ia supernova explosion occurs. Cooling is governed by the thermal conductivity of degenerate gas and crystallization.
key_problems: The exact mechanism of Type Ia supernova explosion
The equation of state at high densities
The influence of magnetic fields on cooling
The mass distribution of white dwarfs
white_dwarf.fun_fact: The nearest white dwarf — Sirius B — shines faintly but is visible with a telescope.

formulas.description: Chandrasekhar limit — maximum mass of a white dwarf
formulas.meaning: M_{\text{Ch}} — Chandrasekhar limit, approximately 1.44 solar masses; M_{\odot} — solar mass, M_{\odot} ≈ 1.989×10^{30} kg

photometry.name: photometry
photometry.description_popular: Photometry is measuring the brightness of light. How the eye senses night is darker than day.
photometry.fun_fact_popular: The most precise photometry notices a star dimming by as little as 0.01%.
photometry.description_simple: Photometry catches the slightest changes in light: a planet blocks a star, or a distant galaxy brightens slightly.
photometry.history_simple: Astrophotometry dates back to Galileo's telescope. Modern CCD photometry developed in the late 20th century.
photometry.how_it_works_simple: The telescope counts photons, like a bucket in the rain: how many drops fell in a minute.
photometry.description: Photometry is a branch of astrophysics that measures the radiation flux of celestial objects in specific spectral bands. It is used for light curves, distance determination, exoplanet search, and variability studies.
photometry.history: Visual photometry began with John Herschel's estimates of stellar brightness in the 19th century. Photoelectric photometry emerged in the mid-20th century. CCD photometry from the 1980s revolutionized the field. Space telescopes such as Kepler (led by William Borucki) discovered thousands of exoplanets via the transit method.
photometry.how_it_works: Measurement of light intensity passing through filters, in digital form. Noises (photon, readout) limit accuracy. Light curves are analyzed to detect periodic signals. It is used in standardizing stellar magnitudes, photometric systems (UBVRI), and calibration.
key_problems: Systematic errors in absolute calibration
Photometric precision for terrestrial exoplanets
Accounting for instrumental effects
photometry.fun_fact: Thanks to photometry, Kepler discovered thousands of exoplanets.

formulas.description: Pogson's formula for stellar magnitudes
formulas.meaning: m — apparent magnitude; F — measured flux; F_0 — reference flux corresponding to the zero-point of the photometric system

ligo.name: LIGO
ligo.description_popular: LIGO is a giant ruler that measures how gravitational waves stretch and squeeze space. Two long arms of a laser beam are compared: if a wave passes, one arm becomes slightly longer than the other.
ligo.fun_fact_popular: LIGO's sensitivity is such that it detects length changes ten thousand times smaller than the size of a proton — like measuring the distance to the nearest star with the accuracy of a human hair's thickness.
ligo.description_simple: Like an ear catching the faintest rustle in silence, LIGO listens to the tremor of spacetime itself. The interwoven laser beams in the interferometer turn the elusive gravitational ripple into an electrical signal.
ligo.history_simple: The idea of the detector goes back to the pioneering work of Joseph Weber in the 1960s, but it was only in 2015 that the advanced LIGO interferometer, built under the leadership of Rainer Weiss and others, first detected gravitational waves from the merger of two black holes.
ligo.how_it_works_simple: A laser beam is split into two beams traveling along perpendicular 4-kilometer arms. Reflecting off mirrors, the beams meet and cancel each other out, but the slightest asymmetry in lengths — due to a gravitational wave — disrupts the perfect cancellation, producing a signal.
ligo.description: LIGO (Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory) is a complex of ground-based detectors using the principle of Michelson laser interferometry with Fabry-Perot cavities in 4 km long arms to register gravitational waves — perturbations of the spacetime metric predicted by general relativity. An incoming wave induces a phase difference in the two orthogonal arms, measured by the shift of the interference pattern.
ligo.history: Attempts to detect gravitational waves began in the 1960s with Joseph Weber's solid-state resonators. In the 1970s, the idea of a laser interferometer was proposed by Rainer Weiss and further developed with the participation of Kip Thorne and Ronald Drever. Key funding for the project was obtained in the 1990s, construction was completed in the early 2000s. The first direct detection — event GW150914 — occurred on September 14, 2015.
ligo.how_it_works: In each arm, the laser beam is reflected many times (about 280 times) between mirrors, effectively increasing the length to 1120 km and enhancing sensitivity. The combined beam is sent to a photodetector. The gravitational wave, by changing the proper length of the arms in opposite directions according to the metric h = ΔL/L, modulates the brightness of the interference spot. The noise reduction system (seismic, thermal, shot) allows measuring relative strains on the order of 10⁻²¹.
key_problems: Limited frequency range: the detector is insensitive to gravitational waves with frequencies below ~10 Hz due to seismic noise, and above ~10 kHz due to shot noise.
Cosmological sources: predicted gravitational waves from inflation remain inaccessible to ground-based facilities, requiring space interferometers like LISA.
Nature of compact objects: it is not always possible to unambiguously distinguish black holes, neutron stars, or exotic objects based on the signal shape.
ligo.fun_fact: The signal from the first detected gravitational wave lasted only 0.2 seconds, but carried energy equivalent to three solar masses converted into spacetime tremors.

formulas.description: The dimensionless amplitude of the gravitational wave measured by the interferometer is determined through the relative change in arm length.
formulas.meaning: h — dimensionless strain amplitude; ΔL — change in arm length; L — original arm length (4 km for LIGO).

phase_space.name: phase space
phase_space.description_popular: Phase space is a map of all possible states of a system: where each object is and how fast it is moving. For a pendulum, it is a circle: height and speed always change together, tracing a closed loop.
phase_space.fun_fact_popular: The phase space of a single particle has six dimensions (three coordinates and three velocities), and a dust speck dancing in a light beam already wanders in a twelve-dimensional world.
phase_space.description_simple: Like a weather map where each point is a combination of temperature and pressure, phase space encompasses all conceivable positions and momenta of a system. The motion of planets, the oscillations of molecules—all are paths in a multidimensional landscape.
phase_space.history_simple: The concept evolved in the works of Hamilton and Jacobi in the 19th century, but Henri Poincaré gave it real depth while studying the stability of the Solar System. He was the first to glimpse the chaotic intertwining of trajectories in phase space.
phase_space.how_it_works_simple: The trajectory of a system in phase space never crosses itself—like a thread laid without knots. This fundamental property, discovered by Liouville, means that clouds of states change shape but not volume, like kneading dough.
phase_space.description: Phase space is a multidimensional symplectic manifold whose points correspond one-to-one to all possible micro- or macrostates of a classical (or quantum in the limit) dynamical system. It is parameterized by generalized coordinates and their conjugate canonical momenta; dimension 2n for a system with n degrees of freedom. The Hamiltonian flow determines evolution, and Poincaré sections reveal chaos.
phase_space.history: The geometric interpretation of mechanics goes back to Lagrange and Hamilton (early 19th century). The concept of phase space was formalized by Liouville (1838), who proved the theorem on conservation of phase volume. Henri Poincaré (1890s) applied it to the three-body problem and discovered homoclinic tangles—the seed of chaos theory. In the 20th century, the Krylov–Bogolyubov school and Kolmogorov–Arnold–Moser developed ergodic theory and KAM theory.
phase_space.how_it_works: The Hamiltonian vector field is generated by the Poisson bracket with the scalar Hamiltonian H(q,p). Liouville's theorem (a direct consequence of Hamilton's equations) asserts the incompressibility of the phase flow: ∂ρ/∂t + {ρ,H} = 0, where ρ is the probability density. Boltzmann's ergodic hypothesis assumes that the trajectory eventually covers the energy surface uniformly, justifying the replacement of time averages with phase averages in statistical mechanics.
key_problems: Quantum phase space: due to the uncertainty principle, the concept of a point in phase space has no exact analogue, and the quasi-probability representation (Wigner function) can take negative values.
Ergodicity and chaos: a rigorous proof of ergodicity for physically realistic systems with long-range forces (gravity, plasma) remains open.
Many-body quantum tomography: reconstructing the high-dimensional phase distribution of entangled states becomes exponentially complex with increasing particle number.
phase_space.fun_fact: Due to Poincaré's recurrence theorem, any isolated system will eventually return arbitrarily close to its initial state—though for a gas in a room, the waiting time exceeds the age of the Universe.

formulas.description: Dimension of the phase space of a system with n generalized coordinates.
formulas.meaning: dim(𝒫) is the dimension of the phase space; n is the number of degrees of freedom. For example, for a single particle in three-dimensional space, n=3, the dimension of the phase space is 6.

formulas.description: Liouville's theorem: the divergence of the phase flow is zero, expressing conservation of phase volume.
formulas.meaning: ρ(q,p,t) is the probability density in phase space; q_i, p_i are generalized coordinates and momenta; the dot denotes the time derivative.

partition_function.name: partition function
partition_function.description_popular: The partition function is like a catalog of tickets in an energy lottery: it lists all possible energy states of a system and indicates the chance of each occurring.
partition_function.fun_fact_popular: Knowing just this sum, you can extract from it temperature, pressure, and even how a kettle on the stove will behave—everything that can be measured macroscopically.
partition_function.description_simple: Imagine a universal handbook of all microstates of a gas, where each is given a weight depending on energy and temperature. This handbook—the partition function—is the key that opens the door from atoms to thermodynamics: heat capacities, phase transitions.
partition_function.history_simple: The idea was born with the statistical mechanics of Ludwig Boltzmann in the 1870s, but in its modern form, the canonical sum was introduced by Josiah Willard Gibbs at the turn of the 20th century. Later, the quantum version accounting for particle indistinguishability was developed by Bose, Einstein, Fermi, and Dirac.
partition_function.how_it_works_simple: The summation is done with an exponential factor: the higher the energy of a state, the smaller its contribution—just as a steep climb on a mountain is encountered less often than a gentle slope on a walk. At low temperatures, only the lowest steps are 'populated'.
partition_function.description: The partition function Z is a central quantity in statistical physics, defined as the sum over all possible microstates of a system, weighted by the Boltzmann factor exp(-βE_i) in the canonical ensemble, where β = 1/(k_B T). It connects microscopic properties to thermodynamic potentials: for example, the Helmholtz free energy F = -k_B T ln Z. For quantum systems, the sum is replaced by a trace.
partition_function.history: Boltzmann introduced the concept of statistical weight and entropy in the 1870s, but the explicit formula Z = Σ e^{-βE_i} for the canonical ensemble was introduced by Gibbs in 1902 in his book 'Elementary Principles in Statistical Mechanics'. Einstein and Bose in 1924 generalized it to quantum gases with integer spin, and Fermi and Dirac to half-integer spin, leading to Bose–Einstein and Fermi–Dirac statistics.
partition_function.how_it_works: All thermodynamic quantities are expressed through Z: internal energy U = -∂ ln Z/∂β, heat capacity C_v = ∂U/∂T, pressure P = k_B T (∂ ln Z/∂V)_T. In the quantum case, Z = Tr(e^{-βĤ}), where Ĥ is the Hamiltonian. For non-interacting systems, it factorizes over single-particle states, but in general requires approximate methods.
key_problems: Calculation of Z for a strongly interacting system: no general analytical methods exist, and numerical calculations are exponentially complex.
Thermalization of isolated quantum systems: the applicability of the canonical ensemble to closed systems via the eigenstate thermalization hypothesis (ETH) remains unproven in general.
The role of long-range correlations: in systems with long-range interactions (gravity, plasma), the extensivity of entropy breaks down, and the usual Z does not yield correct thermodynamics.
partition_function.fun_fact: The partition function of water when freezing undergoes a sharp kink, and from its jump one can compute the latent heat of ice formation—the amount of energy the frost extracts from a puddle.

formulas.description: Canonical partition function for a discrete energy spectrum.
formulas.meaning: Z is the partition function; index i runs over all microstates of the system; β = 1/(k_B T), where k_B is the Boltzmann constant (k_B ≈ 1.38×10⁻²³ J/K), T is the absolute temperature in kelvins; E_i is the energy of the i-th microstate.

formulas.description: Connection to the Helmholtz free energy.
formulas.meaning: F is the Helmholtz free energy; k_B is the Boltzmann constant; T is the temperature; Z is the partition function.

feynman_diagram.name: Feynman diagram
feynman_diagram.description_popular: Particles at the quantum level constantly transform into each other. A Feynman diagram is a visual scheme where lines are particles and the points where lines meet are moments of their interaction. Like a traffic diagram for the microworld.
feynman_diagram.fun_fact_popular: Feynman himself drew these diagrams on napkins in cafes. Now, no research in particle physics is complete without them.
feynman_diagram.description_simple: A Feynman diagram is a graphical way to depict particle interactions as lines in space-time. Analogy: ripples on water: if you throw two stones, circles spread out and intersect, creating a pattern. Similarly, particles move and interact, leaving lines.
feynman_diagram.history_simple: Proposed by Richard Feynman in 1948 to simplify calculations in quantum electrodynamics.
feynman_diagram.how_it_works_simple: Each line corresponds to a particle: photons are wavy lines, electrons are straight lines. Vertices where lines meet show interaction. Analogy: like a culinary recipe: arrows show what mixes with what, so a diagram shows which particles collide and what results.
feynman_diagram.description: A Feynman diagram is a graphical representation of terms in the perturbation series of quantum field theory. Each line and vertex corresponds to a mathematical propagator or interaction vertex. Summing all possible diagrams yields the process amplitude. They elegantly combine special relativity and quantum mechanics in intuitively understandable images.
feynman_diagram.history: Introduced by Richard Feynman in 1948 as an alternative to cumbersome S-matrix calculations. The idea grew out of his work on quantum electrodynamics, where he interpreted positrons as electrons moving backward in time. Later, Julian Schwinger and Sin-Itiro Tomonaga independently developed similar methods, but Feynman's approach became the standard. Feynman, Schwinger, and Tomonaga shared the 1965 Nobel Prize. Over time, diagrams spread to all gauge theories, including quantum chromodynamics and electroweak theory.
feynman_diagram.how_it_works: In quantum electrodynamics, the electron line is represented by the propagator i(γ^μ p_μ + m)/(p^2 - m^2 + iε), the photon line by -i g_μν/(k^2 + iε). The interaction vertex gives a factor -i e γ^μ. Integration over all internal momenta yields the diagram's contribution to the amplitude. Feynman rules allow automatically accounting for Fermi statistics through diagram signs. The superposition principle and gauge invariance ensure the renormalizability of the theory, where infinities cancel when defining physical parameters.
key_problems: Exact summation of an infinite number of diagrams in the non-perturbative regime
Inclusion of gravity without breaking renormalizability
Application in strongly coupled systems, such as quantum chromodynamics at low energies
feynman_diagram.fun_fact: Feynman received the Nobel Prize for developing quantum electrodynamics, largely thanks to these diagrams, which turned the most complex integrals into simple pictures.

formulas.description: Electron propagator in momentum representation
formulas.meaning: S(p) is the electron propagator; i is the imaginary unit; γ^μ are the Dirac gamma matrices; p_μ is the electron's four-momentum; m is the electron mass (m ≈ 9.109×10⁻³¹ kg); p^2 is the square of the four-momentum; ε is an infinitesimal positive quantity ensuring causality.

homotopy.name: homotopy
homotopy.description_popular: Homotopy is like a smooth deformation: turning a cup into a doughnut without tearing. Two objects are homotopic if one can be smoothly transformed into the other without gluing or cutting. It is a branch of mathematics that studies such elusive shapes.
homotopy.fun_fact_popular: Mathematicians joke that from the perspective of topology, a cup with a handle is the same as a doughnut. And homotopy helps rigorously prove such transformations!
homotopy.description_simple: Homotopy is a continuous deformation of one mapping into another. Analogy: imagine a rope can twist and change shape but won't break. If one rope can be rearranged into another without breaking, they are homotopic.
homotopy.history_simple: The concept was introduced by Henri Poincaré in the late 19th century in his works on qualitative geometry. He applied it to classify surfaces.
homotopy.how_it_works_simple: Two loops on a surface are homotopic if one can be shrunk to the other without leaving the surface. Analogy: like a rubber band on a globe: can it be shrunk to a point without slipping off the sphere.
homotopy.description: Homotopy is a fundamental concept in algebraic topology that studies continuous deformations of mappings. Two continuous maps f,g: X→Y are called homotopic if there exists a continuous map H: X×[0,1]→Y such that H(x,0)=f(x) and H(x,1)=g(x). This allows constructing homotopy groups, invariants that characterize the topological structure of a space. Homotopy equivalence generalizes homeomorphism, allowing compression and stretching by continuous deformations.
homotopy.history: The origins of the concept go back to Henri Poincaré's 1895 work on analysis situs, where he defined the fundamental group. Further development is associated with Emmy Noether, who contributed to the algebraization of topology, and L.E.J. Brouwer, who refined the concept. By the 1930s, Witold Hurewicz defined higher homotopy groups. In the second half of the 20th century, homotopy type theory, proposed by Voevodsky, unified homotopy with logic and computer science, gaining recognition in the 2010s.
homotopy.how_it_works: The fundamental group π₁(X) consists of homotopy classes of loops with composition operation. Higher homotopy groups π_n(X) generalize to spheres S^n. Homotopy groups are computed using exact sequences, spectral sequences, and the Hurewicz theorem. For example, π₃(S²)=ℤ is generated by the Hopf map. Homotopy invariance is connected to categorical properties; Quillen's model category theory formalizes homotopy structures. The principle of superposition and continuity underlies deformations, and Noether's theorem on symmetries is reflected in invariants.
key_problems: Computation of homotopy groups of spheres in general
Blake–Sutherland conjecture on homotopy groups of products
Resolution of problems in homotopy type theory, such as the univalence axiom
homotopy.fun_fact: Homotopy groups of spheres are one of the greatest unsolved problems in topology. For example, mathematicians still do not know all homotopy groups of spheres of dimension higher than 2.

formulas.description: Condition for homotopy of maps
formulas.meaning: H is the homotopy between maps f and g; X is a topological space; [0,1] is the unit interval (deformation time); Y is a topological space; f and g are continuous maps from X to Y; x is a point in X

isotope.name: isotope
isotope.description_popular: Isotopes are 'twin brothers' of chemical elements. They have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons, so they behave almost identically in chemistry but may have different masses. For example, all carbon atoms have 6 protons, but carbon-12 and carbon-14 are isotopes with 6 and 8 neutrons, respectively.
isotope.fun_fact_popular: Carbon-14 helps archaeologists determine the age of ancient finds. It is radioactive and gradually decays, so by measuring how much remains, one can find out when the organism died.
isotope.description_simple: Isotopes are varieties of atoms of the same chemical element with the same nuclear charge but different mass. Analogy: like different sorts of apples: all apples are similar and equally useful, but some are larger, others smaller. So are hydrogen isotopes: protium is light, deuterium is twice as heavy, and tritium is even heavier.
isotope.history_simple: The term 'isotope' was proposed by Frederick Soddy in 1913. He noticed that radioactive elements could occupy the same place in the periodic table but have different atomic masses.
isotope.how_it_works_simple: The properties of isotopes are determined by the number of neutrons. In nature, most elements are a mixture of stable isotopes, but there are also radioactive ones. Analogy: like in a family, children may be of the same height but different weights—they have similar character (chemical properties) but different physical parameters.
isotope.description: Isotopes are atoms with the same atomic number Z (number of protons) but different mass number A (sum of protons and neutrons). Nuclides are denoted as ᴬZX. Isotopes of the same element have identical electron shells, which accounts for their chemical identity, but differ in nuclear properties: stability, spin, magnetic moment. The isotopic composition of the Earth reflects nucleosynthesis processes in stars and subsequent evolution.
isotope.history: After the discovery of radioactivity by Becquerel in 1896, Ernest Rutherford and Frederick Soddy showed in 1902 that radioactive decay is accompanied by the transmutation of elements. In 1913, Soddy introduced the term 'isotope' for chemically indistinguishable radioelements with different masses. That same year, J.J. Thomson discovered neon isotopes of masses 20 and 22. The discovery of the neutron by James Chadwick in 1932 explained the existence of isotopes. Today, the study of isotopes underlies a multitude of methods: from radiocarbon dating to nuclear medicine.
isotope.how_it_works: The difference in neutron number leads to a difference in nuclear binding energy, which determines stability. According to the liquid drop model and the Weizsäcker formula, the binding energy depends on Z and A. Radioactive isotopes decay according to the radioactive decay law N(t)=N₀e^{-λt}. Mass spectroscopy allows separating isotopes by mass-to-charge ratio. Quantum effects such as tunneling explain α-decay. The Pauli principle governs the filling of nuclear shells, and neutron resonances play a role in neutron capture.
key_problems: Prediction of stability limits of superheavy isotopes (island of stability)
Measurement of isotope lifetimes with high precision for astrophysics
Understanding nucleosynthesis mechanisms in extreme astrophysical conditions
Creation of isotopes with exotic proton-to-neutron ratios
isotope.fun_fact: Deuterium and tritium—isotopes of hydrogen—are used for thermonuclear fusion. If we mastered controlled fusion, ocean water would become an inexhaustible energy source, as it contains plenty of deuterium.

formulas.description: Isotope notation
formulas.meaning: X is the chemical element symbol; A is the mass number (sum of protons and neutrons); Z is the atomic number (number of protons)

formulas.description: Radioactive decay law
formulas.meaning: N(t) is the number of atoms at time t; N₀ is the initial number of atoms; λ is the decay constant (characterizes the probability of decay per unit time); t is time

quantum_superposition.name: Quantum Superposition
quantum_superposition.description_popular: A falling raindrop can land at any point in a puddle. While it's falling, its position is a superposition of all points in the puddle. Only the impact with the water determines the specific spot.
quantum_superposition.fun_fact_popular: Schrödinger's cat is the most famous example of superposition: in the thought experiment, it is both alive and dead until someone looks inside the box.
quantum_superposition.description_simple: An electron in an atom does not orbit the nucleus like a planet but exists as a probability cloud smeared across multiple orbitals simultaneously. This quantum superposition is not a mixture but the parallel existence of mutually exclusive states.
quantum_superposition.history_simple: Erwin Schrödinger established the concept of superposition in 1926 in his wave mechanics. Louis de Broglie had earlier suggested that particles behave as waves, paving the way.
quantum_superposition.how_it_works_simple: If you throw a stone into water, ripples spread in all directions. A quantum particle behaves similarly: its wave function is a collection of ripples, and superposition allows it to interfere with itself, like intersecting waves.
quantum_superposition.description: Quantum superposition is a fundamental principle of quantum mechanics, according to which a physical system can exist in a linear combination of several quantum states. Mathematically, this is described as a sum of basis states with complex weights. The state of the system before measurement is a vector in Hilbert space, and its evolution follows the Schrödinger equation. Upon measurement, it collapses into one of the basis states with a probability equal to the square of the modulus of the corresponding coefficient.
quantum_superposition.history: Louis de Broglie proposed the hypothesis of wave properties of matter in 1924, which became a prerequisite. Erwin Schrödinger in 1926 developed wave mechanics and introduced the equation named after him, where superposition of solutions is natural. Max Born proposed the probabilistic interpretation of the wave function, explaining the physical meaning of superposition. Later, John Stewart Bell showed that superposition leads to nonlocal correlations, experimentally verified by Alain Aspect.
quantum_superposition.how_it_works: The state of the system is described by the vector |ψ⟩ = Σ c_i |u_i⟩ in Hilbert space. The time evolution is given by the Schrödinger equation: iħ ∂|ψ⟩/∂t = H|ψ⟩. When measuring a physical quantity corresponding to the operator A with eigenvectors |a_i⟩, the probability of obtaining the value a_i is |⟨a_i|ψ⟩|². Superposition manifests in interference effects, for example in the double-slit experiment, where a particle passes through both slits simultaneously.
key_problems: Why does quantum superposition break down upon measurement?
How to describe macroscopic superposition (Schrödinger's cat paradox)?
Does objective wave function collapse exist?
quantum_superposition.fun_fact: Superposition is used in quantum computers: a qubit can be 0 and 1 simultaneously, providing exponential growth in computational power.

formulas.description: Quantum superposition in Hilbert space
formulas.meaning: |ψ⟩ — quantum state of the system, c_i — complex probability amplitudes, |u_i⟩ — orthonormal basis of states, sum over all possible states i

quantum_field.name: Quantum Field
quantum_field.description_popular: Wherever there seems to be emptiness, invisible life seethes. The quantum field is an ocean in which every particle is a ripple on its surface. Photons are light waves on the electromagnetic field, electrons are waves on the electron field.
quantum_field.fun_fact_popular: Even in absolute vacuum, pairs of particles and antiparticles are constantly born and disappear, like bubbles in soda.
quantum_field.description_simple: Imagine an orchestra where each instrument is a fundamental field: the violin is the electromagnetic field, the cello is the electron field. Particles are the notes that sound when these fields are excited. All matter is the music of fields.
quantum_field.history_simple: The idea of the field dates back to Michael Faraday and James Clerk Maxwell in the 19th century, but it was given a quantum form in the 20th century. Wolfgang Pauli and Satyendra Nath Bose laid the foundations of statistics governing fields, and Emmy Noether connected field symmetries with conservation laws.
quantum_field.how_it_works_simple: Throw a stone into a pond and circles will spread. The quantum field is like the surface of water: the circles are particles, and their intensity is the number of particles. Fields permeate space, and their oscillations create everything we see.
quantum_field.description: The quantum field is a fundamental concept of quantum field theory, uniting quantum mechanics and special relativity. Instead of particles as point objects, the basis of everything are fields distributed in spacetime. Excitations of these fields are interpreted as particles, and interactions as an exchange of field quanta. Field quantization leads to the idea of particle creation and annihilation operators. Each type of particle has its own field: electron, quark, gluon, photon, etc.
quantum_field.history: Maxwell's classical field theory (1860s) became the forerunner. In the 1920s–1930s, Dirac, Heisenberg, and Pauli developed quantum electrodynamics. Emmy Noether in 1918 proved a theorem linking field symmetries and conservation laws. Satyendra Nath Bose and Albert Einstein created statistics for bosonic fields in 1924. Wolfgang Pauli formulated the exclusion principle for fermionic fields and the spin-statistics theorem.
quantum_field.how_it_works: The field is described by a Lagrangian, from which the equations of motion are derived via the Euler-Lagrange equations (for example, the Dirac equation for fermions). Quantization occurs by imposing canonical commutation or anticommutation relations: [φ(x), π(y)] = iħδ³(x-y) for bosons, {ψ(x), ψ†(y)} = δ³(x-y) for fermions. The creation and annihilation operators in the field expansion act on Fock space. Interactions are introduced through gauge invariance. At high energies, quantum fields exhibit renormalizability.
key_problems: Unification of quantum field theory with gravity (quantum gravity)
The hierarchy problem
The strong CP problem in quantum chromodynamics
quantum_field.fun_fact: There is the Higgs field, which fills the Universe like molasses and gives particles mass. Without it, electrons would travel at the speed of light.

formulas.description: Expansion of a free scalar field in modes
formulas.meaning: φ(x) — quantized field operator at the spacetime point x = (t, x); a_p and a_p^† — annihilation and creation operators for a particle with momentum p; E_p = √(p^2 + m^2) — energy of a particle with mass m, where p = |p|; integration over all three-dimensional momenta p

wave_function_collapse.name: Wave Function Collapse
wave_function_collapse.description_popular: When a particle is measured, it is as if it suddenly realizes it is being observed and stops playing tricks, choosing one specific state. This instantaneous choice is the wave function collapse.
wave_function_collapse.fun_fact_popular: If you measure identical particles many times, the distribution of results will match the probabilities predicted by the wave function before collapse.
wave_function_collapse.description_simple: The wave function is a cloud of possibilities. When measured, it collapses to a single point, like a soap bubble that pops, leaving a single drop. Collapse is the transformation of the probable into the actual.
wave_function_collapse.history_simple: Erwin Schrödinger introduced the wave function in 1926 but did not explain the measurement mechanism. Max Born proposed a probabilistic interpretation, and John von Neumann formalized collapse as a projection. However, the nature of collapse is still debated.
wave_function_collapse.how_it_works_simple: Like a die in a cup: while it's in the cup, its face is unknown (superposition of all faces). Collapse happens at the moment it lands on the table—a specific number comes up. Only in the quantum world, the die can land on several tables at once.
wave_function_collapse.description: Wave function collapse is a process by which a quantum superposition is reduced to a single definite state as a result of measurement. Mathematically, it is described as the projection of the state vector onto the eigensubspace of the measured observable. Collapse occurs instantaneously and irreversibly, breaking the unitary evolution given by the Schrödinger equation. This is one of the central problems in the interpretation of quantum mechanics.
wave_function_collapse.history: Erwin Schrödinger introduced the wave function in 1926. Max Born at the same time gave a probabilistic interpretation, but the measurement mechanism remained a mystery. John von Neumann in 1932 introduced the projection postulate (collapse) as a separate process, distinct from unitary evolution. John Stewart Bell in 1964 showed that any hidden-variable theory reproducing quantum predictions must be nonlocal, which was experimentally confirmed by Alain Aspect in the 1980s. The question of whether collapse is a physical process or merely an update of knowledge remains open.
wave_function_collapse.how_it_works: According to the measurement postulate (Born's rule), if a system is in superposition |ψ⟩ = Σ c_i |φ_i⟩, and observable A with eigenvectors |φ_i⟩ is measured, then the probability of outcome i is |⟨φ_i|ψ⟩|² = |c_i|². As a result of measurement, the state collapses to |φ_i⟩. This process is described by the projection operator P_i = |φ_i⟩⟨φ_i|. Mathematically, collapse represents an irreversible transformation that turns the superposition into a statistical mixture described by the density matrix ρ = Σ |c_i|² |φ_i⟩⟨φ_i|. In dynamical collapse models (e.g., Ghirardi-Rimini-Weber), nonlinear stochastic terms are added to the Schrödinger equation so that collapse occurs objectively.
key_problems: What is the physical nature of collapse?
Does objective collapse exist or is it a result of interaction with the environment (decoherence)?
Can collapse be avoided in the many-worlds interpretation?
How to include collapse in relativistic quantum theory?
wave_function_collapse.fun_fact: There is an interpretation of quantum mechanics in which there is no collapse at all, and the universe splits into many worlds at each measurement.

formulas.description: Probability of collapse upon measurement (Born's rule)
formulas.meaning: P_i — probability of obtaining result i upon measurement; |ψ⟩ — initial state vector of the system; |φ_i⟩ — normalized eigenvector of the measured observable corresponding to value i; ⟨φ_i|ψ⟩ — inner product in Hilbert space

quantum_teleportation.name: Quantum Teleportation
quantum_teleportation.description_popular: Quantum teleportation is the transfer of a quantum state from one particle to another without moving the particle itself. The original state is destroyed in the process, as if a 'Xerox' burns the original. Not the light itself is transmitted, but the instructions for its assembly.
quantum_teleportation.fun_fact_popular: Even the state of a whole atom can be teleported, but the current record is a few hundred meters via optical fiber.
quantum_teleportation.description_simple: Quantum teleportation works on the 'twin' principle: two partner particles (entangled) feel each other at a distance. If a measurement is performed on one, the other instantly takes on the specified state, but this requires a classical signal that travels no faster than light.
quantum_teleportation.history_simple: The idea was proposed in 1993 by Charles Bennett and colleagues. Theoretically, it relied on the Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen paradox and the work of John Bell. The first experiment was conducted in 1997 by Anton Zeilinger and his team at the University of Innsbruck.
quantum_teleportation.how_it_works_simple: Two friends have 'magic' coins that always land opposite. To transmit your coin, you compare it with one of the pair and report the result over the phone. The friend turns their coin into a copy, and your original loses its special properties.
quantum_teleportation.description: Quantum teleportation is a process of transferring a quantum state over a distance using a separated entangled pair and a classical communication channel. It does not imply the transfer of matter or energy in the manner of science fiction 'transporters'. It is based on nonlocality, the no-cloning theorem, and the Bell protocol. State A is sent to B via a Bell measurement on A and one part of the entangled pair; the result is transmitted over a classical channel, after which B applies the corresponding unitary transformation to their particle, restoring the original state. The original particle irrevocably loses its state.
quantum_teleportation.history: The concept was proposed in 1993 by Charles Bennett, Gilles Brassard, Claude Crépeau, Richard Jozsa, Asher Peres, and William Wootters. The theoretical foundations were the works of Erwin Schrödinger, who coined the term 'entanglement', and John Bell, who proved the inequality confirming nonlocality. The first experimental demonstration with photons was carried out in 1997 by Anton Zeilinger's group (Innsbruck) and independently by Francesco De Martini's team (Rome). Alain Aspect in the 1980s performed crucial tests of Bell's inequalities, strengthening confidence in nonlocality.
quantum_teleportation.how_it_works: The protocol relies on the principle of superposition and the effect of entanglement. Sender and receiver use a pair of maximally entangled qubits in a Bell state. The sender's measurement in the Bell basis on their half of the pair and the teleporting qubit projects the system onto one of the four Bell states, destroying the original qubit. The result (two classical bits) is transmitted over a normal channel. The receiver applies one of four unitary transformations (identity, X, Z, or XZ) depending on the received bits, and their qubit takes on the original state. The process is consistent with the theory of relativity, since the classical signal is limited by the speed of light. The no-cloning theorem guarantees transfer, not copying of information. Initial state |ψ⟩ = α|0⟩ + β|1⟩, entangled pair — e.g., |Φ+⟩ = (|00⟩+|11⟩)/√2. The total system: |ψ⟩⊗|Φ+⟩. After the receiver's measurement, their qubit collapses to a state related to |ψ⟩ by a unitary operation depending on the outcome.
key_problems: Long-distance teleportation over great distances due to decoherence in optical fiber or the atmosphere
Creation of reliable quantum repeaters for long-range action
Teleportation of massive particles and complex states
Integration into scalable quantum networks
quantum_teleportation.fun_fact: Teleportation does not violate the Heisenberg uncertainty principle because the measured particle loses its original state, and the copy is created indirectly.

formulas.description: State of the teleporting qubit
formulas.meaning: |ψ⟩ — state vector of the qubit, α and β — complex probability amplitudes, |α|² + |β|² = 1; |0⟩ and |1⟩ — basis states

formulas.description: Bell state (entangled pair)
formulas.meaning: |Φ+⟩ — one of the four maximally entangled states; 1/√2 — normalization factor; |00⟩, |11⟩ — tensor products of two-qubit states

formulas.description: Measurement in the Bell basis
formulas.meaning: Complete orthonormal basis of four Bell states defining projective measurement on two qubits

ammonia.name: Ammonia
ammonia.description_popular: Ammonia is a pungent gas that smells of 'sal ammoniac' in everyday life. In space, complex molecules are built from it, and on Earth it is needed for fertilizers. Its molecule resembles a pyramid of nitrogen with three hydrogens on top.
ammonia.fun_fact_popular: In Jupiter's clouds, ammonia creates brown and white bands.
ammonia.description_simple: Ammonia is a colorless gas with a sharp odor, consisting of one nitrogen atom and three hydrogens (NH₃). In interstellar clouds, it serves as a marker of dense cold regions where stars form.
ammonia.history_simple: Ammonia in pure form was obtained by Joseph Priestley in 1774, and its composition was deciphered by Claude Berthollet in 1785. In astronomy, it was first detected in 1968 in the interstellar medium via radio emission at a wavelength of 1.3 cm.
ammonia.how_it_works_simple: The ammonia molecule is a triangular pyramid. The nitrogen sits at the top, and the three hydrogens are at the base. This shape makes the molecule slightly 'flip', emitting radio waves of a specific frequency—astronomers use these 'flips' to find ammonia in space.
ammonia.description: Ammonia (NH₃) is an inorganic compound, a colorless gas with a characteristic pungent odor. The molecule has a pyramidal structure (point group C₃v), the nitrogen atom is sp³ hybridized, one of the hybrid orbitals is occupied by a lone pair of electrons. Liquid ammonia is a good ionizing solvent. In space, ammonia is an important component of interstellar ice and gas, participating in the synthesis of amino acids and other prebiotic molecules. It is observed in molecular clouds, comets, and the atmospheres of giant planets. Industrially, it is synthesized by the Haber-Bosch method at high pressure and temperature.
ammonia.history: Although ammonia was known to the ancient Egyptians in the form of sal ammoniac (NH₄Cl) from camel dung, gaseous ammonia was first isolated by Joseph Priestley in 1774, who called it 'alkaline air'. Its composition was determined by Claude Berthollet in 1785. In astrophysics, the first reliable detection of interstellar ammonia was made in 1968 by the group of Cheung and Welch via the inversion transition at 23.7 GHz in the direction of the Galactic center. This discovery confirmed the existence of dense molecular clouds. The spectroscopic parameters of ammonia were later refined in laboratory experiments.
ammonia.how_it_works: Ammonia exhibits the quantum effect of inversion—tunneling of the nitrogen atom through the plane of the three hydrogens. The potential curve of the molecule has two minima separated by a barrier of ~2020 cm⁻¹. The tunneling splitting frequency of the ground vibrational state is 23.7 GHz (1.26 cm), which is used in astronomical observations. Inversion transitions are sensitive to gas temperature and density, making ammonia a thermometer for molecular clouds. In the interstellar medium, ammonia is formed mainly on the surface of dust grains by sequential hydrogenation of nitrogen. In planetary atmospheres, it participates in cycles, forming clouds (on Jupiter and Saturn) and contributing to coloration. Symmetric and asymmetric bending modes lie in the infrared region (950–1100 cm⁻¹). For astrophysics, rotational and inversion-rotational transitions in the microwave range are important. Spectroscopically, ammonia is described by quantum numbers J, K, where K is the projection of the angular momentum on the symmetry axis.
key_problems: Formation of ammonia in the diffuse interstellar medium (gas-phase reactions are too slow)
The role of ammonia in prebiotic chemistry as a source of nitrogen
Detailed spectroscopy of highly excited states for exoplanetary atmospheres
ammonia.fun_fact: Ammonia is one of the few compounds whose maser emission in space is more powerful than that of water.

formulas.description: Energy of the inversion transition
formulas.meaning: ΔE_inv — energy difference between the two lowest inversion levels, h — Planck's constant (h ≈ 6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s), ν_inv — frequency of the inversion transition (ν_inv ≈ 23.7 GHz for the ground state)

formulas.description: Frequency of a rotational transition (linear approximation)
formulas.meaning: ν — transition frequency, B — rotational constant (B = h/(8π²I) for NH₃), J — rotational quantum number of the upper level; I — moment of inertia of the molecule about an axis perpendicular to the symmetry axis

formulas.description: Abundance of ammonia in the interstellar medium
formulas.meaning: X(NH₃) — relative abundance of ammonia, N(NH₃) — column density of NH₃ molecules, N(H₂) — column density of hydrogen molecules

phonon.name: phonon
phonon.description_popular: In solids, atoms are not frozen in place but constantly oscillate. A phonon is the quantum of these vibrations, like a drop of water in a wave.
phonon.fun_fact_popular: Phonons behave like particles: they can collide, reflect, and transfer energy, like tiny invisible balls.
phonon.description_simple: A phonon is a collective excitation of atoms in a crystal lattice, manifesting as a quantum of sound wave. It resembles a wave traveling across a field of grain: each stalk tilts only slightly, but the wave is transmitted over long distances.
phonon.history_simple: The concept of quantized lattice vibrations was introduced by Soviet physicist Yakov Frenkel in 1932; the term "phonon" was proposed by Igor Tamm.
phonon.how_it_works_simple: Phonons carry heat and sound in solids. When you heat one end of a metal spoon, heat spreads via phonons—they pass energy from atom to atom like a relay baton.
phonon.description: A phonon is a quasiparticle, representing a quantum of vibrational excitation of the crystal lattice. In quantum mechanics, elastic vibrations of atoms in a solid are quantized, analogous to how a photon is a quantum of the electromagnetic field. Phonons obey Bose–Einstein statistics because they have integer spin 0 or 1. Acoustic and optical phonons are distinguished depending on the type of vibration: in the former, atoms move in phase; in the latter, in antiphase. Phonons play a key role in the heat capacity of solids, thermal conductivity, and electron scattering, determining the electrical resistance of materials.
phonon.history: The quantization of lattice vibrations dates back to the work of Albert Einstein (1907) and Peter Debye (1912) on the heat capacity of solids. The term "phonon" was coined by Igor Tamm in 1932, and the theoretical foundation was laid by Yakov Frenkel. Since phonons obey Bose–Einstein statistics, an important contribution belongs to Satyendra Nath Bose, who developed quantum statistics for identical particles.
phonon.how_it_works: In the harmonic approximation, the crystal lattice is considered as a system of coupled oscillators. After quantization, the normal modes of vibration are represented by independent harmonic oscillators with quantized energy: E_n = \hbar \omega (n + 1/2). An excitation with energy \hbar \omega is interpreted as a phonon.
