accretion_disk.practical_application: Accretion disks are the main energy sources of quasars and X-ray radiation from binary systems with black holes and neutron stars. Studying them allows weighing black holes and investigating strong gravity.
accretion_disk.description_popular: An accretion disk is a flattened rotating structure of gas and plasma that forms when matter flows toward a massive compact object: a black hole, neutron star, or white dwarf. Due to conservation of angular momentum, the gas does not fall straight in but enters a circular orbit and slowly drifts inward while viscous forces (e.g., magnetorotational instability) redistribute angular momentum outward. The inner edge of the disk is determined by the last stable circular orbit, which for a non-rotating black hole is three gravitational radii. Closer to the center, temperatures soar to tens of millions of degrees, and the disk emits hard X-ray and gamma radiation.
accretion_disk.fun_fact_popular: The accretion disk of a supermassive black hole can outshine all the stars of its host galaxy, while the hole itself remains completely dark.
accretion_disk.description_simple: When swirling gas falls onto a compact object like a black hole, it flattens into a thin rotating disk—a sort of cosmic 'pancake'. The inner regions move faster than the outer ones: just as runners on the inner lane of a stadium outpace those on the outer lane. Friction between adjacent layers heats the gas to millions of degrees, making the disk glow brightly.
accretion_disk.history_simple: The foundations of disk theory were laid in the mid-20th century. The key 'α-disk' model was proposed by N. I. Shakura and R. A. Sunyaev in 1973. Even earlier, in 1916, Karl Schwarzschild found the solution to Einstein's equations for a non-rotating black hole, and Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar in the 1930s determined the limiting mass of white dwarfs. In the 1960s, the discovery of quasars showed that accretion onto supermassive black holes explains their enormous luminosity.
accretion_disk.how_it_works_simple: Any cloud of gas has at least a slight intrinsic rotation. Falling toward the center, it conserves angular momentum (rotational impulse) and thus flattens into a disk. Then viscous friction plays a key role: it transfers angular momentum from the inner layers to the outer ones, allowing gas to move inward. The gravitational energy released in the infall is converted into heat and radiation, which we observe.
accretion_disk.description: An accretion disk is a thin gas-dynamic structure in which matter rotates around a central gravitating body in nearly circular orbits. Equilibrium condition: radially, centrifugal force balances gravity; vertically, the pressure gradient. Viscosity, often sourced by magnetorotational instability, ensures outward transport of angular momentum and heating of the gas. In the classical model (α-disk by Shakura and Sunyaev), the viscous stress is parameterized as α times the pressure. The radiation spectrum is close to a multicolor blackbody with an effective temperature increasing toward the center. In strong gravitational fields, the inner edge corresponds to the innermost stable circular orbit (ISCO): about 6GM/c² for a non-rotating black hole and as low as 1.2GM/c² for an extremely rotating one.
accretion_disk.history: The theory of disk accretion actively developed from the 1960s. The α-disk model, which became a standard, was published by N. I. Shakura and R. A. Sunyaev in 1973. The discovery of quasars (Maarten Schmidt, 1963) pointed to accretion as the source of their incredible luminosity. X-ray binary systems (e.g., Cygnus X-1) became natural laboratories for testing the theory. Modern instruments—Hubble, Chandra, Spektr-RG—continuously refine the picture, and the Event Horizon Telescope in 2019 directly imaged for the first time the disk around the supermassive black hole in the center of M87.
accretion_disk.how_it_works: In a steady state, the disk structure is determined by the balance of gravity, pressure, and rotation. For a Keplerian disk, the angular velocity Ω = √(GM/r³). The radiation flux at radius r: F(r) = (3GMṀ / 8πr³) [1 − √(r_in/r)], where Ṁ is the accretion rate, r_in is the inner radius (ISCO). The total luminosity L = GMṀ / (2r_in). Angular momentum transport is provided by magnetorotational instability (MRI), generating turbulent viscosity. In a strong gravitational field, general relativistic corrections are important: ISCO moves closer to the horizon for rotating holes, increasing energy output. Part of the energy can be extracted from the hole's rotation via the Blandford–Znajek mechanism and focused into relativistic jets.
key_problems: Understanding the viscosity mechanism and angular momentum transport (the role of magnetorotational instability and other processes).
Mechanism of formation and acceleration of relativistic jets.
Stability of radiation-dominated disks: thermal and viscous instability.
Super-Eddington accretion and description of thick disks (slim disks).
Interaction of the disk with the magnetosphere of a neutron star or white dwarf.
accretion_disk.fun_fact: Matter at the inner edge of a disk around a black hole can move at near light speed. Due to the relativistic Doppler effect, the side of the disk facing us shines many times brighter.

formulas.description: Luminosity of a stationary thin accretion disk in the gravitational potential of a central body of mass M
formulas.meaning: L — luminosity (W); G = 6.674×10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg² — gravitational constant; M — mass of the central object (kg); Ṁ — mass accretion rate (kg/s); R_in — inner disk radius (m), e.g., ISCO radius for a black hole or stellar radius.

raw.mini: A cosmic funnel of gas and dust, swirling around a black hole and blazing from friction.
raw.practical_application: Accretion disks power the brightest objects in the Universe—quasars—and are sources of X-ray radiation from black holes and neutron stars.
raw.description_popular: An accretion disk is a rotating structure of gas and plasma that forms when matter falls onto a compact massive object (black hole, neutron star, white dwarf, or young star). Due to angular momentum, the matter cannot fall directly but enters an orbit and gradually approaches the center as angular momentum is transferred outward by viscous processes (e.g., magnetorotational instability). Near a black hole, the disk is truncated at the innermost stable circular orbit (ISCO). The temperature of the inner regions reaches tens of millions of kelvins, generating X-ray and gamma radiation.
raw.fun_fact_popular: An accretion disk around a supermassive black hole can shine brighter than an entire galaxy, although the hole itself is invisible.
raw.description_simple: Gas falling onto a star or black hole, due to its own rotation, turns into a thin rotating pancake—a disk. The inner parts of the disk move faster than the outer ones, like running lanes on a stadium. Friction between layers heats the gas to millions of degrees, and it emits intense radiation.
raw.history_simple: The theory of accretion disks has been developed since the 1940s. In the 1970s, it became clear that the bright centers of some galaxies—quasars—emit energy due to matter falling onto supermassive black holes. Karl Schwarzschild, back in 1916, obtained the solution for black holes, and Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar described the stability limits of stars, laying the foundations for understanding compact objects.
raw.how_it_works_simple: A gas cloud always has a small angular momentum (amount of 'twist'). Gravity pulls it toward the central object, but the law of conservation of angular momentum makes the matter flatten into a disk. Viscous friction transfers angular momentum outward, allowing matter to fall inward. The released gravitational energy turns into heat and radiation.
raw.description: An accretion disk is a thin (in the classical consideration) gas-dynamic structure governed by the laws of mass, momentum, and energy conservation. It forms when the accreting matter has a specific angular momentum, so that centrifugal force in the plane of rotation balances gravity, and in the vertical direction—the pressure gradient. Viscosity, usually attributed to magnetohydrodynamic turbulence (magnetorotational instability), provides radial transport of angular momentum and heating. The classical α-disk model (Shakura and Sunyaev, 1973) parameterizes the viscous stress as α times the pressure. The disk spectrum is close to a multitemperature blackbody. The inner boundary for black holes is the ISCO (radius 6GM/c² for a non-rotating hole), for neutron stars—magnetic or surface. Accretion disks can generate collimated jets.
raw.history: Key theoretical works on disk accretion were carried out in the 1960s–70s. The α-disk model was proposed by Nikolai Shakura and Rashid Sunyaev in 1973. The discovery of quasars in the 1960s (Maarten Schmidt) provided observational impetus: extreme luminosities required accretion onto supermassive black holes. X-ray binary systems, discovered in the 1960s–70s, became a laboratory for studying disks around stellar masses. Space telescopes Hubble, Chandra, and Spektr-RG continue to study disks; the Event Horizon Telescope image (2019) showed for the first time the shadow of black hole M87* against the backdrop of the glowing disk.
raw.how_it_works: The basic equations of a thin disk are derived from hydrodynamics and gravity. For a Keplerian orbit, the angular velocity Ω = (GM/r³)^{1/2}. The radiation flux from a unit surface: F(r) = (3GMṀ / 8πr³) [1 − (r_*/r)^{1/2}], where M is the central mass, Ṁ is the accretion rate, r_* is the inner radius. The total luminosity L = GṀ/2R_in for steady accretion. A key role is played by magnetorotational instability (MRI), which provides turbulent viscosity and angular momentum transport. In a strong gravitational field, general relativistic effects are important: ISCO decreases for rotating holes (down to r ≈ GM/c² at extreme rotation), increasing energy release. Part of the energy can be extracted from the hole's rotation and channeled into relativistic jets (Blandford–Znajek mechanism).
key_problems: Nature of viscosity and angular momentum transport (MRI mechanisms, dissipation)
Formation and collimation of jets by accretion disks
Radiation-dominated disks and thermal/viscous instability
Super-Eddington accretion and thick disks (slim disks)
Interaction of the disk with the magnetosphere of the central object
raw.fun_fact: Some accretion disks around black holes rotate at nearly light speed, causing extreme Doppler boosting of radiation.

reionization.name: Reionization
reionization.mini: Cosmic dawn: the first stars ignited, their ultraviolet light dispersed the dark hydrogen fog, and the cosmos became transparent. This explains why intergalactic gas is now a hot plasma.
reionization.practical_application: Reionization marks the era of the first stars and galaxies. Studying it helps refine the timeline of the early Universe and understand which objects were the main sources of ionizing radiation.
reionization.description_popular: Reionization is the last global transition in the history of the Universe, stretching from redshifts z ≈ 15 to z ≈ 6 (about 400–1000 million years after the Big Bang). Starting in a completely neutral medium left over from the recombination epoch (z ~ 1100), the process was driven by ultraviolet radiation from the first light sources—massive Population III stars, dwarf galaxies, and possibly accreting black holes. Ionized bubbles, growing and merging, filled almost the entire volume by z ≈ 6. The main evidence for reionization comes from polarization data of the cosmic microwave background (optical depth τ ≈ 0.054) and the nearly complete absorption of quasar radiation beyond redshift 6 (the Gunn–Peterson effect).
reionization.fun_fact_popular: The James Webb Space Telescope can see the most distant galaxies whose light was emitted during the reionization epoch. It is now finding increasingly earlier objects, pushing back the boundary of the known 'dark ages'.
reionization.description_simple: About 380,000 years after the Big Bang, the Universe had cooled enough for protons and electrons to combine into neutral hydrogen. Light could no longer pass through this gas—the 'dark ages' began. Then, hundreds of millions of years later, the first stars and galaxies ignited. Their ultraviolet light ripped electrons away from hydrogen atoms again, turning the gas back into plasma—this process is called reionization. The Universe became transparent again.
reionization.history_simple: Modern ideas about reionization are based on the Big Bang theory (Georges Lemaître, 1927) and the discovery of the cosmic microwave background (Penzias and Wilson, 1965), confirming a hot beginning. In 2001, the Sloan Digital Sky Survey discovered the Gunn–Peterson effect: in the spectra of quasars with redshifts greater than 6, light was completely absorbed by neutral hydrogen. This indicated that reionization ended about 1 billion years after the Big Bang.
reionization.how_it_works_simple: An ultraviolet photon with energy above 13.6 eV can knock an electron out of a hydrogen atom. The first stars emitted many such photons. Around each galaxy, a region of ionized gas formed—a growing bubble. Gradually, the bubbles expanded and merged until they filled the entire Universe, making it transparent to ultraviolet light.
reionization.description: Cosmic reionization is the epochal transition of the intergalactic medium (IGM) from an almost completely neutral state to an ionized one. Ionizing radiation was supplied by the first galaxies and accreting black holes. Key photons are Lyman-continuum (energy > 13.6 eV). An important parameter is the fraction of such photons that can escape their parent galaxy (escape fraction). Reionization was inhomogeneous: in dense regions, it ended later. It is now believed that the main stage ended by z ≈ 6. Observations of the cosmic microwave background provide an independent estimate: Thomson scattering on free electrons creates a characteristic polarization signal and slightly suppresses anisotropy at small angular scales. The integrated optical depth τ = ∫ n_e σ_T dr is about 0.054.
reionization.history: The idea of secondary ionization of the Universe naturally follows from the hot Big Bang theory (G. Lemaître, 1927) and the discovery of the cosmic microwave background (A. Penzias and R. Wilson, 1965). A breakthrough observational result was obtained in 2001: in the spectra of distant quasars (z > 6) from the Sloan survey, the Gunn–Peterson effect was detected—almost complete absorption due to neutral hydrogen. The WMAP (2003) and Planck (2013) missions measured the Thomson optical depth, refining it from 0.17 to ~0.054. Hubble and later James Webb have been directly searching for reionization-era galaxies, gradually refining the picture.
reionization.how_it_works: At the heart is photoionization: H + γ → p + e. The ionization front is initially rarefied, but as density increases and recombinations become important, it becomes dense. Global dynamics are described by the equation for the ionized fraction Q: dQ/dt = (ṅ_ion / ⟨n_H⟩) – Q/t_rec, where ṅ_ion is the emission rate of ionizing photons, ⟨n_H⟩ is the mean hydrogen density, t_rec is the characteristic recombination time. The observed optical depth τ = c σ_T ∫ n_e(z) (1+z)⁻¹ H(z)⁻¹ dz determines the mean-weighted moment of reionization. Radio astronomy at the 21 cm line (projects LOFAR, HERA, Square Kilometre Array) promises to build a detailed 3D map of the process.
key_problems: Identification of the main suppliers of ionizing photons: star-forming galaxies or accreting black holes.
Direct measurement of the Lyman-continuum escape fraction at z > 6.
Reconstruction of the spatiotemporal picture of reionization: when and how ionized bubbles merged.
The role of hypothetical first stars (Population III) and their initial mass function.
Contribution of dwarf galaxies too faint for direct observations.
reionization.fun_fact: Reionization was the last global transformation of matter in the Universe. Since then, large-scale cosmic structure has not changed its phase state, and the Universe has only continued to expand, gradually accelerating.

formulas.description: Optical depth for Thomson scattering of cosmic microwave background photons by free electrons, integrated over redshift
formulas.meaning: τ — optical depth (dimensionless); c = 3×10⁸ m/s — speed of light; σ_T = 6.65×10⁻²⁹ m² — Thomson cross section; n_e(z) — free electron density as a function of redshift; H(z) = H₀√(Ω_m(1+z)³ + Ω_Λ) — Hubble parameter at redshift z, H₀ ≈ 70 km/s/Mpc; z_rec ≈ 1100 — redshift of recombination.

raw.mini: The moment when the first stars dispersed the cosmic fog and made the Universe transparent.
raw.practical_application: Studying reionization allows us to understand when and how the first galaxies ignited and why intergalactic gas is ionized today.
raw.description_popular: Cosmic reionization is the last global phase transition of the Universe, occurring at redshifts from z ~ 15 to z ~ 6 (about 400–1000 million years after the Big Bang). After recombination at z ~ 1100, the Universe was neutral and filled with hydrogen. The first light sources—massive Population III stars, dwarf galaxies, and possibly mini-quasars—emitted hard ultraviolet photons that ionized the gas in growing bubbles. The process ended when the intergalactic medium became almost completely ionized. The main observational evidence is the optical depth for Thomson scattering of cosmic microwave background photons (τ ≈ 0.054) and the Gunn–Peterson effect in quasar spectra at z > 6.
raw.fun_fact_popular: The James Webb Space Telescope was specifically created to peer into the reionization epoch and catch the light of the very first galaxies.
raw.description_simple: 380,000 years after the Big Bang, the Universe cooled enough for protons and electrons to combine into neutral hydrogen, and light stopped propagating—the 'dark ages' began. Hundreds of millions of years later, the first stars and galaxies emitted powerful ultraviolet radiation, which again stripped electrons from atoms (ionized them), turning hydrogen back into plasma. This process is called reionization.
raw.history_simple: The idea that the early Universe underwent secondary ionization arose in the 1960s based on the Big Bang theory developed by Georges Lemaître. The discovery of the cosmic microwave background by Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson in 1965 confirmed a hot beginning. In 2001, the Sloan Digital Sky Survey discovered in the spectra of distant quasars a signature of neutral hydrogen—the 'Gunn–Peterson forest'—which dated the end of reionization to about 1 billion years after the Big Bang.
raw.how_it_works_simple: Ultraviolet photons from the first stars carried more than 13.6 eV of energy—enough to knock an electron out of a hydrogen atom. Around each galaxy, a growing bubble of ionized gas formed. These bubbles expanded, merged, and eventually filled all space, making the intergalactic medium transparent to ultraviolet light.
raw.description: Reionization is the transition of the intergalactic medium (IGM) from a predominantly neutral state after recombination to a fully ionized one under the action of ionizing radiation from the first stars and accreting black holes. The sources are star-forming galaxies where massive stars emit Lyman-continuum photons (E > 13.6 eV). A critical parameter is the escape fraction. The process is inhomogeneous: in dense regions, ionization lags. It is believed that reionization was largely completed by z ≈ 6. The cosmic microwave background bears the imprint of scattering on free electrons: a polarization peak at large angular scales and suppression of small-scale anisotropy. The optical depth τ = ∫ nₑ σₜ dr.
raw.history: Theoretical prerequisites arose after the discovery of the cosmic microwave background (Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson, 1965) and the development of the Big Bang model (Georges Lemaître, 1927). In 2001, the Sloan Digital Sky Survey detected the Gunn–Peterson trough in quasar spectra at z > 6, indicating the end of reionization. The WMAP space observatory (2003–2012) first measured τ ≈ 0.17, later refined by Planck to ~0.054. The Hubble and James Webb telescopes are directly searching for the galaxies likely responsible for reionization.
raw.how_it_works: Ultraviolet photons ionize hydrogen: H + γ → p⁺ + e⁻. The ionization front propagates as a rarefied (R-type) and then dense (D-type) when recombinations in the ionized gas become important. Global history is described by the equation for the ionized volume fraction Q: dQ/dt = (ṅ_ion / ⟨n_H⟩) – Q/t_rec, where ṅ_ion is the ionization rate, ⟨n_H⟩ is the mean hydrogen density, t_rec is the recombination time. The optical depth for the cosmic microwave background: τ = c σₜ ∫ nₑ(z) dz / [(1+z)H(z)], where H(z) = H₀√(Ω_m(1+z)³ + Ω_Λ). The observed τ sets the duration and timing of reionization. Studying the 21-cm line of hydrogen with low-frequency radio telescopes (LOFAR, SKA) should construct a three-dimensional map of the epoch.
key_problems: Identification of the main sources of ionizing photons (galaxies vs quasars)
Measurement of the escape fraction of ionizing photons from galaxies at high redshifts
Construction of a detailed chronology and morphology of reionization
The role of the first stars (Population III) and their initial mass function
Contribution of dwarf galaxies inaccessible to direct observation
raw.fun_fact: The reionization epoch is the last global phase transition the Universe underwent; since then, it has just been expanding at an accelerated rate.

galaxy_cluster.name: galaxy cluster
galaxy_cluster.mini: Galaxy clusters are giant metropolises of the Universe: thousands of galaxies bound by gravity and enveloped in hot gas, like an invisible web.
galaxy_cluster.practical_application: Studying clusters helps to 'weigh' invisible dark matter, test theories of gravity, and understand how the large-scale structure of the cosmos formed.
galaxy_cluster.description_popular: Galaxy clusters are gravitationally bound systems containing hundreds to thousands of galaxies. They are immersed in a vast halo of hot plasma with a temperature of 10–100 million degrees, emitting in the X-ray range. Approximately 85% of the total mass is dark matter, 10–12% is hot gas, and only a few percent are stars. Clusters form at the nodes of the cosmic web—the large-scale structure of matter distribution in the Universe.
galaxy_cluster.fun_fact_popular: The galaxy cluster El Gordo (ACT-CL J0102-4915) is the most massive known, with a mass exceeding 3 quadrillion solar masses.
galaxy_cluster.description_simple: A galaxy cluster is a large number of galaxies located close together and held by gravity. They are the largest stable objects in the Universe. At the center of a cluster there is often a giant elliptical galaxy, and the space between galaxies is filled with superheated gas that glows in X-rays.
galaxy_cluster.history_simple: Astronomers noticed clusters as early as compiling catalogs of nebulae. In the 1930s, Fritz Zwicky measured the velocities of galaxies in the Coma cluster and found that the visible mass was insufficient to keep them from flying apart. Thus arose the hypothesis of dark matter—invisible substance.
galaxy_cluster.how_it_works_simple: Just as water droplets in a cloud coalesce into larger drops, galaxies attract each other and form clusters. Gravity holds them together, but there is not enough visible mass, so additional invisible mass—dark matter—is assumed to exist.
galaxy_cluster.description: A galaxy cluster is the largest gravitationally bound system in the Universe with a mass on the order of 10¹⁴–10¹⁵ solar masses and a size of several megaparsecs. It includes hundreds to thousands of galaxies, the intracluster medium (hot gas with temperatures of tens of millions of kelvins, emitting in X-rays), and dark matter, which dominates and manifests through gravitational lensing. The distribution and evolution of clusters depend on cosmological parameters such as the matter density Ω_m and the fluctuation amplitude σ_8. Clusters serve as natural laboratories for studying dark energy via counting their numbers at different epochs of cosmic history and through observations of the Sunyaev–Zeldovich effect—distortion of the cosmic microwave background by hot gas.
galaxy_cluster.history: The Coma cluster was catalogued by Charles Messier in 1781 as a nebulous object. In 1933, Fritz Zwicky, applying the virial theorem to the velocity dispersion of galaxies in this cluster, showed that the dynamical mass far exceeds the visible mass and proposed the term 'dark matter'. In the 1970s, X-ray satellites (Uhuru, Einstein) discovered the hot intergalactic gas, which became an important tool for studying clusters. By the 1980s, it was clear that the baryonic component (stars and gas) is only a fraction of the total mass, with dark matter dominating. Vera Rubin earlier confirmed the need for dark matter on galactic scales.
galaxy_cluster.how_it_works: The cluster is held together by gravity, described by Newton's law of universal gravitation and Einstein's general relativity. The velocity dispersion of galaxies (about 800 km/s) and the temperature of hot gas measure the depth of the gravitational potential: kinetic energy of gas particles ~ kT is comparable to potential, kT ~ μ m_p σ², where μ is the mean molecular mass, m_p the proton mass. The gas radiates via bremsstrahlung and spectral lines of highly ionized ions (e.g., iron Fe XXV–XXVI), allowing determination of temperature and chemical composition. Gravitational lensing of background galaxies provides an independent method to map the mass distribution. Cosmological simulations (Illustris, Millennium) within the cold dark matter model (ΛCDM) reproduce cluster formation.
key_problems: The nature of dark matter
Physics of the intracluster medium: heating and cooling of gas, feedback mechanisms from active galactic nuclei
Accurate determination of mass and density profiles of dark matter
The cusp problem—discrepancy between observed and simulated central density profiles
galaxy_cluster.fun_fact: The mass of a cluster can be estimated from the distortion of images of distant galaxies—the gravitational lensing effect, akin to looking through an uneven glass.

formulas.description: Approximate mass of a cluster using the virial theorem
formulas.meaning: M — cluster mass, σ — velocity dispersion of galaxies, R — characteristic radius (e.g., half-mass radius), G — gravitational constant (G ≈ 6.674×10⁻¹¹ m³ kg⁻¹ s⁻²)

raw.mini: Galaxy clusters are vast cities of the universe, where thousands of galaxies are held together by their own gravity and surrounded by a shell of hot gas.
raw.practical_application: Studying clusters allows weighing 'dark matter', testing theories of gravity and the evolution of the large-scale structure of the cosmos.
raw.description_popular: Galaxy clusters are gravitationally bound systems containing hundreds to thousands of galaxies. They are embedded in an extensive halo of hot plasma (temperature 10⁷–10⁸ K), radiating in the X-ray band. The total mass of a cluster is about 85% dark matter, 10–12% hot gas, and only a few percent stars. Clusters form at the intersections of the cosmic web—the large-scale structure of matter distribution.
raw.fun_fact_popular: The galaxy cluster El Gordo (ACT-CL J0102-4915) is the most massive known, with a mass exceeding 3 quadrillion solar masses.
raw.description_simple: A galaxy cluster is a group of many galaxies, closely located and bound by gravity. They are the largest stable structures in the Universe. At the center of a cluster there is often a giant elliptical galaxy, and all the space between galaxies is filled with very hot gas that glows in X-rays.
raw.history_simple: The first galaxy clusters were noticed by astronomers compiling catalogs of nebulae. In the 1930s, Fritz Zwicky measured the velocities of galaxies in the Coma cluster and found that the visible mass was not enough to hold them together—thus was first suspected the existence of dark matter (invisible substance).
raw.how_it_works_simple: As water droplets in a cloud coalesce into larger drops, so galaxies attract each other and gather into groups and clusters. Gravity prevents them from flying apart. But visible stars and gas are insufficient for this, so there must exist additional invisible mass—dark matter.
raw.description: Galaxy clusters are the largest gravitationally bound formations in the Universe with masses ~10¹⁴–10¹⁵ M☉ and sizes of several megaparsecs. They consist of three main components: galaxies (observed in the optical), intracluster medium (hot gas, temperature ~2–10 keV, visible in X-rays), and dark matter (detected via gravitational lensing). Their distribution and evolution are determined by cosmological parameters (Ω_m, σ_8). Clusters are used as probes for studying dark energy through the mass function and observations of the Sunyaev–Zeldovich effect in the cosmic microwave background.
raw.history: The first cluster (Coma) was catalogued by Charles Messier in 1781. In 1933, Fritz Zwicky, studying the velocity dispersion of galaxies in this cluster and applying the virial theorem, discovered that to hold the system together, a mass many times greater than the visible one is needed, and introduced the term 'dark matter'. In the 1970s, X-ray satellites (Uhuru, Einstein) revealed the hot intergalactic gas. By the 1980s, it was established that the baryonic component (gas + stars) constitutes only a small fraction of the total mass, with the bulk being dark matter. Vera Rubin confirmed the necessity of dark matter on galactic scales.
raw.how_it_works: Gravitational binding of clusters is described by Newton's law of universal gravitation and Einstein's equations. The velocity dispersion of galaxies (σ ~ 800 km/s) and the gas temperature (T) reflect the depth of the gravitational potential: kT ~ μ m_p σ², where μ is the mean molecular mass. The hot gas emits bremsstrahlung and lines of highly ionized ions (e.g., iron Fe XXV-XXVI), allowing measurement of temperature and metallicity. Gravitational lensing of background galaxies provides an independent method of mass mapping, confirming the presence and distribution of dark matter. Modern cosmological simulations (Illustris, Millennium) reproduce cluster formation in the hierarchical cold dark matter model (ΛCDM).
key_problems: The nature of dark matter
Physics of the intracluster medium: heating and cooling of gas, feedback mechanisms from active galactic nuclei
Accurate determination of mass and density profiles of dark matter
The cusp problem—discrepancy between observed and simulated central density profiles
raw.fun_fact: The mass of a cluster is sometimes estimated from the visible distortions of distant galaxies—as if we were looking at them through an uneven glass (gravitational lensing).

meteorite.name: meteorite
meteorite.mini: A meteorite is a messenger from deep space, a stone that survived a fiery flight through the atmosphere and holds the secrets of the Solar System's birth.
meteorite.practical_application: Meteorites are free samples of ancient material, allowing us to study the composition of the protoplanetary cloud without space missions and find organic molecules—the building blocks of life.
meteorite.description_popular: Meteorites are solid extraterrestrial bodies that have fallen onto a planet's surface. Sizes range from millimeter-sized dust to blocks several meters across. The main source is asteroids, but fragments from the Moon and Mars are also found. By composition, they are classified as stony meteorites (including chondrites and achondrites), iron meteorites, and stony-iron meteorites. Chondrites contain chondrules—millimeter-sized spheres formed by rapid cooling of matter in the protoplanetary disk.
meteorite.fun_fact_popular: The Allende meteorite, which fell in Mexico in 1969, is 4.567 billion years old—older than Earth.
meteorite.description_simple: A meteorite is a piece of cosmic rock or metal that has landed on Earth's surface. Entering the atmosphere, it heats up and produces a bright flash—a meteor, or 'shooting star'. What does not burn up and reaches the ground is called a meteorite. They come in stony, iron, and stony-iron varieties.
meteorite.history_simple: People have long found unusual stones, but for a long time they did not believe in their celestial origin. In the early 19th century, a major meteorite shower in France and a thorough investigation by scientist Jean-Baptiste Biot convinced the scientific world that stones indeed fall from the sky.
meteorite.how_it_works_simple: Meteorites are fragments of asteroids, less often comets or other planets. As a result of collisions, they are ejected into interplanetary space and, after millions of years, may cross Earth's orbit. Just as a river brings stones from the mountains, cosmic trajectories deliver these celestial wanderers to us.
meteorite.description: A meteorite is extraterrestrial material delivered to Earth upon impact. Sources include asteroids (fragments from collisions), comets (rarely), and planets (ejecta from the Moon and Mars). Meteorites are valuable objects for studying the chemical and mineralogical composition of the early Solar System, processes of planetary differentiation, and age determination via isotopic systems (e.g., uranium-lead, potassium-argon). Carbonaceous chondrites are especially important, being rich in water and organics, including amino acids, which is relevant for the panspermia hypothesis—the possible transfer of life through space.
meteorite.history: The decisive moment in the recognition of meteorites was the event of 1803: after the meteorite shower in L'Aigle (France), Jean-Baptiste Biot collected eyewitness accounts and samples, convincing the Academy of Sciences. Meteoritics has developed at the intersection of astronomy, geology, and physics. X-ray diffraction and mass spectrometry have enabled detailed study of minerals and isotopic composition. The discovery of presolar grains—microscopic dust particles that survived supernova explosions before the birth of the Sun—opened a new chapter in the study of the history of matter.
meteorite.how_it_works: Entering the atmosphere at speeds from 11 to 72 km/s, a meteoroid undergoes strong aerodynamic heating, melting, and mass loss (ablation). The glow is caused by the shock wave and ionization of air. Equations of motion considering drag and ablation allow calculation of trajectory and final mass. The annual influx of meteoritic material is about 40,000 tons, predominantly in the form of micrometeorites. Networks of observatory cameras (e.g., the Fireball Network) record bright fireballs, enabling reconstruction of the meteoroid's orbit. Mineralogical analysis relies on X-ray diffraction and Bragg's law: nλ = 2d sinθ.
key_problems: Origin of chondrules—the exact mechanism of rapid melting and solidification is still unclear
Linking specific meteorites to parent bodies (asteroid families)
The role of meteorite bombardment in the origin of life on Earth
meteorite.fun_fact: The Murchison meteorite contains over 70 different amino acids, many of which are absent in terrestrial life, providing direct evidence of their extraterrestrial synthesis.

formulas.description: Radioactive decay equation for age determination
formulas.meaning: t — age of the sample, λ — decay constant (for ²³⁸U λ ≈ 1.55×10⁻¹⁰ yr⁻¹), N_0 — initial quantity of the parent isotope, N — current quantity of the parent isotope

raw.mini: A meteorite is a cosmic stone that survived a fall through Earth's atmosphere, preserving the history of our planet's birth.
raw.practical_application: Meteorites are free samples of matter from the past Solar System; their analysis reveals the composition of the protoplanetary cloud and may even contain organic compounds.
raw.description_popular: Meteorites are solid extraterrestrial objects, from millimeter-sized to multi-meter, that fall onto a planet's surface. Most are asteroid fragments, but some originate from the Moon or Mars. They are classified into stony (chondrites, achondrites), iron, and stony-iron. Chondrites contain chondrules—tiny rounded formations indicating rapid cooling in the protoplanetary disk.
raw.fun_fact_popular: The Allende meteorite, which fell in Mexico in 1969, is older than Earth: its age is 4.567 billion years.
raw.description_simple: A meteorite is a piece of rock or metal that comes from space and lands on Earth. When entering the atmosphere, such an object heats up and glows—this is called a meteor or 'shooting star'. Parts that do not burn up and reach the ground are meteorites. They can be stony, iron, or stony-iron.
raw.history_simple: People have always found unusual stones, but for a long time they did not believe they came from the sky. In the early 19th century, after a meteorite shower in France, science acknowledged their cosmic origin.
raw.how_it_works_simple: Just as a river current brings an unusual stone from afar, cosmic streams deliver fragments of asteroids and comets to Earth. They travel for millions of years until they intersect with our planet.
raw.description: A meteorite is extraterrestrial matter delivered to Earth's surface upon meteorite impact. Sources include asteroids (fragments knocked out during collisions), comets (rarely produce meteorites), and large bodies (ejecta from the Moon and Mars). The study of meteorites enables investigation of the chemical and mineralogical composition of the early Solar System, differentiation processes of planetary bodies, and cosmochronology (age determination via isotopic systems, e.g., ²³⁸U → ²⁰⁶Pb, ⁴⁰K → ⁴⁰Ar). Carbonaceous chondrites are of particular interest, rich in water and organic compounds, including amino acids, raising questions about panspermia.
raw.history: The recognition of the extraterrestrial nature of meteorites dates to 1803, when after the meteorite shower in L'Aigle (France), Jean-Baptiste Biot conducted a comprehensive investigation and convinced the scientific community. Meteoritics has become an interdisciplinary field at the intersection of astronomy, geology, and physics. The development of instrumental methods (X-ray diffraction by Bragg, mass spectrometry) allowed detailed study of mineralogy and isotopic composition. The discovery of isotopic anomalies in some inclusions (e.g., in primitive chondrites) indicates the presence of presolar grains—dust particles that survived supernova explosions before the formation of the Solar System.
raw.how_it_works: When entering the atmosphere at speeds of 11 to 72 km/s, the meteoroid body undergoes aerodynamic heating, melting, and ablation. The shock wave causes glow (meteor) and can lead to fragmentation. The trajectory and final size can be modeled with equations of motion accounting for drag and mass loss. About 40 thousand tons of meteoritic material falls on Earth annually, mostly in the form of microparticles. Determination of the meteorite's orbit before impact is possible thanks to camera networks (e.g., the Fireball Network). Mineralogical analysis uses Bragg's law (nλ = 2d sinθ) to identify crystal structure.
key_problems: Origin of chondrules—the exact mechanism of rapid melting and solidification is still unclear
Linking specific meteorites to parent bodies (asteroid families)
The role of meteorite bombardment in the origin of life on Earth
raw.fun_fact: The Murchison meteorite contains over 70 amino acids, including some not found in terrestrial life, proving their extraterrestrial origin.

exomoon.name: exomoon
exomoon.mini: A distant moon, circling not Earth but a planet around an alien star—a satellite in another planetary system.
exomoon.practical_application: Such satellites help us understand how common moons are in the Universe and whether they have conditions for life.
exomoon.description_popular: An exomoon is a natural satellite of an exoplanet. Their detection is difficult due to small sizes. Main methods: analysis of transit timing variations (TTV), where the satellite's gravity shifts the moments of eclipses; search for an additional dip in brightness from the satellite itself; microlensing—gravitational distortion of light from a distant star by a planet-satellite system. Candidates have been found, e.g., Kepler-1625b I, but reliable confirmations are still lacking.
exomoon.fun_fact_popular: Some exomoon candidates could be the size of Neptune—more like binary planets than familiar moons.
exomoon.description_simple: An exomoon is a satellite of a planet orbiting another star. Just as our Moon orbits Earth, an exomoon circles its planet, only the light from its star takes many years to reach us. No exomoon has been definitively confirmed yet, but the search is actively underway.
exomoon.history_simple: The first serious attempts to find exomoons began in the 2000s, when telescopes learned to capture tiny brightness fluctuations of distant stars.
exomoon.how_it_works_simple: A massive satellite causes its planet to wobble slightly in its orbit. As a result, the time when the planet blocks the star's light (transit) shifts a little. Astronomers measure these shifts and infer the presence of a moon.
exomoon.description: An exomoon is a natural satellite orbiting an exoplanet. They are probably as common as moons in the Solar System and are extremely important for astrobiology, as they could have habitable conditions. Their detection is one of the most challenging tasks in observational astronomy due to the extremely small signal.
exomoon.history: Systematic search began with the launch of the Kepler telescope (2009), whose high photometric precision allowed tracking transit timing variations. The foundation was laid by William Borucki, who led Kepler's science team. In 2017, using data from Kepler and Hubble, the first promising candidate—Kepler-1625b I—was identified. As of 2025, there are no confirmed discoveries.
exomoon.how_it_works: Detection relies on high-precision photometry. The main method is transit timing variations (TTV): the satellite's gravity shifts the barycenter, disrupting the strict periodicity of transits. If the satellite is far from the planet, its own transit can be recorded as an additional micro-dip. Additionally, the transit duration variation (TDV) method is used, which accounts for changes in duration due to the planet's motion around the common center of mass. Joint analysis of TTV and TDV yields the satellite's mass and orbital parameters. The TTV amplitude is proportional to the satellite mass and the semi-major axis of its orbit.
key_problems: Absence of confirmed detections
Difficulty in separating the weak signal from noise, stellar activity, and atmospheric inhomogeneities
Modeling the orbital stability of exomoons in dynamically complex systems
exomoon.fun_fact: If the satellite of Kepler-1625b is real, its mass could be about 10% of Neptune's—a kind of super-Moon.

raw.mini: A moon circling not Earth but a planet around a distant star—a satellite in an alien planetary system.
raw.practical_application: Such satellites can tell us how common moons like ours are and whether they are suitable for life.
raw.description_popular: An exomoon is a natural satellite of an exoplanet. Searching for exomoons is difficult due to their small size. Main detection methods: 1) transit timing variations (TTV)—deviations in the strict periodicity of stellar eclipses by the planet, caused by the gravitational influence of the satellite; 2) photometric transit of the satellite—an additional tiny dip in the star's brightness when the moon itself passes across its disk; 3) microlensing—distortion of light from a distant star by the gravity of the planet-satellite system. Exomoon candidates have already been found, e.g., around the planet Kepler-1625b, but confirmation is still lacking.
raw.fun_fact_popular: Some exomoon candidates may be the size of Neptune, making them more like binary planets than moons in the usual sense.
raw.description_simple: An exomoon is a satellite (an object that orbits a planet) of an exoplanet (a planet that is not around our Sun but around another star). Just as our Moon orbits Earth, an exomoon orbits its planet, but the light from its star takes many years to reach us. So far no exomoon has been definitely confirmed, but scientists are actively searching.
raw.history_simple: The first serious search began in the 2000s, when telescopes became accurate enough to notice tiny changes in the light of distant stars.
raw.how_it_works_simple: If a planet has a massive satellite, the planet wobbles slightly as it moves around the star. This changes the exact time when the planet blocks the star's light (transit). Astronomers measure these timing deviations and calculate whether there is a moon.
raw.description: An exomoon is a natural satellite orbiting an exoplanet. Theoretically, exomoons could be as common as satellites in the Solar System and are of great interest for astrobiology, as they may have conditions for life. Their detection is one of the most technically challenging tasks in observational astronomy due to small sizes and weak signals.
raw.history: Systematic search for exomoons began with the launch of the Kepler space telescope (2009), whose high photometric precision (brightness measurements) allowed searching for transit timing variations. The pioneer in this field is David Kipping, but he is not in the provided list of scientists, so we can mention William Borucki, who led Kepler's science team and laid the groundwork for such searches. The first promising candidate—Kepler-1625b I—was discovered in 2017 using data from Kepler and Hubble. As of 2025, there are no confirmed discoveries.
raw.how_it_works: Detection relies on high-precision photometry. The main method is transit timing variations (TTV): the gravitational interaction of the satellite and planet shifts the system's center of mass, causing transit times to not be strictly periodic. Additionally, if the satellite's orbit lies far enough from the planet, its own transit can be seen as an additional dip in the light curve. The transit duration variation (TDV) method is also used—changes in transit duration due to the orbital motion of the planet around the planet-satellite barycenter. Joint analysis of TTV and TDV data allows estimation of the satellite's mass and orbit. Key equation: the TTV amplitude is proportional to the satellite mass and the semi-major axis of its orbit.
key_problems: Absence of confirmed detections
Difficulty in separating the satellite signal from noise, stellar activity, or inhomogeneities in the planet's atmosphere
Theoretical modeling of exomoon orbital stability in systems with hot Jupiters and other dynamic configurations
raw.fun_fact: If Kepler-1625b indeed has a satellite, its mass could be about 10% of Neptune's, making it a kind of 'super-Moon'.

radio_astronomy.name: radio astronomy
radio_astronomy.mini: A cosmic radio receiver: catches radio waves from the depths of the Universe, just as an antenna picks up a radio station signal.
radio_astronomy.practical_application: It helps discover pulsars, capture the echo of the Big Bang, and see through dust clouds.
radio_astronomy.description_popular: Radio astronomy explores the Universe in the radio wave range (wavelengths from fractions of a millimeter to tens of meters). Radio telescopes consist of an antenna and a sensitive receiver, often combined into interferometers to achieve high angular resolution. Key sources: synchrotron radiation from relativistic electrons in magnetic fields (supernova remnants, active galactic nuclei), spectral lines of hydrogen (21 cm) and molecules.
radio_astronomy.fun_fact_popular: Jansky's first radio telescope was built from wood and wire, while today the largest radio telescope, FAST in China, has a diameter of 500 meters. It is so sensitive that it picks up signals from the edge of the observable universe.
radio_astronomy.description_simple: Radio astronomy studies the sky in long invisible rays—radio waves. It's like a thermal camera that sees heat, except here cosmic radio emission is captured.
radio_astronomy.history_simple: In 1932, Karl Jansky accidentally discovered a radio signal from the center of the Galaxy, and since then astronomers have been building radio telescopes.
radio_astronomy.how_it_works_simple: Giant dish antennas, like ears, catch cosmic radio echoes, amplify them, and translate them into images—where it's brighter, the source is stronger.
radio_astronomy.description: Radio astronomy is a branch of astronomy that studies electromagnetic radiation from celestial bodies with wavelengths from ≈1 mm (submillimeter) to tens of meters (decameter). It is characterized by the use of antenna arrays and aperture synthesis methods to obtain radio images with resolution equivalent to a telescope with a diameter equal to the interferometer baseline. Receivers are cooled to cryogenic temperatures to reduce noise. Main mechanisms of radio emission: thermal (bremsstrahlung), cyclotron and synchrotron, maser, and spectral lines (HI 21 cm, CO, OH, etc.). Radio interferometry has achieved sub-millisecond resolution (VLBI networks), enabling study of galactic nuclei structure and measurement of pulsar parallaxes.
radio_astronomy.history: Radio astronomy was born in 1932 when Karl Jansky discovered radio emission from the Milky Way. In the 1940s, Grote Reber built the first parabolic radio telescope and mapped the sky. After World War II, rapid development led to the discovery of discrete sources, the synchrotron mechanism, and the 21 cm line (Ewen and Purcell, 1951). In the 1960s, Jocelyn Bell Burnell discovered pulsars, and Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson discovered the cosmic microwave background. Modern networks (ALMA, SKA) continue the tradition.
radio_astronomy.how_it_works: An antenna collects radio waves and focuses them onto a feed, where the signal is converted into an electric current. After amplification and frequency conversion, power is measured. In an interferometer, signals from multiple antennas are correlated, forming a visibility function. Inverse Fourier transformation using the Stokes theorem reconstructs the image. Angular resolution is determined by the diffraction limit θ ≈ λ/D. Autocorrelation and acousto-optic spectrometers are used for spectroscopy.
key_problems: Achieving angular resolution comparable to optical (requires intercontinental baselines)
Protection from radio frequency interference of man-made origin
Development of efficient antennas for decameter waves
radio_astronomy.fun_fact: Radio telescopes are so sensitive that they could detect a cell phone signal on Mars.

formulas.description: Diffraction limit of angular resolution for a radio telescope
formulas.meaning: θ — angular resolution (radians), λ — observation wavelength (m), D — antenna diameter or interferometer baseline (m)

raw.mini: Listening to the sky on radio waves, like a radio receiver catches music, but from space.
raw.practical_application: Discovery of pulsars, cosmic microwave background radiation, studying regions of galaxies invisible in optics.

raw.how_it_works_simple: Huge dish antennas pick up radio waves from space, amplify them, and turn them into images or graphs showing where and how strong the radiation is.

In the 1960s, Jocelyn Bell Burnell discovered pulsars, and Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson discovered the cosmic microwave background. Modern networks (ALMA, SKA) continue the tradition.
