The gapless band structure hinders application in digital logic.
key_problems: Scalable production of high-quality graphene
Creation and control of a bandgap for transistors
Integration with silicon technology
Environmental safety and toxicity of nanofilms
Achieving series uniformity for industrial applications
raw.fun_fact: Graphene one atom thick is impermeable even to helium atoms—the smallest particles of inert gas. Yet it lets water through, acting as a molecular sieve.

banach_space.name: Banach space
banach_space.mini: A normed space where any sequence that must converge indeed has a limit inside. Like a house where every path leads to a room and does not end in emptiness.
banach_space.practical_application: Solving differential equations, quantum mechanics, optimization problems, signal processing.
banach_space.description_popular: A Banach space is a normed vector space in which any Cauchy sequence (elements that arbitrarily close to each other) converges to a limit lying in the same space. This is a key concept of functional analysis. Examples: spaces of summable functions L^p and continuous functions C[a,b]. In these spaces, linear operators and their spectra are studied, which finds applications in many areas.
banach_space.fun_fact_popular: The Hahn–Banach theorem states that any linear functional defined on a subspace can be extended to the whole space without increasing its norm. This is like the ability to extend a length measurement from a small piece to the entire figure without errors.
banach_space.description_simple: A Banach space is a collection of vectors or functions where you can add, multiply by a number, and measure 'length' (norm). The main feature is completeness: if a sequence of points converges arbitrarily (like cities that get closer and closer), then there necessarily exists a limit point belonging to the same collection. It's like a map where any route converging to a target leads to a point on the map, not hanging in emptiness.
banach_space.history_simple: Named after Stefan Banach, a Polish mathematician who introduced this concept in his doctoral dissertation in 1920. His work laid the foundations of functional analysis.
banach_space.how_it_works_simple: The norm allows measuring distances between elements. Completeness means that approximations do not lead beyond the space: by infinitely improving the approximation of a complex function with simple ones, we eventually get a function from the same set. This guarantees that the approximated quantity indeed has a 'final station'.
banach_space.description: A Banach space is a complete normed vector space. The norm ‖·‖ satisfies the axioms: ‖x‖ ≥ 0, ‖x‖=0 only for zero; ‖αx‖ = |α|‖x‖; ‖x+y‖ ≤ ‖x‖+‖y‖. The metric d(x,y)=‖x−y‖ defines the topology; completeness means that any Cauchy sequence converges to an element of the same space. Banach spaces are the foundation of functional analysis. Key theorems: Hahn–Banach (extension of functionals), Banach–Steinhaus (uniform boundedness), open mapping and closed graph. They generalize finite-dimensional spaces to the infinite-dimensional case. Important examples: L^p, Sobolev spaces, BV, Hardy spaces, spaces of continuous functions. Spectral theory of operators in Banach spaces transfers matrix methods to infinite-dimensional problems, important for integral and differential equations.
banach_space.history: The concept of a Banach space was developed by Stefan Banach in his doctoral dissertation (1920) and subsequent publications. He gave an axiomatic construction. Early ideas belonged to Fréchet, Riesz, and Hilbert. Banach's classic monograph 'Theory of Linear Operations' (1932) cemented the subject. Further development is associated with Schauder, Dunford, Schwartz, Grothendieck, and others.
banach_space.how_it_works: Banach spaces provide a rigorous framework for studying the convergence of sequences and series of functions, even in the infinite-dimensional case. Linear operators, especially compact and integral ones, naturally arise in inverse problems of mathematical physics. The contraction mapping principle (method of successive approximations) relies on completeness and guarantees the uniqueness of solutions to many nonlinear equations. Hilbert spaces are a special case where the norm is generated by an inner product; however, even without an inner product, Banach spaces possess rich geometry (strict convexity, uniform smoothness). Dual spaces, reflexivity, and various topologies (strong, weak, weak-*) serve as tools for analyzing the convergence of operator sequences.
key_problems: Classification of all separable Banach spaces up to isomorphism
The invariant subspace problem for an arbitrary bounded operator on a Hilbert space (solved for some cases, the general case is open)
Existence of a Schauder basis in every separable Banach space (proved by Enflo, but questions remain about properties)
The problem of superreflexivity and equivalent norms
banach_space.fun_fact: Enflo constructed a Banach space where every bounded linear operator has a non-trivial invariant subspace, but the invariant subspace problem for an arbitrary operator in a Hilbert space remains open. (The famous Banach–Tarski paradox of doubling the ball pertains to measure theory, not to Banach spaces.)

formulas.description: Triangle inequality for the norm
formulas.meaning: x, y are vectors in a Banach space; the norm ‖·‖ satisfies this inequality

formulas.description: Homogeneity of the norm
formulas.meaning: α is a scalar (real or complex number); scaling property

raw.mini: A complete normed vector space. Ensures that for any sequence that must converge, the limit indeed exists inside the space.
raw.practical_application: Solving differential equations, description of quantum states, optimization in economics, signal analysis.
raw.description_popular: A Banach space is a complete normed vector space (over the field of real or complex numbers). Completeness means that any fundamental sequence (Cauchy sequence) converges to an element of the space. Examples include L^p spaces (measurable functions with integrable p-th power) and the space of continuous functions C[a,b]. On these spaces, bounded linear operators, their spectra, and topologies are studied.
raw.fun_fact_popular: The Hahn–Banach theorem is a key one, allowing extension of a linear functional from a subspace to the whole space while preserving the norm. This can be imagined as extending a length measurement from a small area to the whole universe without distortion.
raw.description_simple: This is a set of objects (vectors, functions) where addition, multiplication by a number, and 'length' (norm) are defined. The main property is completeness: if the elements of a sequence approach each other indefinitely (Cauchy sequence), then they necessarily have a limit within the same set. Similar to a road atlas: if you build a route from points that are getting closer and closer to the destination, then the final point is also on the map, not somewhere beyond it.
raw.history_simple: Named after Stefan Banach, a Polish mathematician who introduced this concept in his doctoral dissertation in 1920. His work laid the foundations of functional analysis.
raw.how_it_works_simple: Thanks to the norm, distances can be measured. Completeness guarantees that approximate calculations will not lead beyond the boundaries of the space: an infinite series of approximations of a complex function by simple functions will necessarily converge to a function of the same class. It's like being sure that if you approximate something more and more precisely, then that something exists.
raw.description: A Banach space is a complete normed vector space. The norm ‖·‖ satisfies the axioms: ‖x‖ ≥ 0 and ‖x‖ = 0 ↔ x = 0; ‖αx‖ = |α|‖x‖; ‖x + y‖ ≤ ‖x‖ + ‖y‖. The metric d(x,y) = ‖x−y‖ generates the topology; completeness means that any Cauchy sequence with respect to this metric converges to an element of the space. Banach spaces form the central object of functional analysis. Key theorems: Hahn–Banach (extension of linear functionals), Banach–Steinhaus (principle of uniform boundedness), open mapping and closed graph. They generalize finite-dimensional vector spaces, removing the restriction of dimensionality. Important classes: L^p, Sobolev spaces, BV spaces, Hardy spaces, and spaces of continuous functions. Spectral theory of bounded and compact operators in Banach spaces generalizes matrix theory and has fundamental importance for solving integral and differential equations.
raw.history: The concept emerged in the works of Stefan Banach (1920, doctoral dissertation; 1922 - publication). He gave an axiomatic exposition of the theory. Ideas go back to Fréchet, Riesz, and Hilbert. In 1932, Banach's classic monograph 'Theory of Linear Operations' was published, cementing the subject. Subsequent development is associated with the names Schauder, Dunford, Schwartz, Grothendieck, and others.
raw.how_it_works: Banach spaces provide a rigorous framework for analyzing the convergence of sequences and series of functions, allowing one to operate with infinite-dimensional objects. Linear operators, especially compact and integral ones, naturally arise in inverse problems of mathematical physics. The method of successive approximations (contraction mapping principle) relies on completeness and gives uniqueness of solution for many nonlinear equations. Hilbert spaces are a special case with an inner product, but Banach spaces without it still admit rich geometry (strict convexity, uniform smoothness). Dual spaces, reflexivity, and topologies (strong, weak, weak-*) constitute the toolkit for studying the convergence of operator sequences.
key_problems: Classification of all separable Banach spaces up to isomorphism
The invariant subspace problem for an arbitrary bounded operator on a Hilbert space (solved for some cases, the general case is open)
Existence of a Schauder basis in every separable Banach space (proved by Enflo, but questions remain about properties)
The problem of superreflexivity and equivalent norms
raw.fun_fact: There exists a Banach space (constructed by Enflo) in which every bounded linear operator has a non-trivial invariant subspace, but the general case is still unproven. And the Banach–Tarski theorem (paradox of doubling the sphere) is not related to Banach spaces, but is a consequence of the axiom of choice in measure theory.

total_internal_reflection.name: total internal reflection
total_internal_reflection.mini: Light is trapped inside a dense medium at a steep angle of incidence - like water not letting rays out when viewed from below. Basis of fiber optics and diamond sparkle.
total_internal_reflection.practical_application: Fiber-optic communication (internet), prisms in binoculars, medical endoscopes, touch sensors in screens.
total_internal_reflection.description_popular: Total internal reflection (TIR) occurs when light tries to go from a denser optical medium (with higher refractive index n₁) to a less dense one (n₂ < n₁) at an angle greater than the critical one. In this case, it is completely reflected back, and only an evanescent field remains beyond the boundary. This effect is indispensable in waveguides.
total_internal_reflection.fun_fact_popular: The brilliance of diamonds is a result of total internal reflection: due to the high refractive index (2.42), light repeatedly reflects off facets and exits at various angles, creating a play of highlights.
total_internal_reflection.description_simple: When transitioning from a dense medium (water) to a less dense one (air), light incident at a too shallow angle is not refracted but completely reflected. Imagine a ball rolling up a hill: not having gained enough speed, it rolls back down. The critical angle is the boundary beyond which total reflection begins.
total_internal_reflection.history_simple: The phenomenon has been noticed since antiquity: when looking from underwater, the surface can appear mirror-like. In 1611, Kepler first described it mathematically, and later Snellius discovered the law of refraction, allowing the calculation of the critical angle.
total_internal_reflection.how_it_works_simple: Light travels at different speeds in different media - this is described by the refractive index. At the boundary between a 'slow' and a 'fast' medium, the beam bends. If the angle of incidence is too large, the bending becomes so strong that the light does not exit but returns. This happens only when transitioning from an optically denser medium to a less dense one.
total_internal_reflection.description: Total internal reflection is an optical effect at the boundary of two dielectrics when the angle of incidence exceeds the critical one. From Snell's law n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂, when sin θ₂ > 1 the refracted ray disappears. Instead, an evanescent wave, exponentially decaying with depth, appears in the second medium: the penetration depth is on the order of the wavelength. Energy flux across the boundary is zero, but closely spaced objects can interact with this field (frustrated total internal reflection). According to Fresnel formulas, the magnitude of the reflection coefficient is 1, but the phase is shifted; the shift depends on polarization.
total_internal_reflection.history: The first description of total internal reflection was given by Kepler in 'Dioptrice' (1611). In 1621, Snellius discovered the law of refraction, from which the critical angle is directly derived. In the 1820s, Fresnel developed the theory, including evanescent waves. In 1854, Tyndall demonstrated a light guide using a water jet - a prototype of optical fiber. In the 1960s, practical application of fiber optics began.
total_internal_reflection.how_it_works: Condition for TIR: n₁ > n₂ and angle of incidence θ₁ > θ_c. Conservation of the tangential component of the wave vector gives k_x = (n₁ ω/c) sin θ₁. When θ₁ > θ_c, k_x exceeds n₂ ω/c, so the normal component k_z₂ becomes imaginary: k_z₂ = iκ. The field decays exponentially: exp(−κ z), penetration depth δ = 1/κ = λ₀/(2π n₁ √(sin²θ₁ − (n₂/n₁)²)). The evanescent wave carries energy along the boundary but not into the volume. The phase shift upon reflection differs for s- and p-polarizations. Frustrated TIR is analogous to quantum tunneling: when two prisms are brought within a distance of about δ, light partially penetrates into the second.
key_problems: Reducing losses in optical fiber for ultra-long-distance communication
Development of waveguides for integrated photonics
Increasing the efficiency of sensors based on FTIR
Quantum effects in evanescent coupling with single emitters
Materials with record-low absorption for the mid-IR range
total_internal_reflection.fun_fact: In frustrated total internal reflection, photons tunnel through a narrow gap, which is a direct optical analog of the quantum mechanical tunnel effect. This experiment can be performed with two prisms and a laser pointer.

formulas.description: Snell's law
formulas.meaning: n₁ is the refractive index of the first medium, θ₁ is the angle of incidence, n₂ is the refractive index of the second medium, θ₂ is the angle of refraction

formulas.description: Critical angle of total internal reflection
formulas.meaning: θ_c is the critical angle, n₁ > n₂ are the refractive indices

raw.mini: Light trapped inside a dense medium when the angle of incidence is too steep - like water holding light under the surface when viewed from below at a sharp angle.
raw.practical_application: Fiber-optic communication (internet), prisms in binoculars, medical endoscopes, touch sensors in screens.
raw.description_popular: Total internal reflection occurs when light propagates from a medium with refractive index n₁ to a medium with n₂ < n₁ at an angle θ₁ exceeding the critical one θ_c = arcsin(n₂/n₁). In this case, the reflection coefficient becomes equal to one, and the transmitted wave turns into a decaying (evanescent) wave, exponentially decreasing with distance from the boundary. The phenomenon underlies waveguides.
raw.fun_fact_popular: Diamonds sparkle due to TIR: the high refractive index of diamond (2.42) gives a small critical angle, and light, once inside, repeatedly reflects back and forth, exiting and creating a play of rays.
raw.description_simple: When light passes from a denser medium (e.g., water) to a less dense one (air) at a sufficiently shallow angle to the surface, it does not exit but is completely reflected back. This is like a ball rolling uphill: if the speed is low, it won't make it over and will roll back. The critical angle is the steepest at which light still slides along the boundary.
raw.history_simple: The phenomenon has been noticed since antiquity, but it was mathematically described by Johannes Kepler in 1611. Then Snellius derived the law of refraction, from which the critical angle can easily be found.
raw.how_it_works_simple: The speed of light in different materials differs (characterized by the refractive index). At the boundary between a 'slow' and a 'fast' substance, the ray deviates from the perpendicular. At a sufficiently large angle of incidence, the deviation becomes so strong that the ray cannot leave the first medium and is reflected. This effect is possible only when transitioning from an optically denser medium to a less dense one.
raw.description: Total internal reflection is an optical effect that occurs at the boundary of two dielectrics at angles of incidence greater than the critical one. According to Snell's law n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂, when sin θ₂ formally exceeds one, there is no refracted ray. The electromagnetic field in the second medium exists as an evanescent wave, whose amplitude exponentially decays with distance from the boundary (penetration depth on the order of the wavelength). The energy flux into the second medium is absent, but the evanescent field can interact with objects located nearby - this effect is called frustrated total internal reflection (FTIR). The reflection coefficient is calculated using Fresnel formulas and, for angles greater than the critical one, its magnitude is one, but the phase of the reflected light undergoes a jump depending on polarization.
raw.history: The first description of total internal reflection was given by Kepler in 'Dioptrice' (1611). Snellius experimentally discovered the law of refraction in 1621. The critical angle directly follows from his law. In the 1820s, Fresnel derived formulas describing reflection and evanescent waves. John Tyndall in 1854 demonstrated the waveguide effect by directing light along a water jet - this served as a prototype of optical fiber. Modern applications include fiber optics by Kao and Hockham (1960s).
raw.how_it_works: Condition for TIR: n₁ > n₂ and angle of incidence greater than θ_c. The wave vectors must satisfy k_x² + k_z² = (n ω/c)². At the boundary, k_x is conserved: k_x = (n₁ ω/c) sin θ₁. When θ₁ > θ_c, k_x > n₂ ω/c, so k_z₂ = iκ becomes imaginary: the field penetrates into the second medium as exp(−κ z). Penetration depth δ = 1/κ = λ₀/(2π n₁ √(sin²θ₁ − (n₂/n₁)²)). The evanescent wave carries energy along the surface but not into the depth. The phase of the reflected wave for s- and p-polarizations shifts differently, which is used in phase modulators. Frustrated TIR: when two prisms are brought close together with a gap on the order of δ, part of the light tunnels into the second prism - an analog of quantum tunneling.
key_problems: Reducing losses in optical fiber for ultra-long-distance communication
Development of waveguides for integrated photonics
Increasing the efficiency of sensors based on FTIR
Quantum effects in evanescent coupling with single emitters
Materials with record-low absorption for the mid-IR range
raw.fun_fact: In frustrated total internal reflection, photons tunnel through a narrow gap, which is a direct optical analog of the quantum mechanical tunnel effect. This experiment can be performed with two prisms and a laser pointer.

integral.name: integral
integral.mini: Integral is a counter of accumulated total, collecting bit by bit a continuously changing quantity, like a bucket under uneven rain.
integral.practical_application: Calculation of distance traveled from a speed graph, area of a complex shape, prediction of accumulated radiation dose in materials — all through the integral.
integral.description_popular: The definite integral ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx gives a number — the area of a curvilinear trapezoid bounded by the graph of f(x), the x-axis, and verticals x=a and x=b. It is defined as the limit of Riemann sums: Σ f(ξᵢ)Δxᵢ as the maximum length of partition intervals tends to zero. The indefinite integral is a family of antiderivatives, i.e., functions F such that F'=f. The connection is given by the Newton-Leibniz formula: ∫ₐᵇ f = F(b)−F(a).
integral.fun_fact_popular: The integral sign ∫ is a stylized letter ſ (long s), from Latin summa, proposed by Leibniz. Unlike the derivative, the integral smooths a function: even a highly jagged curve can have a smooth integral function.
integral.description_simple: If a cyclist's speed changes every second, the integral of speed over time gives the total distance. Geometrically, an integral is the area under a curve. We divide the region into very narrow strips, sum their areas, and the narrower the strips, the more accurate the result. The integral is the inverse of the derivative: finding a function from its rate of change.
integral.history_simple: Archimedes calculated the area of a parabolic segment by the method of exhaustion, close to integration. In the 17th century, Isaac Newton and Gottfried Leibniz independently reduced integration to finding an antiderivative — this is the fundamental theorem of calculus. Later, Bernhard Riemann gave a rigorous definition through limits of sums.
integral.how_it_works_simple: It’s like tiling a shaped area with mosaic: tiny tiles almost exactly cover the figure, and their total area is the desired quantity. Mathematically, it’s the limit of sums of areas of rectangles as their number increases without bound.
integral.description: Let f: [a,b] → ℝ be bounded. A partition: a = x₀ < x₁ < ... < xₙ = b. Riemann sum: Σ_{i=1,...,n} f(ξᵢ)(xᵢ−xᵢ₋₁), where ξᵢ ∈ [xᵢ₋₁, xᵢ]. The Riemann integral is the limit of such sums as maxᵢ (xᵢ−xᵢ₋₁) → 0, if the limit exists and does not depend on the choice of ξᵢ. Functions with a finite number of discontinuities are Riemann integrable. The Lebesgue integral generalizes to a broader class of functions via measure. Properties: linearity, additivity over intervals. Applied in physics to compute work, mass, flux; in probability theory to find probabilities via densities.
integral.history: Eudoxus (c. 370 BC) and Archimedes (287–212 BC) used the method of exhaustion, the ancient analog of integration. In the 17th century, independent works of Newton and Leibniz formalized the integral as the inverse of the differential. Riemann in 1854 gave the first formal definition, and in the early 20th century, Henri Lebesgue constructed an integral based on measure, solving fundamental problems of interchange of limits and integral.
integral.how_it_works: The fundamental theorem of calculus: if f is continuous on [a,b] and F is any antiderivative, then ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx = F(b)−F(a). Computation reduces to finding an antiderivative. Techniques: substitution, integration by parts (derivative of a product), partial fractions. For complex integrands, numerical methods (trapezoidal, Simpson's) or Fourier/Laplace transforms are used.
key_problems: Creation of a universal algorithm for symbolic integration of all elementary functions (Risch problem)
Exact computation of high-dimensional multiple integrals in quantum physics and financial mathematics problems
integral.fun_fact: The Poisson integral ∫_{-∞}^{∞} e^{-x²} dx = √π, or Gaussian integral, appears unexpectedly in probability theory and statistics, and its square is easily computed by switching to polar coordinates.

formulas.description: Newton-Leibniz formula
formulas.meaning: ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx — definite integral of f over [a,b]; F — any antiderivative of f (F' = f); a, b — limits of integration.

formulas.description: Definition via limit of Riemann sums
formulas.meaning: n — number of subdivision intervals tending to infinity; Δx = (b−a)/n — length of each subinterval; xᵢ* — arbitrary point in the i-th subinterval; Σ — sum over i from 1 to n.

differential_equation.name: differential equation
differential_equation.mini: A differential equation is like a recipe that tells us how fast something is changing, and from that we find out what will happen next. Knowing a car's speed at each moment, we can recover its path.
differential_equation.practical_application: They are needed everywhere: from weather forecasting and calculating satellite orbits to modeling population growth and the operation of electronic circuits.
differential_equation.description_popular: A differential equation describes the relationship between a function and its derivatives — the rates of change. Such equations underlie physical laws: from planetary motion to quantum mechanics. Newton's second law (acceleration proportional to force) is a differential equation: force gives the acceleration (second derivative of position), and the equation allows finding the trajectory.
differential_equation.fun_fact_popular: The Navier-Stokes equations, describing fluid motion, are differential equations for which a million dollars is offered for a solution (one of the Millennium Prize problems).
differential_equation.description_simple: A differential equation relates an unknown quantity (e.g., position) with how fast it changes (rate of change). It’s like knowing the steepness of the slope at each point of a mountain and from that finding the height. The derivative shows the slope (how fast the height changes), and the equation is the rule for how to obtain the height from the slope.
differential_equation.history_simple: People began solving simple differential equations in the 17th century. Isaac Newton and Gottfried Leibniz independently created the mathematics of change (differential and integral calculus), which allowed writing such equations.
differential_equation.how_it_works_simple: You walk along a trail, and you have a device that shows the slope at each step. A differential equation is the rule for how to reconstruct the shape of the trail from the slope. Solving the equation gives the height for any position.
differential_equation.description: A differential equation is an equation containing an unknown function and its derivatives. If the function depends on one variable, it is an ordinary differential equation; if on several — a partial differential equation. The order is determined by the highest derivative. Classes: linear and nonlinear, homogeneous and nonhomogeneous. The Cauchy problem includes initial conditions; a boundary value problem — conditions on the boundary. Solution methods: separation of variables, integrating factor, variation of parameters, series, numerical methods (Runge-Kutta).
differential_equation.history: The concept of differential equation emerged simultaneously with differential and integral calculus at the end of the 17th century. Isaac Newton introduced differential equations to describe motion; Gottfried Leibniz developed the formalism of derivatives and integrals. In the 18th century, Leonhard Euler and Joseph-Louis Lagrange developed methods for solving ODEs, and Jean le Rond d'Alembert — for partial differential equations (vibrating string). In the 19th century, Augustin-Louis Cauchy gave a rigorous theory of existence and uniqueness of solutions. Today the theory includes qualitative analysis, stability, and numerical methods.
differential_equation.how_it_works: A differential equation expresses a local rule: the rate of change of a quantity depends on the quantity itself. The solution recovers the global behavior. Newton's law: \( m\ddot{x} = F(x, \dot{x}, t) \) is a second-order ordinary differential equation. Integrating with initial conditions yields the trajectory. Linear ODEs with constant coefficients reduce to the characteristic equation. Nonlinear ones require linearization or analysis of phase portraits. Partial differential equations (heat conduction \( \partial_t u = \alpha \nabla^2 u \)) are solved taking into account boundary conditions. Methods: separation of variables, spectral decomposition, finite differences.
key_problems: Existence and smoothness of solutions of Navier-Stokes equations (one of the Millennium Prize problems)
Exact solutions of nonlinear equations in the general case
Efficient numerical methods for singular perturbations and multiscale problems
Questions of stability and bifurcations in complex systems
differential_equation.fun_fact: The Korteweg-de Vries equation, describing waves in shallow water, has amazing solutions — solitons that behave like particles upon collisions. It can be solved using methods of quantum scattering theory.

formulas.description: General form of a first-order ordinary differential equation
formulas.meaning: y — unknown function, x — independent variable, f — given function expressing the rate of change of y with respect to x

formulas.description: Newton's second law as a second-order differential equation
formulas.meaning: m — mass of the body, x — coordinate, t — time, F — applied force depending on coordinate, velocity, and time

electromagnetic_radiation.name: electromagnetic radiation
electromagnetic_radiation.mini: Electromagnetic radiation is invisible waves of energy that travel at the speed of light and surround us everywhere: from solar heat to Wi-Fi signals.
electromagnetic_radiation.practical_application: Radio, television, microwaves, X-rays in medicine, visible light, infrared heat — all are electromagnetic waves differing only in wavelength.
electromagnetic_radiation.description_popular: Electromagnetic radiation is propagating oscillations of electric and magnetic fields. They carry energy and momentum without the need for a medium. The spectrum extends from long radio waves to gamma rays. Visible light is just a narrow band in this range.
electromagnetic_radiation.fun_fact_popular: A microwave oven uses radiation with a wavelength of about 12 cm, causing water molecules to rotate and heat food, but a dry plate hardly heats up.
electromagnetic_radiation.description_simple: These are waves, like ripples on water, but made of invisible electric and magnetic fields (force fields). They travel even in the vacuum of space, without air. Light and radio waves are the same thing, just different wave sizes (wavelength).
electromagnetic_radiation.history_simple: James Clerk Maxwell theoretically predicted such waves in the 19th century, and Heinrich Hertz later created them in the laboratory. Scientists realized that visible light is also an electromagnetic wave.
electromagnetic_radiation.how_it_works_simple: If you shake an electric charge, it creates a changing electric field around it, which in turn generates a magnetic one, and so on in a chain — the wave runs forward. It’s similar to jerking a rope: the impulse travels along it.
electromagnetic_radiation.description: Electromagnetic radiation is a disturbance of the electromagnetic field, propagating in vacuum at the speed of light c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s. Classically described by Maxwell's equations, from which a wave equation for fields E and B is derived. Solutions are transverse waves: vectors E and B are perpendicular to each other and to the direction of propagation. Quantum mechanically, radiation consists of photons with energy E = hν and momentum p = h/λ. Spectrum: radio (10³–10⁻¹ m), microwaves (10⁻¹–10⁻³ m), infrared (10⁻³–7×10⁻⁷ m), visible (7×10⁻⁷–4×10⁻⁷ m), ultraviolet (4×10⁻⁷–10⁻⁸ m), X-ray (10⁻⁸–10⁻¹² m), gamma (<10⁻¹² m).
electromagnetic_radiation.history: James Clerk Maxwell in 1865 unified electricity and magnetism, predicting electromagnetic waves whose speed matched the speed of light, indicating the electromagnetic nature of light. Heinrich Hertz in 1887 first experimentally produced radio waves. Wilhelm Röntgen discovered X-rays in 1895. Max Planck (1900) and Albert Einstein (1905) laid the quantum theory, explaining the photoelectric effect. Frederick Herschel discovered infrared radiation as early as 1800.
electromagnetic_radiation.how_it_works: According to Maxwell's equations, a changing magnetic field generates a vortex electric field, and vice versa, creating a self-sustaining wave. The energy of the wave is proportional to the square of the amplitude and frequency (Planck's formula for photons). The Doppler effect gives a red or blue shift when the source moves. Interaction with matter: absorption, reflection, refraction. Spectroscopy uses the decomposition of radiation to analyze the composition of substances.
key_problems: Hierarchy problem: why is the electromagnetic force many orders of magnitude stronger than gravity?
Nature of quantum entanglement in electromagnetic interactions
Interaction of light with metamaterials (e.g., for invisibility)
Quantum decoherence of electromagnetic states in hot environments
electromagnetic_radiation.fun_fact: The electromagnetic field has zero rest mass, so photons always fly at the speed of light, and time does not flow for them.

formulas.description: Wave equation for the electric field in vacuum
formulas.meaning: E — electric field intensity vector, c — speed of light in vacuum, c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s, ∇² — Laplace operator (sum of second spatial derivatives)

formulas.description: Planck-Einstein formula for photon energy
formulas.meaning: E — photon energy, h — Planck constant, h ≈ 6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s, ν — radiation frequency, c — speed of light, λ — wavelength

formulas.description: Relation of the speed of light to electric and magnetic constants
formulas.meaning: c — speed of light in vacuum, μ₀ — magnetic constant, μ₀ ≈ 4π×10⁻⁷ H/m, ε₀ — electric constant, ε₀ ≈ 8.854×10⁻¹² F/m

temperature.name: temperature
temperature.mini: Temperature is a measure of how strongly the tiny particles of matter shake. The hotter, the faster their dance.
temperature.practical_application: Thermometer outside, refrigerator, smartphone processor, weather, cooking food — everywhere we control or measure temperature.
temperature.description_popular: Temperature is a statistical measure of the average kinetic energy of random particle motion. In thermodynamics, it determines the direction of heat transfer (from hot to cold). The Kelvin scale is tied to absolute zero, where thermal motion is minimal.
temperature.fun_fact_popular: In space, far from stars, the temperature of the background radiation is only 2.7 K (−270 °C) — a remnant of the Big Bang.
temperature.description_simple: Temperature shows how hot an object is. Inside matter, atoms (tiny building blocks) are constantly moving; the higher the temperature, the faster they rush around. Absolute zero (−273 °C) is when motion almost freezes.
temperature.history_simple: The first thermometers were created in the 17th century by Galileo and Santorio. Later, Celsius and Fahrenheit devised convenient scales. Lord Kelvin in the 19th century proposed an absolute scale starting from zero.
temperature.how_it_works_simple: When touching a hot radiator, heat flows to the hand: fast-moving particles of the radiator transfer energy to slow particles of the hand — temperatures equalize.
temperature.description: Temperature is an intensive thermodynamic quantity characterizing equilibrium state. The zeroth law of thermodynamics: two systems in thermal equilibrium with a third have the same temperature. Microscopically, in equilibrium, the average kinetic energy per degree of freedom is (1/2)k_B T (equipartition theorem). In statistical mechanics, 1/T = ∂S/∂U. The absolute scale is defined by the Carnot cycle. Practical scales (ITS-90) use fixed reference points. Extreme temperatures are measured by various physical principles.
temperature.history: Quantitative measurements began with Galileo's thermoscope (1593). In 1714, Daniel Fahrenheit created a mercury thermometer and scale. Anders Celsius in 1742 proposed the centigrade scale. William Thomson (Lord Kelvin) in 1848 introduced the absolute scale. Ludwig Boltzmann statistically justified temperature via the Boltzmann distribution. Modern quantum thermometry achieves an accuracy of 10⁻⁶ K.
temperature.how_it_works: Temperature manifests through the Boltzmann distribution: the probability of a state with energy E is proportional to exp(-E/kT). Increasing T populates higher energy levels. Thermal radiation is described by Planck's law and Wien's displacement law. The velocity distribution of a gas is Maxwell-Boltzmann. Measurement: thermometers (liquid expansion), thermocouples (Seebeck effect), bolometers, pyrometers. The second law of thermodynamics forbids a perpetual motion machine, using temperature. Bekenstein-Hawking entropy relates black hole temperature to the horizon area.
key_problems: Definition of temperature in non-equilibrium systems (active media)
Limits of quantum thermometry at ultra-low temperatures
Temperature of black holes and the information paradox (firewall)
Measurement of temperature inside neutron stars via neutrinos
temperature.fun_fact: The highest temperature in the laboratory is about 5.5 trillion degrees (at the LHC), 250,000 times hotter than the Sun's center. The record for cold is a few picokelvins, where atoms almost come to a stop.

formulas.description: Average kinetic energy per degree of freedom
formulas.meaning: ⟨E_k⟩ — average kinetic energy, k_B — Boltzmann constant, k_B ≈ 1.381×10⁻²³ J/K, T — absolute temperature in kelvins

formulas.description: Boltzmann distribution: probability of a state with energy E_i
formulas.meaning: P_i — probability of the state, E_i — energy of the state, k_B — Boltzmann constant, T — temperature

formulas.description: Wien's displacement law for black-body radiation
formulas.meaning: λ_max — wavelength of maximum emission, b — Wien's constant, b ≈ 2.898×10⁻³ m·K, T — absolute temperature

formulas.description: Stefan-Boltzmann law
formulas.meaning: j — radiant emittance per unit area, σ — Stefan-Boltzmann constant, σ ≈ 5.67×10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴, T — temperature

internal_energy.name: internal energy
internal_energy.mini: The internal energy of a body is the energy of motion and interaction of all its microparticles. It's like an invisible 'reserve of vitality' of matter, which grows upon heating and decreases upon cooling.
internal_energy.practical_application: Understanding internal energy makes it possible to efficiently convert heat into work in engines and refrigeration units, as well as to predict chemical reactions and phase transitions.
internal_energy.description_popular: Internal energy U is the total energy of chaotic (thermal) motion of microparticles of a system and the potential energy of their interaction. Unlike the mechanical energy of a body as a whole, internal energy does not depend on the body's speed or position in space, but is determined by its temperature, volume, and chemical composition. The first law of thermodynamics relates the change in internal energy (ΔU) to the amount of heat transferred (Q) and work done on the system (W): ΔU = Q - W.
internal_energy.fun_fact_popular: Even ice at subzero temperature has internal energy: water molecules in the crystal lattice continue to vibrate, and only at absolute zero (−273.15 °C) does this motion theoretically cease.
internal_energy.description_simple: Every body consists of tiny particles — molecules or atoms. They never stand still: in gases and liquids they fly freely, in solids they vibrate around their positions. The sum of the energies of all these motions (kinetic energy — the energy of motion) and interactions between particles (potential energy — the energy of position/interaction) is called internal energy. It can be changed in two ways: performing mechanical work on the body (e.g., compressing a gas) or transferring heat to it (placing it on fire).
internal_energy.history_simple: The concept of internal energy was formed in the 19th century together with the science of heat — thermodynamics. Scientists like James Joule and Rudolf Clausius showed that heat is not a special substance, but a form of energy related to particle motion.
internal_energy.how_it_works_simple: Imagine a soccer ball being inflated. By striking the ball, you compress the air inside, and the particles start moving faster — internal energy increases, the ball heats up. Heat from a campfire also makes the air molecules around it move more energetically — the air's internal energy increases, and it rises.
internal_energy.description: In thermodynamics, internal energy is a state function determined by macroscopic parameters of the system (temperature T, volume V, number of particles N). For an ideal gas, it depends only on temperature and the number of degrees of freedom of the molecules: U = (f/2) N k_B T, where f is the number of degrees of freedom, k_B is the Boltzmann constant, T is the absolute temperature. In real systems, a contribution from intermolecular interactions is added, which leads, for example, to the existence of latent heat of phase transitions. In statistical mechanics, internal energy is expressed via the partition function: U = - ∂(ln Z)/∂β, where β = 1/(k_B T).
internal_energy.history: The idea that heat is related to particle motion dates back to the work of Benjamin Thompson (Count Rumford) at the end of the 18th century, but quantitative description began in the 19th century. James Joule in 1843 experimentally proved the equivalence of mechanical work and heat. Rudolf Clausius introduced the concept of internal energy in 1850, formulating the first law of thermodynamics. Ludwig Boltzmann developed a statistical interpretation, linking internal energy to the microscopic motion of particles. James Clerk Maxwell and others developed the kinetic theory of gases, which gave an explicit form of the dependence of internal energy on temperature.
internal_energy.how_it_works: The change in internal energy of a system occurs according to the first law of thermodynamics: ΔU = Q - W, where Q is heat transferred to the system, W is work done by the system on the external environment. For an ideal gas, internal energy increases with temperature: faster-moving molecules have greater kinetic energy. During phase transitions (melting, vaporization), energy is spent on overcoming intermolecular forces, not on increasing temperature — this is how latent heat arises. In the thermodynamics of non-equilibrium processes, the local change in internal energy is described by the heat conduction equation. Closely related concepts: entropy (measure of disorder), enthalpy (U + pV), free energy. Laws: first law of thermodynamics, second law of thermodynamics (establishing the direction of processes).
key_problems: Exact calculation of internal energy for strongly interacting many-particle systems (e.g., liquids, solids with defects) is a complex theoretical task.
Reconciliation of the second law of thermodynamics with the reversibility of microscopic mechanical laws (Loschmidt's paradox).
Behavior of internal energy in extreme conditions (ultra-high pressures, strong fields) — quantum and relativistic effects must be taken into account.
internal_energy.fun_fact: The internal energy of one liter of water at room temperature is roughly equal to the energy released by burning 10 grams of gasoline. But you cannot extract this energy 'just like that' — the second law of thermodynamics prevents it: heat spontaneously flows only from hot to cold.

formulas.description: First law of thermodynamics
formulas.meaning: ΔU — change in internal energy of the system, Q — amount of heat transferred to the system, W — work done by the system on the surroundings.

formulas.description: Internal energy of an ideal gas
formulas.meaning: U — internal energy, f — number of degrees of freedom of one molecule (for a monatomic gas f=3), N — number of gas particles, k_B — Boltzmann constant (k_B ≈ 1.38×10^{-23} J/K), T — absolute temperature in kelvins.

quantum_state.name: quantum state
quantum_state.mini: A quantum state is a complete characteristic of a microparticle, determining everything we can learn about it: where it can be located, with what probability, and its momentum. It is like a chess position that records all possible future moves.
quantum_state.practical_application: Quantum states underlie lasers, transistors, MRI scanners, and quantum computers—any device that uses quantum effects operates with particle states.
quantum_state.description_popular: A quantum state is a mathematical object (state vector) in Hilbert space (an abstract infinite-dimensional space) that contains all information about a system. It evolves deterministically according to the Schrödinger equation, but the measurement result is fundamentally random: for each observable quantity, there is a set of possible values, and measurement yields one of them with a certain probability. Moreover, the state after measurement collapses to an eigenstate of the measured observable operator. The property of superposition allows for 'ghost' states where a particle passes through two slits simultaneously.
quantum_state.fun_fact_popular: A quantum state can be entangled: two particles separated by kilometers are described by a single state, and measuring one instantly determines the result of measuring the other—Einstein called this 'spooky action at a distance.'
quantum_state.description_simple: In the classical world, we are accustomed to objects having precise positions and speeds. In the microcosm, quantum mechanics reigns: a particle does not simultaneously have a precise location and a precise velocity. Its state is described by a wave function (a mathematical rule) that tells us with what probability we will find the particle in a particular place and with what momentum (mass times velocity). The most astonishing thing is that until we measure, the particle seems to exist in all possible states at once—this is called superposition.
quantum_state.history_simple: The idea of quantum states was born at the beginning of the 20th century when Erwin Schrödinger wrote his famous equation describing the evolution of the wave function. Soon after, Max Born proposed a probabilistic interpretation: the square of the wave function gives the probability of finding a particle at a given point.
quantum_state.how_it_works_simple: Just as a person's shadow on the wall does not show all the details, measuring a quantum state captures only one of the possible 'shadows' of the particle. Before measurement, the electron in an atom does not orbit like a planet but is smeared across a cloud of probabilities. When we 'look' (measure), the cloud collapses into a point—this is the collapse of the wave function.
quantum_state.description: In quantum mechanics, a pure state of a system is described by a vector |ψ⟩ from Hilbert space. For a system in a mixed state, the density matrix ρ = Σ p_i |ψ_i⟩⟨ψ_i| is used. The evolution of a pure state is given by the Schrödinger equation: iħ ∂/∂t |ψ⟩ = Ĥ|ψ⟩, where Ĥ is the Hamiltonian. Observables are represented by Hermitian operators, and their eigenvalues are the possible measurement results. When measuring quantity A, the state is projected onto an eigenspace with probability given by the Born rule. Quantum states can possess symmetries (e.g., bosonic/fermionic permutation properties) and can be coherent—maintaining phase relationships.
quantum_state.history: Key steps in understanding quantum states were taken in the 1920s. Erwin Schrödinger introduced the wave function and his equation in 1926, hoping to bring visualization back to quantum theory. That same year, Max Born proposed the probabilistic interpretation of |ψ|². Werner Heisenberg developed matrix mechanics, and Paul Dirac unified the approaches into a general theory of transformations and introduced bra-ket notation. John von Neumann formalized the concept of Hilbert space for states. Later, in 1935, Schrödinger described the 'cat' paradox and coined the term 'entanglement.' The study of quantum states continues in quantum information and quantum computing.
quantum_state.how_it_works: The state |ψ⟩ contains probability amplitudes, the squared moduli of which determine the probabilities of measurement outcomes. The Schrödinger equation iħ d|ψ⟩/dt = Ĥ|ψ⟩ ensures unitary evolution: superpositions are preserved. Measurement causes an irreversible collapse to one of the possible states with probability |⟨φ_i|ψ⟩|². For mixed states, the density matrix is used, evolving according to the Liouville–von Neumann equation. Entangled states, such as the Bell singlet (|01⟩ - |10⟩)/√2, exhibit nonlocal correlations. The superposition principle enables quantum parallelism—the basis of quantum computing. Decoherence (interaction with the environment) rapidly destroys pure states on macroscopic scales.
key_problems: The measurement problem: why and how does the wave function collapse?
Preserving coherence: creating large-scale quantum states resistant to decoherence.
Interpretations of quantum mechanics: many-worlds, Copenhagen, hidden variables, etc.
Quantum information theory and state complexity: efficient description of multipartite entangled states.
quantum_state.fun_fact: In quantum field theory, the vacuum is not 'just nothing' but a complex quantum state with fluctuating fields, from which virtual particles are constantly being born and vanishing.

formulas.description: Schrödinger equation for quantum state evolution
formulas.meaning: i — imaginary unit (i² = -1), ħ — reduced Planck constant (ħ ≈ 1.0545718×10^{-34} J·s), ∂ψ/∂t — partial derivative of the wave function ψ with respect to time t, Ĥ — Hamiltonian operator (operator of total energy of the system), ψ — wave function describing the quantum state of the system in coordinate representation.

strong_interaction.name: strong interaction
strong_interaction.mini: The strong interaction is the most powerful in nature. It 'glues' quarks inside protons and neutrons and holds them together in the atomic nucleus, like a titanic glue that becomes stronger the farther you try to pull apart.
strong_interaction.practical_application: The energy of the strong interaction powers the Sun and stars through nuclear fusion, and is also used in nuclear reactors and potentially in future thermonuclear power plants.
strong_interaction.description_popular: The strong interaction is one of the four fundamental forces of nature. It is described by quantum chromodynamics (QCD), a theory based on color charges of quarks (analogous to electric charge but of three types: red, green, blue). Massless gluons carry the interaction between quarks and themselves carry color charge, making QCD highly nonlinear. Two key properties are confinement—quarks and gluons cannot exist freely; they are always locked inside hadrons (protons, neutrons, etc.)—and asymptotic freedom—at very short distances, the interaction weakens, and quarks behave almost as free particles.
strong_interaction.fun_fact_popular: The proton's mass is almost entirely (99%) due not to the masses of its constituent quarks but to the energy of the gluon field that holds them together—that is, according to the famous formula E=mc², the binding energy manifests itself as mass.
strong_interaction.description_simple: The atomic nucleus consists of protons and neutrons, which in turn consist of even smaller particles—quarks. Protons have a positive electric charge and should repel each other, but in the nucleus they are held together by the strong interaction. It is much stronger than the electric repulsion, but it acts only at very short distances—about the size of a proton. The strong interaction is carried by special particles—gluons (from 'glue'), which 'glue' quarks together.
strong_interaction.history_simple: In the 1930s, scientists realized that there must be a special attraction in the nucleus, otherwise it would fall apart. Japanese physicist Hideki Yukawa predicted the existence of carrier particles—mesons—in 1935. Later, in the 1960s–70s, quantum chromodynamics (QCD) was developed, where the carriers are gluons and the constituents are quarks.
strong_interaction.how_it_works_simple: Imagine two fish in an aquarium with very thick water: when they are far apart, the water hardly bothers them, but as they approach, a powerful attraction arises due to water pressure that draws them together. It's the same with quarks: the farther they are from each other, the more a 'string' of the gluon field stretches between them, and the energy grows. If you try to pull quarks apart, the energy becomes so great that new particles are born—quark-antiquark pairs—and they combine into new stable particles.
strong_interaction.description: The strong interaction is a gauge field theory with group SU(3) describing the dynamics of quarks and gluons. The QCD Lagrangian: L = Σ_q ψ̄_q (iγ^μ D_μ - m_q) ψ_q - (1/4) G^a_{μν} G^{a μν}, where D_μ = ∂_μ - i g_s T^a A^a_μ is the covariant derivative, G^a_{μν} is the gluon field tensor, and g_s is the strong coupling constant. Quarks exist in six flavors (up, down, strange, charm, bottom, top) and three colors. Confinement of color charges leads to a linear interaction potential at distances ~1 fm (10^-15 m): V(r) ~ σ r, where σ ~ 1 GeV/fm is the string tension. Due to asymptotic freedom, at high energies the coupling constant α_s = g_s²/(4π) decreases logarithmically.
strong_interaction.history: The first indications of the strong interaction appeared in the 1920s with the discovery of the atomic nucleus. In 1935, Hideki Yukawa proposed the meson theory of nuclear forces. The development of accelerators in the 1950s–60s led to the discovery of many hadrons, necessitating the introduction of the quark model (Gell-Mann, Zweig) in 1964. The color degree of freedom and the confinement hypothesis were formulated in the early 1970s, and quantum chromodynamics as a gauge theory with SU(3) group was constructed by Gross, Wilczek, and Politzer in 1973 (Nobel Prize 2004). Experimental confirmation of asymptotic freedom came from deep inelastic scattering.
strong_interaction.how_it_works: The strong interaction binds quarks through the exchange of gluons. Gluons are massless but carry color, so they interact with each other, forming 'gluon chains' and strings. When trying to separate quarks, the potential energy of the string increases, and upon exceeding the threshold for pair production of quark-antiquark, the string breaks with the formation of new hadrons—this phenomenon is hadronization. In high-energy collisions, quarks and gluons form quark-gluon plasma—a state in which they are weakly bound. The residual strong interaction between colorless hadrons manifests as nuclear forces holding nucleons together in the nucleus (an effective interaction analogous to van der Waals forces in molecular physics). Accompanying laws: Pauli principle, gauge invariance, asymptotic freedom.
key_problems: The nature of confinement: a rigorous proof of the impossibility of free quarks existing within QCD is lacking.
The equation of state of quark-gluon plasma and QCD phase transitions.
Computing hadron properties (masses, spins) directly from QCD requires large computational resources (lattice QCD).
The structure of the nucleus at the quark level and the role of gluons in the proton spin (the proton spin problem).
strong_interaction.fun_fact: Quark-gluon plasma, which existed in the first microseconds after the Big Bang, is recreated in accelerators, such as the LHC, by colliding heavy ions at near-light speeds—at such points, the temperature exceeds 5 trillion degrees.

formulas.description: Quantum Chromodynamics Lagrangian
formulas.meaning: ℒ_QCD — strong interaction Lagrangian, ∑_q — sum over quark flavors, ψ̄_q, ψ_q — quark fields, γ^μ — Dirac matrices, D_μ = ∂_μ - i g_s T^a A^a_μ — covariant derivative, g_s — strong coupling constant, T^a — generators of SU(3) group (Gell-Mann matrices), A^a_μ — gluon fields, G^a_{μν} = ∂_μ A^a_ν - ∂_ν A^a_μ + g_s f^{abc} A^b_μ A^c_ν — gluon field strength tensor, f^{abc} — structure constants of SU(3) group, m_q — quark masses.

proper_time.name: proper time
proper_time.mini: Proper time is the time measured by a clock moving with the observer. Due to speed or gravity, his own clock runs slower than an identical clock left at rest.
proper_time.practical_application: Without correction for the difference in proper time between GPS satellites and ground clocks, navigation error would accumulate to 10 km per day.
proper_time.description_popular: Proper time is an invariant quantity that does not depend on the reference frame. It equals the time measured by a clock moving with the object. For a stationary observer, the time of such an object runs slower, and the effect is stronger the higher the speed or the closer a massive body. In everyday life, the slowing is imperceptible, but it is detected by precise atomic clocks.
proper_time.fun_fact_popular: An astronaut who spends six months on the International Space Station ages 0.005 seconds less than his peers on Earth.
proper_time.description_simple: Proper time is the time shown by a clock flying with an object. If the object moves very fast or is in a strong gravitational field, its clock runs slower than an identical clock left on Earth. It is like a stopwatch: for the runner it ticks normally, but from the side it seems that time is stretched for him.
proper_time.history_simple: The idea of time dilation was proposed by Hendrik Lorentz, and Albert Einstein created the special theory of relativity in 1905, which explained this effect.
proper_time.how_it_works_simple: According to Einstein's theory, the speed of light is an absolute limit, and time is forced to slow down for fast objects so they do not overtake light. The closer the speed is to light speed, the more noticeable the difference.
proper_time.description: In special relativity, proper time τ is the length of a world line in Minkowski space, invariant under Lorentz transformations. In general relativity, the definition is generalized through the metric tensor: proper time is measured along a timelike geodesic and depends on the gravitational potential. For a Schwarzschild black hole, the rate of proper time slows down when approaching the event horizon. Physically, proper time is realized in any local periodic processes.
proper_time.history: The concept of local time appeared in the works of Hendrik Lorentz (1895) and Henri Poincaré (1900) as an auxiliary quantity for electrodynamics. In 1905, Albert Einstein gave it fundamental status in special relativity, and Hermann Minkowski geometrized it as world line length in 1908. In 1915, in general relativity, proper time became the basis for describing gravity. Experimental confirmations were obtained in the Hafele–Keating experiments with atomic clocks (1971).
proper_time.how_it_works: In special relativity, proper time τ is related to coordinate time t by dτ = dt √(1 – v²/c²). As v → c, proper time slows sharply. In general relativity, the metric tensor is used: dτ² = –g₀₀ dt², for example, in the exterior Schwarzschild solution g₀₀ = –(1 – rₛ/r), and as r → rₛ (event horizon), proper time tends to zero. Time dilation for moving particles is confirmed by the lifetime of muons in the atmosphere.
key_problems: Compatibility of proper time with quantum mechanics: should time be quantized at the Planck scale?
Are closed timelike curves possible without violating causality in quantum gravity?
proper_time.fun_fact: An experiment with cosmic-ray muons shows that due to relativistic time dilation, the particles manage to fly through the atmosphere, although by laboratory standards they should have decayed earlier.

formulas.description: Relationship of proper time with coordinate time in special relativity
formulas.meaning: τ — proper time, t — coordinate time, v — object speed, c — speed of light (c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s)

formulas.description: Expression through metric tensor in general relativity
formulas.meaning: g₀₀ — temporal component of the metric tensor (for the Schwarzschild metric g₀₀ = –(1 – 2GM/(rc²)), G — gravitational constant, M — mass, r — distance to the center, c — speed of light)

molecular_cloud.name: molecular cloud
molecular_cloud.mini: A molecular cloud is a giant cold agglomeration of gas and dust where new stars are being born right now. Inside it, it is so dark and cold that light from neighboring stars does not penetrate through the thickness of the material.
molecular_cloud.practical_application: Analysis of molecules in such clouds allows tracing chemical evolution from simple compounds to organics from which life could arise.
molecular_cloud.description_popular: Molecular clouds are among the coldest (10–30 K) and densest parts of the interstellar medium, composed primarily of H₂. Their masses range from tens to millions of solar masses, and sizes reach hundreds of light-years. Dust blocks ultraviolet radiation, allowing complex molecules like alcohols and amino acids to exist.
molecular_cloud.fun_fact_popular: In the Sagittarius B2 cloud, ethyl alcohol has been detected in amounts exceeding everything humanity has produced throughout history.
molecular_cloud.description_simple: A molecular cloud is a huge cloud of very cold gas (mostly hydrogen) and cosmic dust. It resembles a giant opaque fog that can stretch over tens of light-years. Gravity compresses the densest clumps, and stars ignite there, like lanterns in the fog.
molecular_cloud.history_simple: The first dark nebula was described by William Herschel as early as the 18th century, but proving that they are real clouds of molecules was only possible in the 1960s with radio telescopes that detected carbon monoxide radiation.
molecular_cloud.how_it_works_simple: Gas and dust are attracted by gravity, forming dense cores. When the temperature and pressure in the core reach millions of degrees, a thermonuclear reaction begins—and a star is born. The remaining material can become planets.
molecular_cloud.description: A molecular cloud is a gravitationally bound region of interstellar gas with a temperature of 10–30 K and a number density of 10²–10⁶ particles/cm³. The main component is molecular hydrogen H₂ (not directly observable due to symmetry), mapped via CO lines. The internal structure is hierarchical: filaments, Jeans cores, protostellar disks. Dust (silicates, carbonaceous particles) serves as a catalyst for chemical reactions and provides cooling through infrared radiation.
molecular_cloud.history: The cataloging of dark nebulae began with Edward Barnard in 1919. In 1937, Bengt Strömgren theoretically justified ionization zones around stars. Radio observations of CO lines by Wilson, Jefferts, and Penzias in 1970 confirmed the molecular composition. Fred Hoyle developed the theory of star formation in clouds in the 1950s. The Herschel Space Observatory (2009–2013) mapped clouds in detail in the far infrared.
molecular_cloud.how_it_works: The gravitational contraction of cloud fragments is described by the Jeans criterion: when the critical mass is exceeded, the gas collapses. Cooling through rotational transitions of CO and thermal emission of dust allows heat to be shed. Magnetic fields and turbulence slow down the collapse. When the central temperature reaches ~10⁷ K, the hydrogen cycle starts, and the protostar enters the main sequence.
key_problems: Why is the observed star formation efficiency (the fraction of gas turned into stars) only a few percent?
What is the role of magnetic fields in maintaining turbulence and preventing rapid collapse?
Are complex organic molecules formed in molecular clouds in quantities sufficient for panspermia?
molecular_cloud.fun_fact: If a molecular cloud of average density were compressed to the size of the Earth's atmosphere, its material would be billions of times more tenuous than air.

void.name: void
void.mini: A void is a cosmic emptiness with a diameter of hundreds of millions of light-years, almost devoid of galaxies. It resembles a gigantic soap bubble from which matter has drained into surrounding walls and filaments.
void.practical_application: Studying voids helps to refine the properties of dark energy and test alternative theories of gravity without resorting to complex ground-based experiments.
void.description_popular: Voids are empty spaces with diameters from 30 to 500 megaparsecs, forming, together with galactic filaments, the large-scale structure of the Universe. The matter density in them is orders of magnitude below average, and the typical volume of voids accounts for up to 90% of the observed space. They naturally arise in cold dark matter models due to gravitational instability of primordial fluctuations.
void.fun_fact_popular: At the center of the largest known void, the Boötes Void, only one dwarf galaxy has been discovered—an absolute record of isolation.
void.description_simple: A void is a vast region in the Universe where there are very few galaxies. Such empty spaces resemble holes in the cosmic web. At the center of a void, the sky would be absolutely black to the naked eye. Gravity over billions of years has pulled matter into clumps, leaving almost empty spaces.
void.history_simple: Astronomers first saw large voids on maps of galaxy distribution in the late 1970s. In 1981, the Boötes Void was discovered—one of the most prominent 'holes' with a size of over 300 million light-years.
void.how_it_works_simple: After the Big Bang, matter was distributed unevenly. In regions with slightly lower density, gravity does not attract matter as strongly, and it 'flows out' into denser regions. Dark energy additionally accelerates the expansion of these voids.
void.description: A cosmic void is a region with density contrast δ = (ρ – ρ̄)/ρ̄ < –0.8, where ρ is the local and ρ̄ is the average density of the Universe. The hierarchy of voids extends from minivoids (~10 Mpc) to supervoids like the Eridanus Supervoid. The spatial distribution of voids follows the cosmological foam model, and their evolution is described by the solution of supercritical expansion in the ΛCDM model. The internal dynamics gives an effective negative sign of gravitation, pushing out residual matter.
void.history: The first voids were identified in samples of bright galaxies (Boötes Void, Kirshner et al., 1981). Yakov Zeldovich predicted the formation of voids through 'pancake' dynamics in 1970. The Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) has cataloged thousands of voids since 2000. The Millennium Simulation (2005) confirmed that in the standard cosmological model, voids are inevitable and reproduce the observed statistics.
void.how_it_works: Voids form from negative density perturbations in the cosmic microwave background. Expanding faster than the Hubble flow, they push matter to the boundaries, where walls form. Dark energy (cosmological constant Λ ≈ 10⁻³⁵ s⁻²) ensures the accelerated divergence of walls. In the spherical model, void growth ends when the internal density drops to ~0.2ρ̄; then Λ dominates.
key_problems: Can models of dark energy be distinguished by the shape and sizes of voids?
Why are isolated galaxies still found in the deepest voids—is this a problem of gas supply?
Are there voids that are absolutely empty, without dark matter?
void.fun_fact: The cosmic microwave background radiation, passing through a giant void, experiences the Sachs–Wolfe effect: the photon temperature decreases by a few millionths of a degree.

gravitational_singularity.name: gravitational singularity
gravitational_singularity.mini: A gravitational singularity is the 'bottom' of a black hole: a point where matter is compressed so strongly that it occupies zero volume, and gravity is infinitely large. Even light cannot leave it, so it is hidden behind the event horizon (an invisible boundary of no return).
gravitational_singularity.practical_application: The study of singularities stimulates the development of quantum gravity theory, which could describe the birth of the Universe in the Big Bang and possibly explain whether wormholes—hypothetical shortcuts through spacetime—could exist.
gravitational_singularity.description_popular: A gravitational singularity is a region predicted by general relativity (Einstein's theory of gravity) where the density of matter and the curvature of spacetime become infinitely large. Such singularities hide at the centers of black holes and probably existed at the moment of the Big Bang. Under these conditions, normal physical laws break down, so scientists seek a more complete theory—quantum gravity, which will unite gravity and quantum mechanics.
gravitational_singularity.fun_fact_popular: Although the singularity is hidden by the event horizon, its powerful gravity holds stars in rapid orbits around the center of the Milky Way, betraying the presence of a supermassive black hole.
gravitational_singularity.description_simple: A black hole is like a funnel in space: everything that crosses the edge inevitably falls to the center. There, at the 'bottom', is the singularity—a point where the density of matter and the curvature of spacetime reach inconceivable magnitudes. Mathematics predicts infinities here, pointing to a gap in our understanding of physics.
gravitational_singularity.history_simple: In 1916, Karl Schwarzschild found a solution to Einstein's equations describing a spherical body where a singularity appeared. Later, John Wheeler coined the name 'black hole', and Stephen Hawking proved that singularities must form when large stars die.
gravitational_singularity.how_it_works_simple: When a very massive star runs out of fuel, its core collapses inward. Gravity squeezes matter so much that even atomic nuclei break apart, and everything slumps into an infinitely small point—the singularity. The curvature of spacetime becomes so steep that any path leads only inward, much like water entering a funnel inevitably goes down the drain. Therefore, neither light nor matter can escape back out.
gravitational_singularity.description: In general relativity, a gravitational singularity is a point (or closed region) on a spacetime diagram where geodesics—trajectories of freely falling particles—break off. Curvature invariants, such as the Kretschmann scalar, tend to infinity. Singularities are divided into spacelike (center of a Schwarzschild black hole) and timelike (in Kerr and Reissner-Nordström solutions). Physically, the appearance of a singularity means that the predictive ability of the theory ends: the equations give infinite answers. Quantum effects are expected to 'smear' the singularity on scales of the order of the Planck length (10⁻³⁵ m), so a theory of quantum gravity is needed.
gravitational_singularity.history: In 1916, Karl Schwarzschild found an exact solution to Einstein's equations for a spherically symmetric mass distribution, which contained a singularity at the center (at r=0). For a long time this was considered a mathematical curiosity. In the 1960s, John Wheeler and his colleagues developed the concept of a black hole as a real astrophysical object. Singularity theorems proved by Roger Penrose (1965) and Stephen Hawking showed that during gravitational collapse a singularity inevitably forms. Later, Hawking applied quantum theory to black holes and predicted radiation that should lead to their evaporation; in doing so, the singularity may be smeared out by quantum fluctuations.
gravitational_singularity.how_it_works: When a star with mass above the Oppenheimer–Volkoff limit (about 3 solar masses) exhausts its nuclear fuel, the pressure of degenerate neutrons is unable to resist gravity. The core collapses, matter is squeezed, and spacetime curves so strongly that the metric of the Schwarzschild equation becomes singular. Inside the event horizon, the radial coordinate becomes timelike, so any motion is directed toward the singularity. Quantum corrections change the scenario: Hawking radiation can carry away mass and, possibly, information; in a full theory of quantum gravity, the singularity is likely smoothed out or disappears.
key_problems: Are singularities real physical objects or do they merely indicate the incompleteness of classical theory? Are they smoothed out by quantum gravity?
Why did the Big Bang singularity have negligibly small entropy, while black holes have enormous entropy?
How to overcome the problem of information loss in black holes (the information paradox)?
Which of the theories of quantum gravity (string theory, loop quantum gravity) describes singularities and gives testable predictions?
gravitational_singularity.fun_fact: In solutions for charged (Reissner–Nordström) or rotating (Kerr) black holes, at extreme parameters the event horizon can disappear, exposing the singularity. Such a naked singularity would be visible to a distant observer, which is forbidden by the hypothetical principle of 'cosmic censorship', formulated by Roger Penrose.

formulas.description: Event horizon radius for a non-rotating black hole (Schwarzschild solution): the singularity lies inside, at r=0
formulas.meaning: R_s is the Schwarzschild radius, G is the gravitational constant (G ≈ 6.674×10⁻¹¹ m³/(kg·s²)), M is the black hole mass, c is the speed of light (c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s)

raw.mini: A gravitational singularity is a point where matter is compressed to unimaginable density, and the force of gravity is so great that even light cannot leave it. It is hidden inside a black hole behind the event horizon (a boundary from which nothing returns).
raw.practical_application: The prediction of singularities drives the creation of quantum gravity—a theory that will one day explain the birth of the Universe and, perhaps, open the way to travel through wormholes.
raw.description_popular: A gravitational singularity is a region where the density of matter and the curvature of spacetime tend to infinity according to general relativity (Einstein's theory of gravity). It arises at the center of black holes and occasionally at the initial moments of the Universe—the Big Bang. In a singularity, known physical laws stop working, and quantum gravity (an attempt to unify quantum mechanics with gravity) is required.
raw.fun_fact_popular: Although the singularity hides behind the event horizon, its influence is palpable: it is the gravity of the singularity that holds stars in orbits around central black holes in galaxies like the Milky Way.
raw.description_simple: A black hole works like a funnel, pulling everything in. At the bottom of this funnel is the singularity: a point where matter is compressed to the limit and the curvature of space (how mass makes it bend) becomes infinite. Here the equations predict infinite values, indicating the incompleteness of our theories.
raw.history_simple: Karl Schwarzschild in 1916 obtained the first solution to Einstein's equations that featured a singularity. Later, John Archibald Wheeler popularized the concept of 'black hole', and Stephen Hawking proved the inevitability of singularities in the collapse of massive stars.
raw.how_it_works_simple: Massive stars, when they die, contract under their own gravity. Gravity is so strong that atoms break apart, and all matter falls into a single point—the singularity. The surrounding space curves so steeply that lines leading outward loop back; therefore, neither light nor matter can escape—like water flowing down a drain inevitably reaches the center.
raw.description: In the framework of classical general relativity, a gravitational singularity is a point (or closed surface) on a spacetime diagram where geodesics—the paths of freely falling particles—break off and cannot be continued. Energy density and curvature invariants, such as the Kretschmann scalar, diverge to infinity. Singularities can be spacelike (as at the center of a Schwarzschild black hole) and timelike (in charged and rotating solutions). Physically, a singularity signals a breakdown of the theory's predictive power: the equations cease to give finite answers. Therefore, it is believed that a real description should include quantum effects smoothing out singularities at Planck scales (about 10⁻³⁵ m).
raw.history: In 1916, Karl Schwarzschild obtained the first exact solution to Einstein's equations for a spherically symmetric mass, which featured a singularity at zero radius. For a long time it was considered a mathematical artifact. In the 1960s, John Archibald Wheeler and his colleagues put forward the concept of a 'black hole' as an astrophysical object. Singularity theorems proved by Roger Penrose (1965) and Stephen Hawking showed that a singularity inevitably forms during gravitational collapse. Later, Hawking applied quantum mechanics to black holes, predicting Hawking radiation, which could lead to the evaporation of black holes and quantum blurriness of the singularity.
raw.how_it_works: During gravitational collapse of a massive star (with mass exceeding the Oppenheimer–Volkoff limit, about 3 solar masses), the pressure of degenerate neutrons cannot stop the contraction. Matter is compressed into a superdense state, and spacetime according to Einstein's equations becomes so curved that the metric becomes singular. In the simplest case of a non-rotating and uncharged black hole (Schwarzschild solution), the radial geodesic coordinate becomes timelike inside the event horizon, and any motion is directed toward the singularity at the center. Quantum effects can change the picture: thanks to Hawking radiation, information about infalling matter might be released, and the singularity itself could become smeared or disappear in a full theory of quantum gravity.
key_problems: Are singularities smoothed out by quantum gravity? Do they have physical reality or are they just a mathematical construct?
Why did the Big Bang singularity have negligibly small entropy, while black holes have enormous entropy?
How to overcome the problem of information loss in black holes (the information paradox)?
Which quantum theory of gravity (string theory, loop quantum gravity) will provide a predictive description of singularities?
raw.fun_fact: In charged black holes (Reissner–Nordström) or rotating (Kerr), a naked singularity is possible: under certain parameters the event horizon disappears, and the singularity becomes visible to an outside observer, which is forbidden by the cosmic censorship hypothesis proposed by Roger Penrose.

laser.name: laser
laser.mini: A laser is a device that creates light of incredible purity and directionality. Its waves move perfectly in sync, like soldiers marching in step, so the beam does not spread and can be focused into a tiny spot.
laser.practical_application: Lasers are used everywhere: they cut and weld metal, read barcodes, transmit terabytes of information over fiber optic cables, and allow measuring the distance from Earth to the Moon with millimeter precision.
laser.description_popular: Laser (Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation) creates coherent monochromatic radiation. The main components: an active medium (gas, liquid, crystal, or semiconductor), a pumping system to supply energy, and an optical resonator—two mirrors between which light is repeatedly reflected. The operating principle is based on stimulated emission: a passing photon 'nudges' an excited atom to emit an exactly identical photon, doubling the radiation. An avalanche of identical photons arises, which after amplification forms a laser beam.
