qubit.mini: Qubit — a tossed coin: in flight it is simultaneously heads and tails. Unlike an ordinary bit, which always lies strictly with one side up.
qubit.practical_application: Qubits enable quantum computers to solve in seconds problems that would take conventional supercomputers billions of years: for example, modeling new drugs or cracking cryptographic codes.
qubit.description_popular: Qubit — the elementary carrier of quantum information. Unlike a bit, rigidly fixed at 0 or 1, a qubit realizes superposition — a mathematical combination of two states described by complex amplitudes. Measurement collapses the superposition into one of the basis states with a probability equal to the squared modulus of the amplitude.
qubit.fun_fact_popular: Even the simplest operation on a qubit is mathematically equivalent to rotating the Bloch sphere — an imaginary three-dimensional sphere on whose surface all possible states live.
qubit.description_simple: A qubit is like a ballerina spinning on pointe shoes. While the dance lasts, her pose continuously changes between classical positions. Stopping the dance means forcing her to freeze in one pose, similarly measuring a qubit yields either 0 or 1. But before measurement, the qubit exists in quantum superposition (a blend of states 0 and 1 simultaneously).
qubit.history_simple: The term 'qubit' was proposed by physicist Benjamin Schumacher in 1995, inspired by work on quantum computing. The very idea of quantum information was developed by Richard Feynman and David Deutsch since the 1980s.
qubit.how_it_works_simple: Any interaction with a qubit is compared to a hand touching a spinning top: it immediately changes its rotation. In the quantum world, qubit control is designed so that they 'feel' each other without direct contact — thanks to entanglement (a special connection at a distance).
qubit.description: Qubit — a quantum system with a Hilbert space of dimension 2. Mathematically described by a state vector |ψ⟩ = α|0⟩ + β|1⟩ in C², normalized by ⟨ψ|ψ⟩ = 1. Physically realized, for example, by two levels of a superconducting circuit, electron spin, or photon polarization. Superposition and entanglement of multiple qubits lead to exponential growth of the computational space.
qubit.history: The predecessor of the qubit was the concept of the Feynman quantum computer (1982). David Deutsch formalized the concept of a universal quantum computer, and the term 'qubit' was introduced by Benjamin Schumacher in 1995, generalizing the idea of a quantum bit for information theory. John Archibald Wheeler earlier proposed the doctrine 'it from bit' about the fundamental role of information, which resonates with the modern view of the qubit as the basic informational atom of the universe.
qubit.how_it_works: The evolution of a qubit is unitary and reversible: operators transform the state through rotations in state space. Measurement breaks unitarity — the superposition collapses into one of the eigenvectors of the measured observable with probability determined by Born's rule. Physically, qubit control is achieved with resonant microwave pulses (for superconducting systems) or laser fields (for ions) implementing rotations by the required angle.
key_problems: Decoherence — loss of quantum properties due to interaction with the environment
Qubit lifetime (coherence time) remains below the threshold for scalable computing
Difficulty of accurate state readout during measurement without destroying information
qubit.fun_fact: The Bloch sphere is not just an illustrative model; any single-qubit operation can be represented as a rotation of this sphere, and sequences of rotations form the group SU(2).

formulas.description: General form of the qubit state in the computational basis
formulas.meaning: |\psi\rangle — qubit state vector; |0\rangle, |1\rangle — orthonormal basis vectors; \alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{C} — probability amplitudes; |\alpha|^2 + |\beta|^2 = 1 — normalization condition, where |\alpha|^2 is the probability of obtaining state |0\rangle upon measurement in the computational basis, |\beta|^2 is the probability of obtaining |1\rangle.

quantum_error_correction.name: quantum error correction
quantum_error_correction.mini: Quantum error correction — repeatedly copying an important message onto several sheets so that even blurred letters combine to form the original. No one reads the notes individually — the secret is preserved.
quantum_error_correction.practical_application: Without it, quantum computers could not run for more than milliseconds: error correction is the only path to reliable computation for deciphering complex proteins or cracking quantum-resistant ciphers.
quantum_error_correction.description_popular: Quantum error correction distributes a logical qubit across several physical ones, creating redundancy. Not the qubits themselves are measured, but syndromes — parities that indicate the type and location of an error without collapsing the encoded information. This allows active detection and compensation of random phase or amplitude flips arising from decoherence and noise.
quantum_error_correction.fun_fact_popular: Modern quantum processors can correct an error faster than it occurs if the correction frequency is above the operation accuracy threshold — this is called achieving the 'fault-tolerance threshold'.
quantum_error_correction.description_simple: Imagine you need to send the word 'yes' over an unreliable line. You can send it three times: if noise distorts one copy, the other two will still indicate the correct answer. Quantum error correction does something similar, but considering that quantum data cannot be observed directly — that would destroy it. Instead, indirect signs are checked, like a doctor listening to the heart without opening the chest.
quantum_error_correction.history_simple: The first working quantum error correction code was devised by Peter Shor in 1995 — he used 9 qubits to protect 1 qubit of information. Later, more economical schemes appeared.
quantum_error_correction.how_it_works_simple: Errors in qubits are like a sudden kick to a spinning top. Correction uses extra qubits that monitor each other, like a group of friends walking in step: if one stumbles, the neighbors immediately correct him without asking exactly where he was going.
quantum_error_correction.description: Quantum error correction is a set of codes and protocols that protect quantum information from non-unitary influences (decoherence) and operational noise. The theory relies on the no-cloning theorem: instead of duplicating a qubit, information is dispersed across an entangled register of ancillary qubits such that projective syndrome measurement does not destroy the logical state. Codes are classified by the types of errors they correct: bit-flips, phase-flips, and continuous shifts.
quantum_error_correction.history: After the discovery of quantum computational supremacy, it became clear that noise catastrophically limits the length of computations. In 1995, Peter Shor built the first code that corrects an arbitrary single-qubit error, using 9 qubits. Andrew Steane independently proposed a more compact version. Later, stabilizer formalisms (Gottesman) and topological codes (Kitaev) emerged, where qubits live on the surface of a torus and are protected from local perturbations by the geometry itself.
quantum_error_correction.how_it_works: The correction process begins with encoding: the logical qubit is mapped into a subspace of a larger Hilbert space via an isometric embedding. Then stabilizers — multi-qubit operators for which the codespace is an eigenspace with +1 — are periodically measured. Non-zero syndromes trigger classical processing that determines the most likely error, after which a recovery unitary operation is applied. In topological codes, information is stored in global degrees of freedom, and local noise cannot destroy it without overcoming an energy barrier.
key_problems: Fault-tolerance threshold: most platforms have not yet achieved a sufficiently low physical error rate
Resource overhead: practical implementation of a logical qubit may require thousands of physical ones
Syndrome measurements themselves introduce errors and require optimization
quantum_error_correction.fun_fact: A topological code based on toric boundaries turns error correction into a game: it is enough to close a chain of measured operators around the holes of the torus to recover quantum information, like a cowboy's lightning twisting into a lasso.

quantum_key_distribution.name: quantum key distribution
quantum_key_distribution.mini: Quantum key — a sealed envelope with wax: any attempt to open it leaves a trace. The sender and receiver do not exchange a secret, but create it on the fly in the light of photons.
quantum_key_distribution.practical_application: Protection of communication channels over distances of up to hundreds of kilometers: banking transactions, government lines, and the future quantum internet are built on invulnerable key distribution.
quantum_key_distribution.description_popular: Quantum key distribution uses the properties of single photons: measuring incompatible bases (for example, horizontal-vertical and diagonal polarizations) inevitably disturbs the state. The BB84 protocol generates a raw key, then through privacy amplification and error correction, the final shared key is extracted, whose security is guaranteed by fundamental laws, not computational complexity.
quantum_key_distribution.fun_fact_popular: The record length of a terrestrial quantum key distribution link is over 500 kilometers without repeaters, achieved using ultra-sensitive detectors and quantum memory.
quantum_key_distribution.description_simple: Alice sends Bob photons with different polarization orientations (like rotating sunglasses). Bob measures them, and then they compare over an open phone line which glasses he was wearing. The matches become the secret key. Eve, trying to eavesdrop, will inevitably alter some photons — and she will be detected by the increased error rate.
quantum_key_distribution.history_simple: The first quantum key distribution protocol — BB84 — was proposed by Charles Bennett and Gilles Brassard in 1984. Later, schemes based on entangled photons appeared, strengthening security.
quantum_key_distribution.how_it_works_simple: It's like two tin cans connected by a string: if someone presses their ear, the tension changes. Key photons are transmitted via optical fiber or even through the air, and any eavesdropping introduces noise, so the line is constantly checked for above-normal noise.
quantum_key_distribution.description: Quantum key distribution is a physical-layer cryptographic protocol in which a secret key is generated by measuring the quantum states of single-photon pulses or through correlations of entangled pairs. Security is based on Heisenberg's uncertainty principle and the no-cloning theorem: any eavesdropping attempt alters the error statistics. Classical phases over a public channel — basis sifting, estimation of the quantum bit error rate (QBER), error correction, and privacy amplification — transform partially correlated bit strings into an ideal key.
quantum_key_distribution.history: The concept of quantum cryptography was proposed by Stephen Wiesner in the 1970s, but the first implementable scheme — BB84 — was formulated by Bennett and Brassard in 1984. Experimental demonstrations in free space and over fiber began in the 1990s. Alain Aspect and his colleagues proved the violation of Bell's inequalities, confirming the inevitability of correlations in entangled states, which formed the basis of protocols using entangled photons (E91, BBM92). Today, QKD is a mature technology with commercial systems and satellite communication (Micius).
quantum_key_distribution.how_it_works: In BB84, Alice encodes bits in polarization states +/+ (0°/90°) or ×/× (45°/135°), randomly choosing the basis. Bob measures in a random basis. After transmission, they agree over a public channel on which shots the bases matched, forming a sifted key. Resulting errors are estimated as QBER. If QBER is below the threshold (about 11%), an error correction code and a hash function for privacy amplification are applied, eliminating Eve's potential information. Security is proven by reducing to the monotonicity of von Neumann entropy and the non-increase of quantum mutual information.
key_problems: Realistic devices suffer from side-channel attacks (laser blinding, detector imperfections)
Quantum repeaters with quantum memory are needed for a global network but are not yet developed
Integration with existing infrastructure and security certification
quantum_key_distribution.fun_fact: The quantum satellite 'Micius' established an intercontinental key between China and Austria over a distance of 7600 km, using entanglement teleportation — an effectively instantaneous correlation even through space.

quantum_annealing.name: quantum annealing
quantum_annealing.mini: An optimization method that acts like a smart rock climber: it doesn't just descend into the nearest hollow, but 'seeps' through the mountain, guaranteed to find the deepest abyss — the best solution.
quantum_annealing.practical_application: D-Wave's hardware quantum annealers solve routing, financial forecasting, and material cutting problems, finding optimal combinations faster than conventional servers for special classes of problems.
quantum_annealing.description_popular: Quantum annealing is a heuristic method for solving integer optimization problems, relying on the principles of quantum mechanics. Unlike the classical analog, where crossing a high barrier requires thermal excitation and is random, here quantum tunneling dominates: the system 'passes' through narrow barriers, effectively leaving local minima and striving for the global one. Programming the problem reduces to setting longitudinal fields and couplings in the Ising model.
quantum_annealing.fun_fact_popular: The D-Wave Advantage processor with 5000+ qubits solves some quadratic optimization problems in milliseconds, while a classical supercomputer would take hours for the same — but only for specially crafted instances.
quantum_annealing.description_simple: Quantum annealing mimics metal annealing. A blacksmith heats metal and slowly cools it so that the atoms settle without defects — yielding a strong structure. In computing, we seek the most 'robust' combination of parameters. Here, instead of atoms, quantum particles work, which can tunnel (pass through energy barriers), so they don't get stuck in local minima — as if a ball could pass through the wall of a dimple and roll into a deeper well.
quantum_annealing.history_simple: The idea appeared in the 1980s-90s as a generalization of the classical simulated annealing algorithm. The first commercial machine based on this principle was created by the Canadian company D-Wave Systems in 2011.
quantum_annealing.how_it_works_simple: The problem is encoded in the energy of an artificial 'quantum magnet' consisting of many elements (qubits). The system is started in a simple but highly uncertain state and the control fields are slowly changed. Thanks to the tunneling effect (seeping through obstacles), it ends up with high probability in the state with the lowest energy — that is the answer.
quantum_annealing.description: Quantum annealing is a specialized paradigm of adiabatic quantum computing. The system is initialized in the ground state of an easily prepared Hamiltonian H_i (e.g., transverse field) and evolves under a slowly varying total Hamiltonian H(t) = A(t)H_i + B(t)H_p, where H_p is the problem Hamiltonian (in the simplest case, the Ising model), A(t) monotonically decreases, B(t) increases. According to the adiabatic theorem, if the change is slow enough, the system remains in the instantaneous ground state and at the end of the anneal gives a measurement corresponding to the minimum of H_p with high probability. Advantage is achieved when the landscape has tall but thin barriers: tunneling through them scales better than thermal activation. In practice, excitations are inevitable due to finite time, noise, and small gaps in the spectrum.
quantum_annealing.history: The idea of quantum annealing dates back to annealing models in neural networks (1989). It was formalized in 1998 by Tadashi Kadowaki and Hidetoshi Nishimori as a quantum version of the simulated annealing method. A practical breakthrough is associated with the development of superconducting circuits: the D-Wave team (founded in 1999) introduced the first commercial 128-qubit annealer in 2011. Theoretical foundations rest on early research on tunneling (Leo Esaki, 1958) and adiabatic evolution.
quantum_annealing.how_it_works: Qubits (superconducting loops with Josephson junctions) form an array described by the Ising model with longitudinal fields h_i and couplings J_ij. Initialization is a deep transverse field Γ, creating a superposition of all binary states. Then Γ is smoothly turned off, and h_i and J_ij are turned on. Evolution is governed by the adiabatic condition: τ ≫ max_t |⟨1(t)|∂H/∂t|0(t)⟩| / (E_1(t)−E_0(t))^2, requiring the annealing time τ to be much larger than the inverse square of the minimum energy gap. The tunneling amplitude is exponentially suppressed with increasing barrier width, so for wide barriers, classical algorithms can compete.
key_problems: It has not yet been proven that quantum annealing provides a fundamental exponential speedup for practically significant problem classes compared to the best classical algorithms.
Decoherence and thermal excitations limit the size and accuracy of existing devices.
The problem of programming arbitrary connectivity graphs is solved by minor embedding, reducing the effective number of qubits.
quantum_annealing.fun_fact: The D-Wave processor is cooled to 15 millikelvin — a temperature 180 times lower than in outer space, so that superconducting circuits operate without thermal noise.

formulas.description: Hamiltonian of the system during annealing
formulas.meaning: H_i — initial Hamiltonian (e.g., transverse field), H_p — problem Hamiltonian (Ising model), s(t) — dimensionless annealing parameter, monotonically changing from 0 to 1 over time T.

quantum_simulator.name: quantum simulator
quantum_simulator.mini: A device that replaces a complex quantum system with its scaled-down, obedient copy — like a test model of a bridge instead of a risky experiment with the real thing.
quantum_simulator.practical_application: Simulators using ultracold atoms have already helped understand the mechanisms of high-temperature superconductivity and simulate chemical reactions that are impossible to calculate on supercomputers.
quantum_simulator.description_popular: A quantum simulator is an analog experimental platform that uses a precisely controlled quantum system to study the behavior of another, less accessible one. Unlike a universal quantum computer, the simulator does not require full error correction and is easier to scale. Popular implementations: ions in Paul traps, superconducting circuits, photonic chips, and optical lattices with neutral atoms.
quantum_simulator.fun_fact_popular: In 2012, a simulator of 300 atoms in an optical lattice reproduced antiferromagnetic ordering — a key effect believed to be responsible for high-temperature superconductivity.
quantum_simulator.description_simple: A quantum simulator is a special 'imitating device'. To understand how electrons behave in a hard-to-reach material, we take a cloud of ultracold (cooled to nearly absolute zero) atoms and lock them in an optical lattice (a grid of intersecting laser beams creating periodic cells). Atoms in such a lattice move and interact by almost the same rules as electrons in a crystal, so by studying atoms, we learn about electrons.
quantum_simulator.history_simple: The idea was voiced by Richard Feynman in 1982: to simulate the quantum world, quantum devices are needed. The first working simulators based on atomic Bose-Einstein condensates appeared in the early 2000s.
quantum_simulator.how_it_works_simple: Researchers create a cloud of atoms in a special state — a Bose-Einstein condensate, where all particles move as a single 'quantum fluid'. Then, using lasers, they form a 'light crystal' (optical lattice) with the desired geometry. By changing the laser intensity and external magnets, they program conditions mimicking the system of interest. A snapshot of the atom distribution at the end of the experiment gives the answer.
quantum_simulator.description: A quantum simulator is a specialized quantum device that implements a controllable Hamiltonian H_sim, isomorphic (or sufficiently approximating) the Hamiltonian of the target system H_target. In the analog version, the parameters of H_sim are directly programmed via external fields and potentials with minimal perturbations; in the digital version, time evolution e^{−iH_target t} is decomposed into a sequence of universal gates (Trotterization). The simulator solves the problem of exponential growth of Hilbert space dimensionality by replacing computation with a direct quantum experiment.
quantum_simulator.history: The concept was proposed by Richard Feynman (1982) and developed by David Deutsch (1985), pointing out the exponential complexity of classical simulation of quantum dynamics. The practical era began with the realization of Bose-Einstein condensates (1995) and optical lattices (2002), when it became possible to simulate the superfluid–Mott insulator transition. Since then, platforms have expanded: in 2012, ion chains demonstrated magnetic ordering, and in 2019, superconducting qubits reproduced the dynamics of cosmological phase transitions.
quantum_simulator.how_it_works: In a basic implementation with neutral atoms, an ensemble of bosons is cooled to degeneracy and loaded into a periodic potential created by the interference of laser beams. The effective Hamiltonian is described by the Bose-Hubbard model: tunneling J between neighboring sites and interaction U of two atoms on the same site. By adjusting the lattice depth, the ratio J/U is controlled, allowing observation of a quantum phase transition. Measurements: time-of-flight spectroscopy yields momentum distribution, and a quantum gas microscope provides the positions of individual atoms. Digital simulators with ions use laser pulses to implement sets of universal gates and algorithmic Trotterization.
key_problems: Imperfect initial state preparation and parasitic noise limit the accuracy and size of the simulated system.
For many relevant problems, the number of required particles or evolution times are still out of reach.
Verification of the simulator's operation becomes fundamentally impossible when classical modeling does not provide a reference answer.
quantum_simulator.fun_fact: The record two-dimensional optical lattice simulator contains over ten thousand atoms — the largest man-made quantum many-body object.

formulas.description: Bose-Hubbard Hamiltonian for atoms in an optical lattice
formulas.meaning: J — tunneling energy between neighboring sites i and j, a_i^{\dagger}, a_i — boson creation and annihilation operators at site i, U — interaction energy of two atoms on the same site, n_i = a_i^{\dagger} a_i — particle number operator, h.c. — Hermitian conjugate.

galaxy_merger.name: galaxy merger
galaxy_merger.mini: A galaxy merger is a grand cosmic collision when two star systems pass through each other like clouds of smoke and merge into one large one. Over billions of years, such mergers have shaped the enormous galaxies we see today.
galaxy_merger.practical_application: Understanding mergers helps predict the future of our Galaxy, which will collide with the Andromeda Nebula in 4 billion years, and explains the diversity of galaxy shapes.
galaxy_merger.description_popular: A galaxy merger is a process of gravitational interaction in which two or more galaxies collide and rearrange into a new system. During the merger, the structure, stellar composition, and star formation rates change. Major mergers (of equal masses) and minor mergers (absorption of a dwarf galaxy) are distinguished. Such interactions are the most important mechanism of galaxy evolution in hierarchical cosmology, where small objects gradually assemble into larger ones.
galaxy_merger.fun_fact_popular: When two spiral galaxies merge, an elliptical galaxy is often born, and the central supermassive black holes also merge, emitting powerful gravitational waves.
galaxy_merger.description_simple: Galaxies, consisting of billions of stars, gas, and dust, sometimes collide with each other. Due to the vast distances between stars, they hardly ever collide directly, but their gas clouds compress, causing a burst of new star formation. Gravity mixes the two systems, and after millions of years, a single new galaxy emerges, often elliptical in shape.
galaxy_merger.history_simple: The idea that galaxies could merge arose after Edwin Hubble proved in the 1920s that many nebulae are other galaxies. Later, astronomers noticed distorted galaxies with 'tails' and double nuclei, which were explained as merger remnants.
galaxy_merger.how_it_works_simple: Two galaxies attract each other by gravity. They slowly approach, pass through one another, stretching tidal tails of stars and gas, then come together again and eventually mix into one entity — much like two whirlpools merging into one large vortex.
galaxy_merger.description: A galaxy merger is a long (100 million to 1 billion years) dynamical process during which dark matter halos, stellar, and gas components of two galaxies mix and relax toward equilibrium. Gravitational instability unites them into a single system; the gas experiences compression and turbulence, triggering a burst of star formation and nuclear activity (accretion onto black holes). They are modeled numerically with dark matter and feedback. At the final stage, an elliptical galaxy forms with depleted gas and an old stellar population.
galaxy_merger.history: In the 1920s, Edwin Hubble established the extragalactic nature of nebulae and created a galaxy classification, later supplemented by the idea of collisions. Fritz Zwicky in the 1930s, studying galaxy clusters, noted interactions and the need for dark matter to hold systems together. In the 1970s, Vera Rubin obtained galaxy rotation curves, proving the dominance of dark halos, which became the foundation for modern understanding of merger dynamics. Modern supercomputer simulations like Illustris trace mergers from the early Universe to the present day.
galaxy_merger.how_it_works: As they approach, two galaxies experience gravitational interaction according to the law of universal gravitation. Tidal forces pull long tails from them, dynamical friction slows their orbital motion, and the components merge after several passes. Gas, unlike stars, undergoes hydrodynamic shocks, cools, and compresses, forming dense clouds from which new stars are born. Central supermassive black holes approach each other to distances where gravitational wave emission (per general relativity) dominates and they coalesce. The process is described by equations of motion of matter in an expanding Universe, with dark matter playing a key role via the gravitational potential of halos.
key_problems: How exactly does feedback from supermassive black holes regulate star formation during mergers?
A complete explanation of elliptical galaxy formation: why do they lose the ability to form stars?
Prediction of the gravitational wave background characteristics from mergers of all supermassive black holes.
galaxy_merger.fun_fact: During a merger, a ring galaxy can form — like the famous Cartwheel Galaxy — when one galaxy passes exactly through the center of another.

formulas.description: Jeans mass — the minimum mass of a cloud capable of collapsing under its own gravity (a key parameter for initiating star formation during mergers)
formulas.latex: M_J \approx \frac{3 k_B T r}{2 G \mu m_H}
formulas.meaning: M_J — Jeans mass, k_B — Boltzmann constant (k_B ≈ 1.381×10⁻²³ J/K), T — cloud temperature, r — cloud radius, G — gravitational constant (G ≈ 6.674×10⁻¹¹ m³/(kg·s²)), μ — mean molecular weight (in atomic units, for molecular hydrogen μ ≈ 2), m_H — mass of hydrogen atom (m_H ≈ 1.674×10⁻²⁷ kg)

planetary_migration.name: planetary migration
planetary_migration.mini: Giant planets are not always born where we see them — like tumbleweeds, they can change orbit under the influence of the gas disk or other planets.
planetary_migration.practical_application: Understanding migration explains why we find hot Jupiters close to stars and helps model the architecture of planetary systems, including our Solar System.
planetary_migration.description_popular: Planetary migration is the change in a planet's orbit due to gravitational interaction with the gas-dust disk in which it formed, or with other massive bodies. Type I migration (for low-mass planets that do not clear a gap) and Type II (for giants that open a gap) are distinguished. Migration produces hot Jupiters, resonant chains, and may explain features of the Solar System like the Late Heavy Bombardment.
planetary_migration.fun_fact_popular: Hot Jupiters — gas giants orbiting their stars with periods of just a few days — are born far out in the cold and then migrate inward, much like an iceberg drifting from polar latitudes to warm waters.
planetary_migration.description_simple: Planets form from a rotating cloud of gas and dust around a young star. Sometimes they change their distance from the star: they move closer or farther away. It’s like a boat on a river: the current (gas disk) can push the planet, altering its course. Such movement is called planetary migration. Because of it, giant planets can end up very close to the star (hot Jupiters) or be ejected outward.
planetary_migration.history_simple: The idea of planetary migration emerged in the late 20th century when astronomers began finding exoplanets that could not have formed so close to their stars. The theory helped explain the strange orbits.
planetary_migration.how_it_works_simple: The planet's gravity creates density waves in the surrounding gas disk. These waves tug on the planet, causing it to change orbit — much like if you rowed with an oar, creating eddies that then push your boat.
planetary_migration.description: Planetary migration is a key process in the formation and evolution of planetary systems, whereby planets change the semi-major axis of their orbit. Main mechanisms: interaction with the protoplanetary disk (gravitational and hydrodynamic effects), planet-planet scattering, and tidal interactions with the star. Type I migration (subsonic, without a gap) describes rapid shifts of planets with mass less than about Neptune's mass, while Type II migration occurs when a gap is opened by massive planets. Migration theory radically changed understanding of Solar System formation, explaining the arrangement of giant planets and the distribution of small bodies (Kuiper belt, Oort cloud). In exoplanetary systems, migration produces the observed diversity of orbital configurations.
planetary_migration.history: The concept of planetary migration was proposed in the 1980s by theorists (Goldreich, Tremaine) to explain the formation of outer planets. With the discovery of the first hot Jupiters (51 Pegasi b in 1995), the idea gained wide acceptance. Modeling of planet-disk interactions (Lin, Papaloizou, Ward) revealed several migration regimes. Modern hydrodynamic simulations, including magnetic fields, continue to refine migration speeds and directions.
planetary_migration.how_it_works: Migration is based on angular momentum exchange between the planet and the gas disk. The planet launches spiral density waves, which create regions of increased pressure that exert a back-reaction gravitational torque (Lindblad effect). In Type I migration, the planet experiences mass inflow through spiral arms, leading to rapid inward drift. For Type II migration, movement is governed by the disk's viscosity: the planet is locked in the gap and follows the disk's evolution. Planet-planet scattering and resonant interactions further perturb orbits. Angular momentum conservation is fundamental; migration timescales can be shorter than planet formation times.
key_problems: What is the exact balance of forces determining the speed and direction of Type I migration in turbulent disks?
How did migration affect the early history of the Solar System and can it explain the hypothetical Planet Nine?
What is the contribution of different migration mechanisms to the observed population of hot Neptunes and super-Earths?
planetary_migration.fun_fact: Some models predict that migration can toss planets directly into the star or eject them into interstellar space — rogue planets, which we already observe.

supernova_remnant.name: supernova remnant
supernova_remnant.mini: A stellar explosion scatters matter, leaving behind an expanding cloud — a cosmic jellyfish glowing for thousands of years.
supernova_remnant.practical_application: Supernova remnants are natural accelerators of cosmic rays and factories of heavy elements; their emission helps study the interstellar medium and explosion dynamics.
supernova_remnant.description_popular: A supernova remnant is an expanding shell of ejected material and swept-up interstellar matter. Inside lies a hot, tenuous bubble bounded by a shock wave that heats gas to X-ray emitting temperatures. Several morphological types exist: shell-type (like Cassiopeia A), plerionic (powered by a pulsar wind, like the Crab), and mixed. Supernova remnants enrich the interstellar medium with heavy elements and launch cosmic rays.
supernova_remnant.fun_fact_popular: The Crab Nebula expands at about 1,500 km/s — while you read this sentence, its diameter increases by a distance comparable to Earth's size.
supernova_remnant.description_simple: When a massive star exhausts its fuel, it collapses and explodes as a supernova. The outer layers are blown off, forming an expanding cloud of gas and dust — a supernova remnant. It's like a burst balloon with glitter: the shell flies outward in all directions, leaving a compact object (neutron star or black hole) inside.
supernova_remnant.history_simple: Supernova remnants have been observed since antiquity: the Crab Nebula is the result of an explosion recorded by Chinese astronomers in 1054. In the 20th century, Fritz Zwicky and Walter Baade linked them to neutron stars.
supernova_remnant.how_it_works_simple: The shock wave pushes matter at thousands of kilometers per second, sweeping up interstellar gas. Collisions heat the gas to millions of degrees, making it glow in X-rays — much like a supersonic aircraft's trail turns into a cloud, but much hotter.
supernova_remnant.description: A supernova remnant is the product of the interaction between ejected stellar material and the surrounding interstellar medium. Evolutionarily, stages include free expansion, Sedov-Taylor (adiabatic), thin shell formation, and transition to a turbulent cloud. Typical explosion energy ~10⁵¹ erg, ejecta mass several solar masses. In plerions, a central pulsar injects energetic particles, creating synchrotron radiation. Supernova remnants are the primary sources of cosmic rays up to the knee energy (~3×10¹⁵ eV).
supernova_remnant.history: Fritz Zwicky in 1934 predicted neutron stars as supernova remnants. The discovery of pulsars by Jocelyn Bell Burnell in 1967 confirmed this connection. Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar earlier calculated the white dwarf mass limit, indicating the inevitability of collapse for more massive stars. Advances in X-ray astronomy (Chandra, XMM-Newton) have allowed detailed study of remnant dynamics and chemical composition.
supernova_remnant.how_it_works: The shock wave propagates through the interstellar medium according to momentum and energy conservation. The Sedov-Taylor solution for a point explosion in a uniform medium is applied: radius depends on time as R ~ t^{2/5}. High-energy electrons in magnetic fields produce synchrotron emission, explaining radio glow. Heavy elements (oxygen, silicon, iron) synthesized in the star are ejected and mixed with interstellar gas, influencing subsequent star formation.
key_problems: What is the detailed structure of magnetic fields in remnants and their role in accelerating cosmic rays?
Why are some remnants asymmetric and does their shape contain information about the explosion mechanism?
What is the contribution of supernovae to interstellar dust and how exactly do dust grains condense in the expanding shell?
supernova_remnant.fun_fact: In the Cassiopeia A remnant, phosphorus and other elements necessary for life have been detected — we literally consist of stardust scattered by such explosions.

formulas.description: Radius of a supernova remnant in the adiabatic phase (Sedov-Taylor solution)
formulas.meaning: R — shock wave radius, E — kinetic energy of the explosion (~10^{51} erg), t — time after explosion, ρ — density of the interstellar medium

nucleosynthesis.name: nucleosynthesis
nucleosynthesis.mini: Nature is an alchemist: hydrogen and helium in stellar furnaces transform into the entire diversity of chemical elements that make up the world.
nucleosynthesis.practical_application: Nucleosynthesis explains the origin of all elements of the periodic table, from carbon in our cells to gold in jewelry, and is used in models of stellar evolution and cosmology.
nucleosynthesis.description_popular: Nucleosynthesis is the set of nuclear reactions leading to the formation of chemical elements. Big Bang nucleosynthesis (in the first minutes after the Big Bang, producing hydrogen, helium, and trace lithium), stellar nucleosynthesis (in stellar interiors, e.g., CNO cycle, triple-alpha process), and explosive nucleosynthesis (in supernovae) are distinguished. Through nucleosynthesis, the Universe evolves from simple nuclei to complex ones, providing chemical diversity.
nucleosynthesis.fun_fact_popular: Iron is the end of the road for stellar fusion: its nucleus does not release energy when fused, so massive stars that accumulate an iron core collapse and explode.
nucleosynthesis.description_simple: In the beginning, the Universe had only the lightest elements — hydrogen and helium. But stars, like giant furnaces, inside themselves glue light nuclei into heavier ones (called nuclear fusion). This creates carbon, oxygen, iron. The heaviest elements, like gold and uranium, are born during supernova explosions or neutron star collisions.
nucleosynthesis.history_simple: The idea that elements are created in stars was proposed by Fred Hoyle in the mid-20th century. He showed how carbon could form in stars, and later, with other scientists, created a complete picture of element origin.
nucleosynthesis.how_it_works_simple: At the center of a star, the temperature is so high that atomic nuclei overcome repulsion and merge. It's like gluing balls of clay if you throw them together with enormous force. Energy is released, making the star shine.
nucleosynthesis.description: Nucleosynthesis occurs in various astrophysical environments. Big Bang nucleosynthesis (BBN) in the epoch 3–20 minutes after the Big Bang fixed the ratio of light isotopes. Stellar nucleosynthesis includes hydrogen burning (pp chain, CNO cycle), helium burning (triple-alpha), carbon, neon, oxygen, and silicon burning. Rapid neutron capture (r-process) requires extreme neutron fluxes, likely in neutron star mergers (kilonovae). Slow neutron capture (s-process) occurs in AGB stars. Heavy elements are ejected into the interstellar medium, enriching subsequent generations of stars and planets.
nucleosynthesis.history: Fred Hoyle in 1954 predicted a resonant state of carbon-12 necessary for synthesis from helium; experiments confirmed this soon after. Ralph Alpher and George Gamow earlier laid the foundations of BBN, calculating helium formation. Margaret Burbidge, Geoffrey Burbidge, William Fowler, and Fred Hoyle (B²FH) published a classic review of element synthesis in stars in 1957. The discovery of a kilonova in 2017 confirmed the r-process in neutron star mergers.
nucleosynthesis.how_it_works: Nuclear reactions obey conservation of charge, energy, and nucleon number. In stars, temperatures reach 10^7–10^9 K, overcoming the Coulomb barrier. The tunnel effect allows reactions to proceed at energies below the barrier. Triple-alpha process: 3^4He → ^12C + γ, and then alpha capture creates oxygen, neon, etc. The iron peak is explained by the maximum binding energy per nucleon. For the r-process, densities >10^22 neutrons/cm³ are required, achieved during de-neutronization in neutron star ejecta.
key_problems: Lithium problem in BBN: predicted abundance of lithium-7 is ~3 times higher than observed.
The exact site of the r-process is not fully determined: how much do kilonovae produce vs. rare types of supernovae?
Details of nucleosynthesis in the first stars (Population III) and enrichment of the early Universe.
nucleosynthesis.fun_fact: Iodine-127, essential for the thyroid gland, is primarily produced in the r-process — meaning your ability to regulate metabolism is thanks to a neutron star collision billions of years ago.

galactic_evolution.name: galactic evolution
galactic_evolution.mini: Galaxies grow and change like cities: they merge, build up outskirts, experience bursts of star formation — booms of construction. This is galactic evolution.
galactic_evolution.practical_application: Understanding galaxy evolution helps predict the future of the Milky Way and search for life in mature systems.
galactic_evolution.description_popular: Galactic evolution describes how galaxies change structure and composition over billions of years. Key processes: gas accretion, star formation, mergers, and tidal interactions. For example, spiral galaxies can transform into ellipticals during major collisions, and supermassive black holes form at the centers of many galaxies.
galactic_evolution.fun_fact_popular: The Milky Way is currently absorbing a dwarf galaxy — its stellar stream stretches across the sky.
galactic_evolution.description_simple: Galaxies are huge collections of stars, gas, and dust. Over time, they change: they merge with each other (like streams into a river), new stars ignite, and old ones fade. This process is called galaxy evolution.
galactic_evolution.history_simple: Edwin Hubble in the 1920s first showed that galaxies come in different shapes, hinting at their development. Later, astronomers like Vera Rubin found that dark matter (invisible mass holding galaxies together) governs their rotation and mergers.
galactic_evolution.how_it_works_simple: Galaxies change because gas inside compresses into stars, and gravity attracts neighboring galaxies. Large galaxies 'eat' small ones, and the shape changes — spirals become ellipticals.
galactic_evolution.description: Galactic evolution is the sum of physical processes that determine the formation, growth, and transformation of galaxies from the early Universe to the present. It includes hierarchical merging (small galaxies merge into larger ones), cold gas accretion from the intergalactic medium (fueling star formation), feedback from supernovae and active nuclei (regulating heating and gas expulsion), and dynamical relaxation. The final appearance of a galaxy — spiral, elliptical, or irregular — depends on its merger history and star formation rate.
galactic_evolution.history: The concept emerged after Edwin Hubble classified galaxies by shape in the 1920s, and James Jeans and others suggested gravitational instability as a formation mechanism. Decisive contributions came from Vera Rubin's 1970s work on galaxy rotation, which pointed to dark matter, and deep field observations (Hubble Deep Field) showing galaxy evolution over time. Margaret Burbidge and Fred Hoyle developed nucleosynthesis theory, important for chemical evolution.
galactic_evolution.how_it_works: Hierarchical growth: small density fluctuations in the early Universe grow into galaxies under gravity (described by Friedmann equations and ΛCDM cosmology). Gas cools and fragments into stars; massive stars explode, enriching the medium with heavy elements. Mergers disrupt ordered rotation, increasing velocity dispersion and turning disks into spheroids. Dark matter provides the gravitational scaffolding.
key_problems: How exactly does quenching of star formation occur?
What is the role of supermassive black holes in evolution?
Why do some dwarf galaxies have almost no dark matter?
galactic_evolution.fun_fact: Colliding galaxies can create rings of stars and gas — for example, Hoag’s Object.

vlbi.name: VLBI
vlbi.mini: VLBI turns a network of radio telescopes into one virtual instrument the size of Earth — detail like a microscope looking at the sky. A cosmic magnifying glass that sees a coin on the Moon.
vlbi.practical_application: Thanks to VLBI, the first image of a black hole's shadow was obtained, and the movement of lithospheric plates is measured with millimeter precision.
vlbi.description_popular: VLBI is a technique in radio astronomy where signals from distant radio telescopes are combined taking into account the delay in wave arrival. Resolving power (ability to distinguish fine details) depends on the distance between antennas, not their size. With baselines of thousands of kilometers, an angular resolution of microarcseconds is achieved — one can see a lighter on the Moon. To combat atmospheric distortions, the method of closure phases is used, combining signals from three or more antennas.
vlbi.fun_fact_popular: The largest virtual telescope — the Event Horizon Telescope — combined stations from Antarctica to Europe to show the silhouette of a black hole in 2019.
vlbi.description_simple: VLBI (Very Long Baseline Interferometry) is a method where several radio telescopes (instruments that capture radio waves from space), scattered across continents, simultaneously observe one object. Signals are recorded with time stamps from atomic clocks (very precise clocks); later computers process them together, as if a single telescope with an antenna thousands of kilometers in size was operating.
vlbi.history_simple: The idea of combining widely separated antennas emerged in the 1960s. The first successful observations were made in 1967 by groups of scientists in the USA and Canada.
vlbi.how_it_works_simple: Imagine two people looking at the same star and precisely noting when its light reaches each of them. If the distance between them is known, from the time difference one can reconstruct details inaccessible to a single observer. VLBI radio telescopes work the same way, only they record radio waves and synchronize by atomic clocks.
vlbi.description: Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI) uses independent recording of radiation at antennas separated by thousands of kilometers, with subsequent correlation of signals. Each station is equipped with a highly stable local oscillator and an atomic frequency standard (maser), allowing time tagging with femtosecond precision. Due to atmospheric and ionospheric phase distortions, the method of closure phases is used — combinations of phase differences of three or more antennas, robust against errors and not requiring full calibration. This allows reconstruction of the true structure of sources. VLBI operates in centimeter and millimeter bands; for observing black holes, 1-mm VLBI is used, placing telescopes in dry highlands (Chile, South Pole).
vlbi.history: The principle of interferometry in radio astronomy was developed by Martin Ryle and others in the 1940s–50s. A scheme with independent local oscillators and magnetic tape recording was proposed in the early 1960s by Soviet and American scientists. The first successful observations in 1967 were performed by groups in the USA and Canada, using rubidium frequency standards. Subsequently, the transition to digital recording and the advent of hydrogen masers increased accuracy; today, real-time data transmission (e-VLBI) via fiber optics is being introduced.
vlbi.how_it_works: Radio telescopes, operating as a single interferometer, record the signal along with precise time stamps from atomic clocks. The correlator later aligns the data, compensating for geometric delays due to Earth's rotation and antenna positions. Resolution is determined by the ratio of wavelength to baseline projection: θ ≈ λ / B. For wavelength 1.3 mm and baseline 8000 km, θ ≈ 20 microarcseconds. For millimeter VLBI, suppression of water vapor is critical, so telescopes are built at altitudes above 3000 m.
key_problems: Creation of space VLBI to extend baselines beyond Earth
Increasing sensitivity at millimeter waves
Automatic calibration and real-time imaging
vlbi.fun_fact: In the 1970s, VLBI was used for geodesy: by measuring how distances between continents change, scientists confirmed plate tectonics with millimeter accuracy.

formulas.description: Angular resolution of the interferometer
formulas.meaning: θ — angular resolution (radians), λ — wavelength of radiation, B — baseline length (distance between antennas, perpendicular to the direction to the source)

stellar_evolution.name: stellar evolution
stellar_evolution.mini: Stellar life — gravitational compression versus nuclear expansion. Born from gas, they shine for billions of years and die as white dwarfs, neutron stars, or black holes.
stellar_evolution.practical_application: Carbon and oxygen in our bodies are the ashes of stellar fires; knowing evolution, we predict the future of the Sun and search for planets around old stars.
stellar_evolution.description_popular: The evolution of a star is a sequence of states under the action of gravity and thermonuclear reactions. On the main sequence (where the star spends ~90% of its life), hydrogen burns in the core. After its exhaustion, the core contracts, the outer layers expand, and the star becomes a red giant. Stars with masses up to ~8 solar masses shed their envelope as a planetary nebula and leave a white dwarf. More massive ones reach iron synthesis, after which they collapse and explode as type II supernovae, giving rise to a neutron star or black hole.
stellar_evolution.fun_fact_popular: The Sun loses 240 million tons of mass every minute, converting it into light, but over its entire life it will use only 0.7% of its substance.
stellar_evolution.description_simple: A star is a ball of gas where gravity pulls everything to the center, and the internal pressure of hot gas (plasma) pushes outward. In the core, at tremendous temperature, nuclear fusion reactions occur: hydrogen turns into helium and releases energy, preventing the star from contracting. When the fuel runs out, the equilibrium collapses — the star swells into a red giant, then sheds its envelope, leaving a white dwarf, or explodes as a supernova.
stellar_evolution.history_simple: In the early 20th century, astronomers (Hertzsprung, Russell) constructed the color-magnitude diagram and noticed that stars of different types lie in certain regions. By the 1950s, physicists explained these sequences as stages of stellar life.
stellar_evolution.how_it_works_simple: Like in a campfire: as long as there is wood (hydrogen), the flame is steady. When hydrogen in the core runs out, the core contracts and heats up, and the envelope expands — the star bloats. Then mass decides the fate: light stars quietly fade away, heavy ones end in a grand explosion.
stellar_evolution.description: Stellar evolution is the process of changing structure and luminosity of a star under the influence of its own gravity and nuclear reactions. It begins with the collapse of fragments of a molecular cloud; the protostar heats up until hydrogen ignites in the core (PP-chain or CNO cycle). On the main sequence, hydrostatic equilibrium operates. After hydrogen in the core burns out, it contracts, and the envelope expands — the star enters the red giant stage. A helium flash initiates helium burning, then carbon, etc., up to iron in massive stars. Final stages are determined by mass: white dwarfs (mass < Chandrasekhar limit ~1.44 M☉), neutron stars (core-collapse supernova) or black holes. Mass loss, rotation, and binarity play important roles.
stellar_evolution.history: The idea of a nuclear source of stellar energy was proposed by Eddington in the 1920s. In 1938, Bethe and Weizsäcker described hydrogen fusion chains. Chandrasekhar in 1931 calculated the white dwarf mass limit (Chandrasekhar limit). Hoyle in the 1940s–50s showed how elements heavier than hydrogen are synthesized in stars, and Burbidge et al. (B²FH, 1957) presented a nucleosynthesis scheme. Modern numerical evolution models consider thousands of reactions.
stellar_evolution.how_it_works: The main equation of hydrostatic equilibrium: dP/dr = –G M(r) ρ / r², where P is pressure, M(r) is the mass inside radius r. Thermonuclear reactions provide energy according to the law ε ~ ε₀ ρ T^n. Evolution is traced numerically considering the equation of state (ideal gas, degenerate electron gas, etc.) and radiation transfer. The key parameter is mass. Stars with mass above ~0.4 M☉ go through the red giant stage. The Chandrasekhar limit restricts the white dwarf mass; exceeding it may lead to collapse into a neutron star.
key_problems: Physics of core-collapse supernovae explosions
Influence of magnetic fields and rotation on late stages
Binary systems: mass exchange, common envelopes
First generation of stars (Population III) in the early Universe
stellar_evolution.fun_fact: White dwarfs with mass close to the Chandrasekhar limit can explode as type Ia supernovae — and these explosions serve as 'standard candles' for measuring cosmological distances.

formulas.description: Equation of hydrostatic equilibrium
formulas.meaning: P — pressure, r — distance from the center, G — gravitational constant (≈ 6.674×10⁻¹¹ m³ kg⁻¹ s⁻²), M(r) — mass enclosed inside the sphere of radius r, ρ — density

formulas.description: Chandrasekhar limit for a white dwarf
formulas.meaning: M_ch — maximum mass at which the pressure of degenerate electrons can support the star; M⊙ — solar mass ≈ 2×10^30 kg

biosignatures.name: biosignatures
biosignatures.mini: Biosignatures are clues of life far from Earth: spectral fingerprints of gases like oxygen or methane that are hard to explain without living organisms. Just as the smell of smoke indicates fire, these gases hint at something alive.
biosignatures.practical_application: Analyzing exoplanet atmospheres for biosignatures is the main path for modern astronomy to search for extraterrestrial life.
biosignatures.description_popular: Biosignatures are a set of chemical compounds, isotopic anomalies, or structures that indicate the presence of life. In the astronomical context, the main targets are gases in exoplanet atmospheres: dioxygen (O₂), ozone (O₃), methane (CH₄), nitrous oxide (N₂O). They are detected by transmission spectroscopy: the planet partially eclipses the star, and its atmosphere absorbs some radiation at specific wavelengths. Contextual factors are also important — for example, abundant methane in an atmosphere without signs of volcanism could be a sign of microbes.
biosignatures.fun_fact_popular: Oxygen on Earth appeared only thanks to cyanobacteria 2.4 billion years ago — before that, the atmosphere was unbreathable.
biosignatures.description_simple: When scientists search for life on other planets, they look for special substances — biosignatures. These can be gases in the atmosphere, like oxygen (which we breathe) or methane. On Earth, these gases are produced by living beings: plants release oxygen, bacteria produce methane. If we see the same gases on a distant planet, it could mean that there is life there.
biosignatures.history_simple: The idea emerged in the mid-20th century when astronomers pondered how to search for life. In the 1990s, the first exoplanets were discovered, making it a reality.
biosignatures.how_it_works_simple: Starlight passes through the planet's atmosphere. Different gases absorb light in their own way, and dark lines appear in the spectrum (rainbow band). That's how we learn what the air is made of.
biosignatures.description: A biosignature is any observable feature (chemical, spectroscopic, morphological) that can be produced by life and has no plausible abiotic source. In exoplanet research, the focus is on atmospheric biosignatures: gases in thermodynamic disequilibrium (e.g., simultaneous presence of O₂ and CH₄), organic compounds (chlorophyll-like pigments), or isotopic shifts (e.g., depletion of carbon-13 in biological tissues). Besides gases, biosignatures such as the "red edge" of vegetation—a sharp jump in reflectivity around 0.7 µm—are considered.
biosignatures.history: The concept was formulated by Carl Sagan and other pioneers of exobiology in the 1960s–1970s. In the 1990s, the discovery of exoplanets (Mayor, Queloz, 1995) turned biosignatures from hypothetical into observable. Modern instruments — James Webb (JWST) and the future Extremely Large Telescope (ELT) — will enable detailed study of terrestrial planet atmospheres in habitable zones.
biosignatures.how_it_works: Transmission spectroscopy: during a planet's transit, some starlight is filtered by its atmosphere. Subtracting the star's spectrum during transit from the out-of-transit spectrum yields the transmission spectrum, where molecular absorption lines are visible. Interpretation is based on laboratory spectra and photochemical models. For biosignatures, context is critical: for example, oxygen can form without life (photodissociation of water with hydrogen escape), so combinations of gases unlikely without biota are sought.
key_problems: How to distinguish a true biosignature from an abiotic mimic?
What are the unknown sources of false-positive signals?
Are there biosignatures for life not based on water?
biosignatures.fun_fact: In 2023, JWST found possible traces of dimethyl sulfide in the atmosphere of planet K2-18 b—on Earth, it is produced exclusively by living organisms.

dark_photon.name: dark photon
dark_photon.mini: The dark photon is an elusive "cousin" of ordinary light: while ordinary photons bind charges, the dark photon binds dark matter particles, remaining invisible to us.
dark_photon.practical_application: The discovery of the dark photon would help explain the nature of dark matter and possibly create new communication technologies through the bulk of the Earth.
dark_photon.description_popular: The dark photon is a hypothetical vector boson associated with a U(1) symmetry in the hidden sector of particle physics, analogous to the photon for electromagnetism. It interacts with Standard Model particles through kinetic mixing with the ordinary photon — a small parameter ε, a "leakage" between dark and ordinary electrodynamics. The mass of the dark photon can range from micro-electronvolts to hundreds of GeV.
dark_photon.fun_fact_popular: The dark photon could explain excess radiation in the centers of galaxies that cannot be attributed to ordinary astrophysics.
dark_photon.description_simple: Ordinary light consists of particles — photons. They carry the electromagnetic interaction (what makes a magnet stick to a refrigerator). Scientists think there might be a similar particle — a dark photon. It "works" like an ordinary photon, but only for dark matter (invisible substance that does not emit light but has mass). That's why we don't see it.
dark_photon.history_simple: The idea of the dark photon emerged at the end of the 20th century when physicists were looking for ways to explain dark matter.
dark_photon.how_it_works_simple: If dark photons exist, they can occasionally mix with ordinary photons, becoming briefly visible. It's like two parallel roads with very rare pedestrian crossings.
dark_photon.description: The dark photon (A') is a massive gauge boson of an additional U(1)' symmetry, broken at some scale. In the minimal model, it arises from adding a "dark sector" to the Standard Model: dark matter fermion fields interacting through a dark photon. Kinetic mixing with the ordinary hypercharge field, described by the operator (ε/2 cosθ_W) F_μν F'^μν, induces a weak coupling of the dark photon to ordinary matter. The mass is generated by the Higgs or Stückelberg mechanism.
dark_photon.history: The concept emerged in the 1980s as a byproduct of grand unified theories. In the 2008–2010s, interest revived thanks to experiments searching for light dark photons (LSND, MiniBooNE) and anomalies in cosmic rays. Leading laboratories, such as CERN (NA64) and SLAC (HPS), are actively searching.
dark_photon.how_it_works: If the dark photon is light (meV–MeV), it could manifest as an additional radiation source or affect star cooling. In experiments, it is searched for via radiation processes (e.g., e⁺e⁻ → γ γ', where the signal is a single photon with missing energy) or through resonant conversion of ordinary photons into dark ones in vacuum chambers (the "light through a wall" scheme). Heavier dark photons decay into charged leptons and can be detected by colliders.
key_problems: What is the mass of the dark photon?
Does it make up all dark matter or only part of it?
Does it explain the muon magnetic moment anomaly?
dark_photon.fun_fact: If the dark photon exists, a powerful laser could be used to "illuminate" the hidden sector and observe the creation of dark particles.

formulas.description: Lagrangian of kinetic mixing of the dark photon
formulas.meaning: ε — mixing parameter (dimensionless), F_{\mu\nu} — field strength tensor of the ordinary photon, F'_{\mu\nu} — field strength tensor of the dark photon

exoplanet_atmosphere.name: exoplanet atmosphere
exoplanet_atmosphere.mini: The distant sky of an alien world, telling of its composition through the color of stolen starlight.
exoplanet_atmosphere.practical_application: Searching for signs of life and assessing planetary habitability by detecting water, oxygen, or methane in the atmospheric spectrum.
exoplanet_atmosphere.description_popular: The main method is transmission spectroscopy: during transit (the planet passing across the star's disk), some starlight is filtered through the upper layers of the atmosphere. Atoms and molecules absorb radiation at characteristic wavelengths, creating a spectral fingerprint. From this, the presence of water vapor, carbon dioxide, methane, as well as temperature and pressure can be determined.
exoplanet_atmosphere.fun_fact_popular: On exoplanet KELT-9b, the dayside is heated to 4600 K — hotter than many stars — and iron and titanium vapors float in its atmosphere.
exoplanet_atmosphere.description_simple: The gas envelope of a distant planet is like a blanket of mixed gases. As the planet passes in front of its star, the starlight seeps through this blanket, and each gas takes a piece of the rainbow — some colors disappear, and we see the "shadows" of molecules.
exoplanet_atmosphere.history_simple: In 2001, astronomers using the Hubble telescope first captured the atmospheric imprint of planet HD 209458 b, seeing traces of sodium in its spectrum.
exoplanet_atmosphere.how_it_works_simple: Imagine looking at a lightbulb through colored glass: the glass absorbs some light, and you see what color it is. It's similar with a planet's atmosphere: we compare the star's light during the planet's passage and without it, and the difference shows what the gases "drank".
exoplanet_atmosphere.description: An exoplanet atmosphere is the outer gaseous envelope of a planet beyond the Solar System. Its study relies mainly on transmission and emission spectroscopy. During transit, starlight passing through the planet's limb is partially absorbed and scattered by the atmosphere, manifesting as an additional eclipse depth that depends on wavelength. Comparing spectra during transit and out of transit yields a transmission spectrum, from which inverse modeling (atmospheric retrieval) reconstructs the vertical temperature profile, chemical composition, and the presence of clouds and hazes. Emission spectroscopy measures the atmosphere's own thermal glow on the dayside, and light curves with phase curves allow construction of temperature maps.
exoplanet_atmosphere.history: The first successful analysis of an exoplanet atmosphere was performed by David Charbonneau's group in 2001 using the STIS spectrograph on the Hubble telescope. Sodium at 589 nm was detected on planet HD 209458 b. Later, the Spitzer infrared telescope and the Kepler space observatory led by William Borucki enabled statistical studies of hundreds of hot Jupiters. With the launch of JWST, the era of detailed chemical analysis of Earth-like planet atmospheres began.
exoplanet_atmosphere.how_it_works: During transit, the fraction of radiation blocked by the planet is (R_p/R_s)^2, and the thin atmospheric shell adds about 2R_p·h/R_s^2 to the absorption, where h is the effective height of the atmosphere. This height is given by the scale height H = kT/(μg), where k is Boltzmann's constant, T is temperature, μ is mean molecular mass, and g is gravitational acceleration. Absorption at specific wavelengths follows the Bouguer–Lambert–Beer law; the depth of spectral lines reflects the abundance of the corresponding chemical elements. Light curves at different wavelengths are recorded photometrically, and spectral resolution allows identification of molecular bands of H₂O, CO, CO₂, CH₄, etc.
key_problems: Influence of clouds and photochemical hazes masking spectral features
Uncertainty in atmospheric escape rates and loss of volatiles
False-positive biomarkers: abiotic sources of O₂, CH₄
Limited signal-to-noise ratio and model incompleteness in retrieval
exoplanet_atmosphere.fun_fact: The ARIEL mission (Atmospheric Remote-sensing Infrared Exoplanet Large-survey), launching in 2029, plans to conduct a systematic spectroscopic survey of about 1000 exoplanet atmospheres.

formulas.description: Atmospheric scale height
formulas.meaning: H — scale height (characteristic thickness of the atmosphere), k — Boltzmann constant (1.380649×10⁻²³ J/K), T — atmospheric temperature (K), μ — mean molecular mass (kg), g — gravitational acceleration (m/s²)

formulas.description: Additional transit depth due to atmosphere
formulas.meaning: ΔF — excess eclipse depth (relative flux decrease), R_p — planet radius (m), h — effective thickness of the absorbing layer (m), R_s — stellar radius (m)

free_floating_planet.name: free-floating planet
free_floating_planet.mini: A wanderer planet, ejected from its home system, silently drifting through interstellar darkness, betrayed only by a fleeting amplification of a distant star's light.
free_floating_planet.practical_application: Revising theories of planetary system formation and estimating the population of potentially habitable worlds hidden in interstellar space.
free_floating_planet.description_popular: Free-floating planets are not gravitationally bound to any star. Their discovery was made possible by two methods: direct observation in the infrared if the planet is young and hot, and gravitational microlensing — when the planet focuses the light of a background star into a characteristic symmetric brightness spike. The light curve conveys information about mass and distance, and the absence of repeated events indicates its isolation.
free_floating_planet.fun_fact_popular: According to one estimate, there could be up to 20 billion free-floating planets in the Milky Way — more than stars — and some of them can hold onto moons.
free_floating_planet.description_simple: Imagine a Jupiter-sized ball that lost its Sun. Such solitary planets form like ordinary planets, but during a game of "gravitational checkers" with their siblings, they get flung out. We notice them when they pass in front of a distant star and briefly magnify its brightness with their gravity, like a lens.
free_floating_planet.history_simple: The first reliable candidates for free-floating planets were discovered in 2004 from their faint infrared glow in the Orion cluster and from microlensing events.
free_floating_planet.how_it_works_simple: Planets are born in gas and dust disks around stars. If another massive planet is nearby or a star flies by, gravitational slingshot can tear a smaller planet from its parent star's embrace, turning it into an eternal wanderer. To find it, astronomers look for "bumps" on light curves — a momentary amplification of light predicted by relativity.
free_floating_planet.description: A free-floating planet (also rogue planet) is a planetary-mass object not gravitationally bound to any star and drifting through the galaxy as an independent body. Such objects form in protoplanetary disks similarly to ordinary planets, but subsequent dynamical instability in the system (planet-planet scattering, tidal interaction with the star, external perturbation from a passing star) leads to their ejection. A less common channel is the collapse of an isolated gas-dust cloud, similar to star formation but at the limit of the smallest possible mass.
free_floating_planet.history: The first candidates were substellar-mass objects in the Orion cluster, recorded in deep infrared surveys in the late 1990s – early 2000s. A key breakthrough came from gravitational microlensing experiments (OGLE, MOA) starting in the 2000s, revealing many short, star-unassociated lensing events. In 2011, a statistically significant population of invisible planetary masses in the Galaxy was announced.
free_floating_planet.how_it_works: The main detection method is gravitational microlensing. When a planet passes near the line of sight to a background star, its gravitational field, according to general relativity, curves spacetime and focuses light, creating two images that are individually unresolved but produce a spike in total brightness. The characteristic angular scale is the Einstein radius: θ_E = √((4GM)/(c²) · (D_ls)/(D_l D_s)), where M is the lens mass, D_l, D_s, D_ls are distances (observer–lens, observer–source, lens–source). The event duration t_E = θ_E/μ_rel, where μ_rel is the relative proper motion, is limited to a few hours for Earth-mass planets. Free-floating planets produce simple, symmetric curves without additional caustics, distinguishing them from lensing campaigns for exoplanets in bound systems.
key_problems: Initial mass function: what is the contribution of isolated cloud collapse versus disk ejection?
Could such a planet have a subsurface ocean and sustain life through radioactive heat?
Distribution in the Galaxy and total number of Earth-mass objects
free_floating_planet.fun_fact: Microlensing can detect planets as small as 0.01 Earth masses at distances to the Galactic center, with events lasting from hours to days.

formulas.description: Angular Einstein radius for gravitational microlensing
formulas.meaning: θ_E — angular Einstein radius (radians), G — gravitational constant (6.67430×10⁻¹¹ m³·kg⁻¹·s⁻²), M — lens mass (kg), c — speed of light (299792458 m/s), D_ls — distance from lens to source (m), D_l — distance from observer to lens (m), D_s — distance from observer to source (m)

numerical_simulation.name: numerical simulation
numerical_simulation.mini: A digital universe in a supercomputer, where the laws of physics turn into pixels and frames, predicting the fate of galaxies and the dance of elementary particles.
numerical_simulation.practical_application: Creation of virtual prototypes in aviation, climate prediction, discovery of gravitational waves by comparing real signals with simulated templates.
numerical_simulation.description_popular: Numerical simulation replaces the continuous differential equations describing physics with difference schemes, where the computational domain is divided into a discrete grid or particle cloud. At each time step, field values (density, velocity, temperature) are updated using approximate formulas that respect conservation laws. This approach allows studying nonlinear systems like turbulence or galaxy formation, for which analytical solutions are absent.
numerical_simulation.fun_fact_popular: The IllustrisTNG project reproduced the evolution of a cube with a side of 300 million light-years, containing millions of galaxies, and scientists can "fly" inside any of them.
numerical_simulation.description_simple: Start a computer game where instead of keys, you set the laws of physics: how bodies attract, how stellar wind blows, how supernovae explode. The computer divides the world into billions of cells and recalculates step by step what happens next. This creates a movie of a galaxy's life that cannot be filmed in real life.
numerical_simulation.history_simple: The first numerical experiments were done by hand back in the 17th century, but it really began with the advent of computers in the 1940s for calculating nuclear explosions. In astrophysics, star cluster modeling began in the 1960s.
numerical_simulation.how_it_works_simple: We take a complex equation describing, for example, how planets move, and break it down into simple time steps: where will the planet be in a second? And in another second? Repeating this millions of times gives the path. The smaller the step, the more accurate, but more computing power is needed.
numerical_simulation.description: Numerical simulation is a method for studying physical systems, based on approximating the governing equations (continuum mechanics, electrodynamics, gravity, quantum mechanics) with discrete analogs and then integrating over time on a computer. A typical scheme includes: choosing a mathematical model (e.g., Navier–Stokes equations or Einstein's equations), discretizing space and time (finite differences, finite elements, spectral methods), setting initial and boundary conditions, selecting a numerical solution algorithm and time step, checking stability and convergence, and visualizing and analyzing results.
numerical_simulation.history: The origins go back to manual numerical orbit calculations performed by 19th-century astronomers. Decisive impetus came from research in turbulence and nuclear physics in the mid-20th century (work by von Neumann and Ulam on the Monte Carlo method). In astrophysics, pioneering were the works of Hénon and Aarseth on modeling star clusters in the 1960s. Henri Poincaré laid the foundations of dynamical systems theory, crucial for understanding chaos in numerical calculations. With the growth of supercomputers in the 1980s–2000s, full-scale cosmological simulations emerged (Millennium, EAGLE, Illustris).
numerical_simulation.how_it_works: It is based on discretizing partial differential equations. For example, in hydrodynamics, the conservation laws for mass, momentum, and energy are written as ∂U/∂t + ∇·F = S, where U is the vector of conservative variables, F are fluxes, and S are sources. The heart of the algorithm is the Courant–Friedrichs–Lewy (CFL) condition ensuring stability: Courant number C = c·Δt/Δx ≤ 1, where c is the wave speed, Δt and Δx are time and space steps. Modern codes use adaptive mesh refinement and parallelization over thousands of cores. For gravity problems, fast Fourier transforms or hierarchical tree methods are used.
key_problems: Insufficient spatial resolution to describe turbulence and star formation
Parameterization of subgrid processes (star formation, feedback)
Beam instabilities and accumulation of round-off errors
Convergence and code verification in the strongly nonlinear regime
numerical_simulation.fun_fact: The "GigaEris" simulation used over 250 billion particles for a single galaxy cluster, requiring about 1 petabyte of disk space.

formulas.description: Courant–Friedrichs–Lewy (CFL) stability condition
formulas.meaning: C — Courant number (dimensionless stability parameter), c — maximum wave propagation speed in the medium (m/s), Δt — time step (s), Δx — spatial grid step (m)

active_galactic_nucleus.name: active galactic nucleus
active_galactic_nucleus.mini: A cosmic beacon smaller than the Solar System, outshining the light of its entire galaxy. A supermassive black hole sucks in matter and accelerates it to near-light speeds, turning it into a glowing disk and jets.
active_galactic_nucleus.practical_application: The jets of active nuclei are natural particle accelerators, helping to study high-energy physics. Their radiation is used to calibrate space navigation systems.
active_galactic_nucleus.description_popular: An active galactic nucleus is the central region where a supermassive black hole (with a mass of millions to billions of Suns) actively consumes matter. The infalling gas forms an accretion disk, in which viscous friction converts gravitational energy into heat and radiation spanning the entire spectrum from radio to gamma rays. Powerful magnetic fields twist some of the matter into collimated jets ejected at relativistic speeds. Various types are distinguished: quasars, blazars, Seyfert galaxies — depending on the line-of-sight orientation and power.
active_galactic_nucleus.fun_fact_popular: Some active nuclei change brightness in minutes, even though their size is like the Solar System — meaning the energy is released from a region smaller than a light-day.
active_galactic_nucleus.description_simple: At the center of almost every large galaxy hides a supermassive black hole (an object with gravity so strong that even light cannot escape). When there is a lot of gas and dust around it, they swirl into an accretion disk (a spiral of infalling matter). Friction heats the disk to millions of degrees, and it begins to shine brighter than all the stars of the galaxy combined. Often, two narrow beams — jets (streams of particles) — burst from the center, shooting out for thousands of light-years. Such a nucleus is called active.
active_galactic_nucleus.history_simple: The first active nucleus was noticed in 1908 as a strange bright center of a galaxy, but its nature was understood later. In 1963, astronomer Maarten Schmidt showed that quasars are distant active nuclei.
active_galactic_nucleus.how_it_works_simple: Like a whirlpool in a bathtub: water (gas) swirls around the drain (black hole), accelerates, and creates noise and splashes (radiation and jets).
active_galactic_nucleus.description: An active galactic nucleus (AGN) is a compact region at the center of a galaxy emitting anomalously high power (up to 10^46 erg/s) across a wide range of wavelengths. The energy is released by accretion of matter onto a supermassive black hole (10^6–10^9 M☉). The infalling gas with angular momentum forms an accretion disk, where magnetorotational instability provides viscosity. The radiation is produced from gravitational potential energy: the efficiency of mass-to-energy conversion reaches 10–40%, far higher than nuclear fusion. Relativistic jets are often observed, formed by the Blandford–Znajek mechanism or through electromagnetic extraction of the hole's rotational energy. The AGN spectrum contains both thermal (disk) and non-thermal (non-thermal jet emission, synchrotron, inverse Compton) components. Classification is based on orientation, the presence of broad emission lines, and radio power. A unified scheme explains the differences between types mainly by the orientation of a dust torus that hides some regions.
active_galactic_nucleus.history: In 1908, Edward Fath discovered emission lines in the nucleus of galaxy M87, but recognition came later. Carl Seyfert in 1943 systematized galaxies with bright nuclei.
