Maarten Schmidt in 1963 interpreted the spectrum of quasar 3C 273 as highly redshifted, proving its extragalactic nature and enormous luminosity. Yakov Zeldovich and Edwin Salpeter independently proposed accretion onto a black hole as the energy source. The modern picture emerged in the 1970s–80s thanks to X-ray observations and the development of disk and jet models.
active_galactic_nucleus.how_it_works: Accretion of matter onto a black hole through a disk, where gravitational energy converts into heat and radiation. Some of the energy can be extracted from the hole's rotation via the Blandford–Znajek mechanism (an electromagnetic process involving the ergosphere), producing jets. The unified model assumes the central source is surrounded by a dust torus; the visible type depends on whether we see the disk directly (Seyfert I) or through the torus (Seyfert II). The beam velocity of jets is close to the speed of light, explaining superluminal motions due to relativistic aberration. Principles: accretion follows the equations of hydrodynamics and magnetohydrodynamics, radiation follows the laws of thermal radiation and synchrotron mechanism, relativistic radiation is affected by special relativity effects.
key_problems: Mechanisms of jet launching and collimation down to parsec scales.
The nature of variability on ultrafast timescales.
The role of magnetic fields in transferring angular momentum in the disk.
The link between AGN evolution, galaxy growth, and star formation.
Formation of the dusty torus and its geometry.
active_galactic_nucleus.fun_fact: Blazar APM 08279+5255 contains a supermassive black hole that consumes the mass of the Sun every few hours, and its jet is aimed directly at Earth.

formulas.description: Eddington luminosity — the limiting luminosity at which radiation pressure balances gravity, limiting the accretion rate
formulas.meaning: L_Edd — Eddington luminosity (maximum radiation power), G — gravitational constant (≈ 6.674×10⁻¹¹ m³/(kg·s²)), M — mass of the central object, m_p — proton mass (≈ 1.673×10⁻²⁷ kg), c — speed of light (≈ 3×10⁸ m/s), σ_T — Thomson scattering cross-section (≈ 6.652×10⁻²⁹ m²)

formulas.description: Accretion efficiency — the fraction of the accreting matter's mass that turns into radiation
formulas.meaning: η — efficiency (dimensionless, for a non-rotating black hole ~0.057, for a rotating one up to 0.42), L — luminosity (emitted power), \dot{M} — accretion rate (mass of matter falling per unit time), c — speed of light (≈ 3×10⁸ m/s)

polarimetry.name: polarimetry
polarimetry.mini: A lie detector for light. When light reflects or scatters, its invisible vibrations acquire a preferred direction — polarization, which tells about the shape of particles, magnetic fields, and even dark matter.
polarimetry.practical_application: In industry, polarimetry determines sugar concentration in solutions (saccharimetry). In astronomy, it reveals dust in exoplanet atmospheres and magnetic fields of stars.
polarimetry.description_popular: Polarimetry is a technique for measuring the polarization state of electromagnetic radiation. Light with a preferred orientation of the electric field is called polarized. The degree of polarization (from 0 to 1) and the angle of orientation provide information about the radiation sources and the propagation medium. Linear polarization arises from scattering by fine dust, reflection; circular polarization arises when passing through a medium with optical activity or anisotropic magnetic fields. In astronomical observations, polarizers and modulators, often with an analyzer, are used to construct polarization maps.
polarimetry.fun_fact_popular: Some scarab beetles see circular polarization of the sky, which helps them navigate even when the Sun is hidden by clouds.
polarimetry.description_simple: Light is a wave (a propagating oscillation of the electromagnetic field) that oscillates perpendicular to the direction of motion. Ordinary light oscillates in all transverse directions randomly. Polarized light oscillates only in one plane, like a rope being shaken up and down. Polarimetry is the measurement of the direction and degree of this orderliness. It helps to know what the light has passed through: for example, reflection from a water surface makes it horizontally polarized, so polarizing glasses remove glare.
polarimetry.history_simple: Étienne Malus discovered polarization in 1808 while looking at light reflected from a window of the Luxembourg Palace through a crystal of Icelandic spar. Later, James Maxwell and others linked polarization to electromagnetic theory.
polarimetry.how_it_works_simple: Just as a slot in a fence allows only a rope oscillating along the slot to pass, a polarizer (a special filter) transmits light with only one direction of oscillation. By the change in intensity after passing through a rotating analyzer, the degree of polarization is determined.
polarimetry.description: Polarimetry is a set of methods for measuring the polarization of light. The polarization state is described by the Stokes vector (I, Q, U, V), where I is the total intensity, Q and U are linear polarization, V is circular. The measurement consists in determining these parameters by passing radiation through polarizers (analyzers) and phase plates (quarter-wave, half-wave) with subsequent intensity recording. In physical problems, polarimetry provides information about the asymmetry of the emitting or scattering medium: synchrotron radiation in magnetic fields is linearly polarized, Mie scattering by interstellar dust polarizes starlight, the Zeeman effect creates circular polarization in spectral lines in magnetic fields. Accuracy reaches fractions of a percent. It is used in remote sensing, astrophysics (protoplanetary disks, polars, jets), and atmospheric physics.
polarimetry.history: Étienne Malus in 1808 described the phenomenon he called polarization, observing reflected light through a calcite crystal. David Brewster discovered the law of the angle of complete polarization upon reflection (1815). Augustin Fresnel and Dominique Arago investigated the interference of polarized light. James Clerk Maxwell in 1865, while formulating Maxwell's equations, theoretically described electromagnetic waves and included polarization as a vector property. William Lawrence Bragg developed X-ray polarimetry. In astronomy, pioneers were Bernard Lyot (inventor of the polarizing filter for the coronagraph) and William Hiltner, who detected interstellar polarization in 1949.
polarimetry.how_it_works: Light passes through an optical path with polarization elements: first, phase-sensitive modulators (e.g., Pockels cell or rotating half-wave plate) change the polarization state, then an analyzer (polarizer) transmits the component along its axis, and a photodetector records the signal. Modulation allows separating the Stokes parameters by phase. In radio astronomy, polarization is measured by correlating orthogonally polarized channels. The degree of polarization p = sqrt(Q^2+U^2+V^2)/I. Key effects: electron scattering (linear polarization), synchrotron radiation (polarization direction perpendicular to the magnetic field), Zeeman effect (circular polarization in spectral lines), reflection from a surface (Brewster's angle). The principle of superposition and statistical signal processing are at the core.
key_problems: Ultimate accuracy in measuring very low polarization (instrumental depolarization).
Difficulty in separating the contributions of different polarization mechanisms along the line of sight.
Calibration of polarization measurements on space telescopes.
Interpretation of temporal variability of polarization in active nuclei.
Detection of B-mode polarization of the cosmic microwave background for primordial gravitational waves.
polarimetry.fun_fact: Iceland spar (calcite) crystals split the image due to double refraction — it was historically the first polarization analyzer, used by Malus.

formulas.description: Brewster's law — the angle of incidence at which reflected light is completely linearly polarized
formulas.meaning: θ_B — Brewster's angle (angle of incidence), n_1 — refractive index of the medium from which light falls, n_2 — refractive index of the medium into which light enters

formulas.description: Malus's law — intensity of light after passing through a polarizer
formulas.meaning: I — intensity of light after the polarizer, I_0 — initial intensity of polarized light entering the polarizer, φ — angle between the polarization direction of the incoming light and the transmission axis of the polarizer

interstellar_object.name: interstellar object
interstellar_object.mini: A visitor from another star system — an asteroid or comet ejected by its parent star and wandering the Galaxy. Such objects whiz past the Sun, revealing the composition of distant worlds.
interstellar_object.practical_application: Tracking interstellar objects helps prepare for sudden encounters with hazardous bodies. Their material is a direct sample of the building blocks of other planetary systems, accessible without traveling to other stars.
interstellar_object.description_popular: An interstellar object is an asteroid or comet not gravitationally bound to any star, arriving from interstellar space. It is distinguished by a hyperbolic orbit with eccentricity >1 relative to the Sun, indicating its extrasolar origin. Two such objects have been confirmed: 1I/ʻOumuamua (2017), a rocky body without a cometary coma but with non-gravitational acceleration, possibly elongated; and 2I/Borisov (2019), a comet with a typical coma and tail but extremely rich in carbon monoxide. It is expected that interstellar bodies pass through the Solar System regularly, but they are difficult to detect due to their faint brightness.
interstellar_object.fun_fact_popular: ʻOumuamua means 'scout' or 'messenger from afar' in Hawaiian, and its speed was so great that it left for interstellar space just a few months after discovery.
interstellar_object.description_simple: An interstellar object is a celestial body (asteroid or comet) that was born near another star and then ejected into interstellar space (the void between stars). Flying through our Solar System, it moves so fast that the Sun's gravity cannot hold it. In 2017, the first such object was discovered — ʻOumuamua, which had an unusual elongated shape, and in 2019, comet 2I/Borisov, similar to our comets.
interstellar_object.history_simple: The existence of ejected bodies had been suspected for a long time, but evidence was obtained only in 2017 when ʻOumuamua was found using the Pan-STARRS telescope. The second object, 2I/Borisov, was discovered by amateur astronomer Gennady Borisov in 2019.
interstellar_object.how_it_works_simple: Imagine playing billiards: a strong hit with the cue can knock a ball from another table onto yours. That's how giant planets with their gravity eject small bodies from a system.
interstellar_object.description: An interstellar object (ISO) is a small Solar System body whose velocity at infinity (v_inf) exceeds the local escape velocity from the Sun, indicating a hyperbolic orbit and interstellar origin. The isotropic distribution of stellar velocities in the Galaxy gives a characteristic v_inf of about 25 km/s. Objects can be ejected from planetary systems as a result of gravitational interaction with giant planets. It is estimated that within the orbit of Neptune, there are about 10^4 interstellar bodies larger than 100 m at any given time. They provide a unique opportunity for in-situ study of the composition of planetesimals from other stars. ʻOumuamua exhibited brightness variations with an amplitude >2.5 magnitudes and a period of ~8 hours, interpreted as an extremely elongated shape (axis ratio up to 10:1) or as a flat disk. Non-gravitational acceleration is explained by outgassing or solar radiation pressure. 2I/Borisov showed a high CO/H2O ratio, distinguishing it from Solar System comets and indicating formation in a cold region of a protoplanetary disk.
interstellar_object.history: The idea of interstellar comets was discussed by theorists (e.g., Jan Oort) as early as the mid-20th century. The first detection was on October 19, 2017, by the Pan-STARRS1 system in Hawaii — object C/2017 U1, later renamed 1I/ʻOumuamua. Its interstellar nature was confirmed by a hyperbolic orbit with v_inf ~ 26 km/s. The second object, 2I/Borisov, was discovered on August 30, 2019, by Gennady Borisov; cometary activity facilitated spectroscopy. Missions (e.g., ESA/JAXA's Comet Interceptor) are being developed to intercept future objects.
interstellar_object.how_it_works: Objects form in protoplanetary disks of other stars as planetesimals; giant planets scatter them, imparting speeds above the parabolic limit relative to the star. Some escape into interstellar space. Upon approaching the Sun, orbital momentum and energy determine a hyperbolic trajectory. Observational manifestations: astrometry for orbit determination, photometry for shape and rotation, spectroscopy for composition. For ʻOumuamua, a radiation pressure model was applied to explain the non-gravitational acceleration. Laws: Newton's law of universal gravitation for the trajectory, the law of conservation of energy for a hyperbolic orbit, equations of motion with non-conservative forces. Gravitational lensing is not applicable (masses are small).
key_problems: Origin of ʻOumuamua's extreme shape: dynamic stretching or a fragment of a disrupted body.
Nature of ʻOumuamua's non-gravitational acceleration: outgassing of invisible volatiles or radiation pressure.
Typical influx rate of interstellar objects and their size distribution.
The possibility of distinguishing an interstellar object from an Oort Cloud body with a perturbed orbit.
Organization of a rapid mission to a passing object for detailed study.
interstellar_object.fun_fact: Upon discovery, ʻOumuamua was first thought to be a comet, then an asteroid, and then non-gravitational acceleration again hinted at a cometary nature — the object remains mysterious.

formulas.description: Velocity at infinity (v_inf) — the excess speed relative to the Sun at a large distance, defines the hyperbolic excess
formulas.meaning: v_∞ — speed of the object at an infinitely large distance from the Sun (hyperbolic excess velocity, for ʻOumuamua ~ 26 km/s), E — total mechanical energy of the body (kinetic+potential), m — mass of the object

formulas.description: Orbital eccentricity — a measure of non-circularity, for interstellar objects e > 1
formulas.meaning: e — orbital eccentricity (dimensionless), E — total mechanical energy, h — specific angular momentum, μ — gravitational parameter of the Sun (μ = G M_sun, G — gravitational constant (≈ 6.674×10⁻¹¹ m³/(kg·s²)), M_sun — mass of the Sun ≈ 1.989×10³⁰ kg), m — mass of the object (cancels out if h is specific)

plasma.name: plasma
plasma.mini: Plasma is a gas in which electrons are torn from atoms, turning it into a soup of charged particles. Lightning and the Sun are examples of plasma in action.
plasma.practical_application: Plasma is used in fluorescent lamps and plasma televisions, and in the future will enable clean energy through nuclear fusion.
plasma.description_popular: Plasma is an ionized gas of free electrons and positive ions. Unlike a neutral gas, it is electrically conductive and subject to the influence of electromagnetic fields. More than 99% of the visible matter in the Universe is plasma: stars, interstellar gas, accretion disks. On Earth, it can be found in lightning, auroras, and artificial devices from neon signs to tokamaks.
plasma.fun_fact_popular: Plasma can spontaneously form structures — 'plasma crystals,' ordered lattices of charged dust particles floating in the plasma.
plasma.description_simple: When a gas is heated to a very high temperature, its atoms break down into positive ions (atoms that lost electrons) and free electrons. This 'electric gas' is called plasma — the fourth state of matter after solid, liquid, and ordinary gas. Plasma conducts electricity and responds to magnetic fields, like a crowd at a disco that moves chaotically but can flash synchronously to the music (electric field).
plasma.history_simple: The word 'plasma' (from Greek 'molded') was proposed by American physicist Irving Langmuir in 1928 while studying electrical discharges in gases. But the phenomenon has long been known: lightning and St. Elmo's fire are plasma.
plasma.how_it_works_simple: When strongly heated, collisions between atoms knock electrons out of their orbits. A mixture of charged particles forms that collectively responds to electric and magnetic fields — much like a flock of birds simultaneously changing direction.
plasma.description: Plasma is the fourth state of matter, characterized by the presence of free charged particles (electrons and ions), due to which it exhibits collective electromagnetic properties. Quasi-neutrality on macroscopic scales is combined with local electric fields that give rise to complex behavior: waves, instabilities, and self-generation of magnetic fields. Plasma is described by magnetohydrodynamics equations and kinetic theory.
plasma.history: Although gas discharges were studied by Michael Faraday in the 19th century, the term 'plasma' was introduced by Irving Langmuir in 1928 while working with ionized gases in electron tubes. Langmuir noticed a similarity between the behavior of the multi-electron medium and living tissue and used the Greek word meaning 'form' or 'molding'. The development of plasma theory in the mid-20th century was stimulated by astrophysics and thermonuclear research; major contributions were made by James Clerk Maxwell (equations of electrodynamics) and Ludwig Boltzmann (statistical kinetics).
plasma.how_it_works: The plasma state is achieved when the thermal motion energy exceeds the binding energy of electrons in atoms. A mixture of charged particles arises, interacting via long-range Coulomb forces, leading to collective effects: Debye screening and plasma oscillations. In a magnetic field, plasma is frozen into field lines, which explains its structures in space. To describe plasma, Maxwell's equations are used in combination with equations of particle motion; in thermodynamic equilibrium, the particle distribution follows the Boltzmann distribution.
key_problems: Controlled thermonuclear fusion: confining plasma with temperatures of hundreds of millions of degrees.
Turbulence in plasma: understanding the mechanisms of energy and particle transport.
The problem of solar corona heating: why the Sun's corona is hotter than its surface.
plasma.fun_fact: In plasma, the phenomenon of 'magnetization' can occur, where electrons and ions gyrate around magnetic field lines at a specific frequency (cyclotron).

formulas.description: Plasma frequency — the natural frequency of collective oscillations of electrons in a plasma
formulas.meaning: ω_p — plasma frequency, n_e — electron density, e — elementary charge (e ≈ 1.602×10⁻¹⁹ C), ε₀ — electric constant (ε₀ ≈ 8.854×10⁻¹² F/m), m_e — electron mass (m_e ≈ 9.109×10⁻³¹ kg)

formulas.description: Debye length — the distance over which the electric field of a charge is screened in a plasma
formulas.meaning: λ_D — Debye length, ε₀ — electric constant (ε₀ ≈ 8.854×10⁻¹² F/m), k_B — Boltzmann constant (k_B ≈ 1.381×10⁻²³ J/K), T_e — electron temperature in Kelvin, n_e — electron density, e — elementary charge (e ≈ 1.602×10⁻¹⁹ C)

jet.name: jet
jet.mini: An astrophysical jet is a narrow stream of plasma shooting out from the poles of black holes or young stars at near-light speed. Like from a garden hose, but instead of water — superheated gas flying through a galaxy.
jet.practical_application: Jets help us understand how black holes regulate galaxy growth, and their nature can be used to test physics of extreme states.
jet.description_popular: A jet is a collimated stream of plasma, moving at relativistic speeds and emitting radiation from radio to gamma rays. It forms in the vicinity of compact objects — black holes, neutron stars, or protostars — due to the accretion of matter and the twisting of magnetic fields. Jets carry enormous kinetic energy and can influence the interstellar medium, creating giant bubbles and shock waves.
jet.fun_fact_popular: The longest known jet (in galaxy J1420-0545) stretches for nearly 16 million light-years — greater than the distance from us to the nearest large galaxy cluster.
jet.description_simple: A jet is a long and very narrow stream of plasma (electrically charged gas) that is ejected from the center of some galaxies or young stars. Imagine a fire hose that blasts out glowing matter with tremendous force, but aimed not in one direction but in two opposite ones, like two sharp beams. These streams can stretch for millions of light-years.
jet.history_simple: The first jets were noticed in galaxy M87 in 1918, but their nature was not understood for a long time. In the 1970s, with the development of radio astronomy, it became clear that they are associated with black holes.
jet.how_it_works_simple: Around a black hole, a disk of gas (accretion disk) rotates. Due to strong magnetic fields and rotation, some of this gas does not fall into the hole but is ejected outward along the rotation axis, like spinning pizza dough — it flies apart.
jet.description: A relativistic jet is a narrowly collimated bipolar outflow of plasma, formed in an 'accretion disk — compact object' system. The launching mechanism involves magnetohydrodynamic processes: the rotation of the magnetized disk twists the field lines, creating toroidal fields that accelerate plasma along the rotation axis to relativistic speeds (Lorentz factor up to 10–100). Jets exhibit superluminal motion, emit synchrotron and inverse Compton components, and are also sources of neutrinos and high-energy cosmic rays.
jet.history: An extended stream in galaxy M87 was first described by Heber Curtis in 1918. For a long time, jets were considered rare, but with the development of radio interferometry in the 1970s, they were discovered in many active galactic nuclei and quasars. Theoretical understanding advanced thanks to the work of Roger Blandford and Roman Znajek (the Blandford-Znajek mechanism for extracting energy from a rotating black hole) and John Wheeler, who coined the term 'black hole' and promoted recognition of their astrophysical role. Space observatories (Hubble, Chandra, Event Horizon Telescope) have allowed observation of jets at high resolution.
jet.how_it_works: Gas falling onto a compact object forms a rotating accretion disk. The frozen-in magnetic field of the disk is amplified by differential rotation. Part of the rotational energy is transferred to the plasma via magnetic stresses, and it is ejected as two oppositely directed streams. For black holes, an additional mechanism is possible: the rotating hole interacts with the magnetic field, inducing an electric potential and accelerating charges along the axis — the Blandford-Znajek process. Conservation of angular momentum plays a key role.
key_problems: The collimation mechanism is not fully understood: why do jets remain so narrow over enormous distances?
Composition problem: the relative contribution of electron-positron plasma compared to ordinary electron-ion plasma is not precisely known.
Formation of extremely powerful jets from quasars at limited accretion rates.
jet.fun_fact: Some jets rotate around their axis, displaying a spiral structure of the magnetic field twisted like a donut — this phenomenon is called a 'magnetic rope'.

formulas.description: Apparent speed of jet motion (superluminal motion)
formulas.meaning: β_app — apparent speed in units of c, β — true speed of the jet in units of c (β = v/c), θ — angle between the direction of the jet and the line of sight

refractive_index.name: refractive index
refractive_index.mini: Light changes speed in different media, causing its path to bend — like a runner turning from asphalt onto sand.
refractive_index.practical_application: Without accounting for refractive index, eyeglass lenses or camera objectives would not focus light, and fiber optic internet would be impossible.
refractive_index.description_popular: The refractive index (n) is a dimensionless quantity equal to the ratio of the speed of light in vacuum (c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s) to its phase velocity in a given medium. When light transitions between media with different n, it undergoes refraction according to Snell's law: n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂. The phenomenon is explained by the electromagnetic nature of light: the interaction of the wave with the electrons of the material slows its propagation.
refractive_index.fun_fact_popular: Diamond has n ≈ 2.42 — one of the highest among natural materials, so cut diamonds sparkle so intensely: light reflects many times inside the stone due to total internal reflection.
refractive_index.description_simple: The refractive index tells how many times slower light is in a substance than in a vacuum (emptiness). When light enters water from air, it is as if stepping on sticky tape — slows down and bends. That's why a straw in a glass looks broken.
refractive_index.history_simple: The law of refraction was discovered by the Dutchman Willebrord Snell in 1621, but for a long time the nature of light remained a mystery.
refractive_index.how_it_works_simple: Light is a wave. In a dense medium (e.g., glass), the wave has a harder time 'pushing through' between atoms, so it slows down. Due to the speed difference at the interface, the wave changes direction — like a line of soldiers marching from hard ground onto a plowed field: the end file slows down first, and the whole line turns.
refractive_index.description: The refractive index (n) is a fundamental optical characteristic of a medium, determining the phase velocity of electromagnetic waves. It depends on the wave frequency (dispersion), temperature, and density of the material. In anisotropic media (e.g., crystals), the refractive index differs for different polarization directions (birefringence). In metamaterials, negative n is possible, leading to inverse refraction.
refractive_index.history: The law of refraction was empirically discovered by Willebrord Snell (1621) and later mathematically formulated by René Descartes. Isaac Newton and Christiaan Huygens debated the nature of light, explaining refraction with corpuscular or wave theories, respectively. In the 19th century, James Clerk Maxwell derived the electromagnetic theory of light, linking the refractive index to dielectric and magnetic permittivities: n ≈ √εᵣμᵣ (for non-magnetic media n ≈ √εᵣ). This allowed quantitative prediction of n from the electrical properties of materials.
refractive_index.how_it_works: Light is an electromagnetic wave. The electric field of the wave polarizes the atoms of the medium, i.e., displaces electron shells relative to nuclei, creating oscillating dipoles. These dipoles re-emit secondary waves that interfere with the original wave, slowing its propagation. On a microscopic level, the refractive index is related to molecular polarizability through the Clausius–Mossotti equation. Dispersion (dependence of n on wavelength) is explained by resonant absorption near natural frequencies of electronic transitions; far from resonances, n decreases smoothly with increasing wavelength (normal dispersion), described by the Cauchy formula.
key_problems: Creating materials with a predetermined refractive index (including negative) for controlling light in nanophotonics.
Accurate prediction of n for complex composites from first principles.
Miniaturization of optical elements with gradient refractive index.
refractive_index.fun_fact: In some transparent materials, such as Teflon, n is very close to 1.3 — like water, so they are almost invisible in benzene (n ≈ 1.50) — they become 'invisible' due to matching refractive indices.

formulas.description: Definition of refractive index
formulas.meaning: n — refractive index, dimensionless; c — speed of light in vacuum, c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s; v — phase velocity of light in the medium, m/s.

formulas.description: Snell's law of refraction
formulas.meaning: n_1, n_2 — refractive indices of the first and second media; θ_1 — angle of incidence, θ_2 — angle of refraction (measured from the normal to the interface).

permittivity.name: dielectric permittivity
permittivity.mini: Dielectric permittivity is a measure of how much a medium weakens an electric field, like a sponge absorbing water: the substance 'absorbs' the tension, preventing charges from feeling each other.
permittivity.practical_application: A capacitor in electronics stores charge better the higher the dielectric permittivity of the insulator between the plates; without it, smartphones would be the size of a suitcase.
permittivity.description_popular: Dielectric permittivity (ε) characterizes the ability of a medium to polarize under an electric field and weaken it. In vacuum, the electric constant ε₀ ≈ 8.85·10⁻¹² F/m. The relative permittivity εᵣ = ε/ε₀ shows how many times weaker the interaction of charges in the medium is compared to vacuum. For water, εᵣ ≈ 80, which explains water's high dissolving power for ionic compounds.
permittivity.fun_fact_popular: Thanks to the huge dielectric permittivity of water (about 80 at room temperature), table salt readily dissolves into ions — water seems to pull the ions apart, weakening their bond by a factor of 80.
permittivity.description_simple: Dielectric permittivity shows how many times weaker the electric force is in a substance than in a vacuum. If two charges are placed in oil, they attract or repel each other more weakly than in air, because the oil molecules polarize (turn, forming mini-dipoles) and partially screen the field. It's like a crowd dampening a rumor by passing it inaccurately.
permittivity.history_simple: The concept arose from Michael Faraday's experiments in the 1830s, who noticed that more charge accumulated between the plates of a capacitor with a dielectric. The term 'dielectric permittivity' was introduced by William Thomson (Lord Kelvin). Later, James Clerk Maxwell included it in his equations, linking it with the refractive index.
permittivity.how_it_works_simple: An electric field tends to align molecules of the substance, especially polar ones (e.g., water), into chains along the field. These aligned molecules create their own weak field directed opposite to the external field, weakening it. The easier the molecules turn, the stronger the weakening, i.e., the higher the permittivity.
permittivity.description: Dielectric permittivity is a tensor quantity in anisotropic media and a function of frequency in alternating fields (ε(ω)). Complex dielectric permittivity ε* = ε' + iε'' accounts for energy losses: the imaginary part ε'' describes absorption. In classical electrodynamics, polarization is described by the polarization vector P = ε₀χₑE, where χₑ is the dielectric susceptibility, and εᵣ = 1 + χₑ. At high frequencies, ionic and dipolar polarization mechanisms 'lag behind' the field, and εᵣ decreases, exhibiting frequency dispersion (Debye relaxation). In the quantum picture, interband transitions and excitons determine the dielectric response.
permittivity.history: Ideas about dielectric permittivity date back to Henry Cavendish's experiments (late 18th century) and systematic studies by Michael Faraday (1837), who introduced the concept of 'specific inductive capacity'. The term 'dielectric permittivity' was proposed by William Thomson in 1845. James Clerk Maxwell (1862) mathematically linked ε and the speed of light c = 1/√(ε₀μ₀), demonstrating the electromagnetic nature of light. Later developments in quantum theory of polarizability (by Hans Bethe, Lars Onsager) allowed computation of ε from molecular properties.
permittivity.how_it_works: An applied electric field E causes displacement of charges in the dielectric: electron shells deform (electronic polarization), ionic lattices shift (ionic polarization), permanent dipoles orient (orientational polarization). Each mechanism creates a dipole moment, and the macroscopic polarization P is the sum of moments per unit volume. Dielectric permittivity ε relates electric displacement D = ε₀E + P = ε₀εᵣE. In alternating fields, a phase shift occurs between displacement and field, leading to dielectric losses. The Lorentz oscillator model or the Debye relaxation model is used for description.
key_problems: Creating dielectrics with ultra-low losses for high-frequency applications (5G, radars).
Developing materials with record-high ε for miniaturization of capacitors.
Accurate calculation of dielectric properties of nanostructures and disordered media from first principles.
permittivity.fun_fact: Some ferroelectrics (e.g., barium titanate) have εᵣ up to 10000, but when heated above the Curie point, they abruptly lose this ability due to a phase transition.

formulas.description: Relative permittivity
formulas.meaning: εᵣ — relative permittivity, dimensionless; ε — absolute permittivity of the medium, F/m; ε₀ — electric constant, ε₀ ≈ 8.854×10⁻¹² F/m.

formulas.description: Relation between electric displacement and polarization
formulas.meaning: D — electric displacement vector (C/m²); E — electric field intensity (V/m); P — polarization vector (C/m²); ε₀ — electric constant; εᵣ — relative permittivity.

thermal_conductivity.name: thermal conductivity
thermal_conductivity.mini: Thermal conductivity is the ability of a substance to transmit the 'tremor' of atoms from hot to cold: metal is like a nimble baton, foam plastic is an awkward sloth.
thermal_conductivity.practical_application: Copper pipes in a radiator quickly release heat into the room, while a wool sweater retains body heat due to low thermal conductivity of air in fluffy fibers.
thermal_conductivity.description_popular: Thermal conductivity (k) is the proportionality coefficient in Fourier's law: heat flux density q⃗ = –k ∇T, where ∇T is the temperature gradient. It characterizes the ability of a substance to transfer the kinetic energy of microscopic particles without macroscopic movement of the substance. In metals, heat transfer is mainly by conduction electrons; in dielectrics, it is by phonons (quanta of crystal lattice vibrations). Silver has the highest thermal conductivity among pure metals (k ≈ 430 W/(m·K)).
thermal_conductivity.fun_fact_popular: Diamond has a thermal conductivity five times higher than copper, although diamond is an electrical insulator. The reason is a rigid crystal lattice and light carbon atoms, along which phonons travel quickly.
thermal_conductivity.description_simple: Thermal conductivity shows how quickly heat flows through a material when one end is hot and the other cold. Imagine a line where each person whispers a message to their neighbor: in metals, 'neighbors' (atoms) are tightly packed, and energy hops quickly; in wood, atoms are loose, so transfer is slow. That's why a metal spoon in hot tea heats up, but a wooden stick does not.
thermal_conductivity.history_simple: Early experiments were conducted by Jan Ingenhousz in the 18th century, comparing how different metals conduct heat. But the scientific foundation was laid by Jean Baptiste Joseph Fourier in 1822, who derived the law of heat conduction — the main equation of heat transfer.
thermal_conductivity.how_it_works_simple: Heat is the jiggling of atoms. In a solid, atoms are tightly bound by springs (chemical bonds). If you heat one end, the atoms there start jiggling more and shake their neighbors, passing vibrations along the chain. In metals, there are also free electrons (tiny carrier particles) that, like agile couriers, distribute energy much faster than lattice vibrations. That's why metals conduct heat superbly.
thermal_conductivity.description: Thermal conductivity is a tensor quantity in anisotropic crystals. In general, it depends on temperature, density, structure, and impurities. The microscopic theory for phonons is based on the Boltzmann kinetic equation for the phonon distribution function. In the Debye model, the lattice thermal conductivity k ~ C_v v_s l, where C_v is heat capacity, v_s is speed of sound, l is the phonon mean free path. The main phonon scattering mechanisms are phonon-phonon collisions (normal and Umklapp processes), scattering on defects and sample boundaries. In metals, the electron contribution dominates, described by the Wiedemann–Franz law: k/σ = (π²/3)(k_B/e)² T, where σ is electrical conductivity.
thermal_conductivity.history: The law of thermal conduction was formulated by Joseph Fourier in 1822 in his 'Analytical Theory of Heat'. For over a century, thermal conductivity remained a phenomenological constant. The kinetic theory of gases (James Clerk Maxwell, Ludwig Boltzmann) made it possible to relate it to molecular motion. In the 20th century, the development of quantum solid-state theory (Peter Debye, Rudolf Peierls) led to the phonon picture and the prediction of lattice thermal resistance. At the end of the century, materials with extreme thermal conductivity (diamond, graphene, nanotubes) were discovered.
thermal_conductivity.how_it_works: In solids, heat transfer occurs via two main carriers: conduction electrons and phonons. Phonons — collective lattice vibrations — behave like a gas of quasiparticles and obey Bose–Einstein statistics. The distribution of phonons over frequencies and wave vectors is described by dispersion relations depending on crystal symmetry. Thermal resistance arises because the phonon mean free path is finite, limited by anharmonicity of interatomic potentials (three-phonon processes), isotopic disorder, defects, and grain sizes. At high temperatures, scattering on other phonons (so-called Umklapp processes) dominates. In nanostructures, the mean free path can be limited by geometric dimensions, leading to reduced thermal conductivity (phonon ballistic regime).
key_problems: Theoretical prediction of thermal conductivity of new materials from first principles without empirical parameters.
Creating materials with thermal conductivity controlled by external fields.
Understanding the role of high-order anharmonicities in ultra-high thermal conductivity systems (graphene, cubic boron nitride).
thermal_conductivity.fun_fact: In superfluid helium-4, thermal conductivity does not obey the classical Fourier law: heat propagates as 'second sound' — a temperature wave moving at about 20 m/s.

formulas.description: Fourier's law of heat conduction
formulas.meaning: q⃗ — heat flux density, W/m²; k — thermal conductivity coefficient, W/(m·K); ∇T — temperature gradient, K/m. The minus sign means heat flows from hot to cold.

formulas.description: Wiedemann–Franz law (electron contribution)
formulas.meaning: k — electron thermal conductivity; σ — electrical conductivity, S/m; k_B — Boltzmann constant, k_B ≈ 1.38×10⁻²³ J/K; e — elementary charge, e ≈ 1.602×10⁻¹⁹ C; T — absolute temperature, K.

viscosity.name: viscosity
viscosity.mini: Honey flows slower than water — that's viscosity: the resistance of a fluid to flow, as if its layers cling to each other.
viscosity.practical_application: The viscosity of motor oil protects the engine, and that of lava determines the style of eruption.
viscosity.description_popular: Viscosity is a measure of the resistance of a liquid or gas to shear deformation. It characterizes internal friction between layers moving at different speeds. There are dynamic (absolute) viscosity and kinematic viscosity (dynamic divided by density).
viscosity.fun_fact_popular: In some liquids, viscosity increases when stirred — this is dilatancy, like a mixture of cornstarch and water: you can run on its surface, but if you stop, you sink.
viscosity.description_simple: Viscosity is internal friction in a fluid (gas) that hinders its flow. Water pours easily, while tar does not, because molecules 'cling' to each other. The stronger the friction, the thicker the fluid and the slower it moves.
viscosity.history_simple: Studies of fluids in the 17th century led to the understanding that the friction force between layers is proportional to the speed of sliding. Later, scientists found ways to measure and calculate viscosity, which helped build pipes and lubricate mechanisms.
viscosity.how_it_works_simple: Imagine passing books from shelf to shelf: if you hand them from person to person, it goes quickly. But if each book gets stuck in hands, movement slows. Similarly, in a fluid, molecules exchange momentum, as if passing 'sticky books', creating resistance.
viscosity.description: For Newtonian fluids, the dynamic viscosity coefficient η relates shear stress τ and velocity gradient du/dy linearly: τ = η du/dy. Kinematic viscosity ν = η/ρ, where ρ is density. In gases, viscosity increases with temperature (due to more intensive momentum transfer); in liquids, it usually decreases (weakening of intermolecular bonds). Non-Newtonian fluids show dependence of η on shear rate, described by rheological models (power-law, Bingham model). Viscosity plays a key role in hydrodynamics, forming the Reynolds number and determining laminar or turbulent regime.
viscosity.history: The concept of viscosity evolved from empirical observations to molecular kinetic theory. Isaac Newton in his 'Principia Mathematica' (1687) formulated the law of internal friction. In the 19th century, Claude-Louis Navier and George Stokes generalized it in the equations of fluid dynamics. The kinetic theory of gases (James Clerk Maxwell) connected viscosity with momentum transfer by molecules. Later, Ludwig Boltzmann (not from the list, but a key figure) derived an equation for the distribution function allowing calculation of viscosity; his work was continued by Maxwell and others.
viscosity.how_it_works: In liquids and gases, viscosity arises from momentum transfer between layers moving at different speeds. In gases, molecules fly freely from layer to layer, carrying momentum and equalizing speeds; in liquids, intermolecular forces dominate, making shear difficult. According to Newton's law of viscosity, shear stress is directly proportional to the transverse velocity gradient. In a microscopic picture, the viscosity of a gas can be expressed in terms of the mean free path and average molecular speed.
key_problems: Accurate prediction of the viscosity of complex fluids (petroleum, biological media) remains a computationally complex task requiring multiscale modeling.
viscosity.fun_fact: Glass is a liquid with enormous viscosity, but medieval windows are thicker at the bottom not because of flow, but because of imperfect blowing.

formulas.description: Newton's law of viscous friction
formulas.meaning: τ — shear stress (Pa), η — dynamic viscosity (N·s/m²), du/dy — velocity gradient (s⁻¹)

formulas.description: Kinematic viscosity
formulas.meaning: ν — kinematic viscosity (m²/s), η — dynamic viscosity, ρ — density (kg/m³)

protoplanetary_disk.name: protoplanetary disk
protoplanetary_disk.mini: A cosmic cradle of gas and dust, rotating around a young star, where planets are born — like dough turning into pastries on a spinning table.
protoplanetary_disk.practical_application: Observations of protoplanetary disks help understand how our Solar System formed and predict the architecture of exoplanetary systems.
protoplanetary_disk.description_popular: A protoplanetary disk consists of gas (mostly hydrogen) and dust, heated by the star's radiation. The inner part is hotter, there ices evaporate; the outer part is colder, where icy planets and comets form. Disks show gaps — traces of forming planets. Disk lifetime is several million years.
protoplanetary_disk.fun_fact_popular: Astronomers discovered a disk around the star HL Tauri with a clear ring structure, revealing planetary embryos.
protoplanetary_disk.description_simple: A protoplanetary disk is a rotating cloud of leftovers from star birth. Over time, planets, asteroids, and comets clump together from this cloud. It's like a whirlpool in a puddle after rain, only the material gathers into lumps.
protoplanetary_disk.history_simple: The idea arose in the 18th century (Laplace's hypothesis). Nowadays, telescopes like Hubble observe disks.
protoplanetary_disk.how_it_works_simple: The star's gravity attracts matter, and rotation makes it flatten into a pancake. In this pancake, dust collides, sticks together, and gradually grows into larger bodies.
protoplanetary_disk.description: A protoplanetary disk is an accretion disk around a young star in which planet formation occurs. It forms from the remnants of a molecular cloud that collapses under gravity. Due to angular momentum conservation, the matter does not fall directly onto the star but forms a rotating disk. Temperature and chemical composition depend on distance from the star: inner regions are rich in metals and silicates, outer regions in ices. Disk dynamics include accretion onto the star, turbulence, radial matter transport, and particle growth from submicron dust grains to planetesimals. Processes: dust sedimentation, coagulation, instabilities. Observed in infrared and (sub)millimeter wavelengths (e.g., ALMA).
protoplanetary_disk.history: The concept of the protoplanetary disk goes back to the nebular hypothesis of I. Kant (1755) and P. Laplace (1796). In the 20th century, the theory was developed by V. S. Safronov, who mathematically described planet accumulation. Observational confirmations came with space telescopes: IRAS (1980s) detected an excess of infrared radiation from young stars, and Hubble and ALMA directly imaged disks.
protoplanetary_disk.how_it_works: Disk formation is a consequence of angular momentum conservation during the collapse of a rotating cloud. Gas and dust falling to the center increase their rotation speed, and centrifugal force in the equatorial plane balances gravity, forming a disk. Further evolution: dust settles to the midplane, grows through sticking. When planetesimals reach ~km size, gravitational focusing kicks in. In the gas phase, planetary migration is possible. Chemical transformations occur in the disk (beyond the ice line). The disk phase ends via photoevaporation or accretion, leaving a planetary system.
key_problems: Mechanism for overcoming the meter-size barrier in planetesimal growth
Influence of magnetic fields and turbulence on disk evolution and accretion
Causes of short disk lifetimes (~3–10 Myr) and their dispersal
protoplanetary_disk.fun_fact: In the protoplanetary disk of the star HD 100546, water snow was detected, indicating the existence of a broad 'ice line' — the boundary where water freezes.

formulas.description: Keplerian rotation speed in the disk at distance r from a star of mass M_*
formulas.meaning: v_K — orbital velocity (m/s), G — gravitational constant (≈6.67×10^{-11} m^3 kg^{-1} s^{-2}), M_* — central star mass (kg), r — distance from star (m).

formulas.description: Disk scale height in hydrostatic equilibrium
formulas.meaning: H — vertical half-thickness of the disk (m), c_s — sound speed in the gas (m/s), Ω_K — Keplerian angular velocity (rad/s, Ω_K = v_K/r).

largescale_structure_of_the_universe.name: large-scale structure of the universe
largescale_structure_of_the_universe.mini: The cosmic web — filaments of galaxies surrounding voids, like soap foam stretched to the size of the Universe.
largescale_structure_of_the_universe.practical_application: Maps of large-scale structure are used to test cosmological models and constrain the properties of dark matter and dark energy.
largescale_structure_of_the_universe.description_popular: Large-scale structure is the cellular distribution of galaxies on scales of hundreds of millions of light-years. It arose from primordial quantum fluctuations stretched by inflation. Under gravity, dark matter collapsed into halos, and gas cooled to form galaxies. Characteristic elements: filaments (threads up to 150 Mpc long), voids (empty regions 20–50 Mpc across), superclusters. The structure resembles a void-filament network.
largescale_structure_of_the_universe.fun_fact_popular: The Hercules–Corona Borealis Great Wall is one of the largest known structures, spanning 10 billion light-years.
largescale_structure_of_the_universe.description_simple: The Universe is not uniform: galaxies gather into long filaments and walls, and between them are huge voids. It's like a sponge with holes. Gravity created this picture, pulling together dark matter (invisible substance) and gas.
largescale_structure_of_the_universe.history_simple: Early hints were found in the 1970s, and 3D maps were built in the 1980s with the CfA survey.
largescale_structure_of_the_universe.how_it_works_simple: After the Big Bang, tiny clumps of dark matter attracted material, gradually forming lumps that stretched into fibers. Where filaments intersected, galaxy clusters arose.
largescale_structure_of_the_universe.description: The large-scale structure of the Universe is a complex web-like configuration of matter distribution on scales >10 Mpc. It consists of filaments, walls, and nodes where galaxy clusters are concentrated, as well as vast low-density voids. The structure was formed by primordial density perturbations that left an imprint on the cosmic microwave background at recombination. Subsequent growth via gravitational instability led to the observed network. Dark matter, forming the scaffolding, and dark energy, governing global expansion dynamics, play crucial roles. It is studied through redshift surveys (SDSS, 2dF). Statistical characteristics: correlation function, power spectrum.
largescale_structure_of_the_universe.history: The idea of a hierarchical structure was first voiced by I. Kant and P. Laplace. In the 20th century, E. Hubble confirmed that galaxies lie outside the Milky Way. The first galaxy distribution maps began in the 1950s (Shapley, Ames). In the 1970s, Ya. Zeldovich developed the theory of structure formation (Zeldovich pancakes). Modern understanding began with the CfA survey (1980s), which discovered the Great Wall. Contributions were also made by F. Zwicky and V. Rubin, who discovered dark matter in clusters and galaxies.
largescale_structure_of_the_universe.how_it_works: Primordial adiabatic density perturbations, arising during inflation, begin growing under gravity after recombination. Dark matter, not interacting electromagnetically, collapses first, creating potential wells. Baryonic matter falls into these wells, heats, cools, and forms galaxies. On scales of ~100 Mpc, a characteristic cellular pattern emerges, explained within the ΛCDM model. According to the theory, small-scale perturbations collapse earlier, forming substructure; larger ones form later — hierarchical clustering. Evolution is described by the Friedmann equations for the background and Newtonian theory for perturbations. Baryon acoustic oscillations — sound waves in the primordial plasma that left an imprint on the distribution statistics at ~150 Mpc — are important.
key_problems: Nature of dark matter and dark energy that determine structure
Cusp problem and missing satellites problem in halo modeling
Impact of feedback from active nuclei and supernovae on galaxy formation
largescale_structure_of_the_universe.fun_fact: The Boötes Void is one of the largest voids, so huge that our Galaxy could be lost inside it without being noticeable from outside.

formulas.description: Hubble's law for the expansion of the Universe
formulas.meaning: v — galaxy recession velocity (km/s), H_0 — Hubble constant (≈70 (km/s)/Mpc), d — distance to galaxy (Mpc).

formulas.description: Critical density of the Universe, separating open and closed models
formulas.meaning: ρ_c — critical density (kg/m³), H_0 — Hubble constant (s^{-1} in SI units), G — gravitational constant (≈6.67×10^{-11} m³ kg^{-1} s^{-2}).

baryon_acoustic_oscillations.name: baryon acoustic oscillations
baryon_acoustic_oscillations.mini: Frozen sound waves of the infant Universe, forever imprinted in the distribution of galaxies — like ripples from a stone thrown into a pond, instantly turned into ice.
baryon_acoustic_oscillations.practical_application: Serve as a standard ruler for measuring cosmic distances and the expansion rate of the Universe, helping to uncover the nature of dark energy.
baryon_acoustic_oscillations.description_popular: Baryon acoustic oscillations are periodic density perturbations of baryonic matter in the early Universe, generated by acoustic waves in the photon-baryon plasma before recombination. The waves had a certain maximum length — the acoustic horizon (about 150 Mpc in current units), which after recombination became a standard ruler. In galaxy distribution, this manifests as a slight increase in the probability of finding two galaxies at a separation of ~150 Mpc. By measuring this scale at different epochs, cosmologists refine the parameters of the Universe's expansion and dark energy.
baryon_acoustic_oscillations.fun_fact_popular: The speed of sound in the primordial plasma reached 57% of the speed of light — faster than any modern sound in matter.
baryon_acoustic_oscillations.description_simple: Right after the Big Bang, the Universe was a hot plasma of baryons (particles of ordinary matter — protons and neutrons) and photons (light). Gravity pulled matter together, and light pressure pushed it back, creating sound waves — baryon acoustic oscillations. After 380,000 years, the plasma cooled, turned into neutral atoms, and the waves 'froze', leaving an imprint: galaxy clumps are slightly more likely at a certain distance from each other (about 500 million light-years), like circles on water becoming solid.
baryon_acoustic_oscillations.history_simple: American astrophysicist Jim Peebles and Russian scientist Yuri Zeldovich predicted this effect in the 1970s, and in 2005 it was first clearly detected in data from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey.
baryon_acoustic_oscillations.how_it_works_simple: In the ancient plasma, gravity compresses matter, but light pushes it back — oscillations begin, like in a musical instrument. When the Universe suddenly becomes transparent (the recombination epoch), light escapes, and the 'sound' freezes. The loudest peak of these waves sets a standard scale that we see as a preferred separation of galaxies.
baryon_acoustic_oscillations.description: Baryon acoustic oscillations are scale-dependent density inhomogeneities in visible matter, formed by the competition between gravitational attraction and radiation pressure in the hot plasma of the early Universe. At recombination (z ≈ 1100), photons decoupled from baryons, and the oscillation pattern froze. The characteristic scale of oscillations — the sound horizon r_s — is computed by integrating the sound speed up to recombination and is ~147 Mpc. In the modern galaxy distribution, a peak in the two-point correlation function at this scale is observed, as well as acoustic peaks in the CMB power spectrum. By measuring BAO at different redshifts, one constructs the Hubble diagram and constrains the equation of state of dark energy.
baryon_acoustic_oscillations.history: The concept of sound waves in prestellar matter dates back to work by Evgeny Lifshitz (1946) on gravitational instability. In the 1970s, Jim Peebles and Yuri Zeldovich predicted that these waves would leave an observable trace. The first convincing detection of the BAO peak was made in 2005 by the SDSS collaboration using a sample of red galaxies. Research on nucleosynthesis by George Gamow and Ralph Alpher played a key role in determining the composition of the primordial plasma, which allowed calculation of oscillation parameters.
baryon_acoustic_oscillations.how_it_works: Before recombination, baryons and photons are tightly coupled by scattering. Gravitational potentials of dark matter attract the baryon-photon fluid; compression increases photon pressure, causing a rebound — this is an acoustic wave. Its speed is determined by the density ratio of baryons to photons: c_s = c / sqrt(3(1+R)), where R = 3ρ_b/4ρ_γ ≈ 0.6 at recombination. By the time of deionization, different oscillation modes freeze in various phases, creating a series of peaks in the power spectrum. The dominant peak corresponds to the wave that traveled a distance r_s ≈ ∫ c_s dt / a(t) from the Big Bang to recombination. Today this scale is seen as a preferred distance between galaxies. Measuring the BAO peak in radial and transverse directions yields the Hubble parameter H(z) and angular diameter distance D_A(z), which is critically important for testing dark energy models.
key_problems: Systematic errors in photometric redshifts limit the accuracy of the Hubble diagram
Nonlinear structure evolution and galaxy biases blur and slightly shift the BAO peak
Uncertainty in the sound horizon value requires independent calibrations from the early Universe, e.g., CMB data
baryon_acoustic_oscillations.fun_fact: In 2014, BAO data detected the accelerated expansion stage of the Universe at a significance level above 5σ independently of type Ia supernovae.

formulas.description: Sound speed in the photon-baryon plasma
formulas.meaning: c_s — sound speed, c — speed of light (c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s), R = 3ρ_b/4ρ_γ — ratio of baryon density ρ_b to photon density ρ_γ, multiplied by 3/4.

turbulence.name: turbulence
turbulence.mini: Vortex chaos in a flow: when a liquid or gas loses smoothness and breaks down into a cascade of eddies, like milk foam in morning coffee.
turbulence.practical_application: Affects wing lift, stability of gas pipelines, mixing of ocean currents, and design of low-drag automobiles.
turbulence.description_popular: Turbulence is a regime of motion of a fluid or gaseous medium characterized by irregular fluctuations in velocity, pressure, and density fields. Flow becomes turbulent when a critical value of the Reynolds number (Re) is exceeded, which expresses the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces. For pipe flows, Re_cr ≈ 2300. In a developed turbulent flow, an energy cascade from large scales to small scales is observed; in the inertial subrange, the Kolmogorov–Obukhov law for the energy spectrum E(k) ∝ k^{-5/3} holds. Turbulence increases frictional drag but enhances heat and mass transfer.
turbulence.fun_fact_popular: The flight of the bumblebee was long considered paradoxical: according to steady aerodynamics, its wings do not generate enough lift. Actually, the bumblebee uses turbulent vortices created by sharp wing flaps.
turbulence.description_simple: Turbulence is such a flow of liquid or gas where particles move not in an orderly, layered manner, but chaotically, with eddies. In a calm (laminar) flow, a stream of water is smooth, but in a turbulent one it breaks into swirling vortices. The faster it flows and the lower the viscosity (flow resistance), the easier turbulence arises. An example is water in a river at a rapid: near stones it boils and foams, mixing with air.
turbulence.history_simple: In 1883, English engineer Osborne Reynolds conducted an experiment: he introduced a thin stream of dye into a pipe with water and saw that at slow flow the dye traveled as a smooth thread, while at fast flow it blurred into chaos. Thus he described the transition to turbulence and introduced a number that helps predict this transition.
turbulence.how_it_works_simple: Large vortices are unstable and break down into smaller ones, those into even smaller ones, and so on until friction grinds the motion into heat. This process resembles a cascade: the energy of a waterfall splits into jets, spray, and foam until the water calms down.
turbulence.description: Turbulence is a complex nonlinear phenomenon described by the Navier–Stokes equations for viscous fluid motion. At Reynolds numbers above critical, the laminar solution loses stability, and small perturbations grow, generating chaotic motion. Turbulence energy is produced at large scales (integral scale L) by shear stresses and cascades down the spectrum to the dissipative scale (Kolmogorov scale η), where viscosity turns kinetic energy into heat. In the inertial subrange (η ≪ r ≪ L), the only governing parameter is the energy dissipation rate ε, and dimensional analysis yields the famous '–5/3' law. Direct numerical simulation (DNS) requires resolving all scales, making calculations extremely expensive at high Re; hence, large-eddy simulation (LES) and Reynolds-averaged methods (RANS) are used. In astrophysics, turbulence plays a key role in accretion disks, star formation, and the interstellar medium.
turbulence.history: Osborne Reynolds in 1883 performed the famous experiment with a dyed stream and introduced the dimensionless parameter (Reynolds number) that determines the transition to turbulence. In 1941, Andrey Kolmogorov created the statistical theory of locally isotropic turbulence, postulating the universality of the inertial subrange. Experimental confirmations of the k^{-5/3} spectrum were obtained in the 1950s–60s.
turbulence.how_it_works: The mechanism of the turbulent cascade: energy is injected into the flow at large scales (e.g., by obstacle flow) and through a sequence of vortex breakdowns is transferred to ever smaller scales. In the inertial subrange, far from both source and dissipation, statistics are homogeneous and isotropic, and the energy spectrum depends only on wavenumber k and dissipation rate ε: E(k) = C ε^{2/3} k^{-5/3}, with constant C ≈ 1.5. Dissipation occurs at scales on the order of the Kolmogorov length η = (ν^3/ε)^{1/4}, where ν is kinematic viscosity. Transition to turbulence is associated with instability of the laminar profile: at Re > Re_cr, small perturbations grow exponentially, forming secondary flows and ultimately chaos. Mathematical difficulties arise because the Navier–Stokes equations are nonlinear and do not admit a general analytical solution; the problem of turbulence closure remains one of the most important unsolved problems of classical physics.
key_problems: General analytical solution of the Navier–Stokes equations for turbulent flows remains unknown (millennium problem)
Closure problem: in averaged models, correlations arise that require semi-empirical hypotheses
The huge gap between the Kolmogorov length scale and the integral scale makes DNS impractical at high Re
turbulence.fun_fact: Even in fully developed turbulent flow, there exist thin regions with very strong vorticity fluctuations — vortex filaments — which may be the cause of anomalous dissipation.

formulas.description: Reynolds number
formulas.meaning: Re — Reynolds number, ρ — fluid density, u — characteristic flow velocity, L — characteristic linear dimension, μ — dynamic viscosity of the fluid. Transition to turbulence usually occurs at Re > 2300 in pipes.

formulas.description: Energy spectrum in the inertial subrange (Kolmogorov law)
formulas.meaning: E(k) — spectral density of kinetic energy per unit mass as a function of wavenumber k (k = 2π/λ, where λ is the vortex size), ε — energy dissipation rate per unit mass, C — Kolmogorov constant (usually about 1.5).

fourier_series.name: Fourier series
fourier_series.mini: The musical analyzer of mathematics: it breaks any complex periodic signal — from ocean tides to electrical oscillations — into elementary sinusoidal notes.
fourier_series.practical_application: Audio and image compression (MP3, JPEG), vibration analysis in structures, seismic data processing, and quantum computing.
fourier_series.description_popular: A Fourier series is a representation of a periodic function f(x) with period T as a sum of trigonometric functions: f(x) = a0/2 + Σ[a_n cos(2π n x/T) + b_n sin(2π n x/T)]. The coefficients a_n and b_n are calculated as projections of the function onto the corresponding cosine and sine waves and show how strongly each harmonic is expressed in the original signal. The expansion is possible due to the orthogonality of sines and cosines. Fourier series are a powerful tool for solving differential equations (e.g., the heat equation) and underpin spectral analysis.
fourier_series.fun_fact_popular: When expanding a rectangular signal into a Fourier series, the sum of all odd harmonics yields a staircase that approaches a rectangle as the number of terms increases, but near the discontinuity, 'bumps' of about 9% of the signal amplitude remain — this is the Gibbs phenomenon, occurring in any series with discontinuities.
fourier_series.description_simple: A Fourier series is a way to write any periodic (repeating) function as a sum of simple waves — sines and cosines — with different frequency, amplitude, and phase. Just as an artist mixes basic colors to obtain a complex hue, a Fourier series mixes simple waves to recreate a complex shape. For example, the sound of a guitar string is not one note, but a fundamental tone plus many overtones (harmonics) that together create the timbre.
fourier_series.history_simple: French mathematician Jean-Baptiste Joseph Fourier developed this method in the early 19th century while studying how heat propagates in solid bodies.
fourier_series.how_it_works_simple: Imagine swinging on a swing: our motion repeats (is periodic). The Fourier series says: this motion can be assembled from several pendulums swinging at different speeds — one slow, another twice as fast, a third three times as fast, and so on. If we correctly adjust the amplitudes (weights) of these pendulums, their sum will give our trajectory.
fourier_series.description: The classical Fourier series is the expansion of a function f(x), integrable on the interval [-π, π], into a complete orthogonal system of functions {1, cos(nx), sin(nx)}n∈N, possessing the property of best approximation in the sense of mean square deviation. The coefficients are calculated by the formulas a_n = (1/π)∫ f(x) cos(nx) dx, b_n = (1/π)∫ f(x) sin(nx) dx. In complex form, this is equivalent to expansion in exponentials e^{inx}, linking the Fourier series to the Fourier transform. For the series to converge to f(x) at point x, Dirichlet conditions must be satisfied (piecewise monotonicity and a finite number of discontinuities). In practice, the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) is widely used — an algorithm that computes coefficients in O(N log N) operations. Fourier series are generalized to arbitrary orthonormal systems in Hilbert spaces, applied in quantum mechanics (expansion of the wave function over eigenstates) and signal processing.
fourier_series.history: The origins of the idea go back to Daniel Bernoulli (1753), who represented string motion as a sum of harmonics. Jean-Baptiste Joseph Fourier in 1807, in his memoir on heat conduction, systematically used trigonometric series and proposed a method for computing coefficients. His work 'The Analytical Theory of Heat' (1822) established this apparatus, despite criticism from contemporaries for lack of rigor. A rigorous convergence theory was built by Peter Gustav Lejeune Dirichlet in 1829, and later developed by Riemann, Lebesgue, and others.
fourier_series.how_it_works: When expanding f(x), harmonics with numbers n form a spectrum. Low n correspond to slow changes, high n to fast details. The integrals for coefficients a_n and b_n are the scalar product of the function and the cosine/sine; thanks to the orthogonality of the basis ∫ cos(nx) cos(mx) = 0 for n≠m, each harmonic 'picks out' only its part of the signal. The sum of all harmonics gives the original function almost everywhere, and when truncated to a finite number, ensures minimum mean square error. The Fast Fourier Transform exploits the symmetry of complex roots of unity, recursively reducing the computation of an N-point DFT to two N/2-point ones, dramatically accelerating processing. In physics, harmonic decomposition is used for time series analysis (astronomy), mode composition of laser beams, plasma diagnostics, and solving Schrödinger equations.
key_problems: Gibbs phenomenon: with a finite number of terms, overshoots remain at the discontinuity and do not vanish with increasing N
Expansion of functions not satisfying Dirichlet conditions may diverge at individual points
Need to adapt to non-periodic signals via windowed Fourier transform or wavelets
fourier_series.fun_fact: The fast Cooley–Tukey algorithm (1965) revolutionized digital processing; according to some estimates, FFT is among the top five most used algorithms in the world — every time you listen to music or watch a video, FFT is at work.

formulas.description: Fourier series expansion in real form
formulas.latex: f(x) = \frac{a_0}{2} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left( a_n \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n x}{T}\right) + b_n \sin\left(\frac{2\pi n x}{T}\right) \right)
formulas.meaning: f(x) — periodic function with period T, a_0/2 — average value of the function over the period, a_n and b_n — Fourier coefficients, x — argument (e.g., time or coordinate), n — harmonic number (integer).

formulas.description: Formulas for Fourier coefficients
formulas.latex: a_n = \frac{2}{T} \int_0^T f(x) \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n x}{T}\right) dx, \quad b_n = \frac{2}{T} \int_0^T f(x) \sin\left(\frac{2\pi n x}{T}\right) dx
formulas.meaning: a_n and b_n — amplitudes of the nth cosine and sine components respectively, T — period of the function, f(x) — original function, n — integer (harmonic number). The integral is taken over one full period.

functional_analysis.name: functional analysis
functional_analysis.mini: The study of spaces where functions are points in geometry. An entire melody becomes a single point, and chords become vectors.
functional_analysis.practical_application: Quantum mechanics: particle states are described by vectors in functional spaces. Image compression: reconstructing a picture from a few measurements.
functional_analysis.description_popular: Functional analysis is a branch of mathematics where functions are viewed as points in infinite-dimensional spaces (e.g., Hilbert and Banach spaces). In these spaces, analogs of distance (norm) and angle (inner product) are defined, and linear operators (mappings that transform one function into another) play the role of matrices from linear algebra. The central idea is the study of the properties of operators and their spectra (generalization of eigenvalues).
functional_analysis.fun_fact_popular: Quantum mechanics was mathematically formulated in the language of functional analysis: a particle's state is a vector in Hilbert space, and physical quantities are operators.
functional_analysis.description_simple: Functional analysis studies functions (dependencies of one quantity on another) as elements of infinite-dimensional spaces (a space with an infinite number of dimensions, such as the set of all possible melodies), similarly to how geometry studies points in a three-dimensional world. It provides tools for working with such spaces, including the concepts of distance (metric) and angle (inner product).
functional_analysis.history_simple: In the early 20th century, mathematicians including David Hilbert and Stefan Banach laid the foundations of functional analysis to solve integral and differential equations.
functional_analysis.how_it_works_simple: Just as a vector (having length and direction) represents a force in physics, functional analysis describes functions as vectors in an abstract space, allowing measurement of their 'length' and 'closeness'.
functional_analysis.description: Functional analysis is a field of mathematics studying topological vector spaces and their mappings. Main objects: Banach and Hilbert spaces, normed and metric spaces, operators (linear, compact, self-adjoint), spectral theory, dual spaces. Key theorems: Hahn–Banach, uniform boundedness principle (Banach–Steinhaus theorem), open mapping theorem, closed graph theorem. The spectral theorem for self-adjoint operators generalizes matrix diagonalization to the infinite-dimensional case, which is critical for quantum mechanics. C*-algebras, distribution theory (generalized functions), and ergodic theory are also studied. Functional analysis finds applications in differential equations, quantum physics, optimization theory, and machine learning.
functional_analysis.history: The roots of functional analysis lie in the works of Volterra, Fredholm, and Hilbert on integral equations in the late 19th century. A decisive contribution was made by Stefan Banach, who in the 1920s systematized the theory of normed spaces (Banach spaces) and proved fundamental theorems. John von Neumann in the 1930s introduced the concept of Hilbert space for a rigorous formulation of quantum mechanics. Development continued in the works of Gelfand, Naimark, Pontryagin, and others.
functional_analysis.how_it_works: Functional analysis investigates the structure of infinite-dimensional spaces by introducing topologies (notion of closeness) and algebraic operations. Just as in the finite-dimensional case linear operators are represented by matrices, in functional analysis operators on functions can be integral, differential, or more general. The spectral theorem (for self-adjoint operators in Hilbert space) asserts that an operator can be diagonalized in a suitable basis, meaning decomposition into eigenfunctions. The Schrödinger equation from quantum mechanics is naturally viewed as an eigenvalue problem for the Hamiltonian operator in the Hilbert space of states.
key_problems: The invariant subspace problem: does every bounded linear operator on a Hilbert space have a non-trivial closed invariant subspace?
The approximation problem: can every compact operator be approximated uniformly by finite-dimensional operators?
The conjecture that every C*-algebra is isometrically embeddable into a von Neumann factor.
functional_analysis.fun_fact: Stefan Banach's early works on functional analysis were written during the Friendly Meetings of Mathematicians at the Scottish Café in Lviv, where he often worked with colleagues, and many proofs were born right on the marble tabletops.

formulas.description: Cauchy–Bunyakovsky–Schwarz inequality for Hilbert space
formulas.meaning: ⟨x,y⟩ is the inner product of vectors x and y, ‖x‖ is the norm of vector x (square root of the inner product with itself).

markov_chain.name: Markov chain
markov_chain.mini: Weather forecast: tomorrow hardly remembers what happened a week ago and depends only on today's state. The future is determined by the present, not the past.
markov_chain.practical_application: Markov chains model queues in stores, search engine algorithms (Google's PageRank), random walks of stock prices, and text generation by artificial intelligence.
markov_chain.description_popular: A Markov chain is a stochastic model describing a sequence of states in which the probability of transitioning to the next state depends solely on the current state (Markov property). Formally: P(X_{n+1}=x | X_n=x_n,...,X_0=x_0) = P(X_{n+1}=x | X_n=x_n). Chains can be discrete or continuous in time, with a finite or countable number of states. Main tools: transition probability matrix, stationary distribution, Chapman–Kolmogorov equations.
markov_chain.fun_fact_popular: The first practical application of Markov chains was the analysis of the alternation of vowels and consonants in "Eugene Onegin" — Markov showed that it is not random and predicted future letters of the text.
markov_chain.description_simple: A Markov chain is a sequence of events where each new event depends only on the previous one, but not on the history beyond that. For example, if you are playing a board game, your next move depends only on the current square of the board, not on how you got there. The chain is defined by states (possible squares) and transition probabilities (chances of jumping from one state to another).
markov_chain.history_simple: Russian mathematician Andrey Markov in the early 20th century studied sequences of dependent random variables and introduced the concept of a chain to show that even in random texts, statistical regularities can be found.
markov_chain.how_it_works_simple: Like in the game "Snake" on a phone, where the snake moves across cells, choosing a direction randomly, but only from the current position. If you know where it is now, it doesn't matter how it got there — this is the memoryless property.
markov_chain.description: A Markov chain is a random process with discrete time and a finite or countable state space, possessing the Markov property: the conditional distribution of future states depends only on the current state and not on the past trajectory. It is defined by an initial distribution and a transition probability matrix P = (p_{ij}), where p_{ij} = P(X_{n+1}=j | X_n=i). The chain is called homogeneous if p_{ij} do not depend on n. Key concepts: classification of states (recurrent, transient, periodicity), ergodicity (existence of a limiting distribution), stationary distribution π (πP = π). For irreducible aperiodic finite chains, there exists a unique stationary distribution to which the state distributions converge. Continuous-time Markov chains are described by an intensity matrix Q. Applications: Metropolis–Hastings algorithm in Monte Carlo, hidden Markov models in speech recognition, queueing theory.
markov_chain.history: Andrey Andreyevich Markov introduced chains in 1906 while studying the law of large numbers for dependent quantities. He was motivated by a desire to refute Nekrasov's claim that independence is necessary for statistical laws. Later, in the 1930s, Kolmogorov developed the general theory of Markov processes. In the mid-20th century, Markov chains became a central tool in operations research, physics (Ising model), and computer science (text generation, PageRank).
markov_chain.how_it_works: Mathematically, a Markov chain is defined by an initial probability vector π^{(0)} and a stochastic matrix P. Then the distribution after n steps: π^{(n)} = π^{(0)} P^n. The memoryless property means that to predict the future, it is sufficient to know only the current state. The long-term behavior is determined by the spectral decomposition of the matrix P: if there exists a unique left eigenvector with eigenvalue 1 (the stationary distribution), then π^{(n)} → π as n→∞. Chapman–Kolmogorov equations: P^{(n+m)} = P^{(n)} P^{(m)}, expressing the composition of transitions. For continuous time: dπ/dt = πQ, where Q is the transition intensity matrix.
key_problems: The problem of accelerating convergence for chains with slow mixing (e.g., in high-dimensional distributions with sharp peaks).
Development of efficient algorithms for estimating probabilities of rare events in complex systems.
Analysis of hidden Markov models with high-dimensional continuous hidden states.
markov_chain.fun_fact: Markov chains underlie the PageRank algorithm that originally brought success to Google: web pages are modeled as states, transitions are hyperlinks; the page rank is the stationary probability of visiting during a random walk with return.

formulas.description: Chapman–Kolmogorov equation
formulas.meaning: p_{ij}^{(n)} is the probability of transitioning from state i to state j in n steps; summation over all intermediate states k.

modular_form.name: modular form
modular_form.mini: Symmetry of a pattern on a tile: you rotate it — the pattern is unchanged. Only the tile is the upper half-plane, and the transformations are fractional-linear.
modular_form.practical_application: Proof of Fermat's Last Theorem, error-correcting codes, foundation for string theory and quantum invariants.
